title: Doing Anthropological Fieldwork
description: In this course we will not only read anthropology, but also do anthropology. Students will learn about field research design and methodology, as well as conduct their own semester-long field-research projects. In order to acquire the skills necessary for participant observation, we will learn how cultural anthropologists select a research topic, survey a field site, design the study, pose theoretical questions, carry out the research, keep field notes, analyze ethnographic data, and then finally, write an ethnography. We will also read examples of ethnography and other forms of anthropological writing such as testimonials and life histories.

title: On the Move: Migration, Labor and Rights
description: This seminar combines theory, history, fiction, and ethnography. We begin with historical and theoretical approaches to understanding why people move. We read ethnography to spotlight the lived human experience of migration. We will learn about migrants' border crossings, building of new communities, experiences in the workplace, and how migration reconfigures and reconfirms power dynamics in the household. The course will examine issues of race, ethnicity, gender, generation, class, sexuality, and religion through the process of migration.

title: Ethnographic Imagination
description: Using ethnographic case studies from a wide range of regions, this course provides an overview of anthropological approaches. Cultural analysis, historical interpretation, and well-known debates in anthropology are covered by reading classics as well as more experimental contemporary monographs. Through readings and class discussion, students are encouraged to consider numerous schools of anthropological thought, and how methodology interrelates with theory. Functional analysis, life histories, collaborative âaction anthropologyâ projects, multi-site studies and âauto-ethnographyâ are included in the range of anthropological styles. Themes critical to our understanding of current world issues are featured, including identity politics, ârace,â gender, nationalism, urban dislocation, development, ecology, violence and globalization.

title: The Ethnographic Imagination
description: An overview of the ways in which anthropologists have studied and written about cultural systems in a number of world regions. Using ethnographic case studies, the course explores the nature of anthropological research, concentrating on various schools of thought and approaches to ethnography, including early functionalism and more contemporary ethnography that focuses on experimental writing, collaborative ethnography, and historical approaches to studying culture. The anthropologists we will be reading examine such issues as ârace,â political organization, gender roles, identity politics, the city, and violence.

title: Anthropology of War & Peace in Darfur
description: All eyes are now on Sudan following the arrest warrant for Sudanese president Omar al-Bashir in accordance with United Nations Security Council Resolution 1593, concerning alleged war crimes in Darfur. The UN Security Council referred the case to Luis Moreno-Ocampo, prosecutor of the International Criminal Court, after the investigation of Sudan's own special prosecutor had not led to war prosecutions, suggesting the failure of institutions of justice within the country. Events of this magnitude are to be expected given the pervasive political violence that engulfed the country following its independence from British rule in January 1956. Ever since, the Sudan was converted into a theatre of atrocity that shattered lives and rendered ordinary citizens perpetual refugees and internally displaced people in a vast territory, the largest in the African continent. Within this context, this course will examine multiple topics pertaining to culture, society and politics. It will probe in depth local, national, international, and transnational responses to the devastation of the region. We will pay considerable attention to questions of representation and the production of knowledge. Since these questions are fundamental to anthropological understanding, we will cover multiple forces that heed John Hall's argument regarding "Culture of Inquiry" in which he argues that contemporary conflicts over knowledge tend to reinforce ideological oppositions when there is an opportunity to understand the complex web of uneven connections that structure the entire range of inquiry's practices (1999). In this course we will examine forces pertaining to ethnicity, militarization, banditry, border politics, diasporic activism, and marginality. Explication of these forces is essential for overcoming the rampant oversimplification of the conflict. We will prope culture and politics and trace the development of a political militancy that overwhelmed social arrangements among peoples with long histories of intermixing in a complex geography.

title: Shamans, Priests and Healers
description: This seminar combines critical issues of medical and political anthropology, by focusing on a range of indigenous communities, their contested worldviews, changing healing practices, and spiritual leaders. Diverse and controversial questions are probed. What is the relationship between individual and community healing? How and why are concepts of body-mind integration significant cross-culturally? How are cultural revitalization movements begun and sustained? What are the flashpoints of intercultural conflict in colonial and post-colonial contexts? How do people perceive and discuss sources of religious and spiritual authority? What are the roots of âreligionâ? What is the shifting relevance of gender in religious leadership? Why are some religions considered âworld religionsâ while others are not? How can we compare and contrast shamans, priests, healers, and âmedicine men?â How are local and academic concepts of authenticity, confidentiality and secrecy constructed and debated? These questions are explored through a survey of anthropology literature, including indigenous scholarship, on âfolk healing,â spirituality, missionaries, sacred sites, and the politics of religion. Readings cover classics and recent literature, with emphasis on Eurasia and Native North America. Readings from other regions enhance comparisons. Analytical approaches feature in-depth study of selected cases, with sensitivity to cultural change, interethnic relations, and degrees of indigeneity. Students are welcome to bring special interests into our discussions through research projects and personal experience with indigenous, âintegrativeâ or âcomplementaryâ medicine.

title: Beyond Conflict, Trauma, and Suffering: Perspectives in Medical Anthropology
description: This course uses different forms of social suffering as an entry point to the study of medical anthropology. Drawing on a variety of genres, but with a focus on ethnography, we will analyze armed conflict, war, genocide, political and structural violence, migration, and human trafficking, as examples of social suffering. We will debate current issues in medical anthropology, including the social and political roots of disease and illness; the local intersection of the individual body, the community, and the state; survivor narratives of pain, loss, and trauma, and the ways that various public policies and interventions aimed at alleviating suffering can actually exacerbate it. We will also review the ethical and practical responsibilities of anthropologists and other social scientists as well as practitioners engaged in understanding and responding to different forms of human suffering. The course is taught as part of the Jesuit Humanitarian Action Network (JUHAN); see //cndls.georgetown.edu/juhan/ The format of the course will include lectures, classroom discussions, individual and group assignments (written and oral). The course should be of interest to undergraduate and graduate students in anthropology, sociology, psychology, nursing, public policy, Foreign Service, and law interested in forced migration, humanitarian relief, development, and human rights. This class is not a survey of medical anthropology; rather it uses medical anthropology frameworks to discuss social suffering of individuals, groups and communities and institutional responses to these problems.

title: African Culture and Foreign Policy
description: Professor Gwendolyn Mikell W 3:15 pm â 5:05 pm ICC 231 This seminar course takes an anthropological approach to contemporary cultural issues in U.S./African foreign policy, as it examines the intersections of cultural agency and national or internationally policies. We explore the dialogues between local, regional, and national levels of culture within Africa, using ethnographies and case studies from development anthropology, political anthropology, gender and development, and engaged anthropology to see what type of policy concerns have arisen over time. Then, using foreign policy articles and dialogues with invited foreign policy experts, we assess how American policy makers have conceptualized and implemented African policy. The state, which is central to our analysis, is not seen as homogenous or status, since it is often the focal point of competing cultural values and interest groups as countries respond to policy problems. The goal is to see the points of intersection between values and interests in U.S. official policy, African cultural realities, and African responses. Some of the foreign policy issues with which we are concerned include market and democracy pressures, HIV/AIDS and African health crises, âgenocideâ and anti-terrorism as security issues, gender and human rights, and Muslim/Christian dynamics. Students produce a research paper on a culture and foreign policy issue for an African country.

title: Anthropological Theory
description: Professor Joanne Rappaport M 5:15 pm â 7:05 pm ICC 210A This course will survey the major debates in sociocultural anthropology that have taken place over the past 75 years. Instead of reviewing the major texts in chronological order, the course will be organized around four anchor texts that are relatively recent, but will allow us to project both backward and forward in time to related debates and issues.

title: Anthropological Theory
description: This seminar will survey the major debates in sociocultural anthropology that have taken place over the past 75 years. Instead of reviewing the major texts in chronological order, the course will be organized around four anchor texts that are relatively recent, but will allow us to project both backward and forward in time to related debates and issues.

title: Women's Movements in the Arab World
description: NOTE: This course will be taught in Arabic. Women have been at the forefront of the âArab Springâ where they played a key leadership role. The dynamic participation of women in the Arab uprisings culminated in the awarding of the Nobel peace prize in 2011 to a female activist, Tawakel Karaman, who comes from a conservative Arab Muslim society, Yemen. This should invite us to re-examine the history of womenâs movements within the Arab World. This course aims to offer an overview of womenâs movements in the Arab World. It will provide students with a conceptual framework that will enable them to understand the successes and setbacks of various Arab womenâs movements. It will also investigate the evolution of womenâs movements in relation to the struggles for national liberation from colonialism/imperialism, and for political, socio-economic, and gender equality within Arab societies in the post-independence Arab states. The course will examine gender trends and womenâs movements in the Arab World by looking at womenâs changing roles as family members, citizens, and national leaders. The course will evolve around several key questions such as: â¢ How do we define womenâs movements in the Arab World, and are they different from feminist movements in other parts of the world? Some argue that âany womenâs organization can be called âfeministâ if it brings women out of the home into public life, and teaches them organizing skills. Others would argue that only those organizations that explicitly call for gender equality can be qualified as âfeminist.â (Rosemary Sayigh, 2003) How is this argument applicable to womenâs movements in the Arab World? â¢Is it more accurate to consider the rise of Muslim womenâs activism as a reaction to the influence of western feminism, or as a part of the Islamic revival in the region that started in the early 1980s and continues today? â¢What are the results of these movements? Specifically how are diverse societal groups in the Arab World reacting to them? The course will focus on contemporary Arab womenâs movements with some reference to the historical background of the earliest of these. Students will be reading original materials such as the writings of early women activists from Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, and Egypt, as well as statements, interviews, news reports and publications from contemporary women's groups. Other essential sources for the course will include publications of womenâs institutes including books and journal articles, and audio-video materials. Students will investigate different approaches to assessing the role of womenâs movements in the Arab World and should be able to develop original ideas and engage in critical thinking on womenâs activism and their struggle for gender equity. This should be reflected in their participation in class discussion, their research assignments, and their in-class presentations and writing. Selected Required Readings: 1. âArab Womenâs Movements.â Al-Raida Magazine: Published by the Institute for Womenâs Studies in the ArabWorld, LAU. Volume XX, No. 100. Winter 2003 2. âArab Feminism: A Critical View.â Edited by Jean Said Makdisi, Rafif Rida Sidawi, and Nuha Bayumi. Published by Center of Arab United Studies and the Lebanese Association of Women Researchers. Beirut. Lebanon. 2012 3. Ellen Fleischman. Jerusalem Womenâs Organizations During the British Mandate, 1920s â 1930s. Published by PASSIA [Palestinian Academic Society for the Study of International Affairs]. 4. Additional Required Readings: Papers and chapters on the topic from various books in Arabic will be available on E-RESERVE/Blackboard

title: Introduction to the Arab World: Theories and Methods
description: An introduction to the theoretical paradigms and the methodologies most appropriate to the study of the Arab World. Covers a spectrum of approaches from Islamic textual analysis and philologically based orientalism through liberal, radical paradigms to modern textual and discourse analysis.

title: Politics of Empire: Middle East
description: The 2003 Iraq war has led to renewed interest in the notion of âempireâ as a key dynamic in international politics. But in the Middle East, the question of empire never went away. For many people in the region, the colonial division of the Ottoman Empire into individual states under European tutelage, Western efforts to keep anti-Communist regimes in power during the Cold War, and the more recent phenomenon of globalization are all examples of the ongoing trend whereby the Middle East has been subjected to imperial domination by the West. This course aims to contextualize contemporary debates in IR with a historical overview of empire in the Middle East from the nineteenth century to the present day, as well as providing the conceptual tools to understand the workings of colonial and imperial power in the region. The course focuses on the changes introduced to Middle Eastern societies by colonialism, the implications of these changes for regional states after independence, and more recent socio-political developments sometimes categorized as neo-colonial forms of domination. While case studies will range from the French occupation of Algeria in 1830 to the US occupation of Iraq in 2003, the course will adopt a thematic rather than narrowly historical approach. Each session will consider one aspect of the empire debate (e.g. theories of imperialism, Orientalism, colonial knowledge, cultural political economy, counter-insurgency practices, space, urban development, neoliberalism, modernity, etc.). The seminars will look at case studies in light of relevant concepts and debates in international relations, political sociology and post-colonial theory, making the course suitable for students with interests in history, anthropology, or cultural studies, as well as political science and international relations.

title: Culture & Society: Arab World
description: This course introduces students to the study of contemporary Arab society, in both broad conceptual terms as well as through details of daily life. The course begins with some initial readings and discussion about anthropological approaches to the study of the Arab world. The class will cover different aspects of contemporary Arab society primarily through readings (mostly ethnographies), but at times drawing on films and other media. The course will be lead as a seminar, with the majority of the time devoted to discussion and critical understanding of the material in the texts. Some of the topics that the course will take up are: family, citizenship, honor, power, gender, âmodernityâ and art. The course will examine how these concepts and the meanings attached to them are understood and constructed locally â by a variety of actors who experience and understand them differently given where they are situated within the Arab world â even as the local is in turn shaped by national and global forces.

title: Human Rights Law in the Middle East
description: The 14-week course will be divided into three sections 1) human rights law in substance and theory; 2) case studies; and 3) practice. It will cover the doctrines that constitute international human rights law, the legal fora in which those laws are enforced and/or adjudicated, the controversies surrounding the application of human rights law in the Middle East, and the politics informing the discourse of human rights in the Middle East. The students are expected to take a very active role in the course by bringing in outside readings, participating in discussions, and preparing presentations for their fellow students.

title: Ethnic and Religious Minorities: Arab World
description: This seminar examines one of the major problems that the Arab world has faced for over two centuries. In the past three decades especially, this issue has become one of tremendous importance for the âsurvivalâ and âdurabilityâ of the nation-state in the Arab region. This course will stress the history and condition of minorities in the Arab world. It will put forward that minorities are still perceived as a threat and a challenge to the authority of Arab regimes that wish to maintain homogenous political, cultural and religious identities. Finally, it will emphasize that it is possible for Arab societies to develop notions of belonging/citizenship, which value participation in economic, social, and political life above ethnicity, religion, or place of birth. NOTE: This is a Content-Based Instruction (CBI) course, designed to give advanced Arabic students the opportunity to acquire strong language skills within their fields of study such as political science, history and economics, among others subjects.

title: Women and Gender in the Arab World
description: Possibly no issue related to the Arab world has garnered more attention than the status of women. Within the region, womenâs concerns have been at the center of very public and political debates and struggles for at least more than a century. This course will situate the current preoccupation with women in the Arab world and the terms of public discourse on âwomenâs issuesâ in the Arab world and beyond, in historical and comparative perspective. Furthermore, it will draw on a variety of disciplines to examine the status, experiences, and desires of women from distinctly different positions within the Arab world â class, ethnic, geographic, religious, etc. Although primarily focused on women, the course will also address issues of males and masculinity, gender relations and sexuality. Finally, the course will also situate the fields of women and gender studies in the Arab world in the larger debates and shifts within feminist theory, examining the ways in which scholarship in and about the region has emerged in conversation with broader feminist theories and projects. This course is a graduate level seminar, which assumes some prior coursework related to the history, society, and/or culture of the region. Advanced level undergraduates will also be considered for enrollment in the course. Specific goals for students in the course are to: 1. Understand the ways in which historical, political and socio-economic contexts shape the experiences (opportunities and limits) of differently situated women. Students will develop these skills through careful reading of rich and varied sources from a range of disciplines, class discussions, and analytical writing. 2. Develop the analytical skills needed to identify basic assumptions that undergird discussions about gender in the Arab world. Students will develop these skills through close and careful reading, historical and comparative analysis, class discussions, and analytical writing. 3. Apply tools of gendered analysis to a wide range of social, political and cultural experiences. 4. Analyze and interpret a variety of âcultural productsâ for the ways in which they represent gender differences, transformations and conflicts. 5. Develop a more nuanced understanding of the ways in which the experience of males in the regional are gendered and how their gendered experiences are intertwined with those of women. 6. Through research, writing, and presentations, develop specific knowledge and expertise about a particular group or community, and/or the gendered dimensions of a specific issue of interest to the student.

title: Refugees: Middle East and North Africa
description: This course will focus on displaced peoples and refugees in the Arab World, using three areas as case studies: Sudan, Palestine, and Iraq, and additional material on the Western Sahara and Somalia. By focusing on humanitarianism, human agency, and aid, the course is designed to encourage us to re-think how we conceive of displaced people within academic, development, and policy perspectives. We will address issues concerned with citizenship, borders, rights, law, human agency, and history, with the intent to include all of these into our perspectives on how we understand displaced people, talk about them, advocate for them, develop policies around them, and "manage" them.

title: Business and Politics in the Arab World
description: Nearly thirty years of neoliberal market reforms have led to the emergence of new business elites in the Arab countries. Arab billionaires hold enormous economic power, they have gained great influence on the media, and business elites have been significant actors in the âArab springâ. The course will review the historical development of Arab capitalists from the 1950s to the present day. Students will use theories from political economy and political sociology to analyse the changing role of business elites in Arab politics.

title: Revolution and Prospects for Change: Arab World
description: NOTE: This is a Content-Based Instruction (CBI) course, designed to give advanced Arabic students the opportunity to acquire strong language skills within their fields of study, such as political science and economics, among others. On January 14, 2011, the Zine Al-Abidine Ben Ali autocratic rule of Tunisia came to an end after 23 years of reign. Less than one month later, Husni Mubarak, the President of Egypt was forced to resign on February 11, 2011, after nearly 30 years in power. Six months later, the Libyan dictator, Muammar al-Gaddafi, who was in power for almost 42 years, was ousted as a result of an armed uprising that costs thousands of victims among Libyan people and an unprecedented destruction of the infrastructure of the country. Meanwhile, Bahrain, Syria, Yemen, Jordan and Morocco have also experienced popular turmoil ranging from armed conflict to peaceful demonstrations. Though regimes in these countries have met these protests with a mix of brutal suppression and modest political and economic reforms, people are still struggling for the sake of dignity, freedom, human rights, equality and genuine political change. These events are still puzzling researchers and practitioners about their causes and implications at both regional and international levels. This class will focus on the many signposts on the road leading to these unrests and the demise of many of these regimes. It will emphasize the social background of actors among the movements and groups who have actively led these popular revolts in the Arab world. It will explore how mainly non-violent citizens across the region succeeded in toppling autocratic rulers, who were presumably stable and durable. The prospects and challenges for upcoming changes through political transition and the role of new elites in this process in countries such as Tunisia, Egypt, Libya and Yemen will be highlighted.

title: Politics of Syria
description: This course examines Syria's turbulent politics since the takeover of the Ba`th party in 1963, with emphasis on its political economy of development and reform, the correlate social transformations, and the background to the uprising. Other themes include Syria's intervention in Lebanon, the Muslim Brotherhood challenge, Syria's regional policy, and Syria's new upper classes.

title: Economics of the Middle East
description: This course will provide a general overview of the economies of Arab countries since 1945. Given the wide scope of the topic and the number of weeks we have, not all countries are covered, and some, such as the countries in North Africa or the Gulf states, are banded together. The first two classes will set the historical and general background for the period under study. The next seven classes will be focused on the following countries: Egypt, Syria, Lebanon, Iraq, Jordan, Saudi Arabia and the Gulf Countries, and finally North Africa. In each of those classes, we examine the economic history of the country, its main political economy issues, and look at its current economic situation. The last four classes deal with general topics that are important across the board such as globalization, labor and migration, Arab economic integration, Islamic economics and finance, and finally the performance of those countries in the last half of the century on an absolute and relative basis.

title: 21st Century Egypt: Reforming, Reporting, and Revolting
description: This course is a multidisciplinary examination of Egypt's politics, economy, culture, and society in the 2000s. By looking at the various trends that unfolded in the country over the last 13 years--from aggressive neoliberalization of the economy under the NDP to the rise in youth disenchantment--the course will include original readings from both scholarly and popular literature about this period. Students will be expected to engage with digital media content, Egyptian popular culture, political treatises, and economic analyses. The course will focus on the various transformative trends in Egyptian society including political Islam and the Muslim Brotherhood; the military establishment and the security forces; liberal and nationalist political groups and parties; civil society, NGOs and human rights advocacy; state, private, and online media; gender and revolutionary feminism; electoral governance; the deep state; and the situation of minorities and rising factionalism.

title: War, Military Politics, and the State in the Middle East
description: This course considers the two main approaches that political scientists have used to study both the phenomenon of warfare and the actors and institutions responsible for conducting warfare in the modern Middle East. The first approach derives from historical sociology and studies the relationship between war-making and state formation. This line of inquiry has led scholars to look at how inter-state conflict has shaped the institutions, politics, societies, and economies of states in the region. While this historical-sociological literature usually takes a macro-level perspective, the second approach focuses more narrowly on Middle Eastern militaries as organizations with their own distinct culture, practices, institutions, and identities. Scholars of comparative politics and political sociology ask questions such as: why do some coups dâÃ©tat lead to military government, while others see the army return to the barracks? Do militaries in the Middle East fight wars differently from armies in other regions? How can we explain differing patterns of civil-military relations across the region? Does national culture affect military strategy and tactics? After introducing these two main approaches, the course focuses on specific examples of wars and military institutions. Case studies may include Iraq, Syria, Egypt, Israel, Iran, Algeria, Palestine, Turkey, Lebanon, and the Gulf states. The course is suitable for students in political science, sociology, international relations, and security studies, but will also be of interest to historians and anthropologists interested in understanding the social and political dynamics of warfare and militarization in the Middle East from a different disciplinary perspective.

title: Modern History and Politics of Egypt3
description: This course will examine the history and politics of Egypt from the beginning of 20th century to our current days. The course will look at the political, economic, social aspects of Egypt. Writings by famous novelists and memoirs will be included to give a broad description of the different periods. The course will analyze the important factors that shaped the different regimes in the last hundred years and the underpinnings of the major problems facing the country.

title: Tutorial: Arab Studies
description: MAAS Student only. Students must seek adviser, director and department approval to receive credit for tutorials which range from 1 to 3 credits. Do not pre-register for a tutorial without prior departmental approval. Please see department for additional details.

title: Internship:  Arab Studies
description: MAAS Students only. Students must seek adviser, director and department approval to receive credit for internships. Internships range from 1 to 3 credits per semester. Do not pre-register for a internship without prior departmental approval. Please see department for additional details.

title: Thesis Research:  Arab Studies
description: MAAS Students only. Students must have completed the required MAAS Thesis form by October 1st and received adviser, director and department approval. Do not pre-register for thesis research unless you have been approved by the department. Please see the department for more details.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Standard Arabic I
description: This intensive multi-media course focuses on developing proficiency in the standard written Arabic language, as well as formal spoken Arabic. It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.

title: Intensive First Level MSA
description: This intensive multi-media course focuses on developing proficiency in the standard written Arabic language, as well as formal spoken Arabic. It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.

It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Standard Arabic I

It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Standard Arabic II
description: This intensive multi-media course focuses on developing proficiency in the standard written Arabic language, as well as formal spoken Arabic. It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.
