Weâll try to identify the strengths and the blind spots of the methods that we study and employ. In addition to reading and rereading theoretical texts together, weâll examine a host of literary and cultural texts: everything from plays, poems, short stories, novels, films, and videos to cartoons, toys, and television shows.

title: Methods of Literary and Cultural Studies
description: This course aims to give students a coherent understanding of various theoretical and critical tools used to interpret texts by introducing them to strategies of close reading and to larger discussions regarding textual analysis. Although the course will not encompass the entire history of literary and cultural criticism, it will examine a range of schools and methods. These schools and methods will be grounded historically and will be situated and contextualized within larger critical conversations that have developed over time. Specifically, we will explore a range of theoretical approaches to literature and culture with recourse to the works of Shakespeare. Not only will we consider the ways in which Shakespeareâs texts have influenced the formation of various theoretical perspectives, but we will also read from his work across different literary genres, and study literary criticism from different theoretical schools on these plays and poems.

title: Literary History I
description: Literary History I is part of a two-semester sequence that surveys Anglophone literary and cultural history. We will be reading texts from the medieval period through the eighteenth century. The course will highlight a number of critical and/or representative texts, genres, debates, developments, and crises illustrative of the time periods studied. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement.

title: Literary History I
description: Literary History I is part of a two-semester sequence that surveys Anglophone literary and cultural history. We will be reading texts from the medieval period through the eighteenth century. The course will highlight a number of critical and/or representative texts, genres, debates, developments, and crises illustrative of the time periods studied. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement.

title: Literary History II
description: Literary History II is part of a two-semester sequence that surveys Anglophone literary and cultural history. We will be examining Anglophone texts produced from the nineteenth century through to the present. The course will highlight a number of critical and/or representative texts, genres, debates, developments, and crises illustrative of the time periods studied. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement.

title: Literary History II
description: Literary History II is part of a two-semester sequence that surveys Anglophone literary and cultural history. We will be examining Anglophone texts produced from the nineteenth century through to the present. The course will highlight a number of critical and/or representative texts, genres, debates, developments, and crises illustrative of the time periods studied. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement.

title: Chaucer
description: A reading and discussion of Chaucer's great (if unfinished) master-work, the Canterbury Tales, a work of first importance to anyone who reads (or writes) narrative fiction. Students will read the work in original Middle English in which Chaucer wrote it, and discuss it in small and in large groups, focusing upon the work's narrative strategies, intellectual constructions, and artistic accomplishment. They will also write, in a variety of ways, about those aspects which particularly take their interest.

title: Medieval and Renaissance Drama
description: The course will begin by proposing that a profound change in the way men and women saw the world took place in Western Europe between 1200 and 1600. After describing and analyzing that change, the course will go on to examine how theatre in England (both its scripts and its performances) reflected and responded to that change. Students will be asked to read Everyman, the Second Shepherdsâ Play, and then plays by Christopher Marlowe (Doctor Faustus), Ben Jonson (Volpone), and William Shakespeare (Hamlet). They will also be asked to read and attend a relevant play in production during the semester, probably Shakespeareâs Measure for Measure at the Shakespeare Theatre in Washington. A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket.

title: Early Modern Revenge Tragedy
description: Revenge plays such as The Spanish Tragedy, The Jew of Malta, Titus Andronicus, Hamlet, and The Revengerâs Tragedy share a fascination for bloody murder and lurid, even incestuous desire. But what else do they have in common besides these notorious themes and a certain literary awareness of one another? This class will begin with a reading of two short classical playsâthe Eumenides, the third play in Aeschylusâs trilogy The Oresteia, and Senecaâs Thyestes, a savage Roman model known to Early Modern writersâplays that locate dramatic revenge at the intersection of justice and violation. The writers of Tudor and Jacobean England, we will find, take the Senecan path of spectacular violation: the five core plays are all haunted by ghosts (ghosts of both the victims and Revenge itself); driven by secret murder; and complicated by disguise and feigned madness. A few other shorter readings will also be required, possibly some secondary material, but definitely a short selection from Machiavelliâs Prince, and âOn Truth,â a brief essay by Sir Francis Bacon, in which Pilate, like the Jacobean revenge play, âwould not stay for an answer.

title: Early Modern Marvels and Monsters
description: If the âmonstrous body is pure cultureâ as Jeffery Cohen has argued, then the literature of the monstrous inscribes the boundaries of culture, defining the normal through the deviant, the knowable through the marvelous, the socially and religiously acceptable through the prodigious and deformed. Starting with Beowulf and its cultural doppelganger Grendel, that founding monster of English literature (a monster imagined well the early modern era) this course lays out a geography of medieval and Renaissance English culture through a reading of the marginal and the monstrous. Besides Beowulf, readings are framed by selections from The Travels of Sir John Mandeville, offering tales and images of an unknown saturated with meaning, and, at the far end of the early modern era, a cluster of texts such as Baconâs short essay âOn Deformity,â and selections from two of Montaigneâs essays that began to naturalize the monstrous. (Yes, a Frenchman sneaks in, but heâs a must!) Within this frame the course would present four subsets of texts: 1) one that takes on the Jew as monstrous, including the Chaucerâs âPrioressâs Taleâ and Marloweâs Jew of Malta; 2) a second that looks at the peril of the transgressive-feminine, as bounded by âThe Wife of Bathâs Tale,â the Shakespearian sonnets of the dark lady, and Dekker and Middletonâs play The Roaring Girl; 3) a third that pulls together a set of short historical readings around The Tempest, investigating the New World Amerindian as an outgrowth of the mythological Wild Man; and 4) a fourth group clustered around Richard III, but including short readings on the âHidious, Frightfull, and Prodigious Signsâ thought to point to religious and political violations. The goal of this course overall is to explore some of the major issues of medieval and Renaissance English culture through texts that point to the often ambiguous margins of that culture and the strange worlds that were imagined to lie beyond.

title: Early Modern Drama
description: Shakespeare is the giant in the field, but he did not emerge in a vacuum. This course looks at the lively theatrical culture in which he flourished and at the generic experiments and stage fads that inspired him. John Lylyâs fantastical comedy Endymion introduced some of the magical ingredients of A Midsummer Nightâs Dream, and Thomas Kydâs revenge play The Spanish Tragedy created a context for Hamlet. Would there have been a Richard III without Christopher Marloweâs Tamburlaine, a Merchant of Venice without Marloweâs The Jew of Malta, or an Othello without the anonymously authored Arden of Feversham? We will also look at some of the roads not taken; in the field of âcityâ comedy, Shakespeare wrote nothing to compare with Thomas Middletonâs A Chaste Maid in Cheapside, with Middleton and Thomas Dekkerâs A Roaring Girl, or with Ben Jonsonâs The Alchemist. The syllabus will include one Shakespeare play, but otherwise the emphasis will be on his colleagues and rivals. Some knowledge of Shakespeare is helpful but not necessary.

title: Shakespeare
description: The course will look closely at five of Shakespeareâs best known plays. (As You Like It, King Henry IV, Part I, The Merchant of Venice, Macbeth and King Lear will probably be the five plays). It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on two significant questions: how might these plays have been brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking answers to those questions the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their understanding of how a script is brought to life on the stage. (The course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend a production of King Henry IV, Part I at the Shakespeare Theatre in Washington. A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket.

title: Shakespeare
description: The course will look closely at five of Shakespeareâs best known plays: Twelfth Night, Othello, Measure for Measure and King Lear will probably be included among them. It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on two significant questions: how might these plays have been brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking answers to those questions the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their understanding of how a script is brought to life on the stage (the course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend two productions of plays by Shakespeare, Othello on campus and (probably) Measure for Measure at The Shakespeare Theatre in Washington. A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the tickets.

A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket.

title: Jacobean Drama
description: This course will survey a range of English drama during the reign of James I (1603-25). Jacobean drama is known for its inventively gruesome tragedies and twisted sense of comedy. We will cover some later works of Shakespeare, as well as major plays by Thomas Middleton, Ben Jonson, John Webster, and John Ford. This course will also consider the social issues that impacted the creation and reception of these plays, including changing gender roles, globalization, domestic politics, religious conflicts, and emerging capitalism. Most of all, we will reflect on the place of theatre in staging these anxieties and desires for an urban public. Two essays, Discussion Board posts, and regular quizzes are required.

title: 17C-18C Women's Writing
description: A curious thing happened when earlier generations of literary critics began to ask how the English-language novel was created: all of the âcreatorsâ were thought to be male authors. In similar fashion we acknowledge widely the contributions of Renaissance and early-modern poets and playwrights like Ben Jonson and John Dryden; but only recently, in the long history of studying literature, have we begun to consider the contributions of a wider range of talented and influential female authors from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. This course focuses specifically on female authors to address two primary objectives: 1) to provide exposure to a mix of well-known and no-so-well-known womenâs writing, illustrative of some of the most important womenâs issues and concerns of the time, including education, property rights, political participation, marriage and divorce customs, and matrimonial law; 2) to examine the question of why many of these influential examples of womenâs writing have been less central to the study of 17C-18C writing as they might have been. This interactive, discussion-based course will cover a provocative selection of both British and early American womenâs writing from roughly 1600-1800, including novels, plays, poetry, essays, and private correspondences. These authors include Margaret Cavendish, Mary Rowlandson, Aphra Behn, Charlotte Lennox, Maria Edgeworth, Mary Leapor, Phillis Wheatley, Anne Bradstreet, and Mary Wollstonecraft, among others.

title: 18th Century Novel
description: True to its name, the novel is widely thought to have ârisenâ as an innovative literary form long after the epic, the tragedy, and the comedy existed. This course focuses on the emergence of the novel in 18th century Britain, paying close attention to the impact the early novel had on both literary form and wider lives and cultures. We will investigate the circumstances surrounding the first novels in English alongside our readings of a variety of 18th century novels, some of which remain famous today (even in popular culture), and others with which you may be less familiar.

title: Early American Fiction
description: One of the least appreciated periods of American literature is that between the founding of the Republic in the late 1780s and the flowering of the American Renaissance in the 1850s with the writings of Emerson and Thoreau, the poetry of Whitman, the novels of Hawthorne, and the publication of Melvilleâs Moby Dick. And yet, during this period some exciting, innovative, and influential fiction was being written. With the founding of the Republic, American writers turned to the task of creating a distinctively American form of literature, and they found themselves having to deal with some distinctively American problems: though the new nation was established on the principle of human equality, in fact, women possessed few of the rights and privileges of men, black Americans were enslaved, and Amerindians were systematically being driven off their lands; though the Revolution was the product of the rational thinking of the Enlightenment, there was a growing recognition of the power the irrational had in shaping everyday life and an anxiety about how the forces of unreason might affect personal and public life; though it was assumed that the American experiment would succeed only if citizens conducted themselves according to the dictates of âRepublican virtueâ (moderation, self-restraint, and self-discipline), the economy and culture of the new nation was changing so quickly and so radically that many felt compelled to reassess Americaâs past and rethink its future. In this class we will see how some early American fiction writers confronted these and other issues. We will read Fosterâs so-called âsentimentalâ novel dramatizing the constraints women lived under immediately following the Revolution; two gothic novels by Brown that deeply influenced Hawthorne and Poe; short stories by Irving and novels by Cooper and Sedgwick which meditate on the past and future of America and (in the case of the latter two novels) the role Amerindians once played in the building of the nation and what role, if any, they would continue to play; and short stories by Hawthorne and Poe, which explore the irrational forces of sin, guilt, and self-hatred. This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. You will be introduced to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field. There will be several short papers, several other writing projects, and a take-home final exam. In this class we will see how some early American fiction writers confronted these and other issues. We will read Rowson and Fosterâs so-called âsentimentalâ novels that dramatize the constraints women lived under immediately following the Revolution; two gothic novels by Brown that deeply influenced Hawthorne and Poe; short stories by Irving and novels by Cooper and Sedgwick which meditate on the past and future of America and (in the case of the latter two novels) the role Amerindians once played in the building of the nation and what role, if any, they would continue to play; short stories by Hawthorne and Poe, which explore the irrational forces of sin, guilt, and self hatred; and two novellas by Melville, which brood over the growing materialism of America and its tragic heritage of slavery. This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. It will introduce you to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field. There will be several short papers and a take-home final exam. PROBABLE TEXTS: Susanna Rowson, Charlotte Temple; Hannah Foster, The Coquette; Charles Brockden Brown, Wieland and Edgar Huntly; James Fenimore Cooper, The Pioneers; Catherine Maria Sedgwick, Hope Leslie; selected short stories of Washington Irving, Nathaniel Hawthorne, and Edgar Allan Poe; Herman Melville, Benito Cereno and, Bartleby: the Scrivener.

title: Early American Women Writers
description: This course investigates how female writers and performers of various kindsâballad singers, parlor poets, lecturers, pseudonymous autobiographers, playwrights and othersâshaped notions of womanhood, race, and American-ness in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. In disparate ways, these writers passed judgment on the newly emerging American culture. Students will have an opportunity to study the varied and complicated strategies that these women utilized to introduce their ideas onto the public stage. To protect their status as respectable wives and mothers, many women voiced their concerns indirectly, composing ballads and poems rife with hidden meanings. Others published their work anonymously, or under a male or female pseudonym. Still others cloaked their judgments by populating the stage with dramatic characters who spoke their views indirectly. We will analyze the implications of the womenâs various avenues toward publication as well as their actual songs, poems, lectures, plays, and autobiographical novels. In the process, we will begin to consider gender, race, and nationality as performances rather than biological or geographic identities. In addition to a variety of theoretical articles, our readings may include works by Lucy Terry, Phillis Wheatley, Susanna Rowson, Judith Sargent Murray, Frances Wright, Maria W. Stewart, Anna Cora Mowatt, Ellen Craft, and Harriet E. Wilson. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: 19th Century British Poetry
description: This course will trace the major forms and thematic concerns of nineteenth-century poetry through a focus on the works of three major poets: John Keats, Christina Rossetti, and Gerard Manley Hopkins.Â Â We will pay particular attention to the relationship between poetic form and meaning, as well as considering how each poet engages with such issues as history, politics, gender and sexuality, and the representation of bodily experience.Â Â In addition to the poems themselves, we will read critical and theoretical accounts of individual poems and poetics more broadly.Â Written assignments will include a number of essays, including some done in class.

title: 19th Century British Novel
description: The 19th C British novel boasts many âbestsâ â human rights blog, time-travel ticket, haunted house explore, pastoral gambol/gamble, urban internship, cultural fix, and socio-political alarm clock, not to mention best bicep builder, back(pack) breaker, and dorm/apartment security system (simply place against the inside of the door you want to ensure stays closed). The 19th C British novel is also arguably the best story about human nature ever told. In fact, a not so buried Victorian secret is that in the 19th C genre wars, the novel fought valiantly against literatureâs former title-holder, poetry, in its proclaimed main objective: to tell a story about what it means to be human. Where poetry delighted in self-consciously ambiguous and introspectively intimate images of memory and myth, the Victorian novel exposed its readers to haunting realities of human experience, challenging human perception as it pushed the boundaries of knowledge and yearning. This course invites its participants to take this voyeuristic Victorian tour through humanity in all its strengths and weaknesses. These stories provoke us to experience the range in the 19th C centuryâs narrative voice as well as examine the tell-tale themes of the era in British literature, including the dynamic conflicts between socio-political change and tradition (rebellions!); urban decay and thrill (just where does decadence appear?); simultaneous loathing and longing for ghosts â those uncanny representations of the past, the monstrous Other, or the fantastical real; scientific and technological invention â progress or intrusion?; industry as both moral and utilitarian; class, gender, and race disenfranchisements and rights; the individual vs. the crowd perception and sympathy; aesthetic (e.g. photography vs. painting) and moral (e.g. Mill vs. Nietzsche) wars; the proud assurance of Home/Empire in light of the savage tantalization of Foreign; the power/ostracization of the traveler. This semesterâs novels, Victorian blockbusters that they are, include stories of love, spies, ghosts, contagious diseases (moral and physical), class and gender struggles, power plays, secretive narrators, murders, religious conversions, travel, madness, greed, imperialism, and probably the worst ever college rejection letter. The novels for Fall 2013 are G. Eliotâs Adam Bede, C. BrontÃ«âs Villette, C. Dickensâ Bleak House, and T. Hardyâs Jude the Obscure. Students will write two short papers, create one panel presentation, and produce a longer final paper. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: 19th Century British Novel
description: The 19th C British novel boasts many âbestsâ â human rights blog, time-travel ticket, haunted house explore, pastoral gambol/gamble, urban internship, cultural fix, and socio-political alarm clock, not to mention best bicep builder, back(pack) breaker, and dorm/apartment security system (simply place against the inside of the door you want to ensure stays closed). The novels for Fall 2013 are G. Eliotâs Adam Bede, C. BrontÃ«âs Villette, C. Dickensâ Bleak House, and T. Hardyâs Jude the Obscure. Students will write two short papers, create one panel presentation, and produce a longer final paper. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Romanticism
description: Do you often feel a strange kinship with the natural world? Do your revolutionary ideals sometimes conflict with the hard realities of politics? Is your idea of a good time a formal dance laced with class-conflict? Writers of the Romantic Period (1789-1832) would say that you are not alone. This course will address these questions and others as they arise in some of the most influential works of early-nineteenth-century British literature. We will read a wide variety of genres and authorsâincluding the political treatises of Thomas Paine and Edmund Burke, the novels of Jane Austen and Mary Shelley, and the lyric poetry of William Wordsworth and John Keats. A secondary consideration will be the relationship of Romantic literature to the development of two major forces in modern life: political partisanship and capitalism. Assignments entail three papers and a small project.

title: Dectective Fiction
description: San Francisco detective novelist, Dashiell Hammett wryly observes in The Thin Man, âThe problem with putting two and two together is that sometimes you get four, and sometimes you get twenty-two.â Only the detective, the investigator of knowledge en route to solving a crime, would judiciously perceive that the same evidence could add up in (at least) two entirely different ways. Not all detectives perform quite as Bogartian a wit as Hammettâs Sam Spade, but detectives of any time and place are a rather singular breed, empowered by a second sight often blind to their own natures. Some wield a gun, others yield to the girl (or guy), while still others fascinate with their âlittle gray cellsâ or exude a mystique almost as alluring as following the track of their gum shoes in pursuit of evil. Our own gameâs afoot as from beneath the fedora, we enjoy clandestine encounters with several classic detective stories, ferreting out the origins of the detective persona and his or her story. Many scholars identify the first western detective as none other than Dickensâ Inspector Bucket (Bleak House) whose disarmingly awkward verbosity masks a mind steeled to track down the killer and uncover justice amidst the London fog. Weâll begin the semester reading a medley of short detective fiction in order to meet the detective in all his or her many guises. How does the madman in Poe inspire the raving Sherlock Holmes? Is there a semblance of Baker Streetâs ego maniac in the fastidious Hercule Poirot? How does the hardboiled cynicism of Marlowe and Hammett resurrect in the Irish Mick âScorcherâ Kennedy? In spring 2014, we will focus on detective fiction of the 20th and 21st centuries set in London, the Dorset seacoast, Dublin, Prague, and Italy. Thereâs nothing like a chill winterâs eve (or balmy spring afternoon!) to curl up with Agatha Christieâs The Murder of Roger Ackroyd, Tana Frenchâs Broken Harbor, Philip Kerrâs Prague Fatale, and Michael Dibdinâs End Games. We will watch the recent spellbinding BBC Broadchurch, which joins the sleuthing forces of the outsider Scots Detective Inspector Alec Hardy with Detective Sergeant Ellie Miller . Student panels will explore the significance of the genreâs identifiable textualities, the âbad boyâ detective in film noir, detective fiction as popular culture as well as brain exercise and ethical catharsis.

title: 19th C. American Literature
description: This course will focus primarily on the tension between civilization and nature in 19th C American literature and culture. In a century when the frontier was progressively colonized and made into spectacle, and when scientific revolutions in natural history created doubt about human exceptionality, nature became a space of both spiritual refuge and hidden menace. As wildness shifted from an exterior landscape to an interior presence, human nature became a source of intense cultural and literary speculation. By tracing a path from transcendentalism to naturalism we will also explore the competing ideas of regeneration and degeneration, of self-definition and loss of identity, of ecstatic connection and destructive mania that inform many literary texts during this century. Among many questions, we will ask: how are American values, particularly individualism, rooted in âself-relianceâ and a free-spirited relationship with nature? Why is nature often a source of liberation for individuals and of paranoia for the dominant culture? Why did the performance of privilege during this period entail the construction of gender and racial others as dangerously wild and in need of domestication? How did evolutionary theory haunt the 19th C and influence the rise of naturalism and literary expressions of the grotesque? Students will contribute weekly short responses to a class discussion forum, will lead a class discussion on a relevant topic, and will write a midterm paper and a final paper. Major works to be discussed include, but are not limited to: Thoreauâs Walden, Douglasâs Narrative of the Life, Melvilleâs Moby Dick, Zitkala-Saâs American Indian Stories, Chopinâs The Awakening, and Norrisâs McTeague.

title: Literary Modernisms
description: In modernist literature, the city often emerges as a catalogue of things. In the beginning of Virginia Woolfâs Mrs. Dalloway, for instance, the eponymous characters finds herself drawn to the âthe carriages, motor cars, omnibuses, vans, sandwich men shuffling and swinging; brass bands; barrel organsâ of London. While Mrs. Dalloway feels uplifted by her sense of connection with the activity of the city, other characters in the text experience alienation. In this course, we will examines how these two poles of experience â connection and alienation â shape accounts of the city in the early twentieth century. In modernist texts, the city is a site for shock, noise, and environmental destruction. At the same time, the city becomes the meeting ground for artistic innovation, social and political movements, and public debate. As we read works by Walter Benjamin, George Simmel, Henry James, Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, T.S. Eliot, and Langston Hughes, we will explore the relationship between modernism and the city: How does the city affect modernist form? How do writers create the space of the city? Throughout this semester, we will also consider the choices readers make when analyzing literature. Through participation in discussion and through the composition of informal and formal papers, students will have an opportunity to practice close reading skills, develop original interpretations of work, and reflect on the processes of thinking and writing about literature.

title: Modern & Avant Garde Poetry
description: This course will examine diverse currents in the explosive, hybridizing, deregulated, re-forming poetry of the early to mid-twentieth century: the revolution of the word. Film and visual art will necessarily supplement our study, since the â-ismsâ or movements we will be most concerned with found expression in a variety of media. Call it the rise of inter-artistic vanguards. The chief tendencies within French Symbolism, Italian Futurism, Russian Futurism, Dada, and Surrealism, together with the multifarious work of Charles Baudelaire and Guillaume Apollinaire, will surveyed. Careful to understand that these movements do not account for the entire poetic field of Modernism, we will position the impulses ranged under these names--as is customary--in relation to broad historical and social changes then underway in the West, taking special notice of responses to World War I, developments in machine technology, and an ongoing urbanization.

title: Contemporary Poems
description: Weâre going to read some wonderful poems; weâll pick them apart, learn what we can about how theyâre madeânot just the form, songlines, voice, but also the echoes, the poems and speech behind them. At the same time, weâll read the things that poets say and think about their own poems, and about poetry (âpoeticsâ). And then weâll test the poems against the claims, and decide for ourselves what works in poems. So weâre operating practically, and building things as we go. I donât assume that you have an extensive background in literary or poetry history, or theory; it may be that you like some particular poems, but that you find the rest of poetry befuddling, and that the whole high-school era search for deep meaning is, as we said in my high school, a âdownerâ and an excuse for obscure and pretentious commentary. I do ask, however, that you be willing to spend serious time with poems, and with the sometimes murky and yes, pretentious commentary that poets provide. The course can be of help to people who want to study poetry and figurative values, and also to those who want to write poems & songs.

title: Literature of WWI
description: "WWI Literature and Film" The somberly sonorous voice of Yeats in his 1920 âThe Second Comingâ perhaps most wondrously confronts both the horror of WWI, the brute itself, and the ensuing madness unleashed upon a new century perceiving its ideals lost but not gone: âTurning and turning in the widening gyre. . . Things fall apart. . . The blood-dimmed tide is loosed, and everywhere [t]he ceremony of innocence is drowned. . . And what rough beast, its hour come round at last, [s]louches toward Bethelehem to be born?â Modernismâs vision ghosts the warâs bitter but resilient afterbirth, offering us a battlefield strewn with individual suffering and resurrection beside the mass shame and redemption. And we, the 2014 readers in Engl 185.1, enter the surreal fray begun in 1914 of chivalric courage that becomes the sacrificial charge to rescue friend and the barbaric attack to defeat foe. This course invites its participants to read a medley of perspectives and genres of WWI literature in order to ask how and why this war both reflected and catapulted change in the ways we understand and value life, provoking such questions as where is our present or immediate identity, both individual and collective? How might we justify an idealistic regard for the past, given our shattered memory? How do we sustain a hope that both trembles before and yearns toward a future? Participants will complete two short argumentative essays and one longer argumentative research paper, come to each class prepared to interact in critical thinking responses to texts and their ideas, and collaborate with colleagues to prepare a panel on such topics as trench warfare; gas: the weapon of moral choice; women on the battlefield; war photography and painting; the Dada art movement; PTSD; the WWI spy. We will view the WWI films that allow us to experience a variety of the Great Warâs fronts: Birdsong, A Very Long Engagement, and Lawrence of Arabia. Class texts for spring 2014 include: one of the newest and most highly regarded historianâs retrospectives of the war, The Sleepwalkers: How Europe Went to War in 1914 (Christopher Clark), All Quiet on the Western Front (Erich Maria Remarque), Mrs. Dalloway (Virginia Woolf), the WWI poets, Farewell to Arms (Earnest Hemingway), and Willam Boydâs Waiting for Sunrise.

title: Modernist Women Writers
description: In the early twentieth century, modernist writers in America and Europe developed literary techniques, such as stream of consciousness, to represent the experience of inhabiting the modern world and to separate themselves from past literary, cultural, and moral traditions. In this course, we will explore the major contributions women writers in particular made to modernism. Specifically, we will be concerned with how these writers reflect on the process of writing itself. That is, what do these writers perceive as the challenges to women writing? Do they believe that there is a distinct way that women write or should write for the modern world? How and why do they innovate narrative, poetic, and dramatic form? How does the emergence of new fields of study, such as psychology, anthropology, and sociology, in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries inform their understanding of identity and sexuality? How do friendships between female writers lead to creative partnerships? To answer these questions, we will read essays, manifestos, poems, short stories, and novels by authors, such as Virginia Woolf, Katherine Mansfield, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Gertrude Stein, Mina Loy, Jean Rhys, and Zora Neale Hurston. In this course, students will practice close reading and critical thinking skills, write analytical papers, and give one presentation.

title: Literature in the Time of Empire
description: The British Empire was the dominant world power for much of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In this course we will study four English-language writers of diverse national and geographical backgrounds whose texts show a distinct awareness of the Empire as a political and cultural force. Beginning with a brief consideration of the literary representation of empire in previous eras, we will turn our attention to the prose and poetry of Rudyard Kipling (born in India), W.B. Yeats (born in Ireland), T.S. Eliot (born in USA), and Derek Walcott (born in St. Lucia, West Indies), to see how the cultural power of England and the English language figure variously in their works.

title: British Theatre since 1950
description: "The productions in London of Samuel Beckettâs Waiting for Godot (1954) and of John Osborne's Look Back in Anger (1956) at the Royal Court Theatre are said to mark a turning point in the history of British theatre in the twentieth century. The plays that followed on the British stage were different in theme and usually in form from those that had been written and produced in the first half of the twentieth century, and they reflected not simply a new kind of theatre in Britain, but a new way of seeing the world outside it. The course will consider some memorable plays by important British playwrights of the last fifty to sixty years, giving attention to theme, form, and possibilities for performance. It will define British loosely, including not only such English playwrights as Terence Rattigan, TS Eliot, John Osborne, Harold Pinter, Tom Stoppard, and Caryl Churchill, but also such Irish playwrights as Samuel Beckett, Brian Friel, Martin McDonough, and Conor McPherson, and the South African playwright Athol Fugard. The course hopes to come to some understanding of the distinct focus and idiom of the British theatre over the last 50 to 60 years and to discover how it might reflect the values and beliefs of the world in which the plays were written and performed. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their appreciation of the theatrical power of these plays. (The course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend at a performance of a relevant play if one is in production in Washington.

title: Postcolonial Novel
description: Postcolonial Novel: The Indian Ocean National literatures (American, English, French, Indian) are becoming increasingly less coherent in a world of forced and voluntary migration, travel and global tradeâour experiences are less and less confined to the boundaries of nations. But none of this is newâfor centuries the Indian Ocean has seen slave transports, movements of migrant workers, trade between East Africa and West Asia, mostly driven by European and Muslim empires. In many ways, the regional economy and culture of the Indian Ocean was a precursor to latter-day globalization. In this class we will read a series of novels that emerge from and represent Kenya, Tanzania, South Africa, Egypt, Indonesia, India, and the waters that lie between them. Our focus will be on connections between the novels of the cosmopolitan world of the Indian Ocean and the legacies of global empires. Texts will likely include (pending confirmation that all are available in the US): Yvette Christianse, Unconfessed. Other Press, New York (2007) Imraan Coovadia, The Wedding. Picador (2001) M.G. Vassanji, The Gunny Sack. Anchor, Canada (2005) Abdulrazhak Gurnah, By the Sea. Bloomsbury (2002) Ishtiyaq Shukri, The Silent Minaret. Jacana, South Africa (2007). Amitav Ghosh, Sea of Poppies. Picador (2009) Amitav Ghosh, In an Antique Land. Vintage (1994) Sophia Mustafa, Broken Reed. E & D Ltd., Tanzania (2005) Michael Ondaatje, The Catâs Table. Vintage (2012)

title: Contemporary India: Film & Lit
description: "REPRESENTING GENDER: CONSTRUCTIONS OF THE HEROINE THROUGH INDIAN LITERATURE AND FILM" In this course we attempt to understand the process of gender inscription in society through reading representations of female figures across media for specific purposes. We begin from the premise that real women are constructed as heroines through different media of expression and for appeal to particular audiences. In this process of construction and representation, a socialisation of gender ideology and an example of gender practice inflect an engage the discourse of gender in the recipient society. In this course the location for this construction and reception is India . Through the prescribed texts, in various media, we explore this process of socialising gender by studying the semiotics of the construction and reception of the heroine figure in Indian literature, film, and genres like advertisements, television serials and womenâs magazines. Our task in this course is to explore what the heroine is supposed to embody, how she is fashioned to do so, and how we may use various critical and aesthetic theories to âreadâ or receive these constructions. Students are encouraged to choose the short stories and poems by authors mentioned unless otherwise specified. The films screened here are meant to âlocateâ the heroine in a social context and study the ways of âbeingâ and becoming a âheroineâ. Particular sections will be shown and discussed in class ; it is required that you watch the entire film before class discussion. At the end of the course we will watch audio, visual or verbal presentations of âheroinesâ from any sphere you select. Your project will be to construct your own heroine and record this construction theoretically through a research report as well as creatively through word, sound and/or picture.

title: Transnational Women's Literature
description: This course examines twentieth and twenty-first century fiction by writers from the U.S., Britain, the Caribbean, and Africa, along with critical writings that explore issues of gender and sexuality, often within the context of transnational and transcultural exchange. We consider how fictionâs formal and stylistic strategies raise questions about gender, sexual, class, and racial identities, as they are caught and produced within histories of colonialism, apartheid, slavery, and war. We also analyze the various ways in which the name âwomanâ has operated as both the basis for, and the prevention of, collectivities that aim to represent and respond to such histories. Readings include novels by Nella Larsen, Virginia Woolf, Olive Schriener, Edwidge Danticat, Jean Rhys, Ama Ata Aidoo, Rebecca West, and Zoe Wicomb. Requirements include short papers and presentations.

title: South African Literature
description: "Translating Apartheid: Intro to South African Literature" An introduction to South African literature and culture organized around the problematic of âtranslating apartheid.â We will touch on as many South African languages and literary traditions as we can, reading both texts in translation and texts in English that thematize the shifting status of English and indigenous languages in the post-apartheid public sphere. We will think about the usefulness of translation and related concepts (cultural translation, transculturation) as metaphors for the transition from apartheid to democratic rule, and also consider contemporary South Africa as a culture in/of translation. Finally, our consideration of the possibility/process of âtranslating apartheidâ will lead us to ask questions about the nature of apartheid itself. No prior knowledge of South African literature/history or translation theory required. Readings to include some of the following: Sol Plaatje, Alan Paton, Sibusiso Nyembezi, Zakes Mda, J.M. Coetzee, Zoe Wicomb, Antjie Krog, Marlene van Niekerk, Ivan Vladislavic, Phaswane Mpe. Course requirements to be determined together as a group, but are certain to include several short papers (with an emphasis on revision), class presentation(s), and regular contributions to class discussion.

title: The Myth of India in Anglo-American Literature & Culture
description: "READING MYTHS OF INDIA IN ANGLO-AMERICAN LITERATURE AND CULTURE" In this course, we shall identify and read cultural texts with the theme of India from the Anglo-American corpus, in order to understand how the creative imagination receives and re-presents an alien entity. We do not propose to juxtapose the ârealâ India against the âimaginedâ ; our purpose is to understand how ideas about an alien entity, here identified as India, form the source of mythical representations, which circulate within the recipient culture, forming a horizon of expectation . This horizon underlies the cultural perceptions that structure engagements and encounters with this alien entity through time and space. Hence our foundational premise for this course is that like all constructs, the idea of âIndiaâ is not fixed but rooted in structures of feeling whose traces we will attempt to discern in cultural production across different media and various genres, diachronically and synchronically. We begin with an âIndiaâ constructed through the exigencies of colonial engagement and the needs and desires of the recipient culture spanning the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. These encounters were varied and yielded some foundational tropes in the representation of India, differing according to the values, needs and political beliefs of the recipients in imperial England. These, in turn, were transported across the Atlantic and impacted upon the work of American writers such as Emerson and Thoreau, who acknowledged this influence in their writing and thinking. Thus, tracing the reception and representation of India as a trope of varied significations in cultural production in Britain and America from the 18th century upto modern times may also be used as a mode of identifying the characteristics both formal and thematic, of different âperiodsâ and âmovementsâ in the histories of these cultures, showing how the artistic imagination constructs and disseminates images of the âotherâ from within the structures of feeling in which it is itself located. The transfer of the myths of India to the audio-visual medium through adaptations of literary texts and the creation of original audio-visual texts ârepresentingâ India add dimensions of affect through the use of a new representational language. Thus in the final stages, we will study how the myths of India are re-visioned by locating them in the marketplace of globalized popular culture, recuperating and reconstructing and domesticating the strange , the unfamiliar and the alien, across time and place for the consumption of a mass audience in the time of global capital. Reception Studies and Semiotics will provide the tools for our reading, with the emphasis on their application in reading the texts, rather than on the philosophical basis of the theories. Apart from 2 writing assignments , as a final project, you will be required to produce your own âvisionâ (myth, reality, document question or any other genre that you choose and are prepared to defend) of âIndia(s)â for presentation at the end of the semester, which may be open to group-grading. At the end of the course we expect to be able to gain skills in âreadingâ cultural texts through understanding: 1. The process of reception of unfamiliar entities and cultures 2. How these are fashioned into cultural constructs by the receiving entity 3. How to âreadâ cultural texts fashioned by reception and influenced by factors like time, place, medium and market Attendance and Participation : 10% One short paper (3-5 pages with revisions): 30% One Research Proposal: 20% One in-class Oral Presentation (15 minutes): 10% Final Project (10 minute presentation and defence) : 30%

title: US Literary History II
description: This course examines U.S. literary history after 1900, surveying American literary production in the context of broader social and artistic developments. Over the course of the semester, we will address many of the principal historical contexts for understanding the development of U.S. literature and culture during the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, focusing on 1) debates about immigration, citizenship, and American democracy; 2) the persistence of social inequality and racial discrimination; 3) the rise of new technologies of communication and mass entertainment; and 4) the globalization of American culture and identity. Authors may include Gertrude Stein, Ernest Hemingway, Sinclair Lewis, Ann Petry, James Baldwin, Vladimir Nabokov, Leslie Marmon Silko, Don DeLillo, Marilynne Robinson, and Junot Diaz. This course requires occasional response writings, two critical essays, and a final exam. Students do not need to have taken U.S. Literary History I to enroll.

title: Modern American Novel
description: We will consider the crucial concerns ceded to American modernist novel by democratic ideology--disillusionment with the past together with anxiety over the future, absorption in the details of the American world together with a profound alienation from it (sound familiar?)âand try to determine how the formal innovations in works by: William Faulkner, Francis Scott Fitzgerald, and Ernest Hemingway, are responses to those issues. Special attention will be paid to the close-reading de facto canonical writers given âdead white menâ have held such historical privilege

title: American Expatriate Writers
description: This course will explore the fiction and poetry of some of the principal American Expatriate writers, a tradition which has emerged as a rich vein in American Literature. In particular, we will investigate how their perception of America, coupled with their interaction with modern art and with each other, stimulated an experiment in realignment of traditional American values with corresponding innovative literary techniques. Short stories by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Gertrude Stein; novels by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Virginia Woolf; poetry by Ezra Pound, e.e. cummings and T. S. Eliot. (Virginia Woolf was not American, but a significant feminist contemporary.) This literature is best appreciated in the context of the history of the times and the writers; consequently, we will view film documentaries which will entail a few additional evening classes. Students will be invited to offer power point presentations on significant issues for their peers in class. Requirements: Three analytical papers or a journal with entries written and submitted throughout the course. Three quizzes and no exams. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Contemporary Drama
description: Why would a newspaper editor like the twentieth-century titan Joseph Pulitzer want to honor excellence not just in journalism but also in the arts, in Drama? Are plays like âbreaking newsâ? Are they, like newspapers, somehow connected to the idea of âfree speechâ or talking back? If newspapers try to report the facts, what function does dramatic literature serve? What unique contributions do plays make to the question of how to represent the âhumanâ? In this course weâll address these questions by studying the past decadeâs winners and top contenders for the Pulitzer Prize in Drama. Weâll encounter the hopes and dreams, challenges and failures, of a diverse set of characters, from a Catholic priest in the Bronx to a tavern owner in the Congo and a Cuban Ã©migrÃ© in Florida. As we practice how to read and write about plays, weâll gain experience in close reading and critical writing. And at the end of the term, weâll collectively create a play of our own, based on interviews with fellow students.

title: Post-WWII American Novel
description: In the wake of World War II, the 20th century bore witness to a dizzying array of new cultural innovations as artists of all stripes sought to grapple with an increasingly interconnected world. In the United States, this impulse toward the new found special purchase in the novel, where authors pushed at the formal limits of prose fiction in an effort to rethink the value and potential of literary culture. This class will trace some of the major developments within the recent history of the American novel, focusing especially on works whose formal innovations are intertwined with the changing socio-political climate of the nation. With Thomas Pynchon, for example, we will think through the emergence of transformative digital communications technologies. On a different front, Toni Morrisonâs The Bluest Eye will offer us an opportunity to discuss the enduring legacies of racism. Later, Junot Diazâs The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao will allow us to think through changing patterns of cultural and linguistic diversity in the United States. We will also read works by a range of other American novelists including Don Delillo, Marilynne Robinson, and William S. Burroughs.

title: Science Fiction and Fantasy
description: This course will investigate the concept of the future within a global cultural imagination, examining a wide range of literary and cinematic genres in science fiction & fantasy. The class will read a short selection from earlier science fiction writersâfrom H.G. Wells and Isaac Asimov to Arthur C. Clarke and Philip K. Dickâand discuss how speculations of the future have evolved over time. We will address how such classic works speak to contemporary issues about the science of geophysical disasters, the destruction of the environment, financial Armageddon, pandemics and contagions, governmental control and the chilling prospects of nuclear war and global terrorism. Other topics will encompass Afrofuturism, utopia and dystopia, myths and legends, vampires, epic quests in historical and fantasy novels and Japanese anime. Other writers may include Octavia Butler, Ted Chiang, Kim Stanley Robinson, Elizabeth Kostova and J. R. R. Tolkien.

title: American Women's Autobiography
description: This course investigates the notoriously devious genre of autobiography. Among the many questions weâll be considering this semester are the following: --What distinguishes autobiography from fiction? --Does it matter if a subject tells the truth in her autobiography? --Do men and women tell their stories differently and is it liberating or damaging to argue for gender difference in writing? --Can women ever really write their lives truthfully through autobiography if it is, as many scholars argue, an inherently male genre? Are there really such things as âwomenâs autobiographiesâ? --Why is autobiography such an enormously popular genre at this moment? --What, if anything, about the language and worldview of these autobiographies makes them distinctively American? --Is the self perceived or created through writing? In an attempt to arrive at some answers to these questions, weâll be reading a wide selection of American womenâs autobiographies throughout the twentieth century. Students will also write their own five page autobiographical essay and a five-page critique of that essay-writing experience. In addition, each student will participate in three different groups: an autobiographical essay reading group, a group that will lead class discussion of one of the assigned texts, and a final project group that will read, discuss, and present insights about autobiographies not assigned in the course.

title: Jewish-American Literature
description: This course examines the novels, novellas and short stories of both established and emerging Jewish-American writers of the past half century. Our inquiry examines literary representations of the Holocaust and its effect on subsequent generations, assimilation and its discontents, the state of Israel as seen in diasporic perspective, God and godlessness, anti-Semitism past and present, the secular/religious divide, and the encounter with the non-Jewish other, among other subjects. Some of the writers to be studied include Saul Bellow, Cynthia Ozick, Bernard Malamud, Philip Roth, Allegra Goodman, Grace Paley, Gary Shteyngart, Jonathan Safran Foer, Lara Vapnyar and David Bezmozgis.

title: Literature of the City
description: "New York City is, arguably, the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century Americaâthe center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic, and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of âold guardâ aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define âThe American Century.â Attention will also be devoted to the various âcounter-culturalâ movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Party, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbornly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America.

title: Pulp Fiction
description: This course will investigate the highly stylized world of popular fiction, including the literary genres of the western, the adventure story, hard-boiled detective fiction, true crime, the historical romance, the suspense thriller, sci-fi and fantasy. We will observe the beginnings of pulp fiction from the dime novels and the penny dreadfuls in the 19th century to the literary boom of pulp fiction in the 1920s and 1930s. We will be connecting major themes in popular fiction to the growth of old time radio programs and to the study of manga, media, television and film and will be watching a series of documentaries and analyzing pulp magazine jackets. Pulp fiction writers may include Edgar Rice Burroughs, Zane Grey, Dashiell Hammett, Mickey Spillane, Ted Chiang, Koushun Takami, Truman Capote, Laura Esquivel and J. K. Rowling.

title: African American Literature: Beginnings-1903
description: The purpose of this course is to study African American literary and cultural production from its beginnings in the 18th century until the publication of W.E.B. Du Boisâ seminal text, The Souls of Black Folk, in 1903. As we examine how African American writers transformed the different literary modes in which they wrote, we will also consider how the literary texts articulate the cultural, political, and national concerns that shaped 18th and 19th century America. Throughout the semester, we will return repeatedly to issues of race, class, gender, sexuality, and religion to consider why these authors are concerned with these particular matters. We will think energetically about the historicity of the texts, and the textuality of history; in other words, we will consider how history enriches our understanding of the texts, and how the texts enrich our understanding of history. In addition to the primary texts and literary and cultural criticism that we will read, we also will engage historical documents. Slavery, emancipation, Reconstruction, and the suffragist movement are just a few examples of the historical moments we must interrogate to make our study most productive. While previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are. Students enrolling in this course should expect to read the primary works by the following authors: Lucy Terry, Phillis Wheatley, David Walker, Maria Stewart, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs, Harriet Wilson, Sojourner Truth, Booker T. Washington, Anna Julia Cooper, and W.E.B. Du Bois. Potential Assignments/Grade determination: class participation (15%), two in-class examinations (40%), one paper (6 pages) (15%), 3-4 quizzes (15%), and 1 final exercise (15%).

title: Black Women Writers
description: In this course, we will read representative writings from 20th-century Black women writers, exploring the ways in which they construct history, nation, race, gender, sexuality, and religion in their works. We will also examine how their works challenge and complicate hegemonic notions of history, nation, race, gender, sexuality, and religion. Students will be able to discuss several aspects of Black women writers, including, but not confined to, the differences and similarities between their texts and how these similarities and differences reflect their changing historical, social, and political perspectives. Black women writers have engaged two important concerns: How can art be both beautiful and political? What role does the written word play in struggles for freedom? We will also explore how Black women writers use the literary imagination to transmit cultural knowledge. Beginning with Nella Larsen's modernist novels Quicksand and Passing, we will examine representative texts by Black women writers from a variety of critical approaches, particularly critical race theory, Black feminist theory, and African American cultural studies. Others writers may include Zora Neale Hurston, Lorraine Hansberry, Alice Walker, Gloria Naylor, and Toni Morrison. This course is reading and writing intensive. Students may expect short papers, quizzes, and a research project.

title: African American Poetry
description: In this class, weâll explore African American poetry and culture from Phillis Wheatley to hip hop. We'll pay special attention to the social movements that have nurtured African American art, including the New Negro/Harlem Renaissance and the Black Arts Movement, and ask questions about what kind of political work art can do. Weâll encounter a wide range of poets, from Paul Laurence Dunbar and Gwendolyn Bennett, to recent Pulitzer prize winner Tracy K. Smith, Lupe Fiasco, and Kendrick Lamar. We will also devote extensive study to the pivotal careers of poets Gwendolyn Brooks and Amiri Baraka, and consider issues of identity,form, power, and voice with respect to the changing social conditions of African American experience.Â

title: Native American Literature
description: This course introduces students to a range of twentieth-century texts â poems, novel, memoirs, and scholarship â by canonical Native American writers such as Zitkala-SÃ¢, Linda Hogan, Louise Erdrich, DâArcy McNickle, James Welch, and M. Scott Momaday. In their work, these writers grapple with the rich, complicated histories and traditions that have emerged from colonization, the contact between indigenous nations and the U.S. We will explore how these representative writers express individual and tribal identities, revise stereotypes and contest colonization. For example, we will analyze how these authors reframe stories of victimization as narratives of survivance and strength. While respecting the specific cultural context of each writer and the demands of the literary genre of each text, this course will identify shared thematic concerns and literary strategies. To help us better appreciate the cultural work these texts do, we will also read native scholarship and work by non-native scholars on historical and cultural issues relevant to the texts such as forced removal, Indian boarding school programs, and sovereignty. The course will end with an investigation of recent controversies over the representation and appropriation of indigenous images in popular media by performers such as Drew Barrymore and Gwen Stefani.

title: Asian American Literature
description: "Asian America: The Local & the Global" In this course we will investigate current trends in the reading and writing of Asian American literature. We will focus particularly on how Asian American identity and resistance is to be imagined, especially within the contexts of the âlocalâ and âglobal.â âLocalâ refers here to two different usages: the âlocalâ as in the local community or local politics, as well as âlocalâ as it refers to Hawaiian literature and culture. Both definitions as well as the concept of the âglobalâ offer a useful means of situating various Asian American communities, their values, and their anxieties. While the âlocalâ has stood as the first home of Asian American political movementsâwhether within internment camps, ethnic enclaves, geographical areas, historical landmarks, or universitiesâthe âglobalâ has become a powerful means of articulating how Asian American identities are also irrevocably shaped by migration, colonialism, and global capitalism. Asian American critics have also simultaneously turned their attention to the âlocalâ politics of Hawaii, and questioned how national and international models of Asian America are altered when we factor in the Hawaiian right to sovereignty and the claims of its indigenous peoples. By looking at three modes of claiming Asian American identityâthe indigenous, the national, and the global, and through the reading of recently acclaimed works of fiction written by a number of ethnically diverse Asian American writers, we will discuss how Asian American writers have represented postcolonial histories, civil and imperialist wars, transcontinental migrations, national disenfranchisement and dispossession, as well as the creation of community, identity, and a literary tradition. The course operates primarily through discussion and close reading; students will be asked to submit short weekly writing analyses, raise questions about the reading assigned for that day, and submit two longer essays. The course particularly privileges engagement, inquiry, and careful analysis.

title: 20th C. American Nature Writing
description: This course examines key twentieth-century texts in the American tradition of writing about human relationships with the natural world. We will read canonical non-fiction nature writing by authors such as Mary Austin, Aldo Leopold, Edward Abbey, Annie Dillard, Terry Tempest Williams, N. Scott Momaday and Rachel Carson, and we will tackle fiction that addresses environmental issues by authors not usually considered to be nature writers. The non-fiction for the course will include: Leopoldâs Sand County Almanac (1949), excerpts from Abbeyâs Desert Solitaire (1968), and Momadayâs The Way to Rainy Mountain (1969), and the novels will include: Louise Erdrichâs Tracks (1988), Barbara Kingsolverâs Animal Dreams (1990), and Carl Hiaasenâs Native Tongue (1991). The course assumes that nature is a social and linguistic construct and that depictions of the land and its inhabitants both reflect and influence the way Americans relate to the natural world. Aiming to deconstruct dualisms such as human/nature and civilization/wilderness, the course will explore a variety of questions: How is nature represented in the books we read? What characterizes nature writing as a genre? How have concepts of nature and wilderness changed over the century? How do contemporary environmental events and perceived crises impact the literary imagination? What relationship do the texts suggest between environmental degradation and human exploitation? To help us make sense of this literature, we will read ecologically informed criticism by scholars from interdisciplinary fields.

title: Faulkner
description: This course will investigate one of the greatest American literary achievements of the modern era. We will begin with Go Down, Moses, a collection of interrelated short stories as an entree into the world of Faulkner. Subsequently, we will read and discuss, in chronological order, the major works of Faulknerâs most prolific years: The Sound and the Fury; Sanctuary; As I Lay Dying; Light in August; and Absalom, Absalom. We will discuss these works in terms of predominant themes, psychological and sociological, and Faulknerâs corresponding experimentation in a rich variety of literary techniques. We will also view and discuss The Reivers in movie form. Requirements: a mid-term examination, two 5 page papers and one 10-12 page final paper (or a journal). This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: "Culture and Power: An Introduction to Cultural Studies" This course will focus on a history and examination of the set of theories, practices and methodologies which define the field of Cultural Studies. By reading and situating the theory, we can critique the production and consumption of cultural objects, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, style and subculture, with attention to the specific tensions between language and visual images. Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression.

title: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: "Culture and Power: An Introduction to Cultural Studies" This course will focus on a history and examination of the set of theories, practices and methodologies which define the field of Cultural Studies. By reading and situating the theory, we can critique the production and consumption of cultural objects, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, style and subculture, with attention to the specific tensions between language and visual images. Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression.Â This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Political Documentary
description: In an age of simulation and virtuality, documentary has curiously emerged as a privileged genre in the contemporary culture industry based on the claim that it can present the most accurate and authentic view of our increasingly unstable world. Once a minor genre, documentary now enjoys an unprecedented popularity, commercial viability, and political impact by using evidence from the world to legitimate its usage as a source of ârealâ knowledge. This course will explore the fundamentals of style, form, and structure, of documentary film , video, photo-journalism, as well as other forms such as animation that are not commonly associated with the representation of âreality.â Questions we will ponder include: what is the nature of âthe politicalâ in political documentary? what relation between knowledge and pleasure does the documentary propose that differs from narrative fiction? how do our own beliefs determine what we see? As a Level I elective, the class will emphasize close reading, textual analysis and writing in the form of several short papers. No prior knowledge of film is necessary. Films will include: The Power of Nightmares: The Rise of the Politics of Fear, Standard Operating Procedure, Thin Blue Line, The Weather Underground, The Murder of Fred Hampton, When the Levies Broke, Control Room, What Farocki Taught, Road to Guantanamo, Redacted, Sicko, Fahrenheit 9/11 Waltz With Bashir Chicago 10. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Problems in Postmodernism
description: "Problems in Postmodernism: Literary Hybrids, Remixes and Remakes" This course examines a variety of literatures that are crossed or mixed or remade in some way, and as such, complicate our understanding of authorship, creativity and literary value. Mashed-up films and remixes that we find on the internet are the most obvious examples of such forms today, but there are many more less obvious examples dating back to the last century and earlier. These works give us an opportunity to ask important questions about our current cultural moment and literature generally: What makes something literary? How is literature adapting to digital culture and the globalizing consumer culture connected to it? How do changes in the way we view creativity, originality and authorship affect literature, and what do these changes require of us as readers, viewers consumers and citizens? To answer these questions we will take a critical, often comparative, approach to the material. In addition to examining primary works in fiction, poetry, the graphic novel, film and audio, we will follow developments in cultural theory, remix theory, visual rhetoric and semiotics (the theory of signs). The course material, as well as the concepts we will discuss and develop in class, will be the basis for two writing assignments and an in-class presentation. In connection with these assignments, we will discuss topic and thesis development, paper organization and research and examine a variety of secondary sources that will help us situate our comments and critiques. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Literature of War
description: "Literature of War: Examining the War Experience through Personal Narratives, Fiction, and Film" This course examines the experience of war by from the perspective of the soldier, his or her family, veterans, and prisoners. We will read fictional works and personal narratives, and watch documentary films and Hollywood movies, dating from the Civil War up to and including the War in Iraq. Through these stories we will examine how soldiers cope with the challenges of war, including the âfogâ of the battlefield; tests of personal courage; fear of death and injury; the role of peer bonding; survivor guilt; and post-traumatic stress disorder. We will examine moral questions that arise for soldiers and commanders. We will look at the challenges of war from the perspective of families âon the home frontâ and the difficulties that veterans face in reentry into civilian life. We will consider how the experience of war may differ for women soldiers and veterans. We will also consider the enemyâs âwar stories,â including narratives that convey the experiences of German and Japanese soldiers. We will gain an appreciation of significant differences in how specific wars are portrayed given the perspective and rhetorical agenda of authors and producers.

title: Introduction to Film Theory
description: âWhen is the last time you saw a film?â Well, that depends, you might be thinking. What do you mean by film? Film can refer to a strip of celluloid, a sequence of still images projected rapidly to give the illusion of motion, a 90-120 minute motion picture, or a thin layer covering the surface of something. âIntroduction to Film Theoryâ is itself a film in that last sense, because it is going to cover all ways these different meanings of film have helped filmmakers, critics, and philosophers explain what motion pictures are, how they work, and what they make us feel. If you want to know why a movie makes you cry, even when you donât want to cry, film theory is going to tackle that question. If you want to know whether iTunes videos still count as film, it will answer that too (or at least try to). If you want to know what is so special about motion picturesâwhy we love them, learn from them, and even revile themâthen âIntroduction to Film Theoryâ can help. It surveys the history and major approaches to studying film, including ontology, semiotics, materialism, psychoanalytic criticism (including feminist and queer theory), genre theory, and phenomenology. Those are big words for big schools of thought, but we will scrutinize each in turn as we watch thought-provoking films (thereâs that word again) from the history of world cinema and try to puzzle out how it is that movies can change the way we feel about our world.

title: Post-WWII American Cinema
description: This course will examine cinema production in the United States after WWII until approximately 1965--a period in which the terms "The Cold War," "Containment Culture," "The Red Scare," "The Atomic Age," and "The Children's Decade" have been retroactively applied. Students can expect to examine a variety of cinematic texts from a mix of critical lenses, including class, race, gender, sexuality, and disability.

title: Television and American Society
description: This course examines the history of television and its complex relationship to American culture and society. As a source of information, a purveyor of entertainment, and a tool of socialization, television had a profound impact on almost every aspect of 20th century American life. Over the past decade, new technologies and media convergence have done little to erode its significance. Our investigation of the medium will begin with televisionâs origins in radio and trace its aesthetic and economic development over the past half-century. It will cover a range of topics from broadcast regulation to identity politics. Through a variety of theoretical and methodological approaches, we will consider how television shapes and is shaped by our national preoccupations.

title: History of New Media
description: This course explores the relationship between communications technology and culture, focusing on the period from electronic industrialization through Web 2.0. This is not a class in the history of the web or technology, however; rather it is a historiography, a study of how we obtain and transmit historical knowledge. Thus we will interrogate how media history gets written, by whom, and in the service of what commercial interests or intellectual traditions. In order to destabilize assumptions of progressâthe cult of the ânew and improvedââwe will explore the design and reception of media technologies in reverse chronological order and through five thematic matrices: distance, screens, copyright, interactivity, and mass culture. We will study a wide range of communications devices, from the telegraph to the smart phone, the remote control to the graphic-user interface, and expand our historical interpretations of them through blogs, techno-memoirs, written exams, and class discussion.

title: Introduction to Visual Literature
description: "LITERATURES OF SIGHT AND SOUND" Literature and the visual arts exist largely as separate cultural and aesthetic categories. There are many works, though, that mix forms from these two categories, examples from books to paintings, to video, to digitally animated text, that combine pictures, drawings or other graphic elements with printed words or text. For decades such combinatorial strategies have been common in advertising and in popular forms like comics. Increasingly, they are now used to literary ends, in work, that in aggregate, seems to beg the construction of a new, hybrid category: a visual literature.
