While it can be argued that examples of visual literature can be found in many historical periods, the application of the term to work of the last sixty years seems to be the most productive, given the unusually wide range of examples, and especially given the recent proliferation of electronic forms that demonstrate a similar hybridity and logic. The focus of this class will be to examine contemporary âvisualâ literature and spillover forms in sound and performance and consider their implications. We will be especially interested in understanding how so-called visual works merge the traditions of literature and the visual arts and challenge the way we read, view and value aesthetic objects, in a culture increasingly dominated by technology as seen on the Internet, on television, in film, video and graphic images. The course material, as well as the concepts we will discuss and develop in class, will be the basis for two writing assignments and an in-class presentation. In connection with these assignments, we will discuss topic and thesis development, paper organization and research and examine a variety of secondary sources that will help us situate our comments and critiques."

title: Introduction to Critical Theory
description: "We will familiarize ourselves with the interpretation of literature and culture as practiced in the US today by reading both canonical and newer works in the field, arranged along a thematic axis. We will make a gesture towards broad coverage of 20th C. Euro-American literary theory, but will focus more on symptomatic approaches to issues of contemporary relevance. Broad fields of theory will be examined each week including Marxism, feminism, structuralism, psychoanalysis, post-structuralism, postmodernism and postcolonialism. Our special focus will be on the contemporary artist and critic in movements for social change and the realization of a âsocial practiceâ as highlighted for example in our Lannan Center for Poetics and Social Practice. Student involvement in planning the syllabus is encouraged. Please make yourself familiar with principles of professional integrity: //gervaseprograms.georgetown.edu/honor/system/ Additionally, some of our courses talk about learning goals. In this seminar we will explore the alternative language of high impact practices. Analysis of the National Survey of Student Engagement (NSSE), a survey administered at thousands of colleges and universities that asks students about their experiences inside and outside the classroom has identified a set of eight or nine particular learning experiences that have the highest correlation with student success (retention, persistence, GPA) and association with the NSSE indicators of âdeep learning gainsâ (which include among other self-reported qualities of learning experiences: âexamining the strengths and weaknesses of your own views,â âtrying to better understand someone elseâs views,â and âlearning something that changed how you understand an issueâ). Here is a list of high impact practices: â¢ Achieve at high performance levels â invest time and effort â¢ Interact with faculty and peers about substantive matters â¢ Feel accountable for their thinking and communication â¢ Experience diversity (engage multiple perspectives) â¢ Respond to more frequent feedback (often from multiple sources) â¢ Reflect, synthesize, integrate and attend to underlying meaning â¢ Discover the relevance of learning through real-world application â¢ Demonstrate competence in some public or semi-public way

title: Introduction to Queer Theory
description: This course provides an overview of the theoretical, historical and political contexts for âqueer theoryâ as it intersects with and informs the evolving interdisciplinary fields of sexuality and gender studies. Topics to be considered include: the emergence of modern sexual identity; queer community-building and world-making, theoretical and political remappings of the body and of sexuality; diasporic, transnational and global queer studies; the relationship between queer theory and (trans)gender studies; queer pasts and queer futures. Students will be expected to engage actively in both in-class and online discussion, to give one in-class presentation and to write several short papers and a final multimedia project.

title: Law and Literature
description: The course focuses on justice in the law, literature and culture context. How does reading or rereading law and literature classics like "To Kill a Mockingbird," "Billy Budd," and "The Trial" through the lenses provided by the great philosophers of justice, by neuroscientists and by contemporary political philosophers make a difference? Students will be asked to bring contemporary issues on justice to the class and to respond to writing and research prompts weekly as well. Three longer essays, a field trip to the Supreme Court (with related writing assignment) and an in-class final exam will also be required. Some of the questions we will be asking include: Can justice be reduced to a rationally-imposed formula? What is the relationship between emotions and justice? Do babies have a sense of justice? How about animals? How do individual justice, social justice and global justice approaches interact? And what do we make of trolleyology?

title: Prison Literature
description: Using the fact that the U.S. has 6.8 million people on probation, parole, in jail or prison in America, this course will investigate the history of imprisonment by interrogating the theoretical and philosophical grounding provided by Michel Foucaultâs Discipline and Punish. We will read (and sometimes perform) a selection of literature by and about the imprisoned from works by Kate Richards, Jack Abbott, Etheridge Knight, Patricia McConnell; Miguel Pinero, Kody Scott, Kathy Boudin, Jimmy Santiago Baca, Bryonn Bain, etc. We will balance these literary expressions by prisoners with exploration of agency and moral responsibility in life stories of prisoners in Speaking of Crime by Patricia E. OâConnor. The massive use of prisons in America as discussed in Michelle Alexanderâs The New Jim Crow (2010) will ground our discussion of the future of prisons and recent small declines in numbers incarcerated. Former prisoners who have studied in Georgetownâs Prison Outreach Program will actively participate in the course, especially since this is the 30th anniversary of this outreach into prisons. Students will be asked to write reflectively and analytically on topics in the texts (BlackBoard postings, etc.), take one mid-term, lead some discussions and make oral presentations. All students will engage in a research project on a topic of their interest reflecting current issues and conflicts in imprisonment and their relation in the literature to historical, social, political, and economic practices that affect or that are impacted by the criminal justice systemâs uses of incarceration. Students involved in Georgetownâs outreach into prisons and those working with adjudicated youth may be interested in taking this section 02 of the course for community-based learning credit. Our class will participate in GUâs Lannan Symposium March 31 & Ap1 that will feature poetry and theatre depicting the struggles of ex-prisoners to find work in our âprecariousâ economy.

title: Literature of Exploration
description: This course will introduce students to the literature of travel and exploration. Reading poetry and fiction, we will pay close attention to certain recurrent themes in the genre of the exploration narrative, such as the encounter with savages or barbarians; the perils unleashed by the explorer; the voyage as a journey into the self; the hunger for the unknown, or utopia; and the conjunction of beauty and violence in the explorerâs world. Texts for the course include Mary Shelleyâs Frankenstein; Alfred Tennysonâs âUlyssesâ; Joseph Conradâs Heart of Darkness; Jean Rhysâs Wide Sargasso Sea; and a selection of poems from John Donne, John Keats, Charles Baudelaire, Ezra Pound, Robert Lowell, and Kamau Brathwaite. In discussing and writing about these works, we will place a great deal of emphasis on the use of textual evidence. Students will acquire a basic vocabulary and understanding of literary âdevices,â and will be asked to incorporate this new knowledge into their shorter and longer analytical essays.

title: Religion and Literature
description: "âFor most of human history, religion and literature have been virtually inseparable, everywhere.â âAmitav Ghosh This course will explore how world literature and film address such critical matters as the origins of the universe, the problem of evil, the nature of suffering, salvation and enlightenment, death and the afterlife, mysticism and the occult, war and peace, divine judgment and the apocalypse. Our reading of a wide range of cultural works will be informed by the study of world religions and their sacred texts, including short selections from the Rig-Veda, the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, the Book of Revelation, the Popol Vuh, the Qurâan, the Hebrew Bible, the Tao Te Ching and the Buddhist Pali Canon and Sutras, among others. We will also discuss the significance of religious and philosophical thought in science fiction and fantasy, Japanese anime, world mythologies and art history. Readings may consist of works by Dante Alighieri, Matsuo Basho, Graham Greene, Hermann Hesse, U Sam Oeur, Gita Mehta, Tayeb Salih, T. S. Eliot, Elie Wiesel, Miguel Ãngel Asturias, C. S. Lewis, J. R. R. Tolkien and Ursula Le Guin.

title: Introduction to Disability Studies
description: This course examines the role that disability plays in our culture. We will study the way disability gets constructed in discourses ranging from law and policy, to film, novels, memoirs, poetry, and new media, and consider how conceptions of disability both ground and threaten notions of normality. Some issues we will explore include disability, prenatal testing, and abortion rights; defining mental illness; disability and constructions of masculinity; the concept of ""deaf gain;"" and higher education and neuro-diversity. We will explore a variety of texts by disabled artists, from poetry in American Sign Language (ASL), to a number of recent memoirs chronicling life with autism. We will also examine some of the historical and legal foundations for the Americans with Disability Act (ADA) and other developments in the disability rights movement.

title: Reading Motherhood
description: Motherhood is deemed one of the most ânaturalâ experiences binding women together across time and space. But as feminist poet and essayist Adrienne Rich famously argued in her landmark work Of Woman Born, it is also a social institution with its own history and ideology. Our course examines this institution as a shifting, historically and culturally specific phenomenon given particularly potent life in cultural representations: that is, the literature, film, television, advertising, video, comics, etc. that surround us in everyday life. Analyzing foundational criticism and theory about motherhood alongside a variety of predominantly U.S. cultural textsâfrom I Love Lucy and Imitation of Life to Roseanne, Toni Morrisonâs Beloved, Wendy Leeâs Happy Family, and the recent documentary film Google Babyâwe explore how differing notions of motherhood are constructed, contested, negotiated. One premise of the course is that motherhood cannot be universalized as an experience or as a right (not all women are urged or even permitted to mother); it is not innate nor necessarily a biological relation. And while the syllabus focuses largely on the U.S., its structure will address how western and non-western political relations are increasingly embedded in global circuits of motherhood via transnational adoption, surrogacy, and reproductive technologies.

title: Women Writers, Feminist Readers
description: This Level I course focuses on the field of feminist literary theory and criticism. In particular, we will explore questions and theories surrounding the vexed category of âwomenâs writing.â Feminist criticism/theory brought the study of women writers into the mainstream of English as a discipline, and despite raising myriad controversies, research and courses on womenâs literature remain highly popular. Focusing on this aspect of the fieldâtheories of womenâs writing and how to read it--serves as one revealing in-road to its history, aims, contributions, and problems. After exploring key concepts, issues, and debates, the course will take a two-pronged approach: weâll read a series of literary texts that now occupy a centralâsome might say âcanonicalââplace in the feminist critical archive by authors Phillis Wheatley, Charlotte Bronte, Nella Larsen, Virginia Woolf, Toni Morrison, and Julia Alvarez, while simultaneously examining diverse critical/theoretical approaches to each text (a mix of early and more recent work) as a way of charting the fieldâs development over time. We will read a series of At the same time, we will examine a diversity of feminist critical and theoretical approaches to each text (a mix of early and more recent work) as a way of charting its development as a field.

title: Poetry and Politics
description: This course will bring together two of the most significant threads of twentieth century American poetics, threads that are almost never read together: the Black Arts Movement of the 1960s and the L=A=N=G=U=A=G=E poetry of the late 1970s and 1980s. Both schools of writing emerged fromâand often in radical opposition toâthe protest movements of the 1960s and â70s, and an examination of their visions of aesthetic and political praxis, despite their differences, will yield a deep understanding of the radical poetics of the late 20th century. Through readings of the groupsâ theoretical and poetic writings, we will obtain a grasp of their basic artistic and political ideas, their interpretations of culture (particularly music), their points of convergence and crossing, and their legacies to the poetry of the final decades of the 20th century and the opening years of the 21st. This final task will take us to the radical poetries that have grown out of one or both movements to articulate their own multi-cultural poetics. Over the course of the term, we will thus engage in a full exploration of the possibilities for radical poetic praxis. Probable writers: Amiri Baraka, Gwendolyn Brooks, Audre Lorde, Ishmael Reed (Black Arts); Charles Bernstein, Lyn Hejinian, Susan Howe, Ron Silliman, Hannah Weiner (L=A=N=G=U=A= N=G=E); Amiel Alcalay, Gloria AnzaldÃ¹a, Theresa Hak Kyung Cha, and Myung Mi Kim (multi-ethnic poetics); Max Roach and John Cage (music); and relevant criticism. Requirements: faithful attendance, engaged reading, regular response papers, one short essay, one longer research essay.

title: Utopia and Dystopia
description: This course explores the concepts of Utopia and Dystopia. Sir Thomas More coined the term utopia as a combination of Greek words meaning happy place and no place/nowhere. Far from describing a never never land, utopias often represent cultural protest against unjust institutions or policies and propose political or social reforms. In recent years, there has been increasing attention to dystopic visions reprsenting oppressive totalitarian regimes, environmental degradation, and/or technological oppression. This is an interdisciplinary course. Students will read literature, watch films, and look at artistic portrayals of utopia and dystopia. The films we will discuss include Nineteen Eighty Four, Blade Runner, Gattaca, Hunger Games, and Japanese animation. A premise of this course is that Utopian Studies is directly relevant to contemporary social problems and important to thinking creatively about solutions. In our discussion of texts and films and in student projects, we will focus on the connection between utopian thought and contemporary challenges such as ethnic and racial conflict, economic inequality, the dehumanizing effect of technological dependence, and alternatives to traditional nuclear family relationships. How can utopian thought or utopian experiments help us address these challenges? What warnings are provided by dystopian literature, film, and art? Students will be encouraged to explore a particular areas of "utopia studies" that are of interest to them such as Utopian communities, Utopia and religion, Utopia and gender, Utopia and race, Utopia and cosmopolitanism, Utopia and political philosophy, Utopia and economics, Utopia and bioengineering, Utopia and the environment, Utopia and animals, Utopia and art, Utopia and music, Utopia and architecture. Students who wish to do so are invited to "construct" their own utopian societies. Students may work individually or collaboratively with other students as they prepare a final paper or project.

title: Satire
description: What does satire do? While contemporary pundits make "straightforward" arguments with varying degrees of success, outlets like The Onion and The Colbert Report take a satirical approach not only to entertaining audiences, but to conveying principles they deem important. This course explores the art and function of the satirical tradition that predates (and includes) these contemporary satirists. We will cover satire, in prose and in poetry, from its roots in antiquity (Horace, Juvenal) to contemporary novels, punditry, and television, with significant coverage of the formative years of satire in English, the 17th and 18th centuries. Dryden, Pope, and Swift will figure prominently in this course, as will the under-acknowledged satirical writings of women like Aphra Behn, Mary Chudleigh, and Mary Wortly Montagu.

title: Comics and the Graphic Novel
description: Recent decades have born witness to an explosion of popular and critical interest in comics and the graphic novel, with older works providing the basis for Hollywood adaptations and newer ones reviewed beside literary bestsellers in the pages of major periodicals. Amidst this hubbub, it can be difficult to remember that the comics medium has a history all its own. In this course, we will explore the tangled path that comics took on their way into the public eye. From seminal newspaper comics of the early 20th century to the influence of the medium on Modernist painting, from the 1970s underground comix revolution to celebrated masterworks of the past decade, and beyond, we will explore the vast range of possibilities that this medium has embraced over the course of its history. In the process, we will work to determine what it means to talk about comics in an academic environment, striving to collectively develop a critical vocabulary to describe texts that have often been dismissed as mere popular fluff. Over the course of the semester, we will read works by a wide range of artists working in a variety of genres, including Jaime Hernandez, Chris Ware, Rebecca Dart, Alison Bechdel, and Alan Moore.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: This course is an introduction to the art and discipline of creative writing. Our exploration will take a number of forms: close study of the elements of craft (imagery, voice, character, etc.); vigorous discussion; analysis of the fiction, poetry, drama and creative non-fiction of published authors and of your peers, with special emphasis on the workshop process; and, above all, intensive writing of your own, both inside and outside of class. The course will culminate in a portfolio of your selected and revised work.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: This is an introduction to the practice of imaginative writing. The course is divided into three parts, according to the form of the writing. The first part deals with verse and covers the elements of verse composition and a few of its modes: location, syllable, line, sound structure, figure, and the rest. The second part deals with prose: the basic unit of composition is the sentence rather than the verse line. We will try or hand at fabulous documents, multiple points of view, and iterated memories. The third ventures into the domain of visual literature, which seeks to reconcile the disparate systems of the word and the image. Here we will consider the calligramme or pattern poem, the use of the alphabet as a basic element in concrete and visual poetry, and the merging of form, color, and text in narrative space. The main work of the course will be the making of your own imaginative works, which you will circulate for discussion from time to time. In the belief that writing and reading are inseparable, you will also read a range of creative and critical texts, in order to expand your awareness of the possibilities of the written word. The close study of these works is meant to suggest approaches you might take in your own creative projects.

title: Introduction to Fiction Writing
description: In this class, youâll immerse yourselves in the art and discipline of fiction writing, with particular focus on the short story. Partly this means acquiring a writerâs critical eye for fiction, so youâll study the basic elements of successful fiction (character, plot, description, etc.) and use these tools to read and analyze contemporary short stories. Of course, the writer above all learns through doing; youâll therefore write a great deal of your own fictionâregular exercises as well as more fully developed and revised work. Much of this development will happen as a result of the workshop process, where youâll give one another extensive feedback on work submitted to the class as a whole.

title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
description: In a workshop format, we will study a wide variety of published poems, and write and discuss our own poems, paying close attention to matters of form, image, figure, rhythm, voice, the "plot." Other activities we will pursue: a short critical paper on a recent book of poems; memorization of single poems; revision; a portfolio of your work; an anthology of poems by others; a class reading; visit by poets; attendance of local poetry readings; more revision.

title: Creative Nonfiction Workshop
description: An intensive writing workshop focused on discovering, researching and crafting creative nonfiction narrative. Readings and writing across the spectrum of this innovative âfourth genre.â Students should expect to write constantly and to contribute, as critical readers and writers, to the common cause of the workshop.Â

title: Script Writing
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the fundamental elements of writing for the large (film) and small (television) screen. It roots in the core precept that plot is character is action. Which means that the first object of study for all dramatic writing must be the examination of character. In workshop exercises, complemented by theoretical models from Keirsey and Greimas, students develop techniques for character analysis and development. At the same time, week by week, they examine character development in iconic films from a wide range of periods and genres. By the end of the term each student will have generated a short (20 minute) film for television and a short (20 minute) film for the wider screen. Students should note that in addition to class meetings during the week, they are also required to attend throughout the term regular film screenings on Sunday afternoons.Â

title: Introduction to Rhetoric
description: Why don't Democrats' arguments seem persuasive to Republicans, and vice versa? How did Dwyane Wade convince LeBron James and Chris Bosh to sign with the Miami Heat, and thereby change the course of NBA history? This class, at its core, asks the following questions: what is persuasion? How can I be persuasive? What persuades me? And, perhaps most importantly, what is at stake in our answers to these questions? This course will begin by considering theories and practices of persuasion in ancient and contemporary contexts. We will then examine multiple case studies of rhetoric in public life, moving back and forth between analyzing others' rhetoric and exercising our own rhetorical imaginations. Finally, you will create a case study of your own, focusing on local or national politics, campus debates, advertising campaigns, activist issues, and the like. This class will involve extensive reading, writing, and classroom discussion.

title: Writing to Be Heard
description: âSo what?â and âWhy should anyone care?â are the two fundamental questions that anyone writing for a diverse audience must address: by necessity, public intellectuals must explicitly confront these questions every time they begin a new piece throughout their careers. As a matter of professional survival, public intellectuals also must develop a writing style that negotiates between high and low cultures, addressing themselves to audiences composed of educated non-specialists, as well as the erudite. âWriting To Be Heardâ is a course for the serious aspiring critic and writer. It explores 20th rigorous practice in making complex ideas come alive for a wide audience. Students will study critical writing and trace its influence in key historical moments where questions of religion, sexuality, patriotism, class, and race have been debated. The course begins by exploring the elusive and changing definitions of âthe public intellectual.â It goes on to focus on the work of prominent twentieth-century critics such as H.L. Mencken, Edmund Wilson, Dwight Macdonald, Betty Friedan, Norman Mailer, Barbara Ehrenreich, Cornell West, Katha Pollitt and Melissa Harris-Perry. Throughout the semester, students will benefit from visits by working public intellectuals in print, radio, on the blogosphere, and in film. Selected Texts: H.L. Mencken, The Vintage Mencken; Edmund Wilson, Literary Essays of the 1920s and 30s; Norman Mailer, Armies of the Night; Katha Pollit, Virginity or Death! The Cornell West Reader, Barbara Ehrenreich, Nickel and Dimed

title: CBL: Reading, Teaching, Social Reflection
description: Reading, Teaching and Social reflection is intended for students interested in teaching, and besides the usual classes, involves working for two hours one morning a week (the morning is chosen by the student) as a teacher's aide in one of three inner-city Catholic schools. These schools include Assumption School in Anacostia, Immaculate Conception School in Shaw, and Sacred Heart Bi-lingual School in Mount Pleasant, all of which have been welcoming Georgetown students for the past 13 years. The focus of the course is on teaching reading in the primary grades, and students from this class have gone on to Teach for America, the Inner-City-Teaching-Corps and to degree programs in Education at Berkeley, Columbia Teachers College and Harvard, among other places.

title: Sursum Corda
description: Community Tutoring: Sursum Corda 1 This course introduces students to the methods and practices employed in the Sursum Corda / Georgetown University Tutoring Program, and introduces students to the Sursum Corda community, where, since 1970, Georgetown students have been instructing K-6 Sursum Corda children in language arts, and assisting them in other ways as well. The teaching commitment is Tuesdays and Thursdays 6:30-8:30pm in the community, and a Wednesday evening seminar at Georgetown. Requirements include (but are not limited to) the regular keeping of a journal, a midterm exam, and reflection paper. Community Tutoring: Sursum Corda 2 A continuation of Community Tutoring Sursum Corda 1, this class continues work with a Sursum Corda child, usually (but not invariably) the same one the Georgetown student has taught in the past, and includes a continuation of the earlier journal and a research paper, usually one on the Sursum Corda community and based in part upon the Fr. Horace McKenna papers preserved in Georgetownâs Special Collections on the fifth floor of Lauinger Library. It also involves working with, and giving advice to, beginning tutors, and assisting in other ways with the program.

title: Medieval English Literature
description: In this course, we will read some of the finest works of English literature, all of them composed during the great flowering of literary culture that took place during the late fourteenth century, the Age of Richard II. Our primary focus will be on the works of Geoffrey Chaucer, who questions all of the established verities of his time with his distinctive irony; but we will also take up selections from William Langland's magnificent, challenging poem, Piers Plowman; Julian of Norwich's Book of Showings; and the exquisite masterpieces, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight and Pearl. Research into the informing contexts of these works -- including art history, manuscript culture, and the political and social history of Ricardian England -- will help us to understand them more fully; and we will borrow from a range of critical approaches, including feminism and ecocriticism. All readings will be in modern English, but we will learn enough Middle English to dwell on selected passages with greater care.

title: Shakespeare's Exotic Romances
description: From puckish sprites to island savages, from man-eating bears to speaking statues, Shakespeareâs plays are populated with otherworldly or extraordinary elements that we might term exotic. Interestingly, most of these examples of exoticism occur within the context of romance, understood in two primary senses: as a genre encompassing Shakespeareâs last four plays, and as a gendered narrative trajectory of the knight-wins-damsel variety. Rather than simply survey his last plays, this course will try to deconstruct that generic category by drawing connections to two earlier plays and asking how Shakespeare makes exotic the conventions of romance. Students will have the opportunity to engage with a variety of theoretical perspectives, as well as delve into archival documents. Primary texts include A Midsummer Nightâs Dream, Othello, A Winterâs Tale, Cymbeline, Pericles, and The Tempest.

title: Defoe, Swift, Pope
description: The contemporaries Daniel Defoe (1660-1731), Jonathan Swift (1667-1745), and Alexander Pope (1688-1744) lived during what most of us would consider the inception of modernity. Over the course of their lives, Britain underwent an unprecedented transformation: traditional values, traditional ways of thinking, and traditional grounds of knowledge were questioned, undermined, and in some cases utterly repudiated; the sense of what constituted personal identity and selfhood and what faith one should put in individual experience was intensely scrutinized; assumptions about gender roles came under pressure; the basis of wealth and social status shifted radically from land to capital; life because more urban, more crowded, and more impersonal; popular, mass culture increasing replaced elite culture. Swift, Pope, and Defoe responded quite differently to these changes, sometimes rejecting the modern world as a threat, sometimes embracing it for its new possibilities, sometimes reaching a viable if not particularly comfortable accommodation with it. In this course, we will read some major works in which these three authors tried to come to terms with modernity: Swiftâs Tale of a Tub and Gulliverâs Travels; Popeâs Rape of the Lock, Essay on Man, and some of his later satires; and Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe, Moll Flanders, and Journal of the Plague Year. As a Level 2 elective, the success of this course depends entirely on the commitment of all students to reading the material closely, engaging it seriously and enthusiastically, researching what others have thought and said about it, and then sharing with classmates what they have learned. I have some ideas about these three authors that I want to share with you, but the shape and direction of our discussions will be determined largely by your own interests and your active participation in class. Each student will be asked to make at least one presentation and to write one short paper and a culminating research paper (about 15 pages) on a topic of their own choosing.

title: 18th Century Quixotism
description: A candidate for president running without the backing of a major party is "quixotic"; a grassroots political activist fighting establishment interests is "quixotic"; an entrepreneur venturing into a saturated market without a solid business plan is "quixotic," too. How did this concept of imprudent or heroic idealism, based on Miguel de Cervantes' Don Quixote, find its way into literature, language, and politics in the English-speaking world? This seminar focuses on the eighteenth-century emergence of the idea of quixotism as a literary phenomenon, a form of political propaganda, and a rich character attribute used to portray people, ideas, religions, and philosophies as heroic, silly, or depraved. We will learn how to decode eighteenth-century quixotism in its many moments of contradiction, to understand the role of quixotism in the development of the novel in English, and to relate Enlightenment notions of quixotism to issues in contemporary political and cultural discussions.

title: The Other Victorians
description: From doilies and ornate wallpaper to the stiff-necked curates and gossiping parlor ladies of Masterpiece Theater, the Victorian period (1837-1901) has long been stereotyped as a Great Age of Conformity. Â Criticism has replayed these popular images of Victorian orthodoxy by charting the powerful forces of normalization in this era: the rise of the middle class, the ascent of commodity capitalism, the establishment of modern gender hierarchies, and the consolidation of heteronormative âdecency,â to name just a few. Â But where canonical approaches to Victorian literature and culture have focused on literatureâs ability to produce sameness, this class will focus on differenceÂ -- and on how literary thinking helped imagine it. Rather than attending to those modes of Victorian life most characteristic of the age, we will explore forms of being and thinking that were counternormative, antihegemonic, insurgent, queer, and strange. Â Our concern, in other words, will be on the possibility for otherness in an age of homogenization. When mass culture and social sameness threaten to subsume all difference, how might literature imagine the new? Because these questions are simultaneously historical and theoretical, our readings will be organized along two axes, the balance shifting in the graduate and undergraduate sections.Â First, we will examine literary and critical writing from the Victorian era: canonical novels, key poetry, political theory, and items of erotica, ephemera, philosophy, and orientalism. Â Second we will sample modern thinking about difference from literary and cultural critics like S. Marcus, D.A. Miller, N. Armstrong, and M. Poovey; and philosophers like G.W.F. Hegel, M. Foucault, T. Adorno, R. Williams, J.L. Nancy, G. Deleuze, and E. Grosz.Â No prior knowledge of Victorian literature is required Victorian texts may include some of the following (balance of primary and secondary texts shifts for graduate and undergraduate sections): Emily Bronte, Wuthering Heights & selected poetry; Elizabeth Gaskell, Cranford; Darwin, from The Origin of the Species; A.C. Swinburne, Poems and Ballads; Matthew Arnold, from Culture and Anarchy and selected poetry; John Stuart Mill, from The Subjection of Women; Robert Browning, selected poetry; Sir Richard Francis Burton, The Kasidah of Haji Abdu El-Yezdi; Edward FitzGerald, âThe RubÃ¡iyÃ¡t of Omar KhayyÃ¡mâ; poetry and painting of the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood; Lewis Carroll, Alice in Wonderland and selected photographs; Oscar Wilde, selected poetry & from De Profundis; Michael Field (a.k.a. Katherine Bradley & Edith Cooper), selected poetry.

title: Dickens
description: A course in which we will read three major novels, analyze them closely, and consider them within their historical context. TEXTS. Itâs crucially necessary for students to have in hand the following editions for our class discussions. Charles Dickens. Bleak House. Penguin. Paperback. ISBN 978-0-143972-3 Charles Dickens. A Tale of Two Cities. Penguin. Paperback. ISBN 0-141-43960-2 Charles Dickens. Great Expectations. Penguin. Paperback. ISBN 978-0-143956-3 THE WAY WE WILL WORK Essays. During the semester each student will write four essays each of roughly five pages in length. These essays will approach Dickensâ novels in three different ways: 1. Sources. Here students doing research work in the library will explore the relationship between a passage from a Dickens novel and source texts which report on the material conditions of human life and action in the world which he is describing. These source texts can be documents contemporary with Dickensâ own life or later studies. These papers must feature footnotes and bibliography in MLA or Chicago Book of Style format. This essay must emerge from work done with books and periodicals in the library. Web research by itself is not satisfactory. 2. Art. Here students will examine carefully one or more specific aspects of Dickens as a writer of novels. These will be essays in the critical analysis of fiction. Students who include references to the work of other literary critics must supply footnotes and bibliography in MLA or Chicago format. 3. Re-Imagining. Here students will choose a passage from a Dickens novel and in some imaginative way use what Dickens has written as the basis for their own creative invention. These essays will be graded âYes,â âYes Remarkable,â or âNo.â Students may only write one âre-imaginingâ paper during the semester. Students will be assigned membership in one of six Report Groups and on the days appointed for their group will hand in an essay. During the semester each student must do one of each of the three kinds of essays listed above; the student is free to choose from among the first two the form of the studentâs fourth paper. During our class discussion I will ask the students handing in a paper on that day to tell the class a bit about their work and its consequences in reading the Dickens novel under discussion. Quizzes. Each class will begin with a short [usually five question] factual quiz on the dayâs assigned reading. Class Participation is vital for an excellent performance in this course. Semesterâs End: Students will write an essay titled âMy Dickensâ of roughly four to five pages in length in which they will discuss one or two aspects of Dickens about which they have come to have strong ideas. The essay will consider (in some fashion) all three of the novels we have discussed. A more detailed description of this project will be distributed in April.

title: Wordsworth and Coleridge
description: This course will examine the poetry of Wordsworth and Coleridge during their most productive years, examining the beginnings of âThe Recluseâ, highlights from Lyrical Ballads, âThe Two-Part Preludeâ, and the many shorter works that were to follow. Students will read Coleridgeâs great poems, including âKubla Khanâ, âThe Ancient Marinerâ and âChristabelâ. Throughout the course, the emphasis will be on the development of the two poets and their interaction with each other. The emphasis throughout this course will be on disciplined, close analysis of text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. The aim will be to enable students to reach the point at which they are able to read and analyse poetry with confidence. The elements of prosody are thus essential concomitants, and for that reason a principal course text will be Philip Hobsbaumâs Metre, Rhythm and Verse Form (Routledge), which students will be expected to read and memorize. Students should be prepared to write regular short essays on aspects of the poetry. Each essay will contribute to your overall assessment, as will your attendance record and performance in class. Students are expected also to attend one-to-one tutorials with Professor Wu in order to discuss their work. There will be occasional tests on technical aspects of prosody. All these various activities will contribute to each studentâs final grade. This will be a challenging course which will make considerable demands on your time and energies.

title: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: In this course, weâll examine late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century womenâs anti-slavery texts, in hopes of understanding how concepts of religion, race, and gender became entangled during the formative years of the American republic. What propels black and white women to enter the risky world of abolitionism, and how do they protect themselves as they enter fiercely contested public debates about race? What are the limits of their literary imaginings? What is the relationship between self-love and sympathy within women's abolitionist performances? Weâll track the changes in how abolitionists gained access to the public sphere or redefined the âprivateâ sphere, and search out archival materials as we analyze anti-slavery poems, essays, plays, speeches, short stories, letters, and autobiographical novels. In addition to a variety of theoretical articles, our readings may include Phillis Wheatleyâs poems, Susanna Rowsonâs plays, Maria W. Stewartâs speeches, and Ellen Craftâs narrative, along with a host of lesser-known anti-slavery texts. Students in American Studies, African American Studies, Performance Studies, Womenâs Studies, Literary History, and Justice & Peace Studies may be especially interested in the material of this course.

title: Reading Emily Dickinson
description: This course addresses contemporary debates in literary-historical and literary-critical analysis though a sustained focus on Emily Dickinson's life, work and influence. In addition to extensive training in poetic analysis, we will conduct a comprehensive review of critical approaches to Dickinson's work from formalist, historicist, biographical, feminist, queer and deconstructionist perspectives. The reading load for the course will be substantial, including historical, critical and theoretical texts as well as Dickinsonâs poetry; students will also research the history of print and online publication of Dickinsonâs work. In addition to a number of critical writing assignments, there will be a required multimedia final project (no previous multimedia experience required).

title: Modern British Class Novel
description: This course examines literary representations of the British working class during a charged, transformative era in modern British labor history from the 1890s through the 1940s: the rise of the Labour Party, along with other Socialist movements; trade union activism, culminating in the 1926 General Strike; implementation of the welfare state; and the flourishing of âIndependent Working-Class Educationâ through the Labour College movement. This same period proved to be an exciting moment of literary ârevolutionâ as well, producing new modernist styles of narrative alongside more traditional forms of realism. Drawing on readings in social/cultural/literary history and theory about this period as well as current theories of class as a gendered and racialized construct, we will explore a range of novelsâand in a few cases, short fictionâ that address class conflict and an emerging working-class identity written by more celebrated as well as lesser-known authors who themselves span the working and middle/educated classes: the 1890s âslum novelâ of East End life, such as George Gissingâs Thyrza and Arthur Morrisonâs A Child of the Jago; E.M. Forsterâs Howardâs End; D.H. Lawrenceâs stories, such as âOdour of Chrysanthemumsâ and âTickets, Please,â and his novel Sons and Lovers; Katherine Mansfieldâs modernist short stories; Ellen Wilkinsonâs Clash; Ethel Carnie Holdsworthâs This Slavery; excerpts from Robert Tressellâs The Ragged Trousered Philanthropists and Lewis Grassic Gibbonâs Scottish trilogy A Scots Quair. Weâll complement these texts with selections from literary and educational magazines produced either by or for working-class writers/readers, such as the âPlebsâ Magazine and Left Review. Weâll pay particular attention to thinking through notions of class shame, resistance, and guilt, studying how all three shape narrative form as well as content.

title: Transnational Modernities
description: Mexico, Turkey and Bengal: Parallel Literatures, Parallel Modernities. The course attempts to look at the parallel trajectory of modernity in the literary and historical texts of three very different regions during the twentieth century. Comparative study of themes such as political struggle, melancholy, feminist selves, language and national identity in Mexican, Bengali and Turkish fiction , and in writers as diverse as Juan Rulfo, Rabindranath Tagore and Ahmet Hamdi Tanpinar. The course will also be a course in Urban Studies as well, as a particular year â 1947 â will be considered in three cities : Calcutta, Mexico City and Istanbul. Finally, the course will also look at parallels in Turkish, Mexican and Bengali cinema. The larger aim of the course is to circumvent conventional, Euro-American approaches to World History and Literature.

title: Reading Toni Morrison
description: In this course, we will read the writings of Nobel laureate Toni Morrison, exploring how Morrison represents history, nation, race, gender, sexuality, and religion in her works. As Morrison indicates in her Nobel Lecture, language represents dominant ideologies, including ideologies that can prevent the circulation of new and generative knowledge and that can hinder the exchange of ideas. Her works, in many respects, challenge and complicate hegemonic notions of history, nation, race, gender, sexuality, and religion, making them more expansive. Reading her novels, literary and cultural criticism, Nobel lecture, and short story âRecitatif,â we will examine the âcultural workâ that Morrison performs as a cultural conservator. In particular, we will think about how Morrisonâs writing produces discourses that augment, refute, complicate, and even reinforce many of the dominant ideas surrounding identity that circulate in contemporary culture. Students will be able to discuss several aspects of Toni Morrisonâs writing, including, but not limited to, the differences and similarities between Morrisonâs texts and how those similarities and differences reflect her changing historical, social, and political perspectives. How does Morrison achieve her goal of writing literature that is both beautiful and political? Beginning with The Bluest Eye, we will examine Morrison's works from a variety of critical approaches, particularly critical race theory, Black feminist theory, and African American cultural studies. This course is reading and writing intensive. Students may expect short papers, quizzes, and a research project.

title: Shadows of Hitchcock
description: This course will examine films of director Alfred Hitchcock and various filmmakers he has influenced, including Brian DePalma, John Carpenter, Jonathan Demme, Roman Polanski, Michelangelo Antonioni, Park Chan-wook, Gus Van Sant, Pedro Almodovar, and George Romero. On a deeper level, however, this is a class about intertextuality. Using Hitchcock as a case study, we will define and sometimes trouble the boundaries between remake, parody, imitation, influence, homage, allusion, and pastiche.

title: Literature and Theory of Race
description: In this course we will explore how race has functioned to create US culture in the past and how it operates differently (or not) today. We will analyze literary and other cultural representations of race, primarily within the context of the United States, alongside theories of race and how the concept of difference and otherness has evolved through different historical periods and various national identities. How has âraceâ as a category and a means of differentiation evolved and been redefined depending upon various legal, political, and social contexts and how is it relevant (or not) in the era of global capitalism? **If you have not taken the prerequisites for the class but are still interested in taking it, please contact the professor.**

title: Technologies of the Text
description: Do you ever wonder how certain books became bestsellers? Do you think about how illustrations influence the interpretation of particular literary works? Or how their original appearance in magazines affects how we read them today? Or even how the physical form and marketing of books can determine who reads them? What about the effects of forgery, plagiarism, and anonymous publication? This course addresses such questions. We will consider the term âtextâ from many perspectivesâincluding digital media, experimental books, art objectsâand even musical scores and dance-notation âtexts.â We will ask how such forms are redefining the terms âauthorâ and âreaderââand how they are changing our definitions of âliteratureâ and ânewsâ and âsocial networking.â We will also consider how various textual forms affect novels, plays, and poetry. How is literature influenced by, say, Thackerayâs emphasis on his novelsâ drawings, Joyceâs preoccupation with pagination, or Emily Dickinsonâs use of paper scraps and handmade notebooks? How did the serialization of Dickensâs novels in periodicals transform their composition, distribution, and readership? What do William Blakeâs bizarre paintings tell us about his poetry? In the past, we have also read influential pieces by Austen, Defoe, Sterne, Pope, Wordsworth, Byron, Hemans, the Brontes, and Woolfâas well as some recent works within literary and electronic textual studies. In the field of experimental texts and new media, we will also discuss (at least briefly) Graphic Novels (Comic Books), Fan Fiction, Online Readerships, Videogames, Blogging, Online Journalism, Hypertexts, Animeâas well as Podcasting, Wikis, RSS-Feeds, Web 2.0, and the new E-books. Each semester, we develop special topics in the seminar according to the particular research interests of each class member. In past years, for instance, we have read the most popular hypertext ever writtenânamely, âPatchwork Girl,â a powerful take-off on the novel Frankenstein (which we also discuss during the course). We have also read graphic novels such as the award-winning Fun Home, Akira, Persepolis and The Watchmen (we look at the film versions of the latter two as well). And we also consider many texts from a cross-cultural perspective, including American versions of Japanese anime and other non-Western genres. During previous terms, we have also examined the historical origins of todayâs print cultureâincluding the history of libraries, museums, and private art collections. We have discussed the origins of copyright law, comparing the first amazing trials with todayâs debates about digital copyright (including the famous Napster and Mickey Mouse cases). And we have investigated notorious internet hoaxes (including the âLonely Girlâ scandal)âand traced their origins to the most intriguing art and literary forgeries (such as those by Thomas Chatterton). If you have any questions about the course, please contact Prof. Michael Macovski at msm44@georgetown.edu.

title: Lannan Poetry Seminar
description: Lannan Poetry Seminar encourages dynamic critical thinking and creativity with a view to examining literary forms and practices; the work of individual authors; the relationship between writing and other arts; and the place of writing in contemporary culture. Fellows study and discuss the work of the yearâs scheduled guests in the Lannan âReadings and Talksâ series, participate in writing workshops, and then produce an independent final project (creative or critical) under the guidance of the instructor. Enrollment limited to those who have been accepted as Lannan Fellows the previous autumn.

title: Advanced Poetry Workshop
description: This workshop will help writers work to complete a portfolio of poems. What can you discover, what can you say in poems? For this workshop weâll read poems intensely; discuss published poems, and drafts of our own poems; write critical prose on a number of short books of poems; acquaint ourselves with poetic forms and some useful prose arguments by poets; assemble anthologies of our favorite poems; attend readings; and even give a poetry reading ourselves. Weâll give and receive helpful criticism of poems in a group setting, and in conferences with me. This workshop offers you a chance to follow up on the early promise and challenge of individual poems, and to build a real manuscript. Prerequisites: one college-level course in creative writing (any genre) or an equivalent background; and some working familiarity with poetry, gained either by coursework or individual study. Please bring copies of three of your poems to the first class meeting. Permission of instructor required.

title: Intermediate Fiction Writing: Short Story
description: This course will build on a basic familiarity with fiction writing in order to deepen, develop, and complicate that understanding, particularly in the context of the short story, a form we will explore intensively. Class sessions will consist of workshopping, analysis of contemporary short fiction, and discussion of complex issues of craft and fictional purpose. Admission by permission of the instructor ONLY. Interested students should e-mail sample writing (5-10 pages) and personal statement about what they hope to get out of the class, by April 2nd, toÂ dhe8@georgetown.edu.

title: Experimental Fiction
description: In this advanced writing workshop, we will read key texts of 20th and 21st-century experimental fiction. Using these writings as springboards, we will create our own weekly experiments. The class is student-led, and demands enormous commitment from its members. Expect to read sexually transgressive, politically charged, mixed media works. Expect to expand your notion of what constitutes a text. Expect to exceed your own expectations of your writing.

title: Intermediate Scriptwriting
description: For students who have successfully completed English 289. This course builds on the earlier course's emphasis on narrative by concentrating on the structure and rhythm of the individual scene and on the use and misuse of dialogue. The class meets Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays at 9:00 AM, with mandatory screenings on most Sunday afternoons through the term.

title: Advanced Scriptwriting
description: For students who have successfully completed English 289 and 456. Taught as individual tutorials at times to be determined by the student together with the instructor. Students are required to start the term with a detailed treatment for an original feature and by the end of term to have completed a full-length script for that feature.

title: Advanced Scriptwriting
description: Students who demonstrate aptitude in Script Writing may apply for permission to take Intermediate Scriptwriting. Working in small groups, accepted students will write an original short script which they will then shoot during the second half of the term. Because time within the semester is limited, students are encouraged to discuss script ideas with the instructor before the beginning of the term.Â

title: The Writer's Perspective
description: The purpose of this seminar is to dissect and interrogate not only the use, but the choice and function of detail across a broad range of visual and literary art, from photography to poetry, narrative fiction, essay, and journalism. We will examine the particular emphasis certain artists have placed on âseeingâ in order to understand both the aesthetic and political consequences of each artistsâ argument for how to see, or in some cases, how we fail to properly see the world around us.

title: Hybrid Forms
description: This writing focused seminar will examine the literary techniques and approaches used by novelists, journalists, and essayists in creating narratives that challenge the distinction between fiction and nonfiction, novel and autobiography, journalism, essays, and short stories. We will study the âliteraryâ techniques used by contemporary essayists and journalists, such as John DâAgata, Katherine Boo, and Mark Danner along with hybrid texts from writers such as Philip Roth, Junot Diaz, Sherman Alexie and Hans Magnus Enzensberger.

title: Capstone: Shakespeare
description: Students will collaborate with the instructor on the content of this course which is open to a wide range of Shakespeare-related topics. Applicants are required to meet in person and/or virtually with the instructor during the spring 2013 semester to help develop the syllabus, which will be based on the interests of all the participants. Some possible topics for study include: literary or popular re-writings of Shakespeareâs plays, Shakespeare in performance, the authorship controversy, Shakespeare biography, creative projects based on Shakespeare, literary theoretical approaches to Shakespeare (genre, race, gender, queer, historicist, psychoanalytic, material culture, etc.), translation of Shakespeare, Shakespeare and law, etc. We will select one of Shakespeareâs plays to read and consider within the context of other cultural texts from the early modern period; this historicist grounding will sharpen our consideration of subsequent engagements with Shakespeareâs work in different historical and cultural contexts. In the course of the semester, students will provide readings for the syllabus, make presentations of their project in class, and produce a substantial final project which could take the shape of a long essay, a video, a performance, a screen/play, a poem, prose fiction...

title: Capstone: 19th Century Comedy
description: A Capstone Seminar discussing comedy as a genre, the comic or what causes people to laugh, and hence joking and laughter. We will read some theoretical work both by classic writers like Freud, Bakhtin, and Frye, and also many recent discussions of comedy theory by a wide range of contemporary theorists and critics. Nevertheless the central focus of the seminar will be on primary texts including: Austenâs Pride and Prejudice, a hefty portion of Byronâs Don Juan, Dickensâ A Christmas Carol, dramatic monologues by Robert Browning, the Alice books of Lewis Carroll, examples of mid-nineteenth century cartooning from Punch and other illustrated magazines, Gilbert and Sullivanâs operetta Patience, and Oscar Wildeâs The Importance of Being Earnest. Students will take responsibility for leading seminar discussion and will report on recent scholarship and criticism dealing with the course topic and its texts. As the semester moves towards its end students will develop individual essays in which they will discuss one or more of the theoretical issues raised by the course in the light of both one or more texts from the course itself, and one or more texts from their earlier studies at Georgetown. This project will enable each student to draw together ideas and knowledge from work done as an English Major and will permit a retrospective summation for some of that work. During these final weeks in workshop sessions we will discuss research, reading, writing, and how to continue to do each better.

title: Capstone: US Literature and Commodity Culture, 1890-1930
description: In this seminar, we will examine the meaning and representation of âthingsâ in a diverse group of U. S. cultural texts from late-19th and early-20th century America, a period widely recognized as pivotal for the emergence of a culture mass consumption and an era of extensive U. S. external imperialism and economic expansion. Surveying a variety of representational forms--including literary and film texts, newspaper and magazine advertisements, and promotional and documentary materials about the display of âthingsâ in the 1893 Chicago Worldâs Fair--as well as a range of critical and theoretical writings on the subject, we will focus on the interrelations of gender, sexuality, race, ethnicity, class, and nation in U. S. commodity culture. In particular, we will examine the diverse desires and identifications articulated through commodities in U. S. cultural texts, and the complex ways in which individuals use commodities to construct (or deconstruct) individual and collective identities. We will conclude by considering a few examples from early-21st century television and film, to consider some developments in U. S. commodity culture in our contemporary moment. Although the content of our discussions each week will be shaped by the interests of seminar members, topics covered by the readings include: the ""feminization"" of consumption, and the relationship between feminism and consumerism, during this period; the import of representations of ""Americanization"" through consumption in immigrant fiction from this era; the international, imperialist significance of images of American ""abundance"" and ""conspicuous consumption"" in advertisements and prose narratives; the relationship between commodity consumption and the politics of ""passing"" in African American fiction; the saturation of commodity culture, especially advertising, with racial signifiers and imagery, and the racial fantasies activated in and by mass consumption; and the historically and theoretically complex relationship between cinema and commodity spectacle. Authors considered will likely include: Willa Cather, Kate Chopin, Stephen Crane, Theodore Dreiser, Henry James, James Weldon Johnson, Nella Larsen, Edith Wharton, Anzia Yezierska, as well as a range of literary and cultural critics and theorists. Visual âtextsâ we will examine will likely include: Coco Fusco and Guillermo GÃ³mez-PeÃ±a, The Couple in a Cage; Ethnic Notions (Dir. Marlon Riggs); Dirty Pretty Things (Dir. Stephen Frears); and episodes from Queer Eye for the Straight Guy, Sex in the City, and Girls. Course requirements: weekly 1-2 page reflection papers; an oral presentation; and a seminar length (20-page) final essay.

title: Capstone: Queer Cinema
description: Capstone: Queer Cinema

title: Capstone:  Testamonial Fictions
description: The course surveys mostly post-Cold War literary and film representations of US Latino diasporic communities negotiating the historical, political and cultural legacies of extreme state-sponsored violence on the part of dictatorial regimes in their homelands. One prominent thread running through Testimonial Fictions is the unique impact of sexualized traumatic political violence on the individuals and communities who experience and survive it, and especially how that impact manifests itself in the sexual and gender politics of the US-based diasporic communities in question. Case studies will include Dominican America after Trujillo, Haitian America after the Duvaliers, Cuban America ""after"" Castro, and Guatemalan/Salvadoran America after the 1980's-era juntas. Writers and texts include Julia Alvarez (In the Time of the Butterflies), Junot DÃ­az (Oscar Wao), Edwidge Danticat (The Dew Breaker and Brother, I'm Dying), Reinaldo Arenas (Before Night Falls), Cristina GarcÃ­a (A Handbook to Luck), Hector Tobar (The Tattooed Soldier) and William Archila (The Art of Exile). Film work will include adaptations of some of these texts but also original titles like Roman Polanski's Death and the Maiden, Jonathan Demme's The Agronomist, and Cary Fukunaga's Sin Nombre. Course readings will also include significant critical and scholarly work in Latin American and US Latino Studies, as well as in critical, political, gender and sexuality theory.

title: Capstone:  Honors Thesis
description: This course is for students accepted into the Honors Program. Students will be expected to read and comment on critical and theoretical texts related to the honors projects, to read and comment on each other's work, and to present their own work for critique. Attendance and engagement as well as periodic written responses are required. Student should expect to complete about 25 pages of the honors thesis by the end of the course.Â

title: Tutorial For Thesis Writers
description: ENGL 496 is the second semester of the year-long Honors Program in English, and it is open only to senior English majors who have enrolled in ENGL 495 in the fall. Work done on the thesis during the spring term constitutes a required 1-credit course, which represents the completion of the thesis in coordination with the mentor. The final draft of the thesis is due to the faculty mentor by mid-March. In April during their Senior year, successful candidates (those receiving a minimum of an "A-" in the judgment of the student's faculty mentor and a second anonymous faculty reader chosen by the Honors Committee) will make brief presentations of their thesis projects at an open meeting of the Thesis Colloquium, to which all faculty and students are invited.

title: Medieval English Literature
description: In this course, we will read some of the finest works of English literature, all of them composed during the great flowering of literary culture that took place during the late fourteenth century, the Age of Richard II. Our primary focus will be on the works of Geoffrey Chaucer, who questions all of the established verities of his time with his distinctive irony; but we will also take up selections from William Langland's magnificent, challenging poem, Piers Plowman; Julian of Norwich's Book of Showings; and the exquisite masterpieces, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight and Pearl. Research into the informing contexts of these works -- including art history, manuscript culture, and the political and social history of Ricardian England -- will help us to understand them more fully; and we will borrow from a range of critical approaches, including feminism and ecocriticism. All readings will be in modern English, but we will learn enough Middle English to dwell on selected passages with greater care.

title: Transformational Tudor Theater
description: The Theatrical Transformation of the Public Sphere Habermas describes the public sphere as a space of public discourse outside official culture. He locates it in the newsletters and coffee houses of eighteenth-century Europe, but historians have since attempted to make claims for the earlier development of a public sphere in seventeenth-century England, especially in connection with the Civil War. This seminar will test the idea of taking the concept yet another step back, to the commercial theater of Tudor and Stuart London as a site of public culture. Building also on such classic work as Jonathan Dollimoreâs argument about âradical tragedy,â we will search out plays that engaged actively with contemporary political, social, and economic issues and events. These may include the collaborative Gorboduc (1561), the anonymous Arden of Faversham (1592), Shakespeareâs Richard II (1595), Thomas Heywoodâs The First and Second Parts of King Edward IV (1599), the collaborative Witch of Edmonton (1621), Thomas Middletonâs Game at Chess (1624), and others. We will discuss ways in which these works address political thought, social theory, and international relationsâas well as the citations and censorship that give evidence of their impact on public discourse.

title: Shakespeare's Exotic Romances
description: From puckish sprites to island savages, from man-eating bears to speaking statues, Shakespeareâs plays are populated with otherworldly or extraordinary elements that we might term exotic. Interestingly, most of these examples of exoticism occur within the context of romance, understood in two primary senses: as a genre encompassing Shakespeareâs last four plays, and as a gendered narrative trajectory of the knight-wins-damsel variety. Rather than simply survey his last plays, this course will try to deconstruct that generic category by drawing connections to two earlier plays and asking how Shakespeare makes exotic the conventions of romance. There will be a strong theoretical/critical component, as well as some archival research. Primary texts may include A Midsummer Nightâs Dream, Othello, A Winterâs Tale, Pericles, and The Tempest.

title: The British 1790s
description: This course will cover the development of political ideas and literary culture throughout the 1790s in Britain. It will therefore deal with such figures as Thomas Paine, Edmund Burke, Erasmus Darwin, Robert Burns, Mary Wollstonecraft, Anna Laetitia Barbauld, Joseph Johnson, and William Godwin, as well as the early careers of writers who were to become identified with Romanticism: Lamb, Hazlitt, Coleridge, Wordsworth and Blake. It will be an extremely demanding course in that the reading load will be very heavy, for all members of this class will be expected to keep up with the schedule. Furthermore, students will present to the rest of the class presentations concerning individual writers of the period, narrating important events in which they were involved and some of the intellectual concepts that came out of their work. The emphasis throughout will be not on theoretical discussion but on close, accurate analysis of what the various writers actually said. There will also be frequent tests about the writers covered throughout this course. All these activities will contribute to your final grade. Students are advised to take this course only if they can give it the time and energy it will demand.

title: The Other Victorians
description: From doilies and ornate wallpaper to the stiff-necked curates and gossiping parlor ladies of Masterpiece Theater, the Victorian period (1837-1901) has long been stereotyped as a Great Age of Conformity. Â Criticism has replayed these popular images of Victorian orthodoxy by charting the powerful forces of normalization in this era: the rise of the middle class, the ascent of commodity capitalism, the establishment of modern gender hierarchies, and the consolidation of heteronormative âdecency,â to name just a few. Â But where canonical approaches to Victorian literature and culture have focused on literatureâs ability to produce sameness, this class will focus on differenceÂ -- and on how literary thinking helped imagine it. Rather than attending to those modes of Victorian life most characteristic of the age, we will explore forms of being and thinking that were counternormative, antihegemonic, insurgent, queer, and strange. Â Our concern, in other words, will be on the possibility for otherness in an age of homogenization. When mass culture and social sameness threaten to subsume all difference, how might literature imagine the new? Because these questions are simultaneously historical and theoretical, our readings will be organized along two axes, the balance shifting in the graduate and undergraduate sections.Â First, we will examine literary and critical writing from the Victorian era: canonical novels, key poetry, political theory, and items of erotica, ephemera, philosophy, and orientalism. Â Second we will sample modern thinking about difference from literary and cultural critics like S. Marcus, D.A. Miller, N. Armstrong, and M. Poovey; and philosophers like G.W.F. Hegel, M. Foucault, T. Adorno, R. Williams, J.L. Nancy, G. Deleuze, and E. Grosz.Â No prior knowledge of Victorian literature is required Victorian texts may include some of the following (balance of primary and secondary texts shifts for graduate and undergraduate sections): Emily Bronte, Wuthering Heights & selected poetry; Elizabeth Gaskell, Cranford; Darwin, from The Origin of the Species; A.C. Swinburne, Poems and Ballads; Matthew Arnold, from Culture and Anarchy and selected poetry; John Stuart Mill, from The Subjection of Women; Robert Browning, selected poetry; Sir Richard Francis Burton, The Kasidah of Haji Abdu El-Yezdi; Edward FitzGerald, âThe RubÃ¡iyÃ¡t of Omar KhayyÃ¡mâ; poetry and painting of the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood; Lewis Carroll, Alice in Wonderland and selected photographs; Oscar Wilde, selected poetry & from De Profundis; Michael Field (a.k.a. Katherine Bradley & Edith Cooper), selected poetry.

title: Reading Race in American Literature
description: In this course, we will examine Anglo-American and African American narratives from 1845 to the present to understand better the social construction of race in American society. Slavery codified the "color line." In 1903, W.E.B. DuBois wrote that the problem of the twentieth century in the United States would be âthe color line.â We will be particularly interested in how the binary of black and white signifies, symbolizes and reifies social and cultural currency, interests and conflicts as depicted in American literature. In many ways, the history of the United States and the history of race are intricately woven. Although race is foundational in US history, constructions of race are rather fluid. We will both interpret and interrogate the nation's racial constructions, and what those constructions reveal about nation building and national identity. In this course, we are concerned with how American writers on both sides of the color line construct and represent race? By pairing representative texts, we will explore how thematically, stylistically, and culturally the texts speak to each other. We will follow, for example, an arc that takes us, in Anglo-American literature, from the seemingly expressiveness and authority in terms of race encoded in The Scarlet Letter to lack thereof in Pynchon's The Crying of Lot 49. Of the African American texts, from Douglass's Narrative to Morrison's A Mercy, we will explore a move from physical bondage to socio-psychological bondage, predicated on and complicated by race. Critical race theory, Black feminist theory, and psychoanalytic theories will inform our reading. Requirements may include short critical papers, oral presentations, energetic class participation, and a research paper. Authors may include Douglass, Twain, Chopin, Cather, Fitzgerald, Faulkner, Larsen, J.W. Johnson, Hurston, Wright, Walker, and Morrison.

title: Joyce and Beckett
description: Recent Joyce and Beckett criticism has seen a move away from a reading of these two authors as deracinated, transnational modernists, and towards a more material and political reading of their work in historical context. This shift has come about thanks in part to an increasing interest in the intersection between modernism and colonialism, and between Irish literature and postcolonial studies. Our reading will situate Joyce and Beckett in this context, while also paying attention to the philosophical tradition in Beckett criticism, which remains a strong and compelling countering force to the situated criticism of recent years. The aim of this seminar is to introduce you to scholarly arguments in Joyce and Beckett criticism, and to equip you for further research in the fields of modernism, postcolonial theory, and the novel. The aim is also, shamelessly, to take pleasure in reading some excellent writing! Primary texts will include Ulysses, A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, Dubliners, More Pricks than Kicks, Murphy, Molloy, Malone Dies, The Unnamable, Waiting for Godot and other short Beckett plays.

title: Black Pop Culture and Politics
description: In this course we will examine the relationships that black (popular) culture has to black political activity by foregrounding how formal and informal politics mutually constitute each other. As Gene Jarrett explains, formal politics refers to âgovernmental activity, public policy, law and social formations,â while informal politics refers to âcultural media, representation and subjectivityâ (Representing the Race). Throughout our examination of different aspects of black (popular) culture, we will consider how these various cultural modes of performance implicitly and explicitly shape black political activity. As we historicize black cultural modes of expressivity, we will consider how, for example, did rhythm and blues song performances during the 1960s shape black political consciousness during the Civil Rights and Black Power Movements. In our examination of the politics of gender during the 1970s era, we will think energetically about how the Blaxploitation film eraâs representations of black gender and sexuality resulted in social formations that undermined black political progress. In terms of cultural media to be examined, televangelism, reality television, self-help television shows, rhythm and blues music, film, magazines, and political television and radio shows will serve as our primary cultural sources to explore how black (popular) culture negotiates, complicates, severs, and reaffirms the relationship that exists between formal and informal politics. We will draw upon the methodologies of cultural studies, black religious studies, black gender and sexuality studies, civil rights historiography, literary studies, film studies, and African American Studies to assist us in our scholarly inquiry. We will pay keen attention to the intersections of race, class, gender, and sexuality in our discussions of the politics of blackness, aiming to understand better the variety of political interests and concerns that constitute the too often simplified categories of âblackâ and âblack political interests.â Students should expect to make a scholarly presentation during class, serve as a respondent, complete and present a syllabus for an undergraduate course, write an abstract for a conference presentation, write a final research paper, and actively engage the readings, primary texts, and seminar conversations.

title: 20C Poetry
description: "The Poetry of Witness and the Poetics of Lyric Space" In this seminar we will read 20th and 21st century poetry of witness, in English and in translation, as well as short lyric prose and readings on the theory and philosophy of witness. Poets will include Celan, Tsvetayeva, JabÃ¨s, Trakl, Oppen, and Komunyakaa, among others, and we will also read philosophers Emmanuel Levinas, Walter Benjamin, and Giorgio Agamben and works on trauma theory. We will be interested in the construction of lyric selfhood in the aftermath of extremity, including war, imprisonment, political oppression and exile.

title: Appalachian Literature
description: Wendell Barryâs 2012 Jefferson Lecture at the National Endowment for the Humanities contemplates Wallace Stegnerâs concepts of âboomersâ who âpillage and runâ and âstickersâ who âsettle and love the life they have made and the place they have made it in.â This course interrogates what it means to be "Appalachian," "hillbilly," or "from the mountains." In this course we will investigate the literature, the history, culture, and stereotypes of the Appalachian region. We will focus on the mountains as they figure physically and metaphorically in the language and lives of those who have hunted in, lived in, stayed put in, extracted from, left, returned to, or been haunted by the remoteness and the richness of the region.
