title: Advanced Oral Communication
description: In this course, students will enhance their oral communication skills up to the high intermediate or low advanced level by engaging in various communicative activities such as describing events and facts, giving formal presentations, expressing ideas and convincing others. Students will also improve their written skills by reading a wide range of topics/issues and writing essays based on the readings with expanded vocabulary. It is also expected that students will deepen their linguistic and sociocultural understanding by having exposure to different speech/written styles, various expressions and sociocultural contexts. At the end of this course, students will be able to communicate with higher fluency and accuracy in both speaking and writing. Prerequisite: KREN-212 or equivalent.

title: Business Korean I
description: This course emphasizes advanced oral language skills and specialized vocabulary used in a wide range of Korean business situations. The first part of the course emphasizes basic terminologies and phrases often used in the Korean business setting. The second part of the course focuses on developing situational dialogues based on the previously built vocabulary. The goal of this course is to provide students with both linguistic skills and practical information they need to conduct business with Koreans. They will understand the Korean way of thinking when doing business and develop critical thinking and cultural awareness through different responses in situational dialogues between Westerners and Koreans. Some Hanja (Sino-Korean characters) will also be introduced to fortify studentsâ understanding of the Korean language used in real life situations, as well as professional settings. Also, Korean business etiquette, Korean customs, and culture will be taught. Students will watch videos showing actual business transactions in Korea. Thus, through dialogues related to business situations, and multimedia, students will acquire the practical knowledge of key aspects of Korean business practice. Prerequisite: KREN 212 or the equivalent level of knowledge of Korean.

description: This course emphasizes advanced oral language skills and specialized vocabulary used in a wide range of Korean business situations. title: Readings in Korean: Current Affairs I
description: This is a course designed for students who have a high intermediate level of Korean language proficiency. A wide variety of readings will be introduced to help students read contemporary materials in depth. This course will introduce major events and relevant news of Korea in order to obtain a better understanding of current issues in Korea: Koreaâs trends in education, foreign relations, economics, North Korea, socio-cultural topics. This class will discuss Korea in the 1970s and up to Korea today to appreciate the dynamic change that Korea has been undergoing. Although this course will involve these reading materials, each class will integrate all five skills (listening, speaking, reading, writing, culture) as much as possible. Students will apply increased Korean fluency and vocabulary from the reading to writing of short essays and class discussion with advanced level proficiency. Active participation in class discussion, oral reports, and written assignments are required. This course also aims to equip students with some knowledge of modern Korean history and current topics through multimedia resources. Prerequisite: KREN-???

title: Introduction to Korean Literature Up To 1900
description: This course introduces the classical literature of Korea from the 1500s up to the early 1900s. In this course, students will learn the literature of that period and the social, historical, cultural background of these novels. The mid 1500s was the time of great development on all levels of society under King Sejong the Great who invented the Korean written language Hangul. With his support for the arts and literature, there was an unprecedented boom of literature written in both the traditional method of Chinese characters and the newly introduced Hangul. Before the introduction of Hangul, education was mainly and almost exclusively for men, but after Hangul was introduced women finally had a way to express their ideas or simply write. There was particularly a great increase of women anonymously publishing various works written in Hangul. The goal of this course is to gain a fundamental understanding of Korean language and culture, which is expressed in Korean literature. The students will study selected texts from the Joseon dynasty up to the enlightenment period of the early 1900s. Students will naturally learn the historical background of tradition and modern trends of pragmatism (âShilhakâ) creating social turbulence and confusion in this era and how this is reflected in the literature. In each section, the students will discuss what they have read, and they will write a paper based on the discussion. This course will provide the tools for students to compare the Korean literature with other countriesâ literature including European literature from around the same period. Some examples will include comparing Geum-oh-shin-hwa with Shakespeareâs works and Hong-gil-dong-jeon with Robin Hood. Prerequisite: KREN-331, KREN-332 or equivalent

title: Korean Literature After 1900
description: This course introduces the literature of Korea from the early 1900s to 1945. In this course, students will study the literature of this period and the social, cultural, and historical background of these works. The literature of this period greatly reflects the social and cultural conflicts and struggles of Koreans against the Japanese colonial rule. The experience of living in a dark and tragic period motivated Koreans to produce a wide range of literature that expressed their sorrows and inner struggles. Many of the famous literary works of Korea are from this period, and particularly works that appeal to national identity thrived. Frequent topics of this era include yearning for independence, the tragic life of the Koreans under the harsh Japanese rule, and self reflection. In each section, the students will discuss what they have read, and they will write a paper based on the discussion. This course will provide the tools for students to compare the literature written by Koreans who lived through the colonial experience in China, Japan and Korea. Prerequisite: KREN-331, KREN-332 or equivalent

title: Studies on Korean Thoughts and Religion
description: This course aims to search for the origin and development of Korean Thoughts and religion by first examining the three pillars of Korean Thoughts â????(hongiksasang, ????(gyeongcheonsasang)(âWorship of Heavenâ), and ?(hyo)(âFilial Pietyâ). In order to study????, students will study Dangun(the Founder of Gojoseon), the Oldest Kingdom of Korea(2333B.C.-108B.C.) and the history of Gojoseon and for????which influenced Korean belief, students will examine Korean traditional religion (particularly national religion âminjok-jonggyoâ in ca.1900) and shamanism which continues till today. As for ?, students will study figures who were exemplars of ? and legends and stories that reinforce ? up the Then the course will be expanded to explore how this tradition has developed throughout the centuries by studying Korean history and culture and tracing back to the roots of the origins of Korean tradition to areas such as Mongolia and further north. Prerequisite: ???

title: Studies on North Korea
description: Before the Korean peninsula was divided into South Korea (R.O.K.) and North Korea (D.P.R.K.) in 1948 after the Second World War, the two countries shared the same language, culture, and 5, 000 year history. But since this division, the language, culture, and history of these two countries has diverged greatly. The Democratic Peopleâs Republic of Korea (DPRK) became a country that poses a threat to both regional and international stability. The course will give students a foundation of knowledge about North Korea in general, and, in particular, will enable them to understand the historical background and the present situation of North Korean society, including its language and culture, through the study of original materials. Students will learn about and critically analyze language, social, and cultural issues, including: a. How Korean language in North Korea has become different from South Korea; b. How the understanding of Korean history, which has deep roots in both North and South, has changed in the two countries, and what this means for the analysis of democracy in the South and communism in the North; c. Whether North Korea will ever be able to move beyond its ideology of âJucheâ (translated as âsubjectivity/ownership of identityâ(?) in the context of a discussion about nationalism, communism, and capitalism in Koreaâs recent history. d. What sort of national identity or ideology might offer a social-cultural foundation for the possible unification of Korea; e. While learning North Korean, students will learn vocabulary and expressions used in printed and/or audio-visual resources that reflect cultural and social life in North Korea. Humanitarian aid and development cooperation are among the topics to be offered for discussions. The course materials are mainly based on scholarly works, news reports, and North Korean written documents/propaganda. Readings include historical texts, in recent periodicals and broadcast news. Guest speakers will be invited for some topics. Prerequisite: KREN-310, 331, or equivalent language level.

How the understanding of Korean history, which has deep roots in both North and South, has changed in the two countries, and what this means for the analysis of democracy in the South and communism in the North; c. Whether North Korea will ever be able to move beyond its ideology of âJucheâ (translated as âsubjectivity/ownership of identityâ(?) in the context of a discussion about nationalism, communism, and capitalism in Koreaâs recent history. d. What sort of national identity or ideology might offer a social-cultural foundation for the possible unification of Korea; e. While learning North Korean, students will learn vocabulary and expressions used in printed and/or audio-visual resources that reflect cultural and social life in North Korea. Humanitarian aid and development cooperation are among the topics to be offered for discussions. The course materials are mainly based on scholarly works, news reports, and North Korean written documents/propaganda. Readings include historical texts, in recent periodicals and broadcast news. Guest speakers will be invited for some topics. Prerequisite: KREN-310, 331, or equivalent language level.

title: Korean Diaspora in NE Asia I
description: The goal of this course is to study when, how, and why the Koreans began to emigrate from Korea to Manchuria and Russia. The course will also examine what kinds of immigration policies, particularly in Russia, were developed toward Korean immigrants in this period. The students will learn: â¢The history of the Northeast Asian and Russian regions during this period (China, Korea, Japan, and Russia and Central Asia); â¢The political, social and cultural situations of Korean immigrants in each region; â¢How Koreans attempted to maintain their cultural (national) identity; â¢The present situation of the Korean diaspora and ethnic Korean communities in these regions. Through this course, students will gain a deeper insight into Korean history from the period of 1845-1945 and will gain an understanding of themes including Imperialism and world politics. Students will also be able to expand their own cultural identity through the incorporation of a global perspective. Prerequisite: KREN-310, 331, or equivalent level.

title: Korean Diaspora in NE Asia II
description: The goal of this course is to study when, how, and why the Koreans began to emigrate from Korea to Japan and Sakhalin. The course will also examine what kinds of immigration policies were developed toward Korean immigrants in this period. The students will learn: â¢The history of the Northeast Asian regions during this period (China, Korea, Japan, and Russia and Central Asia); â¢The political, social and cultural situations of Korean immigrants in each region; â¢How Koreans attempted to maintain their cultural (national) identity; â¢The present situation of the Korean diaspora and ethnic Korean communities in these regions. Through this course, students will gain a deeper insight into Korean history from the period of 1845-1945 and will gain an understanding of themes including Imperialism and world politics. Students will also be able to expand their own cultural identity through the incorporation of a global perspective. Prerequisite: KREN-310, 331, or equivalent level.

title: Economic Development: Latin America
description: This course is aimed at students interested in the political economy of Latin America. The class will examine the politics of economic development of a number of Latin American countries. The required texts are also intended to show how politics and economics have always been intertwined when it comes to the making of public policy. At times, economic and methodological questions will be examined in detail. Therefore some knowledge of economic concepts will be helpful, although it is not a requirement to take this class. Topics to be discussed include: the Great Depression, import-substitution industrialization (I.S.I.), the 1980s debt crisis, episodes of hyperinflation and stabilization programs, economic liberalization and recent experiences with state reform. Particular emphasis will be placed on the study of some of the theories in political economy that have inspired the adoption of state reform in the region. This course has benefitted from the Doyle Fellowship initiative, which aims to promote diversity and enhance global awareness in the classroom. Requirements: Students are expected to attend class regularly and complete all assigned reading materials prior to lectures. The final grade will be calculated as follows: 1) Class participation (10%) 2) Midterm (25%) 3) Take-home exam (25%) 4) Final exam (40%) Very Important: I reserve the right to conduct unannounced short quizzes in class if I notice that students are not reading the assigned texts as scheduled. In such case, the studentsâ final grade will also include grades earned in the quizzes. Further information about this requirement will be spelled out in class. title: Sustainable Development in Latin America
description: What is sustainable development, and why is it such a misused word? What development models are more conducive to sustainability? How do factors such as physical geography (climate, land characteristics, topography, location), human geography (relationship between people and their natural environment), institutions, politics and policies influence sustainable development in Latin America? And what explains the divide in Latin American society along so many fault lines? Against the backdrop of historical post-colonial and settlement patterns, we will explore these questions. We will analyze the concepts of carrying capacity and sustainable development in Latin America, and uncover the nexus between poverty, natural disasters and natural resource degradation, combining theory with practice through the analyses of specific case studies, and/or assessments of relevant programs in Latin America and their effectiveness. We will also examine urbanization and migration patterns, natural resource and tourism management conflicts from a holistic and multi-disciplinary point of view, and seek to understand how growth without equity, socio-spatial polarization, and the unequal access of communities to services undermine sustainable development as well. B. Expected Learning Outcomes: Students will become familiar with the geographical and other factors that continue to influence economic, social and human development in Latin America, and the pressing issues related to sustainability. Students will have an overall understanding of socio-spatial inequities in Latin America, key barriers to development, natural resource management conflicts, and the current discourse on measures to address social exclusion and environmental degradation. Students will combine theory with a review of practice, and understand the choices and constraints that Latin American policymakers, stakeholders and communities face to address sustainability and achieve sustained welfare improvements. C. Content and Structure of Seminar: This seminar is structured in three main segments. The first segment or block deals with conceptual and theoretical issues pertaining to sustainability and its various definitions, as well as an overview of Latin Americaâs geographical features and the important variables of socio-economic and environmental change. This leads into the second segment which is the main body of the course, and requires analysis of the sustainability of policies and practices in the past as well as current case studies, drawing on a wide range of examples of natural resource management conflicts. The third segment links issues of population growth, demographic change, migration, urbanization, and social exclusion to the dilemma of unsustainable development, thus integrating the human element into the overall analysis. The course content is underpinned by the structure and highly interactive nature of the seminar, with emphasis placed on individual/group presentations, discussion, critical debate, and studentsâ contribution to the learning of others in the class. Grading will encompass studentsâ attendance and participation, work on weekly written summaries, verbal presentations and responses to challenge questions posed by the instructor, as well as a final individual student research paper and a group project. D. Textbooks and General References Ponting, Clive, A New Green History of the World: The Environment and the Collapse of Great Civilizations, New York, Penguin Group, 2007. (Mandatory) Sachs, Jeffrey D, Economics for a Crowded Planet, New York, The Penguin Press, 2008. (Mandatory) Gallup, John Luke, Gaviria, Alejandro, Lora, Eduardo, Is Geography Destiny? Lessons from Latin America, Palo Alto, Stanford University Press, 2003. (Strongly Recommended) Inter-American Development Bank, Outsiders? The Changing Patterns of Exclusion in Latin America and the Caribbean, Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2007.(Mandatory: can be downloaded free of charge) Mann, Charles, 1491: New Revelations of The Americas before Columbus, New York, The Penguin Press, 2005.(Not Mandatory) Diamond, Jared, Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies, New York: W.W. Norton & Company, 1999. (Not mandatory) Further readings will be assigned on BB according to specific topic and case study of the session.

title: Topics: Illicit Global Economy
description: This course surveys the political economy of illicit economic activities in the Americas. Courses in international relations usually ignore the illicit flows of capital and trade of goods, services, and even people, notwithstanding the substantial impact of this activity on the global economy and on state and citizen security. Students will learn the prevailing theoretical approaches to explaining the illicit global economy, with particular focus on the Americas. The materials will cover topics such as the role of organized crime in political campaign finance and in the exploitation of natural resources, corruption and money laundering. Finally, the course also will reflect on the state of intelligence sharing among international actors and its effectiveness in combating crime in the region.

title: Populism&Democracy in Latin America
description: No term in Latin American studies is politically more controversial and conceptually more ambiguous than populism. âPopulismâ is commonly overused in political debates, because it is frequently invoked to disqualify opponents, as much as in political analysis, because it is convenient to quickly capture its multiple legacies. The downside of this is that we have lost conceptual rigor and historical anchorage, to the extent that we keep discussing what populism is and is not. We also face contemporary challenges: to determine whether and how âpopulismâ contributes or impairs the regionâs ongoing democratization processes. On the basis of a series of relevant cases, this graduate seminar addresses the study of populism; first, from a historical and conceptual perspective, to then move on to examine the so-called current populist experiences in the region. The study of populism is a window used to look deeper into the multiple challenges faced by democracy in Latin America.

title: Ethnography of the Colombian Conflict
description: Course Goals & Description How is the Colombian conflict lived day-by-day by its protagonistsâhuman rights activists, guerrillas, paramilitary, members of the military, poor urban dwellers, peasants? What can the ethnographic study of the everyday realities of this conflict tell us, that we cannot learn from more macro-level sociological and political analysis? Anthropologists, filmmakers, sociologists, and oral historians have endeavored over the past fifty years to document the impact of conflict on Colombian society. This course will explore their work. Reading knowledge of Spanish is required. title: Indigenous Social Movements of Latin America
description: This course provides a perspective on the uses of ethnic identity as a resource for grassroots social and political movements, economic development programs, and the broader processes of nation-building in Latin America. The course will expose students to theories, themes, issues and empirical studies related to the utilization of indigenous cultural resources in social mobilization, citizen political participation, constitutional reform and forging alternative models for socioeconomic development.

title: Inter-American System: OAS and the Inter-American Democratic Regime
description: This course touches on several inter-connected and crucial issues related to the promotion of democracy from a multilateral institution such as the Organization of American States (OAS). It briefly reviews the evolution of inter-American relations, including disagreements and agreements on principles and institutions of hemispheric cooperation for security, development and democracy. It looks at US interventions and Latin American attempts to contain it with the establishment of principles such as non-intervention and the equality of states. It examines the development of a hemispheric consensus on collective security and democracy and a commitment to promote and defend it. It views democracy as system of government based on political culture and introduces a theory of promoting it from an inter-governmental organization. It identifies the instruments, âpowers,â complexities and limitations this new OAS role has, including the tension between the mandate to promote democracy and the principle of non- intervention. More specifically, the course examines the origins, principles, purposes, structure and functions of the OAS, concentrating on its democracy promotion role. Special emphasis is placed on the development and application of legal and diplomatic instruments such as the Inter American Democratic Charter, the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, and on the activities/programs the organization carries out in fulfilling that role, such as electoral observations and technical assistance for institutional strengthening and promoting democratic values and practices. It points out OAS achievements and shortcomings, and brings to fore the criticism it faces, the existing misconceptions about its nature, and proposes ways to reform and strengthen it. At the end of course, students will be expected to demonstrate considerable knowledge and understanding the OAS role in the promotion and defense of democracy, and will be able to evaluate its performance. They will also be able to identify the Inter-American instruments and activities the OASâ member states have developed for that role, and critically analyze and assess their application and impact in particular cases or circumstances.

title: Inter-American System: OAS and the Inter-American Democratic Regime
description: The objective of this course is to provide a better understanding of the inter-American system and the Organization of American Statesâ (OAS) role of promoting democracy and developing the inter-american democratic regime. To that effect, the course will examine the inter-american system and OAS origin, principles, purposes, structure and functions, its legal and diplomatic instruments and the activities/programs the organization carries out in fulfilling its role. It will present different approaches to promoting and defending democracy from an international organizationâs perspective. It will identify the instruments generated at the âhigh politicsâ diplomatic level to protect and restore democracy when it is interrupted, and their applications in Haiti 1991, Peru 1992, Guatemala 1993, and Honduras 2009. It will analyze other instruments available to the organization for âlow politicsâ efforts, such as electoral observation missions, political observation/mediation missions, as well as technical assistance programs to strengthen institutions and promote democratic values and practices. It will also discuss instruments and actions to promote the observance of human rights. The course will look also into the limitations and tensions (i.e., promoting democracy vs the principle of non-intervention) facing the OAS efforts, and will examine what critics have to say about this new OAS role. At the end of the course, students will be expected to demonstrate considerable knowledge and understanding of the OAS' role in promoting and protecting democracy in the Western Hemisphere, and will be able to evaluate its performance in that regard. More specifically, students will be able to identify the Inter-American legal/diplomatic tools and activities the OASâ member states have developed to perform that function, and critically analyze and assess their application and impact in particular cases or circumstances.

title: Poverty, Well-Being and Social Exclusion in Latin America
description: Most countries in Latin America have realized very important progress in terms of economic growth and development in recent years. At the same time the region is confronted with the persistent challenges of poverty, inequality and social exclusion. In particular specific groups, including indigenous peoples, single-parent households or marginalized youth continue to be among the poorest and most excluded throughout the region. This course analyzes the political economy of persistent poverty, inequality and social exclusion in Latin America as key impediments for human development. A special emphasis will be placed on discussing different approaches to international development and human development, including Amartya Senâs Capability Approach, Human Well-being, the Livelihoods Approach and the Rights-Based Approach. The course aims to provide students with the necessary theoretical and practical background to better understand the underlying causes of persistent poverty and social exclusion throughout Latin America. It will enable the students to study different approaches and concrete cases studies on how to improve the well-being and human development of marginalized groups in an environment of rapid economic and social change within the region. The course is divided into three parts: Part I: Theories of poverty, human development, well-being and social exclusion Part II: Key Issues of marginalized groups with an emphasis on indigenous peoples development, and III: Practical case studies of international development.

title: LATIN AMER STUD PRO-SEMINAR
description: This course is for undergraduate students writing the Latin American Thesis. All students enrolled in this course meet during their senior spring semester to discuss the research they are conducting, to learn about the projects that other students are carrying out, and to share ideas and research techniques. Students work on set assignments that lead to the completion of their thesis (proposal, annotated bibliography, drafts, oral presentations etc.). Each stage of the thesis is to be prepared in consultation with and approval of the thesis advisor, presented in class, and submitted to the advisor and the professor of the pro-seminar.

title: Origins and Transformations
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the study of the History in the Latin American Studies Masterâs Program. It is designed to be interdisciplinary and provide students a good overview of recent production on historical topics on Latin America. The hope is that some Masterâs students will as a result choose History as their concentration. The theme of the course is on the making of the nation-states in Latin America. This topic is especially important not just because this is the theme that has warranted a great deal of attention over the past decade or so. It also incorporates new perspectives such as Gramscian and postmodern analysis, resistance studies, and the old modernization paradigm. On might argue that this topic remains on the cutting edge of the field and is the subject of many of the newest and most innovative works in Latin American History broadly defined. Since it is impossible to cover Latin American History as a whole, students will receive an intensive preparation on a topic that should be of interest to those who will work in other disciplines as well. The basic formula is discussion of one book a week, building from one to another. I will provide some historical background before each country is discussed. It is important that each book be read beforehand in its entirety and that it is read critically. It is expected that students bring their own questions to class, though the professor will also contribute his own questions. Since I expect everyone to have done the reading, I will maintain the right to ask questions of anybody in the class and especially of those who have been silent. So â it is vital to stay on top of the reading and to take sufficient time to think about it in analytical terms. Class participation is critical for a good grade in this course. Three analytical essays will be required at regular intervals. They will focus on the central issues raised in that section of the course and must include discussion of each assigned text from that section. Each essay should reflect independent thinking and move towards an understanding of larger patterns implicit in the works rather than just a discussion/summary of each book. I will look for original interpretations; these will receive the highest grades.

title: Origins and Transformations
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the study of the History in the Latin American Studies Masterâs Program. It is designed to be interdisciplinary and provide students a good overview of recent production on historical topics on Latin America. The hope is that some Masterâs students will as a result choose History as their concentration. The theme of the course is on the making of the nation-states in Latin America. This topic is especially important not just because this is the theme that has warranted a great deal of attention over the past decade or so. It also incorporates new perspectives such as Gramscian and postmodern analysis, resistance studies, and the old modernization paradigm. On might argue that this topic remains on the cutting edge of the field and is the subject of many of the newest and most innovative works in Latin American History broadly defined. Since it is impossible to cover Latin American History as a whole, students will receive an intensive preparation on a topic that should be of interest to those who will work in other disciplines as well. The basic formula is discussion of one book a week, building from one to another. I will provide some historical background before each country is discussed. It is important that each book be read beforehand in its entirety and that it is read critically. It is expected that students bring their own questions to class, though the professor will also contribute his own questions. title: Culture and Power
description: LASP 502:01, Culture and Power in Latin America Spring 2014 Professor Bryan McCann ICC 610a, 7-3552, bm85@georgetown.edu Office Hours: Mon 3:30-5 and by appt. The anxieties and aspirations of any society can be read in its cultural works. Canonical works have no monopoly on these expressionsâfleeting ephemera, pop kitsch and marginalia often speak as eloquently about deep cultural preoccupations as towering works of genius. But the densest and most ambitious cultural works often synthesize the most intricate and heartfelt concerns. And because they do so in ways that are open to multiple, evolving interpretations, they repay attentive and repeated investigation. When these works are accepted, celebrated and continually invoked as touchstones of a societyâs characteristics and intellectual life, they become particularly important for the scholar. We cannot understand modern Latin America if we have scant knowledge of its canonical literature, film, music and visual art. This course will concentrate on reading great novels and viewing great films of 20th century Latin America. We will not understand these works as vulgar reflections of particular moments, but as vital claims, manifestations and projects for particular approaches to modernity. We will analyze their internal workings, their context and their subsequent importance in that light. The primary goal is to achieve a deeper understanding of modern Latin American cultural history through sustained inquiry into complex, ambitious works. Additional goals include the establishment of a frame of reference for the historical development of Latin American literary and cinematic development and the honing of skills in research, analysis, rhetoric and prose.

title: States and Societies
description: This course will analyze the major political trends and issues that have shaped Latin American governments and politics throughout the course of the twentieth century as well as the key issues confronting the region as it begins the 21st century. The study of Latin America has been a rich source of theory for political scientists, contributing to a general understanding of development issues, regime change, military rule and democratic transition, among other themes. As such, given the developments of the last decades (the return of military leaders to their barracks which ended in some cases decades of authoritarian rule, the great difficulties that practically all nations in the region have had in consolidating democratic governance, the rise of new social movements, the rise of a new left, exacerbated social and security tensions caused by drug trafficking and organized crime, and finally the persistence in some countries of old forms of armed violence) the theoretical models drawn from Latin America have also been applied to other nations in other regions experiencing comparable changes, in Eastern Europe and in parts of Asia and Africa. This course will examine several of these broad trends of politics in the region, focusing on the actors and processes which have shaped institutions, policies, parties, movements and political events in contemporary Latin America, The second half of the course will examine recent changes in politics, principally those relating to new social movements, identity, gender and ethnicity, drug-trafficking, political parties, and newer post-cold war conflicts. We will draw on some of the best social science literature, together with videos and films that elucidate contemporary political themes. The aim of this course is to promote a more comprehensive understanding and familiarity with the social science literature and approaches that have been used to study a key and vital region in international politics and the emerging global economy and polity.

title: States and Societies
description: The course examines the development of states and societies in Latin America and the Caribbean from a political science perspective. Theories of economic and political development, regime change, military rule and democratic transition, and globalization will be employed to understand the regionâs political history. In addition, particular attention will be given to the contemporary challenges and issues facing Latin American countries today, including the consolidation of democratic governance, the rise of new social movements, security tensions caused by drug trafficking and organized crime, and the persistence of economic inequality within and between nations.

title: Political Economy of Latin America
description: This course will focus on the political economy of Latin American development. The course will study the evolution of thinking on policy approaches to economic challenges through the study of theory, history, and data. While the emphasis will be on current problems, thinking, and policy, we will also examine historical cases and the development and evolution of ideas. The course requirements include participating in class discussion, several short papers, one take-home midterm, and one cumulative final. Grades will be based on the papers (10% each for two, 20% for reforms paper), midterm (20%), cumulative final (30%), and class participation (10%). Learning objectives for each topic will be distributed with review questions so that you can understand our goals for the different sections.

title: Economics for Latin American Studies
description: Prof. SÃ¶ren Gigler, b.gigler@lse.ac.uk Office: ICC 484, 687-5948 Office hrs: R 4-6, F 10-12 & by appt This course will attempt to fill a gap that exists for regional studies students who canât get credit for intro econ classes that are taught at an undergrad level, yet need the background acquired in micro, macro, and international economics courses in order to excel in Political Economy, Economics, or Government concentrations in a Latin American Studies masters. It is assumed that some students will have had some exposure to these topics long ago, while others will have avoided them like the plague, yet now, belatedly, realize that they really should understand concepts such as opportunity cost, elasticity of demand, and the like. In addition, they need to be able to look at graphs and tables without fainting in order to better understand the policy dilemmas captured in them. This course will address such issues. As a graduate level course, it will move briskly through a wide range of topics generally covered in a more leisurely albeit complete manner in the freshmen courses of micro and macro, focusing especially on the issues of special interest to Latin American economic policy debates, emphasizing international topics as well as how to find, interpret, and analyze basic economic data. The class will be a lecture class. Students will be expected to complete worksheets/problem sets for almost every module to help them develop, master, and apply key concepts. Unfortunately, I am forced to evaluate students and give grades at the end of the semester. In order to do this, there will be one take-home midterm, two in-class midterms, and a final exam. Grades will be determined by the two exams, one group presentation and problem sets/class participation/improvement ( 30, 30, 30, and 10% respectively â i.e., 30% for the midterm, 30% for the final, and 10% other). TOPICS: 1. Introduction: What is economics? Why do economists disagree? Thinking about markets and exchange on both domestic and international levels. Does the government matter? 2. Useful concepts and tools â opportunity cost, comparative advantage, ceteris paribus, consumer choice, prices as signals, incentives, marginal analysis, elasticity, economic rent, market power, market failure, schedules and curves 3. Interpreting data, presenting data, and economic evidence 4. Supply and demand 5. Trade â why do we trade? Similarities and differences between domestic and international exchange 6. Factor marketsâ inputs and costs, outputs and prices 7. Product markets â pure competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, monopoly 8. Public sector economics: externalities, public goods 9. GNP and its components 10. Keynes and Keynesian economics, government spending and taxes, budget deficits (fiscal policy) 11. Inflation and money (monetary policy) 12. International trade and intl finance: exchange rates, international debt READINGS We will be covering an extremely wide range of topics, and your grades (as explained above) will be based on your ability to demonstrate your mastery of them. I have assembled a group of readings that I think will help you achieve this understanding. You should draw on them as needed. You are all graduate students, so I assume that you are motivated to learn and will read. Specific objectives for the different topics are listed in the accompanying âObjectivesâ handout, and serve as a self-assessment tool throughout the semester. 1. Texts: Microeconomics in Context (available in bookstore) and Macroeconomics in Context (on Blackboard). Series written by Neva Goodwin, Julie A. Nelson, Frank Ackerman, and Thomas Weisskopf. The macro textbook draft (like the Micro text) is put out by the Global Development and Environment Institute of Tufts University. These books attempt to highlight the social and environmental issues in economics, and the relevant chapters for the macro text are available on blackboard under Course Documents. 2. Chapters from various other texts will be placed on Blackboard. These are noted below. 3. Every student should read at least one of the following books which explain economics in plain and direct English, written in general by economic journalists. They explain basic concepts in an applied way that I think is easier to grasp than most economic textbooks. I would most highly recommend the first book as it is the best written and most entertaining of the bunch (and also the most recent), but they are all quite good. (a) Tim Harfordâs The Undercover Economist, (b) Charles Wheelanâs Naked Economics: Undressing the Dismal Science, or (c) Frederick S. Weaverâs Economic Literacy: Basic Economics with an Attitude. I will place all three of these on reserve, although they are also readily available in bookstores and on-line. Unfortunately Harfordâs book, as it is so new, is only available in hardcover. You can find rough concordances between chapters from each book and class coverage at the end of the syllabus. 4. Additional readings will be assigned from time to time throughout the semester, I will use email to send messages letting you know, and will also likely bring short articles to class. 5. Additional Reference texts: any standard economics textbook would be a good reference, the library is full of them (most under call number HB 171.5). Examples are Mankiw, Baumol and Blinder, Samuelson, Taylor, etc. I will be drawing on Boyes and Melvinâs Fundamentals of Economics from time to time. There are also several basic texts available on line. The library has Tom Gormanâs The Complete Idiotâs Guide to Economics available electronically. Another one that I will use at times is Roger McCainâs Essential Principles of Economics: A Hypermedia Text (available at âwilliam-king.www.drexel.edu/top/prin/txt/EcoToc.htmlâ ). A comprehensive listing of on-line textbooks is available at www.oswego.edu/~economic/newbooks.htm should you wish to find others. HONOR CODE: While Georgetown undergrads have a formal honor code, for graduate students it is less formal. However, our commitment to academic integrity should not be lower. I fully expect you all to acquaint yourselves with the Honor Code and its commitments, and to live up to them. I encourage you to work in groups as you work through the exercises and grapple with the concepts, some of them difficult, of this class. However, exams (even take home versions) should be completed individually, without consulting other students. And, of course, I expect individual efforts on in class exams.

title: Current Topics in LA Pol Econ
description: LASP 513 Current Topics in Latin American Political Economy Seminar: the Rise and Frustrations of the Latin American Middle Class This class is designed to allow PE concentrators a more in-depth examination of political economy concepts as applied to Latin America. We will examine the factors affecting policymakersâ decisions and the economic and social consequences of policies. The theme for this seminar is: the Rise and Frustrations of the Latin American Middle Class. Over the past decade more than 73 million Latin Americans were lifted out of poverty. During this time, the middle class grew 50% and now represents 30% of the population, according to World Bank statistics. We will examine the policies and conditions that gave rise to these improvements. We will also examine whether Latin American countries are stuck in a middle income trap â where countries grow rapidly but then stagnate at middle-income levels and fail to graduate into the ranks of high-income countries - and analyze the rising pressures from a recently empowered middle class, illustrated by this summerâs large-scale protests in Brazil, ongoing protests regarding education in Chile, and Mexicansâ new use of social media to shame poorly behaving elites. The main purpose of the class is to provide an intellectually stimulating environment within which the student can delve into more depth in a number of economic concepts and to stimulate the studentsâ individual research. The focus will be on guiding the students in the preparation of a research paper, with guidance from the professor and feedback from colleagues. The class will combine lectures, class discussions and individual research to examine the macroeconomic, microeconomic and social policies implemented over the past years and their results as well as the role of the shifting external environment. Students will spend the semester researching a policy topic of their choice, which they will present to the group. Students will also be responsible for providing constructive peer review to two colleagues, with the objective of producing publishable work.

title: Current Topics in LA Pol Econ
description: The purpose of this research seminar is to provide an opportunity for CLAS students interested in Political Economy to gain a more in-depth understanding of selected topics covered in the introductory PE course, LASP 504 and to build and practice valuable research skills. The course will be divided into three sessions of four weeks each, during which we will cover a broad topic in depth. We will meet for two lecture sessions to cover the substance of the issue and review the relevant contemporary issues, then students will prepare a research paper to be presented in class on the fifth class. Intermediate classes will be used for bilateral consultations with the professor. Students will serve as peer reviewers for each other.

title: Trade in the Americas: Econ, Politics, Policy
description: This course provides an introduction to basic economic, legal and political concepts related to trade policy. We will examine contemporary issues related to and affecting Latin Americaâs trade policy such as the rise of China and East Asia as trading partners; the natural resource curse; energy market dynamics; the environmental implications of trade and underlying microeconomic and logistical issues that affect countriesâ competitiveness. We will also examine the bifurcated (or trifurcated) trade policy approaches in the region with a view to understanding the bases thereof and assessing future prospects.

title: Trade in the Americas: Econ, Politics, Policy
description: This course provides an introduction to basic economic, legal and political concepts related to trade policy. We will examine contemporary issues related to and affecting Latin Americaâs trade policy such as the rise of China and East Asia as trading partners; the natural resource curse; energy market dynamics; the environmental implications of trade and underlying microeconomic and logistical issues that affect countriesâ competitiveness. We will also examine the bifurcated (or trifurcated) trade policy approaches in the region with a view to understanding the bases thereof and assessing future prospects.

title: Energy Security: Western Hemisphere
description: The seminar is divided into four interrelated building blocks: (i) World Energy Overview, comparing the Energy Balances of United States, China, Europe, Mexico and Brazil; (ii) the dynamics of the world petroleum market, world regional petroleum balances with Scenarios to 2030; (iii) petroleum balances of the western hemisphere with country and trade blocs analysis with Scenarios to 2030 and (iv) the potential for Hemispheric Energy Cooperation as a means of fostering regional energy security.

title: Energy Security: Western Hemisphere
description: The seminar is divided into four interrelated building blocks: (i) World Energy Overview, comparing the Energy Balances of United States, China, Europe, Mexico and Brazil; (ii) the dynamics of the world petroleum market, world regional petroleum balances with Scenarios to 2030; (iii) petroleum balances of the western hemisphere with country and trade blocs analysis with Scenarios to 2030 and (iv) the potential for Hemispheric Energy Cooperation as a means of fostering regional energy security.

title: Gender and Sustainability
description: This course will focus on the gendered perspectives of environmental sustainability and eco-efficient agriculture in order to reach food security and equitable access to resources. The course will present an overview of gender analysis to understand patterns of land use and conservation strategies, both individually and collectively and how male and female farmers contribute differently to conservation and agricultural production efforts. Students will be presented with real case studies from Africa, Asia and Latin America, documenting gender gaps in the access to a large range of agricultural resources, including land and farm labour, forest, livestock, extension services, and other assets. Current international trends and research streams on gender and agriculture will be discussed and analyzed with the aim to influence policy to reach lasting change.

title: Political Economy of Cities: Latin America & Asia
description: This course will address the political economy of cities with a focus on Latin American and Asian cities. It will begin with general background, move onto issues and sectors, and then address city cases (approximately one-third each). The general background will address history, analytical tools, and cities as actors in the international system; and sectors/issues will address transport, land use, energy, housing, water, and water sectors coping with the challenges of inequality, climate change, and rapid urbanization. The proposed cities for class discussion include Curitiba, Mexico City, Bogota, Santiago, Bangalore, Hong Kong, Seoul, and Singapore. Students will be expected to take one of these cities and select one other city and follow them through the semester, lead the class discussion on âtheirâ city, and produce a series of short papers leading to a final paper comparing the two cities, their challenges, the policy responses, and results.

title: Peace Processes / Post-Conflict Reconstruction in LA
description: This seminar will examine internal armed conflicts, successful and unsuccessful peace processes, and experiences of post-conflict peacebuilding and reconstruction in Latin America, primarily in Central America and the Andean region during the past three decades. It will approach these issues from three related perspectives: 1) theoretically, using some of the best recent literature derived from the growing experience of international involvement in internal conflicts across the globe; 2) through an in-depth examination of specific Latin American cases -- principally Colombia, El Salvador, Guatemala, Nicaragua and Peru; and 3) through an analysis of specific issues incorporating experiences from Latin America and other international cases, such as: applying justice in conflict and post-conflict settings; the involvement of outside actors such as the United Nations and World Bank; comparative experiences of DDR (Disarmament, Demobilization and Reintegration); experiences in local level peacemaking; and gender approaches to DDR and peacebuilding. During the final part of the semester, students will simulate actual negotiations in one of the countries, playing the role of a government representative, guerrilla leader, international mediator, civil society leader or other actor in one of the peace processes being studied.

title: Citizen Security & Democracy in Latin America
description: Citizen security, taken to mean the protection of individual persons, property, and democratic institutions, from threats posed by crime, corruption, and natural disaster is among the most pressing concerns throughout most of Latin America and the Caribbean. It is a leading obstacle to high-quality democracy in the region. Following overview sessions about theory and data, the course focuses mainly on threats posed by various types of crime, common and organized, violent and nonviolent. Our orienting questions are: What explanations are given to account for trends in different types of crime in different national and local settings? What are effects of crime on democratic governance? What types of government and civil society responses appear effective in controlling crime? What responses worsen crime?

title: Regional Studies Research Methods
description: This practical, skills-based course aims to give students a solid foundation in the acquisition, analysis, interpretation and presentation of quantitative data. The course begins by taking a step back from data to discuss the nature of the social scientific enterprise, with a special focus on causal inference. After building a solid foundation in descriptive statistics, weâll discuss the challenges of research with observational data. Weâll spend a lot of time discussing and thinking about the central problem of endogeneity, and after diving into probability theory, weâll begin to develop the tools to develop and test hypotheses. While weâll be engaging with some of the most fundamentally bedeviling questions of social science, the focus throughout the course will be on conceptual understanding and applied, practical skills.

title: Methods for Qualitative & Participatory Action Research
description: The objective of the course is to provide students with an introduction to Qualitative and Participatory data collection and analysis to be able to understand main streams of thought in the qualitative evaluation field. The course will be divided in two parts; first, we will work on case studies, literature reviews, focus groups and interviewing skills to collect meaningful qualitative data and to triangulate the findings in order to determine specific categories or results. The second part of the course will include a module on Participatory Action Research (PAR), in particular Participation in Human Inquiry theory and tools for data collection. Students will be involved in a practical exercise and will be tasked with conducting a qualitative evaluation through interviews, mapping, photography or the interpretation of symbols conducting a real life project of their choice as part of the class work. Course Goals and Expected Outcomes for students: - To understand and master qualitative and participatory techniques to be used in research projects - To foresee possible pitfalls to be addressed during qualitative data collection. - To be able to establish research related priorities - To better manage their time while focusing on âthickâ and ârichâ meaningful data.

title: Politics of Human Rights: LA in Comparative Perspective
description: This advanced seminar will focus on the politics of human rights and the challenges of applying international human rights and humanitarian law in countries confronting major violations of both. The primary regional focus of the course will be on Latin America, however the seminar will encourage cross-regional and comparative analysis with key cases in Africa, Asia, Middle East and Europe. It will examine such issues as the jurisprudence of the Inter-American Court of Human Rights and the International Criminal Court; the evolution of U.S. human rights policies; changing approaches to transitional justice in Guatemala, El Salvador, Uruguay and Argentina; feminicide in Mexico and Guatemala and This advanced seminar will focus on the politics of human rights and the challenges of applying international human rights and humanitarian law in countries confronting major violations of both. The primary regional focus of the course will be on Latin America, however the seminar will encourage cross-regional and comparative analysis with key cases in Africa, Asia, Middle East and Europe. It will examine such issues as the jurisprudence of the Inter-American Court of Human Rights and the International Criminal Court; the evolution of U.S. human rights policies; changing approaches to transitional justice in Guatemala, El Salvador, Uruguay and Argentina; feminicide in Mexico and Guatemala and sexual violence as an act of war in the Congo and in Colombia; the rights of internally displaced persons, in Colombia and the Sudan; historical memory , reparations and justice in Colombia, Peru and South Africa; human rights and the war on terrorism; human rights and the consolidation of democracy. The seminar will provide an opportunity to undertake original research, as well as to discuss, debate, and refine that research through constant dialogue and debate with other seminar members over the course of the semester.

title: Beginning swimming
description: This course is designed for the student who wishes to overcome their fear of the water and learn basic swim skills. Students will learn and practice key elements of the crawlstroke and back float and stroke. Student should have their own swim goggles.

title: Tennis I
description: This class is for students who are new to tennis or have taken some lessons. Each class gives instruction focusing on developing excellent technique. There are a lot of drills performed to help develop good form.

title: Tennis II
description: This class is for students with basic skills or who have taken lessons. The class reinforces the proper techniques for ground strokes, serve and volley. This class performs many drills to work on including shot making and court positioning. It also addresses both singles and doubles strategy.

title: Introduction to Racquetball
description: Learn techniques, practice drills, and approaches to the game of racquetball during these lessons. Class emphasizes technique and basic racquetball skills. No prior experience necessary. Student can borrow a racquet for class use.

title: Squash
description: Instruction from basic skill levels through intermediate. Students will be introduced to the rules of the game and challenge you to improve your squash and fitness level. Racquets are provided for those who do not own one.

title: Ballet I
description: This course has been created as an introduction to the basic principles of ballet. Concentration is on learning basic barre and center work. Students participate in ballet technique classes twice a week. No background in dance is required.

title: Jazz Dance
description: This course has been created as an introduction to the basic principles and history of jazz dance. Students participate in dance technique classes twice a week. Every class includes a warm-up period as well as short routines. No background in dance is required.

title: Intermediate Jazz Dance
description: This course builds on Jazz I technique. Students participate in dance technique classes twice a week. Every class includes a warm-up period as well as working on intermediate vocabulary used in full length dances.

title: Modern Dance
description: This course has been created as an introduction to the basic principles and history of modern dance. Students participate in dance technique classes twice a week. Every class includes a warm-up period and short routines. No background in dance is required.

title: Yoga
description: In this 12 week (24 sessions) class you will learn and master a basic Hatha Yoga routine as well as the five principles of Yoga for physical, mental, and emotional well-being.

title: Introduction to Ballroom Dance
description: This course has been created as an introduction to the basic principles and history of American Ballroom Dance. Students will learn Swing, Foxtrot, Waltz and Cha Cha. Students will learn several variations for each dance as well as lead/follow technique. No background in dance is required.

title: Introduction to Golf
description: Get started on your way to a lifetime of enjoyment with this introduction to the game of golf. The course teaches the basics of golf, golf etiquette and rules of the game. Students will have the opportunity to work on their golf swing and putting in the Yates indoor driving range and leave the class with confidence to take it out to the course.

title: The Persuit of Peace
description: This course, the counterpart to âThe Problem of War,â will approach the subject of peace and related contemporary issues from a variety of perspectives â historical, analytical, comparative-empirical, humanistic, ethical, interdisciplinary, and policy. Consistent with the foregoing LSP goals, the required reading assignments, class discussions, and major research paper(s) seek to foster analytical thinking, comparative study, interdisciplinarity, and humanistic values-based assessments of the practical challenges of managing conflict and making peace in the contemporary world in a variety of contexts â global, international, national, and local. Policy options and choices will be evaluated according to their expected and actual costs, benefits, and moral implications.

title: The Persuit of Peace
description: This course, the counterpart to âThe Problem of War,â will approach the subject of peace and related contemporary issues from a variety of perspectives â historical, analytical, comparative-empirical, humanistic, ethical, interdisciplinary, and policy. Consistent with the foregoing LSP goals, the required reading assignments, class discussions, and major research paper(s) seek to foster analytical thinking, comparative study, interdisciplinarity, and humanistic values-based assessments of the practical challenges of managing conflict and making peace in the contemporary world in a variety of contexts â global, international, national, and local. Policy options and choices will be evaluated according to their expected and actual costs, benefits, and moral implications.

title: The Federalist Papers: Creating a New Nation
description: Thomas Jefferson said, âA morsel of genuine history is a thing so rare as to be always valuable.â (September 8, 1817 to John Adams). This seminar focuses such morsels of genuine history, namely, on the drafting of the Constitution, the Ratification Debate of 1787 to 1788, and on The Federalist Papers. The personalities and actions of Washington, Madison, Hamilton, Jay, and Franklin are central to the narrative story, but the course gives special prominence to the Anti-Federalist opposition to the Constitution (such as Patrick Henry) and to the adoption of the Bill of Rights. In the end, we confront the question: how well has the institutional framework and political philosophy argued for by The Federalist survived in an age of terrorism, economic meltdown, and increasingly strident partisanship?

title: Why Friendship and Beauty Matter
description: Why Friendship Matters Aristotleâs argument for the meaning and possibility of happiness leads to the conclusion that âthe activity of reason with its proper virtue will be perfect happiness.â The opening paragraph of his discussion of friendship, however, seems to clearly contradict this conclusion. In Book VIII of his Nicomachaen Ethics he explains why the need for a certain kind of relationship overrides goodness as a necessary condition for happiness. In this explanation, he shows how the mindâs relation to itself requires a particular kind of relation to another person. Why Beauty Matters There is a mystery about beauty. The evidence seems to indicate that in all stages of our history as human beings and wherever we went, we have had the talent and the drive to create beautiful things and to surround ourselves with beautiful settings. What is not clear is why. In his Critique of Aesthetic Judgment Kant examines our liking for beauty, the creation of fine art, and the role of beauty in culture. And along the way, he provides an answer to this question.

title: Alienation and Self-Identity
description: One of the most perplexing and pervasive problems of our time, the alienation or estrangement of humans from various aspects of their âgivenâ reality, will be examined with attention given to these personal areas: the sense of the hostility of nature, guilt in relation to others, despair over oneself, and the sense of estrangement from some totality or ultimacy of being. The discussion will be pointed toward ways individuals must learn to cope with the various determinants of existence and come to an understanding of who they are. This interaction of "alienation" and "self-identity" will be dealt with in terms of personal, social, and transcendental dimensions.

title: Path to the Future: The US in the 21st Century World
description: In 1941, the publisher Henry Luce predicted the coming of what he called the âAmerican Century.â According to Luce, the time had arrived for Americans âto accept wholeheartedly our duty and our opportunity as the most powerful and vital nation of the world and in consequence to assert upon the world the full impact of our influence, for such means as we see fit.â In the decades that followed, many Americans enthusiastically accepted this challenge and the remaining decades of the 20th century bore witness to their efforts. But what have been the consequences for the United States, and the world, in the âpresentâ? Although much of the attention directed toward US foreign policy in the last several years has been focused the âWar on Terrorism,â a vast number of other significant issues also present the United States with challenges and opportunities in the 21st century. While not ignoring the âWar on Terrorism,â this course also will examine some of the other areas of policy as a means of assessing the current global status of the United States and providing insight regarding its âpath to the future.â

title: Values Issues in International Affairs
description: Is the use of force justified, or even obligated, in world politics? Are the rules for moral behavior of nations different from those for individuals? Can there be just wars, either in classic times or in the nuclear age? When the imperatives of non-intervention and national security conflict, which should take priority, and who assigns such priorities? Is the nation-state still the appropriate unit for world politics, and if not, what options might we have, such as regionalism, divided sovereignty, or world government? These and other values issues are analyzed in this course that provides a broad survey of moral issues in foreign policy.

title: The Court, The Constitution , and the Shaping of the American Nation
description: Tracing the development of the United States through the key Supreme Court decisions from Marbury v. Madison to Bush v. Gore , this course focuses on the power center at the other end of the Avenue instead of the White House. John Marshall, not Jefferson, laid the groundwork for the modern American nation by articulating decidedly non-Jeffersonian concepts in Marbury and McCulloch . Earl Warren, not Congress or the President, implemented the Second Reconstruction through Brown v. Board of Education . Warren Burger toppled a vast criminal enterprise operated out of the Oval Office by saying, in essence, the emperor has no executive privilege clothes ( United States v. Nixon ). Other issues include the Commerce Power; the constitutional twilight zone (Presidential warmaking short of war); the Court's foray into intra-branch warfare (which led to FDR's Court-Packing Plan); the Court's employment of the "equal protection" clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to cut the Gordian knot of racial apartheid, and also to create "penumbral" constitutional rights found nowhere in the text of the document.

title: The Medieval Synthesis: Art and Religion in the Middle Ages
description: The artistic achievements of the Middle Ages (for this course, the ninth to fourteenth centuries) vividly express the religious beliefs and aspirations of the period. In the context of religion and art as a universal cultural phenomenon of "Medievalism," this course will address a series of trans-cultural questions about the development of human values. Slide-illustrated lectures, class discussions, and student presentations will be used to explore the "medieval synthesis" both in terms of particular cultures (German, French, Italian, British) as well as in an integrated sense. The relationship between Christianity and Judaism and Christianity and Islam will also be discussed.

title: Ethical Problems in Contemporary Society
description: Using issues about the relation of religious beliefs and moral convictions, this course will provide an introduction to basic moral concepts. Students will examine the relation of moral teachings to the broader theological or philosophical framework in which they occur, analyze positions taken by religious moralists on particular issues, and compare those positions with those of secular thinkers. The course will not develop any particular theological position in the examination of selected issues; rather it will consider the worldview and position of representative religious and secular thinkers on these issues. Additionally, the students will consider arguments for and against moral absolutes, the so-called "situation ethics" debate. With this foundation, this course will apply these principles to specific moral issues such as war, sexuality, abortion, and euthanasia.

title: The Triumph of Democracy?
description: Students will have an opportunity to take up directly the question of democracyâs future by a selective examination of its evolution in the Modern period. The theory and practice of democracy have established a dialectic between themselves over time: the expectations of theory become subject to amendment as experience with democracy accumulates. Classical theorists of modern democracy, such as J.S. Mill, would neither recognize nor approve of what democracy has become. Contemporary theorists are divided over justifying existing democratic practices and the contrary effort of establishing nobler standards by which to measure democratic life. This reaffirmation of democratic idealism seeks to extend democracy beyond the political sphere into the workplace and within the family.

title: Contemporary Science and Society
description: Science and technology address perdurable philosophical questions of human societies and culture, and reciprocally influence, shape â and are influenced and shaped by â social, economic and geo-political agendas and directions. In short, science does not occur in a social vacuum, and societal trends are affected by, and influence the scope, trajectories and valence of scientific developments and their use or misuse. The 20th Century has been referred to as âthe Age of Technologyâ, and rightly so, as perhaps no other era has borne witness and impact to the rapid and broad rise of science and technology as a social force. But, this reality prompts questions of what are the current and future effects of what philosopher Hans Lenk has called the âTechnological Imperativeâ, how will societies assert meaning, value, and utility of extant and new scientific discoveries and technological capabilities, and how might we â as organizations, communities, and perhaps populations at large - negotiate the ways that science and technology should and should not be employed in pursuits of human flourishing, and socio-cultural agendas.

title: The Foundations of Modernity
description: Contemporary political discourse very frequently makes use of such terms as Conservatism, Liberalism, Socialism, Republicanism, Federalism, and Postmodernism, among several others. Yet, as one attends to the manner in which these âisms are employed both in spoken and written language, one is left with the impression that they do not connote the same thing to different authors and speakers, not to mention the audience to whom these terms are addressed. Not only does there appear to be a disjunction between usage and meaning, but also, and perhaps more significantly, it seems to be the case that contemporary understanding of these terms may not accurately reflect the ideas of those who initially developed the underlying concepts and positions which are represented by these âisms. In other words, when we make use of a term such as Socialism, are we using it in a manner which is in accord with what Socialism was understood to mean when it was established by Karl Marx in the 19th century?

title: Managerial and Organizational Ethics
description: Major issues in contemporary managerial and organizational ethics will be addressed from a liberal arts âvaluesâ perspective in this course. Case studies, audio-visual vignettes, and management simulations and exercises will be combined with class discussion to analyze the following topics among others: organizational due process; employee rights; employment discrimination; managerial values and organizational culture; ethical decision-making in an international and global context; organizational governance; environmental issues; consumer and community stakeholder issues; and ethical codes of conduct. (If you have taken Business Ethics: A Liberal Arts Perspective , do not register for this course.)

title: The Book of Genesis
description: To quote the flyleaf from Robert Alterâs translation of Genesis, which we will use in class: âGenesis begins with the making of heaven and earth and all life, and ends with the image of a mummy â Josephâs â in a coffin. In between come many of the primal stories in Western culture: Adam and Eveâs expulsion from the Garden of Eden, Cainâs murder of Abel, Noah and the Flood, the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Abrahamâs binding of Isaac, the covenant of God and Abraham, the saga of Joseph and his brothers. These are stories we attend to throughout our lives, for their beauty, their emotional resonance, their philosophical weight, and their sacredness. They connect us with one another and with generations to come.â In this course we will explore together the stories from the book of Genesis, with special attention to their literary quality, their ethical content, and their theological implications.

title: The 1960s: Culture and Conflict
description: âThe Times They Are A-Changinââ is the title of a Bob Dylan song from 1964. Perhaps the title itself encapsulates an emerging mood in America during this decade of transformation. The inclusive years of the 1960s begins with the election of John Kennedy as the 35th President of the United States in 1960 and concludes with the end of the Vietnam War on January 27, 1973. This entire period was, in fact, more than a time of change: it was a time of formation. More than 70 million young Americans of the post-war years â âbaby boomersâ - were coming of age and not liking the direction America was going. Having experienced the conservative and lucrative post-war fifties with the advent of television, rock & roll and super highways, Americaâs youth generally rejected any association with their parentsâ generation. They experimented with new and radical ways of thinking that powerfully challenged the very fabric of American life. To be sure, many of the revolutionary ideas from the sixties are shaping life in the West today. The 1960s was not only a decade of transformation in American history, but an era of formation and influence that would lay the foundation for generations to come. âThe 1960s: Culture and Conflictâ is a graduate course that will review the political landscape and cultural milieu coming from the Eisenhower post-war era, while examining new and ostensibly radical ideology, protest movements, and counterculture of the period that often united politicians and dissidents in similar causes. By design this course is interdisciplinary, as students will have the opportunity to select themes in political science, the arts (music, entertainment, and media), religion, philosophy and sociology (culture studies) for their semester research paper.

title: Cosmos and Christ: Can They Be Mutually Enlightening?
description: Course Number: LSHV-486 Knowledge of the physical universe was extremely limited when Jesus of Nazareth lived and died and, as his believers believed, rose from the dead. Beliefs about him developed into formal doctrines in the next 4 centuries. Knowledge of the physical universe today is, by comparison, torrential. What these two bodies of knowledge have to say to each other is a discourse that is long overdue for those who have an interest in each. The course doesnât promise that a satisfactory integration will be arrived at but only that a greater degree of integration will be attempted. The effort is not motivated by apologetics but by the need for a wholeness if and to the extent that it can be achieved. This is the challenge. The style of the classes will be a discussion of readings, lectures, interaction between the two lecturers, and the participation of the class. This course will embrace at least three of the General Learning goals elaborated by the Graduate Liberal Studies enterprise: a) what gives ultimate meaning to human life; b) where is human life heading?; c) how are human beings related to nature and creation as a whole?
