title: BIG DATA:  VISUAL ANALYTICS â âMind, Machines & Innovationâ- *In partnership with DC Government & Bill Hudnut
description: BIG DATA: VISUAL ANALYTICS â âMind, Machines & Innovationâ- *In partnership with DC Government & Bill Hudnut: Visual analytics is the convergence of analytical reasoning and technology, advanced through visual interactive interfaces, allowing a new level of speed and communication. This new course will address the emerging dominance of big data and new technologies to utilize it, within the framework of real estate. We will deviate from traditional Excel modeling, to faster-paced visual communication of analysis, utilizing some of the most popular software programs available. Excel will be used as a âdata siloâ, while programs such as Tableau will take over for custom analysis that would not have been possible with traditional modeling. Integrating new technologies to change the delivery of standard analytics, this course will give students a cursory overview of effectively utilizing tools within the emergence of big data to visually represent traditional analysis. New applications allow individuals the freedom to drive custom analysis instantly, and visually, without traditional coding skills. The landscape within CRE is quickly changing, and pure linear analysis will no longer provide satisfactory results. Data has become too vast and complex for standard delivery. These are skills that will be in demand, rather for a traditional analyst, or a presenter looking to stand out by delivering answers to complex questions live. â¢ Transform data to visual and interactive dashboards â¢ Quickly address issues through analytical reasoning facilitated by visual interactive interfaces, through technology and innovative new approaches â¢ Utilize your ability to filter pertinent data, and present it in an easily comprehensible format This course will be directly tied to analysis to support the DC Government, specifically, The Office of the Deputy Mayor for Planning and Economic Development, and their Corporate Assistance Unit. Corporate Assistance is undertaking a new actionable study that studentâs will take part in through this course, entitled: Â· âDC Tech Initiative: Transforming Office Markets through Emerging Urban Clustersâ By participating in this class, your work may be used to help assist in the analysis being completed for this study, which will facilitate strategic steps the unit is undertaking. Additionally, there will be several guest speakers with students having the ability to network and meet local public and private sector leaders driving some of the most exciting new real estate developments in the Nation.

title: Airports
description: Over the past century aviation has evolved from being a novelty for hobbyists intrigued by the thought of flight to a major driver in the global economy. Governments of all forms and sizes have invested trillions of dollars in developing the infrastructure necessary to support and promote commercial aviation in hopes that the movement of people and goods will have a positive impact on local, regional, and national economies. However, investment in aviation infrastructure is no longer limited to the public sector. The airport business model is evolving, and this evolution will present new opportunities for real estate investment on and around major international airports. The purpose of this course is to introduce the student to development opportunities on and around major air transportation facilities. The course will not only focus on the U.S. airport management and finance models, but will also include case studies from other regions of the world including Asia and the Middle East. Subjects to be covered include, but not be limited to, techniques for forecasting airport activity levels (passenger and cargo), how airspace around aerodromes are protected for avigation and navigational aids easements which, in turn, can restrict or limit land development opportunities, socio-economic impacts of major international airports on regional economic development and land valuation, land use compatibility, airport governance, and case studies of successful real estate development ventures around airports in the U.S. The course will be led by Mr. James Jarvis. Mr. Jarvis is a Senior Vice President of Ricondo & Associates, Inc. (R&A). R&A is the worldâs largest privately owned company devoted exclusively to airport planning and business management consulting. In addition to Mr. Jarvis, airport management professionals who specialize in commercial real estate development will offer their perspectives on what makes private real estate development opportunities viable at their facilities. Amazon.com and Dickâs Sporting Goods logistic centers and corporate headquarters are but two examples of private companies who have invested in lands on or immediately adjacent to major international airports, and many others are following suit. Why have they done so, and why have the airports that host their operations agreed to let them develop lands owned by the airport? These and other interesting case studies will be reviewed during the semester. At the conclusion of the course students will prepare and present a moot proposal to develop a parcel of land adjacent to a major international airport in the U.S. The proposals and presentations will be evaluated and critiqued by a committee of airport managers.

title: Real Estate Investment Trusts
description: The Real Estate Investment Trusts course presents comprehensive insight to this crucial area of real estate investment. The course touches on the origins of REITs in the 1960s, but mainly focuses on the structure and operation of current public REITs: how their acquisitions, dispositions, new developments and capital stack are determined and analyzed, how they adhere to the âcompany strategy,â and how trends in the broader commercial real estate sector affect these companies, and their share prices. While REITs are the easiest, and certainly most liquid, way for individuals to own commercial real estate, no two REITs are alike. The REITs course, taught by Jonathan Morris, is based upon the experience of a real estate professional. Professor Morris served as an executive officer for several public and private REITs: Vice President & Director of Acquisitions for The Charles E. Smith Companies (which became Archstone-Smith, then was sold to Lehman Brothers and Tishman-Speyer); Vice President & Director of Acquisitions for Boston Properties, Inc. (NYSE: BXP); Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer for Government Property Investors,Inc., a private REIT sponsored by Brown Brothers Harriman. Step into the shoes of a seasoned REIT executive who acquired iconic Washington, D.C. assets such as Metropolitan Square, home of The Old Ebbitt Grill (for Boston Properties, NYSE: BXP); 100 East Pratt Street in Baltimore, Maryland and Riverfront Plaza towers, located on the banks of the James River in Richmond Virginia (both also for BXP).Using a new text written by the Vice President of Investor Relations for a public REIT, this course will include real time use of Twitter, the Wall Street Journalâs noted âThe Property Reportâ published each Wednesday in the INVESTING section along with current research reports from the nationâs leading REIT research analysts, with possibly a visit from a top analyst who follows the office and industrial sector. The goal, by semester end, is to be conversant with REIT nomenclature and structure and be able to decide whether any particular announced transaction by a public REIT is in keeping with its stated mission and also, whether it is accretive, dilutive, or earnings neutral. The course will include guest speakers from the industry along with a site visit to a related asset developed and/or acquired by a notable public REIT.

title: Due Diligence
description: Due Diligence: Commercial Real Estate Transactions course will provide an overview of what REITS, developers, investors and lenders require in terms of due diligence, permitting and underwriting for land and improvements for commercial transactions. From pre-contract through the due diligence and closing phases of the transaction the full array of checklist items will be examined through a technicianâs perspective. Whether its acquisition of the fee, ground lease or securing debt, each step requires pre-closing due diligence: valuation, tax & flood certification, title search, title insurance, survey, zoning certification, property condition assessment, environmental phase analysis, comprehensive property and casualty insurance and more. Youâll learn how this array of pre-closing issues culminates in the escrow and closing phase of the commercial real estate deal. We will have guest speakers in the commercial real estate industry that will provide actual practical and transactional experience. The goal is for you to have a practical and working knowledge of (1) the framework of language necessary to understand and work in the due diligence process; and (2) what it takes to bring together and close a commercial real estate transaction.

title: Retail Real Estate Development
description: MPRE 723-01 RETAIL REAL ESTATE DEVELOPMENT Fall 2012 Mike Bush, Esq. Vice President Real Estate â Giant Food (Retired) Retail real estate has a major impact on our built environment and daily living. This seminar explores how well-known projects in metropolitan Washington serve the complementary objectives of developers creating wealth, and of retailers delivering goods and services to shoppers. Students will tour retail centers like Gallery Place, Friendship Heights, Bethesda Row, and Downtown Silver Spring to explore how and why: â¢ Developers decide where to locate new projects â¢ Tenant mix is determined â¢ Retailers decide where to place a store â¢ Developer and retailer use market leverage in lease negotiation to allocate prospect of success and risk of failure â¢ Developers control a site, obtain zoning, design it to attract shoppers, and build and lease it â¢ Some projects are successful, and some struggle â¢ âLocation â Location â Location!â may be a myth Tours, readings, and class discussion will expose students to basic considerations of retail development: â¢ Development â a Non-Linear Gauntlet â¢ Market Analysis / Site Selection â¢ Pursuing Maximum Return â¢ Financial Feasibility: Cost Vs. Return â¢ Controlling The Site: Assembly / Options / Due Diligence â¢ Zoning For Use And Density â¢ Site Layout / Engineering â¢ Tenant Mix â¢ Leasing: Financial Covenants â¢ Leasing: Operating Covenants â¢ Design â¢ Construction â¢ Development Careers

title: RE Entrepreneurship
description: Real Estate Entrepreneurship is a course designed to explain and explore entrepreneurship in the context of the business of real estate. Utilizing the dynamic Washington, DC market as a backdrop, the course will use case studies, share experiences from accomplished guest speakers, and draw from the instructorsâ first-hand understanding to immerse students in the when, where, how, and why of undertaking entrepreneurial endeavors. It discusses the theory and practice of entrepreneurship generally, the motives of entrepreneurs as compared with other businesspeople, and the specific application of entrepreneurship to the real estate sector. The coursework will focus on the mechanics of putting together deals, creating real estate businesses, the constant need to âsellâ as an entrepreneur, and paths to becoming an entrepreneur, with emphasis on the constant balancing act required in a start-up operation. At the conclusion of the course, students will have enhanced insight and skills to undertake an entrepreneurial real estate venture either now or at some future point in their careers.

title: Distressed Properties and Real Estate Workouts
description: Real estate companies, banks and investment firms face strategic challenges during rapidly changing economic cycles. This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneys, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. Topics include: â¢ Evaluating financing sources and structures â¢ Monetary and non-monetary loan default resolution â¢ Developing an asset or portfolio plan â¢ Purchasing and selling real estate in a credit constrained market â¢ Bankruptcy and consensual workouts â¢ Developing and implementing workout plans and alternative strategies â¢ Evaluating your overall position within the market â¢ Strategic planning in a rapidly changing economic environment

title: Distressed Properties and Real Estate Workouts
description: Real estate companies, banks and investment firms face strategic challenges during rapidly changing economic cycles. This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include; real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneys, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. Topics include: â¢ Evaluating financing sources and structures â¢ Monetary and non-monetary loan default resolution â¢ Developing an asset or portfolio plan â¢ Purchasing and selling real estate in a credit constrained market â¢ Bankruptcy and consensual workouts â¢ Developing and implementing workout plans and alternative strategies â¢ Evaluating your overall position within the market â¢ Strategic planning in a rapidly changing economic environment

title: Private Equity
description: The courseâs objective is to introduce the world of real estate private equity investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio, as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the private equity investment platform from the perspective of 1) an investor, 2) the general partner of an institutional style fund, and 3) the developers who access this capital. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. From the perspective of an investor, we will examine the justification for real estate in the context of a portfolio of varied alternatives. Then, we will consider the role of real estate private equity in satisfying a real estate investment allocation requirement. We will also consider the varied strategies available for accessing real estate exposure. And, lastly, we will review the due diligence appropriate for selecting a real estate investment sponsor (or âGPâ). From the perspective of an institutional fund sponsor (or GP), we will consider the challenges, and opportunities, associated with capitalizing an investment platform with investors organized via a private equity style fund. We will also look at the unique regulatory and tax issues associated with such a fund. And, review acquisition and asset management issues critical to a successful result. From the perspective of a developer, seeking equity for a real estate project, we will consider the benefits and risks associated with raising equity from an institutional fund. The capstone of the course will be the organization, by students, in groups of three to four, of a real estate private equity investment proposal suitable for an institutional investor. Each group will their proposal to a committee comprised of the course instructor and other industry practioners. This proposal will be accompanied by a full business plan for the fundâs general partner sponsor.

title: Real Estate Private Equity
description: The courseâs objective is to introduce the world of private real estate investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the alternatives from the perspective of both a general partner of a fund, but also from the perspective of an investor in a real estate private equity fund operator. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. First, we will examine the rationale for private versus public real estate investment in the context of an overall real estate portfolio strategy. Once the theoretical justification is made, we will examine various strategies for gaining private real estate exposure, including reviewing private funds, fund of funds, property and/or geographic specific funds as well as private REITâs. As part of this course, we will examine different fund structures, review fund governance, how the different vehicles are marketed as well as tax issues affecting each of the different investment structures. Furthermore, we will review best in practice due diligence procedures for various investments. Then we will review the implementation of the investment strategy including a review of the investment and asset management processes. Given the current state of the markets, we will examine the investment process in the context of evaluating distressed property opportunities. In addition, we will cover negotiating joint ventures, execution of venture business plans, and troubleshooting when plans go awry. Course Description for MPRE 728-01, Spring Semester 2013, Instructor, Roger Staiger: A look at how Private Equity underwrites real estate transactions and structures project returns. Financial Engineer of real estate returns for equity investors will be demonstrated and modeled.

title: Investment Banking
description: The Real Estate Investment Banking course presents the opportunity to apply real estate finance principles to actual real life transactions through the use of case studies. The course builds on MPRE 621, Foundations of Real Estate Finance, and MPRE 735, Structured Finance, but MPRE 735 is not a prerequisite. Students will get a review of the real estate capital markets and investment underwriting and analysis with the remaining course classes taught through interactive discussion of a variety of real world case studies that cover topics including: Levered Investment Analysis, Debt Recapitalizations, Equity Investments, Note Purchases, and Lending Analysis from a lenderâs perspective.

title: Investment Banking
description: The Real Estate Investment Banking course presents the opportunity to apply real estate finance principles to actual real life transactions through the use of case studies. The course builds on MPRE 621, Foundations of Real Estate Finance, and MPRE 735, Structured Finance, but MPRE 735 is not a prerequisite. Students will get a review of the real estate capital markets and investment underwriting and analysis with the remaining course classes taught through interactive discussion of a variety of real world case studies that cover topics including: Levered Investment Analysis, Debt Recapitalizations, Equity Investments, Note Purchases, and Lending Analysis from a lenderâs perspective.

title: Building a Career in Commercial Management
description: The course objective is to introduce and examine various careers in commercial real estate with a concentration on management. The course outlines various career alternatives and the required skills for employment and advancement a real estate career. There is a segment on ethical and performance standards. The class will review various avenues of potential employment and how to make meaningful introductions and employment evaluation. The class will be augmented by a variety of guest speakers from the real estate industry: Developers, Asset Managers, Broker, Construction Executive and an Executive Recruiter specializing in real estate.

title: Real Estate Economics
description: This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Among topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. The core course introduces concepts and techniques of economics that pertain to urban planners. The governmentâs role in the economics of cities and in development, national welfare and housing policies, public and private finance, and the cost-benefits associated with development are addressed. The course addresses state and regional economic policies that affect cities and regional economies, as well as finance, investment, fiscal resources and redevelopment initiatives. Real estate development case studies from a variety of innovative local projects in the Washington region are included in the course content. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speaker. Problem sets, case studies, tests, and class participation are included in grading.

title: Real Estate and Urban Economics
description: Course Description This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Among topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speaker. Texts Urban Economics and Real Estate Markets by Denise DiPasquale and William C. Wheaton (Prentice-Hall); A Primer on U.S. Housing Markets and Housing Policy by Richard K. Green and Stephen Malpezzi (Urban Institute Press).

title: Structured Finance
description: The Structured Finance Course examines how large commercial real estate financings are put together. The course will explore the evolution of the real estate finance transaction. Who the players were and who they are now. How have structures changed with the influence of Wall Street as a major player having entered the market in the early 90âs and the issues they faced during the credit crisis of late 2007? We will examine the options available and the problems currently facing both Borrowers and Lenders. The course will give a practical overview of how we finance the entire capital structure from senior loans to mezzanine tranches to equity. Guest lecturers, who are major forces in the business, will tell you their stories and impart their wisdom in this challenging time for structured finance. Upon completion of this course, students should have a full grasp of how deals are financed. As a result, the student should be an asset to their employer in this field. The course will include some lecturing, discussion and a significant amount of team projects designed to teach the student the anatomy of âthe deal.â Class participation (20%), significant team projects including the creation of a finance memorandum, as well as specific research assignments to help train the real estate mind (30%), how well the professor feels you grasp the knowledge (20%), and a final examination (30%) will serve as the learning and assessment tools.

title: Structured Finance
description: Real Estate Equity and Debt: Structuring/Underwriting the Investment, ongoing Ownership and Dispositions of Real Estate is a âhand onâ course to find, structure, purchase and operate real estate investments. The course will provide detailed real life view on all aspects of Real Estate investment including: â¢ Where to find property â¢ Initial review of a package/information on an investment â¢ How to underwrite all aspects of an investment â¢ Understanding different types of debt and equity â¢ Structuring debt and equity for an investment â¢ Due diligence and closing a transaction â¢ Legal documentation of a transaction â¢ Now that you own a propertyâ¦â¦what goes wrong and what do you do.

title: Bankruptcy
description: Bankruptcy laws can impair contracts, stay collection efforts, setoffs and lien enforcement and cause reversal of pre-bankruptcy payments and other transfers of property. This course examines the impact of the United States Bankruptcy Code on the real estate industry. The primary focus of the course is on the bankruptcy issues that affect real estate properties and projects in business chapter 11 cases. Topics covered by the course include: â¢ The bankruptcy automatic stay â¢ Adequate protection of a mortgagee's interests â¢ Use of cash collateral â¢ Single asset real estate case considerations â¢ Treatment of unexpired nonresidential real property leases â¢ Voidable transactions, including fraudulent conveyances and preferences â¢ The plan confirmation process, including cramdown â¢ Substantive consolidation

title: RE Portfolio and Derivatives
description: This two part course will focus on portfolio creating and risk mitigation. Part I will review portfolio fundamentals, stochastic modeling, and construct multiple portfolio models to include Markowitz and Oracle OptQuest. Part II will review derivative products and how they are used to hedge systematic risk at the portfolio level. The course is designed to exemplify the benefits of physical asset aggregation into a portfolio to eliminate unique (unsystematic) asset risk. After this demonstration, market risk (systematic) is discussed and, through the use of derivatives, mitigated. Students will leave the class understanding portfolio theory as well as the basics of derivatives and the method(s) derivatives are utilized to mitigate risk at the portfolio level.

title: Construction Management
description: The core business of real estate development is the design, construction and delivery of buildings and improvements to real property. This course presents the fundamentals of (1) project delivery methods, (2) preconstruction services and contractor procurement, (3) field and project management processes, (4) construction materials, equipment and systems, (5) design and construction contracts, (6) technology tools, sustainability and trends in construction, and (7) ethical considerations.

title: Transit-Oriented Development
description: Transit Oriented Development (TOD) â Gain insiderâs knowledge on this emerging concept, which is part real estate product and part public policy. Learn about the theory and practice of TOD, one of the few types of development that has strong support in the environmental community, and responds to an emerging demographic market. Students will develop a thorough understanding of the essentials needed by transit agencies and local officials, the principles for developing successful projects, and the basics of transit, traffic, and parking issues. A range of guest lecturers will add unique perspectives of practitioners - transit officials, environmentalists, planners, and developers, as well as in depth knowledge of market demand, niche housing, and financing. Key distinctions between urban and suburban projects will be clarified. Examples of some of the better projects, and some of the lesson learned, will be drawn from US and Washington area developments.

title: Sustainable Development & Construction
description: In todayâs real estate environment owners, tenants, and jurisdictions are all asking how sustainability can be incorporated into new and existing buildings. Sustainable Construction and the impact on building operation is a graduate level course which builds on the foundation of the Green Building and Construction Management courses, and allows students to understand the link between design, construction and ultimately building operations. The course will present how the design impacts the construction and operation of the building as they relate to sustainable practices. The course will cover, the impact of design decisions upon construction and operations, including cost benefit analysis of design decisions. We will examine sustainable construction practice and how it impacts on owners and tenants and leases. What state and local legislation is driving sustainable building construction and operations. Ethical considerations of sustainability practice in construction and operations.

title: Construction Law & Project Management
description: The course uses construction law as an organizing principle to impart a practical understanding of the issues arising in construction contracting and project management. The class will apply the standard-form AIA contract documents (owner-architect, owner-contractor and contractor-subcontractor) and construction law principles to several actual case studies in a Socratic (tutorial, rather than lecture) format. MPRE students will hone their analytical, advocacy and negotiating skills on frequently arising issues, including the relationship of the parties to the construction setting; important contract principles and provisions; the impact of varying project delivery systems and pricing mechanisms on both public and private projects; the intricacies of project administration, including issues related to changes in the scope of the work, delays, defective work, substantial and final completion and warranties; suretyship; liens; and dispute resolution.

title: Construction Estimating & Procurement
description: The goal of the course is to provide students with an in-depth understanding of how a general contractor pursues, estimates, bids and procures work. The course will cover the full range of activities from conceptual estimating, to scoping and bidding projects, to the buyout and award of subcontracts. Upon completion of the course, students should have a greater understanding of how a general contractor estimates and submits their pricing and proposals to both public and private clients, as well as how the general contractor evaluates subcontractors, compares subcontractorsâ pricing and scopes, and how the general contractor awards subcontracts for the various trades.

title: Construction Estimating & Procurement
description: The goal of the course is to provide students with an in-depth understanding of how a general contractor pursues, estimates, bids and procures work. The course will cover the full range of activities from conceptual estimating, to scoping and bidding projects, to the buyout and award of subcontracts. Upon completion of the course, students should have a greater understanding of how a general contractor estimates and submits their pricing and proposals to both public and private clients, as well as how the general contractor evaluates subcontractors, compares subcontractorsâ pricing and scopes, and how the general contractor awards subcontracts for the various trades.

title: Introduction to International Real Estate Investment
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn how to participate in international real estate transactions. This translates into working on investments involving foreign investors in the United States, as well getting a clear understanding of the motivations and subsequently the cultural differences encountered of US investors investing overseas, taking a close look at development, real estate brokerage, architecture and corporate users. The course will bring in speakers with experiences of working with different nationalities to relate case studies and work in their sectors. The schedule of the speakers, especially those from out of town, will determine which class they speak at and this schedule will be constantly updated. Readings preparing for the next class will follow the sessions, topic and speakers, as revised. Class participants will be given $100 million to invest in the US, and on the last day of the class present orally for no more than 12 minutes and provide a written report on how, why and where they invested the monies, which will represent 70% of their grade. They will be asked to also present investing $100 million in their chosen country from the US, which will contribute 15% to their grade. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

title: Opportunities in International Real Estate
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn how to participate in international real estate transactions. This translates into working on investments involving foreign investors in the United States, as well getting a clear understanding of the motivations and subsequently the cultural differences encountered of US investors investing overseas, taking a close look at development, real estate brokerage, architecture and corporate users. The course will bring in speakers with experiences of working with different nationalities to relate case studies and work in their sectors. The schedule of the speakers, especially those from out of town, will determine which class they speak at and this schedule will be constantly updated. Readings preparing for the next class will follow the sessions, topic and speakers, as revised.

title: Selected Regions in Int'l RE: Europe
description: The course will examine real estate markets â countries and sectors - in the European Union and adjacent countries, tracing the expansion of the European Union as well as the emerging markets in Central and Eastern Europe from the Fall of the Berlin Wall through the recent global financial crisis and looking ahead at new opportunities. Speakers involved in these property markets will be invited to come in as well as to talk on Skype to give first hand updates. The course will begin with an analysis of the established EU core real estate markets in Western Europe. The UK will be looked at in depth along with Ireland. The UK lease has traditionally been regarded as the most Landlord friendly and we will contrast this with the French lease. We will look at London, Paris, Amsterdam, Frankfurt and Madrid as the historic favored investment capitals. We will look at the role of the Chartered Surveyor and the UK Lawyer and compare them to their US counterparts. We will look at why Europeans invest abroad and the roles of their Pension Funds and other Institutional Investors. We will look at the make-up of the big owners of European Real Estate and look at where Americans have invested in Europe. We will analyze the goals and leadership of the European real estate advisors and their global reach, as well as the European Capital Markets. We will also cover the rapid expansion and contraction of the Irish and Spanish property markets. For more than 50 years, real estate in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) remained cut off from private market forces. Following the fall of the Berlin Wall, the expansion of the European Union starting in the 1990s generated tremendous demand for modern office, retail, residential, industrial and hotel facilities. Anticipating cap rate compression, developers rushed to complete projects that could be sold once these countries either joined the European Union, or simply became less exotic and more acceptable to institutional lenders and investors. Today, with a more than 20-year track record of development, investment, leasing and sales â through several cycles and more than one âcrisisâ â the region provides a wealth of development case studies that offer lessons for predicting real estate trends in other Emerging Markets across the globe. This section will start with a brief historical context and study the nuances of individual country markets and the evolution of office, retail, and hotel markets across the CEE region. We will look at specific deals based on first-hand experience and follow market trends to examine the differences and similarities between EU, Central European and Russian markets, large and small nations and dynamic verses incremental approaches finishing with a look at Turkey as both a long-time NATO ally and an emerging EU candidate.. Our goal will be to build a framework for the analysis of new markets and the measurement and management of real estate risks using development and investment criteria such as cap rates, loan rates and terms, and varying types of investors. Ultimately, geographic distinctions fade away in favor of market-driven categories for core, value-added and opportunistic investments.

title: Real Estate Investments
description: Course Description: The Real Estate Investments course will prepare students for all aspects of making a leveraged income-producing real estate acquisition. Students will learn how to evaluate a projectâs viability by learning how to perform a market analysis, determine economic feasibility, structure debt, raise and structure equity, and perform tax analyses. Topics will include discounted cash flows, net present values, internal rate of return analyses, sensitivity analyses and before- and after-tax cash flow analyses. Distressed debt and equity restructures will be introduced. The class structure will include lectures, class discussion, readings, guest speakers, preparation & discussion of case studies, and a team project. Students will engage in actual, current investment and development projects. Methodology: Lectures, class discussion, readings, ARGUS, guest speakers and preparation and discussion of case studies and term project. Instructors will discuss actual investment and development projects as well as stressed debt workouts.

title: Negotiations
description: Overview: This course will cover approaches for managing differences between individuals and groups as seen through the lens of negotiation. Students will investigate models of negotiation decision-making. The goal of the course is to provide students both a conceptual and practical grasp of negotiating concepts which are applicable in their professional and personal lives. Principal focus will be on interest-based negotiation with consideration given to the psychological underpinnings of difficult conversations. This approach is now taught in business schools, law schools and school of public policy and widely taught as better business practice to profit and non-profit groups. It has also been the basis for resolving violent conflicts. Students will explore, through role exercises, two and multi-party negotiations. Focus will be both on the importance of substance and procedural considerations in complex negotiation. The conceptual framework is applicable in real estate negotiations as well as in other public policy and personal settings. Please Note: This course is based upon experiential learning. Thus a substantial amount of personal learning is accomplished through participation in and critique of class negotiation exercises. The value of the exercise is often dependent on the participation of your fellow students. Therefore, students should make every effort to attend all classes.

title: Independent Study
description: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which, in some manner, cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the studentâs Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Independent Study
description: Spring Independent Study: The NAIOP Independent Study Real Estate Case Competition, NAIOP Maryland/DC Capital Challenge course offers MPS/RE students the opportunity to participate in a real estate case competition generously sponsored by the local chapter of one of the leading commercial real estate trade associations. In addition to the Georgetown MPS/RE program, teams from the graduate real estate programs of the University of Maryland and George Washington University are participating. Working in teams, students on the Georgetown MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team visit the project Site, analyze its current utilization, assess competitive sites in the regional submarket, and develop alternative plans for putting the Site to its highest and best commercial real estate use. Students conduct substantial research, write reports on their research findings, and apply that research to developing such plans. A group of top industry advisors who are provided by NAIOP Maryland/DC advises and assists the MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team in developing its Case. Industry advisors include architects, developers, financiers, engineers, and land use attorneys. This is an incredible opportunity for students to work closely with some of the top real estate professionals in Greater Washington. The MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team presents its ultimate findings to the panel of NAIOP Capital Challenge judges, hopefully emerging victorious. Go Hoya Capital Challenge Team!!!! The subject of the upcoming Case Competition is forthcoming. Additional Independent Study Course options: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which in some manner cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the studentâs Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Capstone
description: The required Capstone Course marks the culmination of a studentâs career in the Master of Professional Studies in Real Estate program, and the studentâs transition from journeyman to advanced real estate professional. The Capstone Course offers the student the opportunity to explore a major real estate topic in significant depth and make a substantial contribution to knowledge in a selected sector of the real estate profession. Based upon previous courses and professional experience, the student formulates a Capstone Project topic. Under the tutelage of a senior real estate professional from Metropolitan Washington or beyond, the student researches the chosen topic, participates in any appropriate activities related to the topic, and produces a substantial work which constitutes a significant contribution to real estate knowledge. Because MPS/RE encompasses institutional, financial, legal, marketing, accounting, environmental, planning, design, engineering, and other disciplines, it is impossible to specify a single genre of Capstone Project, and the Capstone Project is thus defined chiefly by quality. With the fullest support of the entire MPS/RE program, the student has maximum freedom to pursue a real estate topic that embodies the very essence of the studentâs professionalism, and should be one of the best pieces of work the student ever does. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Applied Ethics in Sports
description: A course that allows students to explore their own values and challenges them to codify and commit to their own code of ethics in relation to professional codes of conduct and best practices. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Ethics course.

title: Sports Leadership and Management
description: An examination of management issues and organizational behavior in the industry, relating mostly to staffing, motivation, and communication. In application, it introduces theories on leadership and the tools and techniques of how to effectively lead a sports organization on a daily basis.

title: Diversity and Social Responsibliity in Sports
description: A thorough introduction to the history and socioeconomic underpinnings of sports. This course examines the issues of ethnicity, gender, and international scope in relation to the modern sports world. Through readings, discussion, and role-playing, this course explores the stereotypes, misconceptions, and social elements of a changing sports landscape.

title: Sports Marketing Strategy
description: An examination of marketing issues specific to the sports industry, based on the application of basic marketing principles to a range of sports organizations. This course will analyze the field of sports and lifestyle marketing with the practices, applications, and strategy of mainstream marketing. Course content will include a historical overview of sports marketing, delving into areas such as the team-side versus agency-side of the industry, including sponsors and corporations, sporting goods, special events, and sports enterprises in general.

title: Sports Communications and Public Relations
description: An analysis of the role of public relations and mass media in sports communication. Hands-on training in the tools and technology of sports PR is combined with an examination of the publicâs relationship with athletes and sports. This course explores the progressive techniques and activities used to promote sporting events. Discussion topics include brand awareness, media management, message recall, and the examination of four news mediums: television, internet, print, and radio.

title: Sales Promotion, Licensing and Sponsorship
description: An exploration of techniques used to sell and generate revenue in the business cycle. Topics include sales proposal development, sponsor solicitation, licensing rights, new business development, endorsements and merchandising, commercialization of technology, and corporate partnerships.

title: Sports Digital Media and Consumer Engagement
description: A review of how to develop and apply essential data in marketing decision-making in the sports industry, including database design, website development and management, privacy issues, and social media. This course considers the application of marketing principles, research methodologies, design implementation, and the analysis and interpretation of data. Incorporating horizontal and vertical distribution systems that integrate with the digital marketplace, the role of technology is explored in relationship to providing management the necessary support and maintenance of customer profiles. Students will gain an understanding of distribution channels, segmentation, demographic characteristics, and e-marketing methods and their applications.

title: Sports Business and Finance
description: This course provides a broad overview of the sports business marketplace, including the financial and accounting acumen necessary for executives to succeed in sports organizations. The goal is to provide hands-on and practical experience to real-time business challenges. Topics as they relate to these sectors include fiscal and budgetary control, ownership, and the skills used in the day-to-day operations of professional and amateur sports organizations.

title: Sports Law, Contracts, and Negotiation
description: This course provides an in-depth overview and analysis of legal principles, ethical issues, and salary cap negotiations in professional sports. Issues that are identified, analyzed, and explored include athlete representation, labor law, antitrust exemption, arbitration, collective bargaining, salary cap administration, free agency and the application of ethics as a method in the decision making and problem solving process.

title: Sports Event Planning and Facility Management
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the complexity involved in major sports events and facilities in relation to their social, cultural, and global environment. Featured topics include the analysis of goals and resources, the development of the revenue streams, design and planning, scheduling and operations, staff management, and the implementation of action deadlines.

title: Economics, Brand Management and Under Armour
description: An economic analysis of a variety of issues affecting the sports industry, including ticket pricing, luxury taxes, competitive balance, apparel and retail strategies, revenue sharing, the impacts and rationale of government subsidies, and the global currency marketplace.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry. Action Items: * The course will include an internship handbook and will follow a monthly meeting schedule with the internship advisor and program director. * Students will keep a weekly journal that will detail their objectives, activities, benchmarks and assessment tools. * At terms end, student will submit a project portfolio to the internship advisor and program director.

title: Internship II
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry.

title: Internship Lab
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry.

title: Sports Entreprenuership
description: The goal of this course is to provide the students with a conceptual, strategic, and technical understanding of how to think and act entrepreneurially in the sports industry. We will focus on top-line processes for creating a start-up or applying entrepreneurial methodologies within a larger organization. Through a combination of readings, case studies, guest speakers, discussion and hands-on participation, students will come away with an improved understanding of entrepreneurship and the foundation to apply it in a variety of professional contexts. Throughout the course, students -- working alone or in teams -- will build out a start-up idea. It can be an actual business they want to pursue, but that is not a requirement, as long as they approach their theoretical business as if they were going to start it.

title: Creative Media Enterprise in Sports
description: The convergence of linear television and media â particularly with tablets, mobile and video-on-the-go â has transformed the economics of digital content and had a commanding impact on the sports community. This course will introduce students to how new media enterprises can be employed by sports organizations to create and provide content to consumers and economically influence the business of sports. It explores a variety of integrated and converging platforms and focuses on current digital technologies and channels of distribution. The class gives students hands-on experience with a wide range of tools and provides a framework to critically engage with various technological and cultural products. The course uses practical training to prepare current and future leaders to make responsible management decisions that yield both economic and social value. We will examine a variety of stakeholders (leagues, teams, athletes, sponsors, broadcasters and other companies) whom can now create their own sports properties, platforms and formats and seek transformative solutions for the challenges they face. Students will get hands-on experience by directly engaging with stakeholders (ex: Monumental Network) and understand the underlying goal of expanding the revenue base for a sports organization, reflected in the feasibility of a transmission studio in the organizationâs home playing facility (ex: Verizon Center). The course will also emphasize the impact of digital communications (traditional terrestrial television, websites, web video, blogs, podcasting, viral marketing, social media) on the lives of consumers and sponsors. Goals and Objectives: â¢ Evaluate the economic impact of digital technology and social media in the sports industry â¢ Analyze and determine potential new revenue streams in the sports setting through digital avenues beyond the traditional categories of tickets, food concessions, merchandise, in-house advertising, radio and television â¢ Describe how social media can be used to enhance and expand customer relationship for businesses in the local DMA (designated demographic market area) â¢ Understand and utilize a variety of digital technologies from the perspective of various stakeholders (leagues, teams, athletes, sponsors, broadcasters and companies) â¢ Create and provide content for various digital platforms including mobile devices and apps, tablets and social media

title: The Business of Global Sporting Events
description: Global Sporting Events such as the Olympics, the World Cup, the Super Bowl, and other annual championships, etc., have the ability to bring not only revenue to a city but can define a countryâs image to the world. This interdisciplinary course will focus on the business of putting on a global sporting event from the bidding process to the building of facilities, sponsorships to game day execution and fan engagement. The connection between the development of the economy of a country and sports is becoming stronger. This course will look at the many parts that make up a countryâs path to hosting a global sporting event, how trends are changing in choosing host cities, how sustainability is a dominant factor, and how profit is the measurement of success.

title: Redskins FedExField Externship
description: Students in this course will be responsible for assisting in streamlining the game day operations processes for the Washington Redskins at FedExField throughout the 2013 season. Students will be assigned to work in different sections of the stadium during each home game in order to identify and rectify problems that occur. Additionally, students will assist in working various sections of FedExField as needed in order to gain a better understanding of how each department operates. A positive and professional image is required at all times. This course will provide students with an understanding of the requirements, as well as the complexities, involved in operating, planning, managing and executing operations for a NFL team during the season and on game days. Featured topics include event planning, stadium operations, safety and security, emergency protocol, guest services, ticketing/admission staff management, communications and the implementation of action plans. This course including 8-10 home game days (all day) and 6-8 Thursday night class sessions that are tentatively scheduled from 6:00pm â 8:30pm

title: Sports Culture and Diplomacy
description: This course is designed to allow students to achieve a comprehensive understanding of why sports has the power to connect people across cultures, ethnicity, gender and socioeconomic background and develop an understanding of how they can use sport and cultural diplomacy to develop relationships, understanding, and inter-group collaboration as well as peace building and conflict resolution. The course will provide a unique perspective into relating sports diplomacy to volunteerism, international relations, peace building, leadership, etc. Students will analyze the past, present, and future and gain practical and theoretical foundation for initiating a Sport and Cultural Diplomacy Entrepreneurship project related to personal interest and future career aspirations.

title: Capstone
description: A semester-long opportunity for students to apply what they have learned in the program to produce one substantial piece of work under the tutelage of an industry sponsor and the program faculty. The subject will be appropriate to the students chosen interest. Board members and industry guest lecturers will visit and students will have the opportunity to present their work for the purpose of review and advice. Each student will be assisted in devising a strategy to support the topic of interest, appropriate to the course goals, by semesters end. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Ethics in Technology Management
description: Provides a firm foundation of essential concepts, skills, and strategies to allow for informed decision-making and effective leadership. The course emphasizes ethical responsibilities and core values endemic to the professional world, cutting across several disciplines but focusing particularly on the connections between applied ethics and areas such as technology, business management, and law. You will explore real-world dilemmas and the framework for reaching ethical decisions. Topics could include intellectual property rights, government regulations, privacy, and others. Professional guests from various fieldsâsuch as business, law, and governmentâwill present case studies representative of their experiences. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Ethics course.

title: Management of Technology
description: The Management of Technology course provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. You will examine the requirements of an organization to optimize its competitive strategy and core competencies. You will learn different tactical, strategic, and organizational factors relative to various information systems, as well as how to gauge the effectiveness of an organizationâs information system. The course addresses some of the unique issues in managing different types of IT professionals, from senior-level architects to junior-level infrastructure support personnel. You will explore tools and techniques related to hiring, developing, assessing, and retaining staffâas well as theories/methods of managing remote workers and virtual teams.

title: Management of Technology
description: The Management of Technology course provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. You will examine the requirements of an organization to optimize its competitive strategy and core competencies. You will learn different tactical, strategic, and organizational factors relative to various information systems, as well as how to gauge the effectiveness of an organizationâs information system. The course addresses some of the unique issues in managing different types of IT professionals, from senior-level architects to junior-level infrastructure support personnel. You will explore tools and techniques related to hiring, developing, assessing, and retaining staffâas well as theories/methods of managing remote workers and virtual teams.

title: Project Mgmt for IT Professionals
description: Project Management for IT Professionals will master both the practical and theoretical sides of project management for IT professionals. In keeping with the ANSI standard for project management, this comprehensive course covers such core concepts as triple-constant theory, critical path method, and tracking earned value. Youâll follow the project lifecycleâfrom planning to monitoring to controlâand learn how to avoid the most common challenges of successful project management.

title: Project Mgmt for IT Professionals
description: Project Management for IT Professionals will master both the practical and theoretical sides of project management for IT professionals. In keeping with the ANSI standard for project management, this comprehensive course covers such core concepts as triple-constant theory, critical path method, and tracking earned value. Youâll follow the project lifecycleâfrom planning to monitoring to controlâand learn how to avoid the most common challenges of successful project management.

title: Organizational & Technology Change Management
description: This course is designed to introduce Project Risk and Change Management related to the integration of new technology initiatives. In this course we will use a combination of presentation, discussion, and research to understand the risk of new technology project failure in the absence of a comprehensive change management strategy. The process is introduced from the systems view of organizational change and includes data collection methods (qualitative and quantitative), diagnosis, and the management of system-wide technology change integration. In addition to the practical component of the course of study, substantive and validated theory is integrated that spans 50+ years of change management literature.

title: Organizational & Technology Change Management
description: This course is designed to introduce Project Risk and Change Management related to the integration of new technology initiatives. In this course we will use a combination of presentation, discussion, and research to understand the risk of new technology project failure in the absence of a comprehensive change management strategy. The process is introduced from the systems view of organizational change and includes data collection methods (qualitative and quantitative), diagnosis, and the management of system-wide technology change integration. In addition to the practical component of the course of study, substantive and validated theory is integrated that spans 50+ years of change management literature.

title: Technology Entrepreneurship
description: This course examines the startup process from both sidesâexploring the perspective of the entrepreneur as well as the corporation pursuing a model of open innovation. Based largely on case study discussions, the course focuses on intellectual property, high-tech product development, venture finance, high-tech market strategy, strategic alliances, and entrepreneurial leadership skills.

title: Marketing Technology Products & Services
description: Discover how to leverage the Internet and identify new marketing opportunities for technologybased products and services. In this up-to-theminute course, youâll learn to develop and execute marketing plans that integrate social networking tools and other emerging media options. Content also covers market research, strategy development, customer segmentation,and competitor analysisâwith a special focus on business intelligence and related technological methods of effective information and resource management.

title: Software Life Cycle Management
description: Gain a deeper understanding of the process of analyzing and designing enterprise-wide systems. This course focuses on the software engineering principles that can be leveraged for software application development. Youâll focus on object-oriented analysis and design concepts using Unified Modeling Language methodology use cases, classes and object diagrams, inheritance,behavioral modeling, data abstraction, and information hiding. Additional topics include overloading, polymorphism, and programming languages such as .NET and Java.

title: Business Intelligence & Data Analytics
description: Today data is everywhere. The most competitive commercial companies are harnessing it to formulate their go-to-market strategies, make course corrections and outthink the competition. Governments are using data to develop solutions to social issues, and prevent terrorist attacks. This course explores how government agencies, commercial companies and nonprofits are using Data Marts, Data Warehouses (DW) and Business Intelligence (BI) to achieve their strategic goals and objectives. During the course students are introduced to the concepts through real-world demonstrations of data warehousing and business intelligence using current platforms available in the marketplace from vendors like Microsoft, Microstrategy, Oracle, and others.

title: Managing Information Security
description: The Managing Information Security course is designed for managers charged with protecting IT systems, this course prepares you to anticipate the threat of Internet attacksâand develop effective strategies to defend against them. Learn the core concepts of data security, signature algorithms, key distribution, hash functions, and identification schemes. Youâll even explore public/private key cryptography, secret key schemes (DES and IDEA), public key schemes (RSA and ElGamal), and systems based on elliptic curves. The pertinent implementation aspects and current security estimations will be covered for all schemes.

title: Crafting a Mobile Strategy
description: Mobile devices are becoming a focal point for many business sectors and business models. These devices represent a unique opportunity to engage consumers, employees, or many other end users. According to industry organization CTIA, wireless subscriptions in the U.S. now outnumber the total population. How should IT departments approach mobile devices? What goes into creating a mobile app? Whatâs the difference between ânative appsâ and âmobile optimizedâ? The pace of innovation in the mobile field is rapid. Healthcare has created a new term âmHealth- to describe patient and provider engagement via mobile devices. In addition, recent presidential orders have placed an emphasis on the role of mobile devices for many federal agencies and their IT departments. Sports teams are leveraging mobile devices to improve fan experiences within and away from arenas. Some business sectors have embraced the mobile device as a platform for consumer engagement and acquisition, while others have not. However, crafting a mobile environment is not a âone and doneâ solution. A number of key decisions and strategy are involved in creating a âmobile strategyâ.

title: Service-Oriented Architecture
description: In the Service Oriented Architecture course you will learn the standards of SOA implementation and gain a thorough understanding of the value, benefit, cost, effectiveness, and challenges of moving to an SOA. In this course, youâll master the basics of modeling, designing, and implementing an SOAâand discover the theories and best practices behind interoperability.

title: Cloud Computing & Virtual Data Centers
description: The Cloud Computing and Virtual Data Centers course explores the emergence of Cloud Computing and Virtual Data Centers in Information Technology. The course provides a complete introduction to cloud computing, virtualization, legacy hardware and software considerations and approaches to transitioning Information Technology from legacy systems to a shared managed service model, Various Cloud Computing models will be covered including Private, Public, Hybrid and Community Clouds. Management of Cloud Computing Providers using automated tools and Service Level Agreements will be discussed, along with security and privacy considerations. Students will learn how to effectively plan, implement and manage Cloud Computing in virtual data centers and complete introductory coursework in VMware Virtualization software.

title: IT Security Compliance/Forensics
description: This course examines the various laws related to information security and protection of data focusing on private and government sectors and contemporary policy issues related to forensics. Also covered in the course are information security operations that may be at issue in legal proceedings such as the identification, preservation, collection and analysis of digital evidence for legal purposes. Legal and ethical issues will be covered. Best practices and tips for collecting forensic data will be discussed.

title: Information Assurance & Risk Assessment
description: This is an overview of enterprise security, privacy and information security assessment and management, and cyber security. This course intends to concentrate on tangible and intangible costs and examines concepts of information assurance and security risk assessment security. This course will help students understand how to assess information security risks and develop solutions. Topics covered include social engineering, risk assessment, recovery and response, enterprise security, and formal techniques and policies.

title: Cryptography & Network Security
description: This fundamental course covers the theory of encryption and standard protocols for data communications and network security. Students will learn the goals of cryptography such as data privacy, authenticity and integrity. Topics that may be covered include PKI, digital signatures, message authentication codes, hash functions, etc. An examination of network security defenses and countermeasures are also covered.

title: Cryptography & Network Security
description: This fundamental course covers the theory of encryption and standard protocols for data communications and network security. Students will learn the goals of cryptography such as data privacy, authenticity and integrity. Topics that may be covered include PKI, digital signatures, message authentication codes, hash functions, etc. An examination of network security defenses and countermeasures are also covered.

title: Threats/Vulnerabilities
description: Focused on threats, vulnerabilities, patch management, incident response, and security operations to identify and protect against internal and external threats. Various security threats will be covered including hacker attacks, e-mail borne viruses, backdoor problems, and internal sabotage. Cybercrime, cyber security, and global information security will be discussed. Students will learn how to prevent and respond to such types of attacks using various assessment tools, intrusion detection systems, etc.

title: Cyber Threats & Vulnerabilities
description: Focused on threats, vulnerabilities, patch management, incident response, and security operations to identify and protect against internal and external threats. Various security threats will be covered including hacker attacks, e-mail borne viruses, backdoor problems, and internal sabotage. Cybercrime, cyber security, and global information security will be discussed. Students will learn how to prevent and respond to such types of attacks using various assessment tools, intrusion detection systems, etc.

title: Healthcare Standards & Policy
description: The importance of standards and the effect of varying standards in implementing and maintaining healthcare technologies will be covered in this course. This course will cover coding terminology and standards including voluntary standards from SDOs. This course will also cover logical operators for naming conventions, standards and the evolutions of standards. In addition, this course will study âmeaningful useâ standards, rules, specifications and what healthcare organizations need to do in order to meet these standards.

title: Healthcare Standards & Policy
description: The importance of standards and the effect of varying standards in implementing and maintaining healthcare technologies will be covered in this course. This course will cover coding terminology and standards including voluntary standards from SDOs. This course will also cover logical operators for naming conventions, standards and the evolutions of standards. In addition, this course will study âmeaningful useâ standards, rules, specifications and what healthcare organizations need to do in order to meet these standards.

title: Practical Clinical Informatics
description: Ever wonder what clinicians could do with the data from an insulin pump? From your exercise monitoring app? What about data from a radiological scan? In this course, we explore the many venues from which clinical data is collected, integrated and used to support patient care. The business models of the various clinical data vendors are also examined to understand the business incentives to share - or not to share - clinical data.

title: Cyber Security in Healthcare
description: In this course, we review the venues in which clinical data is collected, aggregated and used. We look at specific cybersecurity challenges for each venue and describe the attack surfaces and hacker incentives for each. Discussions of patient privacy tradeoffs, HIPAA and the incentives to aggregate data will show why this is a complicated issue that goes beyond simply worrying about user id's and passwords. Uses of aggregated data for both beneficial purposes and for cybercrime will be presented.

title: Systems Requirements & Analysis
description: Gives you a strong understanding of the system development life cycle (SDLC). You will learn methods of gathering, analyzing, and prioritizing business requirements with a focus on the ease of use for IT staff and end-users alike. The course covers many of the specifics of gap analysis through correctness and completeness methods, the use of case models, process and data modeling, database design principles, and more. In addition, the course covers SDLC models, Unified Process and CASE tools, Rapid Application Development methodologies, Package Evaluation and Selection, and more. Finally, the course will be well-grounded in practice through the coverage of relevant requirements standards (IEEE Standard 830) and current government practices for requirements management (i.e., System Requirements Specifications (SRS).

title: Systems Requirements & Analysis
description: Gives you a strong understanding of the system development life cycle (SDLC). You will learn methods of gathering, analyzing, and prioritizing business requirements with a focus on the ease of use for IT staff and end-users alike. The course covers many of the specifics of gap analysis through correctness and completeness methods, the use of case models, process and data modeling, database design principles, and more. title: Financial Analysis for Managers
description: Provides theories and practical techniques related to acquiring, accounting for, and allocating an organizationâs financial assets. It analyzes basic business problems that managers face as they make technology decisions for their organizations. Some of the topics include return on investment, theories of portfolio management, financial statement and discounted cash flow analysis, interest rate determination, capital budgeting methodologies, concepts of risk and return, asset pricing, and valuation models. You will apply the knowledge you gain to issues you face within your work environment and to real-world examples through business cases and case studies.

title: Financial Analysis for Managers
description: Provides theories and practical techniques related to acquiring, accounting for, and allocating an organizationâs financial assets. It analyzes basic business problems that managers face as they make technology decisions for their organizations. Some of the topics include return on investment, theories of portfolio management, financial statement and discounted cash flow analysis, interest rate determination, capital budgeting methodologies, concepts of risk and return, asset pricing, and valuation models. You will apply the knowledge you gain to issues you face within your work environment and to real-world examples through business cases and case studies.

title: Capstone
description: The Capstone Course is the culmination of the student's academic and professional experience in the Technology Management program. Over the course of the semester, students will be asked to apply the knowledge gained during the program to a project.
