With a focus on technology for social good, students will incorporate the skills necessary for analyzing key issues, thinking creatively, and making sound decisions in order to develop and execute plans. Projects will address global and local challenges. During the semester in which students are enrolled in the Capstone Course, it is strongly recommended that they only take one additional course along with it. Students must earn a grade of B or better in the Capstone Course in order to graduate.

title: Capstone
description: The Capstone Course is the culmination of the student's academic and professional experience in the Technology Management program. Over the course of the semester, students will be asked to apply the knowledge gained during the program to a project. With a focus on technology for social good, students will incorporate the skills necessary for analyzing key issues, thinking creatively, and making sound decisions in order to develop and execute plans. Projects will address global and local challenges. During the semester in which students are enrolled in the Capstone Course, it is strongly recommended that they only take one additional course along with it. Students must earn a grade of B or better in the Capstone Course in order to graduate.

title: Ethics and Planning Practice
description: The core course explores the values and decisions related to the ethical practice of urban planning in a democratic society. Students investigate the ethical decisions as well as the professional issues they may encounter as a planner. The course reviews the theories of urban planning and community and the environmental and social issues involved in planning. The plannerâs role in regard to the ethical treatment of all members of a community, as well as issues of social justice, diversity, economic equity, resource allocation, environmental health, environmental sustainability and public process are also included in course content.

title: Urban Planning History and Theory
description: The core course examines the history of the planning profession and how the plannerâs role has evolved over time. An overview of major planning theories and rationales such as equity, sustainability, community and community inclusion are discussed. The physical design of cities, the impact of urbanization and cityâs response to technological advances are examined. Students analyze several present day scenarios facing urban communities and gain perspective through knowledge of planning theories and strategies taught throughout the course.

title: Methods in Urban Planning Research and Analysis
description: The core course prepares students to analyze quantitative data and mapping used in the practice of urban and regional planning. Students become familiar with the sources of data and their relevance to making urban planning decisions. Students use publicly available data sets and data obtained from communities in the Washington region as a basis of course inquiry. Several data sources are examined, including GIS (Geographic Information Systems), census data, community data, and prior plans. Through readings, lectures and lab sessions, students gain knowledge of the skills and tools that accompany the practice of urban planning. Students are introduced to graphic delineation and mapping and the presentation methods used in planning studies. Note: this course is not an in-depth GIS course, which is offered as a specialization course.

title: Economics for Planners
description: This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Among topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. The core course introduces concepts and techniques of economics that pertain to urban planners. The governmentâs role in the economics of cities and in development, national welfare and housing policies, public and private finance, and the cost-benefits associated with development are addressed. The course addresses state and regional economic policies that affect cities and regional economies, as well as finance, investment, fiscal resources and redevelopment initiatives. Real estate development case studies from a variety of innovative local projects in the Washington region are included in the course content. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speaker. Problem sets, case studies, tests, and class participation are included in grading.

title: Legal Foundations of Planning
description: Students in this core course study landmark land use law cases that built the legal foundations ofthe urban planning profession. Students examine land use law, and the law as a method by which the form and quality of neighborhoods and communities is changed. They also evaluate the various legal issues that arise in the course of the planning, policy making, and regulation process impacting development and resource utilization. The powers and constraints of zoning law are examined, as well as environmental and housing policies and regulations that govern architecture and neighborhood form.

title: Housing Policy in the U.S.
description: This course will review urban policies targeting the creation of subsidized housing in the last 50 years and explore contemporary trends and challenges related to the housing crisis in American cities. Readings and case studies will be supplemented with field visits and guest lectures. The course will include an in-depth research project allowing students to document affordable housing issues in a community context.

title: Contemporary Issues
description: This seminar is delivered in concert with the Urban and Regional Planning Public Speaker Series and will afford students the opportunity to directly engage leading professionals who are reinventing and remaking the contemporary practice of urban planning. The underlying theme of the seminar will explore new frameworks of professional practice which engage the following core issues: Who is providing the leadership necessary to overcome systemic issues in contemporary cities? What boundaries or barriers are preventing the formation of social, economic and environmental capital and how are these barriers being dismantled? How are new channels of stakeholder engagement a fundamental component of building the political capital necessary to advance community development or catalyze strategic investments?

title: Transit-Oriented Development
description: Transit Oriented Development (TOD) â Gain insiderâs knowledge on this emerging concept, which is part real estate product and part public policy. Learn about the theory and practice of TOD, one of the few types of development that has strong support in the environmental community, and responds to an emerging demographic market. Students will develop a thorough understanding of the essentials needed by transit agencies and local officials, the principles for developing successful projects, and the basics of transit, traffic, and parking issues. A range of guest lecturers will add unique perspectives of practitioners - transit officials, environmentalists, planners, and developers, as well as in depth knowledge of market demand, niche housing, and financing. Key distinctions between urban and suburban projects will be clarified. Examples of some of the better projects, and some of the lesson learned, will be drawn from US and Washington area developments.

title: Urban Planning Lab
description: This course is an introduction into the elements and practices of community planning by directly engaging external place-based organizations. Core objectives of the class are to engage in urban analysis and derivation of urban planning strategy. Examples of successful urban analysis, planning initiatives and community design strategies will be introduced as case studies of professional practice. Coursework will emphasize exploring methods of documentation, interdisciplinary analysis and effective communication of planning concepts. A final project presentation will be made to a group of outside professionals.

title: Globalization of Intersocietal Relations
description: The purpose of this course is to track and understand the evolution of the political, economic, social, and cultural relations of the world's people. The course will examine the establishment of national states in Europe and its subsequent global expansion, and the impact of that expansion on peoples with different political and social traditions. It will also include the impact of the world wars and twentieth-century ideological conflicts on inter- and intra-societal relations; the post-1945 transformation of Latin American, African, and Asian societies and their relations to the international system; the growing influence of a dynamic and pervasive global economy; the expanding roles of regional and transnational organizations and actors; and the rise transnational actors and problems. Extrapolating from past and current trends, the course will conclude with some speculation on the future of international and intersocietal relations.(Not required for MSFS/MA in History majors).

title: International Relations: Theory and Practice
description: The first part of the course offers a brief historical background to the theory of international relations. Part II examines in detail mainstream theoretical arguments (i.e., structural, unit-level, cognitive and world society) about the nature of international relations and seeks to demonstrate the utility of theory for practitioners. The third part of the course applies these theories to a variety of important issues such as nationalism, ethics, environmental change, security, North-South relations, and trade and development. The course concludes with a discussion of several scenarios of the future that emphasize different trends in international relations.The main objectives of the course are to provide students with a useful set of analytical tools, an appreciation of the complexity of the field and of the value of different approaches to it, and an understanding of some of the major issues facing contemporary policymakers. In addition, the course offers students an opportunity to refine presentation and discussion skills.

title: Development Orthodoxies
description: âInternational Developmentâ as a field of academic inquiry and field operations is both relatively recent and constantly changing. Successive theories have been promoted vigorously, and then discredited as incomplete, ineffective or even pernicious. The âbest practicesâ associated with each of these theories have been first widely publicized and then gradually abandoned as outdated and flawed. This course will examine the most significant theories (or âorthodoxiesâ) of international development over the last 50 years. These include classical development theories (such as Rostowian âtake-offâ and modernization theories), critiques from the left (such as dependency theory), human capital and basic human needs approaches, neoclassical theories (such as the Washington Consensus) and more recent theories of social capital and institutional reform. For each of these orthodoxies, the course will explore how development theory has shaped development practice. It will systematically focus on four distinct aspects or questions: â¢ key dimensions or definitions of development (that is, âwhat is development?â); â¢ theories of social change underlying these definitions (that is, âwhat drives development?â); â¢ critical assumptions of these theories (that is, âwhat are the limitations of this perspective of development?â); â¢ practical implications of these theories, including the bureaucratic structures, professional vocabularies, programmatic approaches, operational tools and instruments to manage international development (that is, âhow can we shape and manage development?â). Students will be expected to participate actively in class seminars, contributing one Issues Paper to guide seminar discussions during the term. Additional written assignments (including a country development assessment paper and take-home exam) will also be required.

title: Development Economics: Theory, Evidence, & Policy
description: The principal objective of this course is to familiarize students with the basic tools and theoretical concepts that development economists most frequently use both to analyze and understand the leading economic development issues and to give relevant policy advice to decision makers. Related empirical evidence is also examined. Students are expected to attain a level of proficiency in development economics whereby they can hold their own and feel comfortable discussing economic development issues, particularly policy-related issues, with development economists and economic policymakers. The course starts with the basics: the meaning and measurement of development, including how to access and use important databases, the relationship between economic growth and development, and the overall growth and development experience of countries and regions in the developing world. In addition, the course offers a general introduction to multivariate regression analysis through a workshop with hands-on computer lab experience, aimed at giving students access to the vast empirical literature that now uses these methods. The course then turns to the major subject areas, including: (i) theories of economic growth and their relevance for developing countries; (ii) growth accounting or growth empirics; (iii) the underlying long term determinants of economic growthâinitial conditions, economic policies, institutions; human capital, exogenous factors; (iv) theories of structural change, including the policies, institutions and conditions at both local and national levels that enable countries to progress from rural agrarian societies to developed industrial economies; (v) poverty and inequality and the linkages between them and economic growth and development; (vi) policymaking, the role and functions of the state, public goods and externalities and macroeconomic management including the role of central banks and exchange rate regimes; (vii) foreign trade, finance and investment in the developing world; and (viii) the role and effectiveness of foreign aid and debt relief. The course ends with an overview of the current state and frontiers of development economics. The format of the classes is a mix of lectures and seminar-style discussions. Students are encouraged and expected to actively participate.

title: Public Private Partnerships
description: TBA

title: Foundations of Corporate Finance
description: The course objective is to provide a foundation in the fundamentals of financial accounting, analysis and decision making and to gain practical experience in applying analytical tools to practical situations. Considerable preparation and classroom time will be devoted to exercises that will challenge the studentâs grasp of what is being learned. A two-session Excel workshop will integrate accounting and financial analysis precepts with basic instruction on spreadsheet modeling and analysis. By the end of the program, students should have developed competencies in the following area, with specific skill sets in the areas highlighted: â¢Understand the principles of accrual accounting and how to critically evaluate the financial statements of an enterprise to make an informed assessment of the viability and strength of that enterprise. â¢Understand the capital budgeting process, how to model the financial performance of an enterprise in Excel (or comparable) spreadsheet format, how to apply the analytical tools and decision criteria that will be introduced throughout the course to analysis of specific investment situations. â¢Understand how different forms of risk impact enterprise and securities valuation, and corporate decision making. Understand basic decision theory and risk assessment and mitigation strategies. â¢Understand the fundamentals of stock and bond valuations and principles of long term financing. Understand options for raising debt and equity on capital markets and the securities issuance process.

title: Investment in Emerging Markets
description: This course will offer a unique investors point of view of Emerging Markets emphasizing different characteristics of frontier, emerging and developed country indices. Analyzing asset allocation decisions within Emerging Market countries will be a key theme of this course. Economic theory and accompanying evidence will be extensively employed to identify and explain sources of relative returns between Emerging Market countries. Relative performance trends within a country, at sector or individual stock level, will also be discussed in detail. Investment vehicles available to todayâs global investors will be presented with real live examples including ETFs, Closed End Funds, ADRs or investments in locally listed stocks/bond and OTC derivative instruments. Limits to market efficiency, restrictive or unpredictable regulatory frameworks, transaction costs and ownership limitations still create headwinds to investors in emerging markets, but improvements in fiscal & monetary policies and relative political stability offer unique investment opportunities for the next decade or the next century for some strong believers of Emerging Markets story. While keeping the main focus on rationale of portfolio investments in emerging markets, the course will also cover theoretical foundations of economic growth, business cycles, and economic policy in Emerging Market economies. Recommended to: Public officers covering international markets (Treasury, FED, State Department etc..), Consultants, Country Analysts, Portfolio Managers, Traders, Asset Allocators and to anyone who is interested in international finance with Emerging Markets focus. Course Organization Each participant of the course will be assigned to one emerging market country as a Country Analyst. Country Analysts are responsible to closely follow macroeconomic, political and major market driven events of the country they are covering. Active participation in information sharing between country analysts via group e-mails is required during the course of this class. At the end of the semester, analysts are expected to be able to explain sources of relative performance of the country they covered and also show ability to provide actionable investment ideas utilizing theoretical and practical tools they acquired during the semester.

title: Public Finance
description: TBA

title: Chinaâs Energy Challenges:  Technology, Policy and Sustainability
description: In this course we will examine Chinaâs key energy challengesâchallenges for security, for sustainability, and for innovating and adopting new energy technologies. Given global concern about the impact of Chinaâs rapidly increasing energy needs on global supplies and its ability almost singlehandedly to change the global climate system, this course will examine Chinaâs options for energy supply and use, and the economic, environmental, and political ramifications of Chinaâs energy choices at home and abroad. Beginning with the premise that an understanding of Chinaâs energy system is crucial to understanding its economic and environmental limitations, the first section of the course examines Chinaâs energy industry in detail. We will explore how Chinaâs modern political and economic situation has shaped its energy pathway, and the importance of energy in fueling Chinaâs rapid economic development. The middle section turns to the examination of impact of Chinaâs energy use on Chinaâs natural environment, the current energy technology and policy options available to reduce this impact, and the strategies the government is promoting to develop new clean energy industries for use at home and abroad. The final section shifts to looking at China in a global context, examining the internationalization of Chinaâs policy-making with respect to the environment and particularly Chinaâs crucial role in the international climate change negotiations.

title: International Business Government Relations in the Global Economy
description: The purpose of the course is to give students an understanding of how and why governments and businesses interact across a wide spectrum of situations and issues. The course will examine how the structures, environments, interests, agendas and constituencies of businesses and governments shape their relationships and drive outcomes. Part I of the course will examine the transactions that form the core of business-government interaction, not only as they tend to occur in the U.S., but in other countries as well. This includes taxes, employer-based healthcare, so-called âbailouts,â regulation, antitrust, and judicial/legal action, among others. Students will help choose what other types of interactions are studied, and these could range from import/export regimes to intellectual property to cybersecurity. Part II of the course will examine the nuances of business-government interactions in a range of contexts: how U.S. businesses relate to the U.S. government; how international companies relate to the U.S. government; how U.S. businesses relate to foreign governments; and how multinational corporations relate to multi-lateral institutions. Part II will also examine special scenarios such as when business and government are the same (state owned enterprises). This course will rely heavily on the case method and will make use of high level government and business guest speakers who have direct experience with the issues and frameworks discussed. The course will allow students to hone critical skills such as issue management, impact analysis, business-government systems analysis, situational and policy analysis, advocacy and concise oral and written argumentation. Select cases will feature situations that challenge students to consider the various ethical dimensions of business-government relations. The format of the course will be a combination of case discussions, topical discussions, and lectures, as well as guest speakers. Students will be evaluated based on a take home mid-term exam, a take-home final exam, an issue brief/presentation that each student will prepare on a different topic, and class participation.

title: International Business Government Relations in the Global Economy
description: The purpose of this course is to give students a firm grasp of how and why governments and businesses interact the way they do across a wide spectrum of situations and issues, and to impart skills students will need to operate in a business-government relations milieu. In addition to looking at the interaction between business and government per se, we will examine how the structures, environments, interests, agendas, and constituencies of both parties shape the interactive relationship, and drive issue and policy outcomes. The course will start with an analytic, organizing framework. We will look at governments and examine how their logics, environments, interests and agendas shape their policy responses to issues involving businesses. We will then examine businesses and how their logics, environments, interests and agendas shape their responses to issues involving governments. We will then leverage the frame developed to provide insights into business-government relations across a range of issues, actors, and geographies, in such spheres as environmental policy, intellectual property, developing country debt, national competitiveness, privatization, labor migration, and government regulation. As we move thru the course the skills of advocacy, issue analysis, policy analysis, impact analysis and concise oral and written communication will be introduced and reinforced.

title: International Mediation: Strategy and Methods
description: This seminar explores the role of mediation as an instrument of conflict management and a foreign policy technique. Students will consult both theoretical and case study materials, and become adept at analyzing the suitability of diverse mediatory approaches and actors to concrete conflict situations at diverse stages of the conflict life cycle, using a five-phase model of mediation tradecraft. Adopting the mediatorâs perspective, they will address the challenge of how to design and conduct mediation as a form of third party intervention in violent international conflict. The course will identify and explore strategies and tactics used at different points to overcome obstacles to a mediated settlement in intractable conflicts. Mediation analysis exercises will be used to illustrate the perspectives of mediators and conflict parties, and to introduce the phased model of mediation strategy.

title: Analytical and Statistical Skills
description: This course is designed as an introduction to the use of quantitative methods for graduate students. The progression of this course includes the theory and rubric of scientific research designs and the practice in generating, interpreting, and ultimately employing quantitative methods to critically assess empirical works as well as informing oneâs own research questions. The goals of this course are to develop statistical literacy coupled with analytical and research abilities. We will cover topics such as descriptive statistics, including measures of central tendency, dispersion, and association; inferential statistics, including probability theory, Normal distributions, and statistical significance; and OLS regression, including the use of dummy variables, interactions, factor analysis, and extensions to regression for categorical and limited dependent variables. The course is designed to enable students to critically assess and contribute to a broad range of quantitative work in the policy setting (e.g. risk assessment, policy analysis), applied fields (e.g. development, political organization), and applied theoretical work in international affairs (e.g. academic and professional works such as Foreign Affairs). The course will include weekly exercises in the discussed analytical techniques (including independent lab work) and three policy papers that include presentations. It will include a final exam and a participation evaluation.

title: Global Powers, Institutions, and Governance
description: Todayâs world faces global problems requiring global solutions. And yet the institutional architecture that exists to deal with these issues is largely outdated, reflecting the post-WWII era rather than present day realities. The rise of new powers, growing cracks in the Westphalian order, and the emergence of non-state actors have made crafting international responses exceedingly complicated. The result has been a shift away from formal global institutions toward soft structures, like the G-20, and to regional approaches. This course examines the struggle to develop global governance in the midst of a rebalancing world with a focus on the actors driving the global order, gaps in existing institutional structures and emerging responses. The course is taught from a practitionerâs perspective with the instructor drawing on her professional background, including as Foreign Minister of Spain and Legal Counsel of the World Bank Group. Given the practical approach of the course, students are expected to write 3 short (5-7 page) policy memos and to participate in a simulated exercise.

title: Quantitative Analysis in Decision Making
description: Quantitative Analysis in Decision Making is an introduction to modeling, analyzing and solving situational decision problems under risk. Decision making for planning, policy and resource allocation relies increasingly on quantitative analysis. Through use of the Case Study Method to develop good reasoning and modeling skills, students will be able to analyze, understand and solve problems and begin to develop professional insight into a variety of situations that arise in todayâs social, business and public-sector settings. The class will enable the student to support policy positions and recommendations with meaningful analysis, and will include the study of Decision Trees, Cognitive Decision Theory, Risk Identification and Mitigation, Monte Carlo Simulations, Ethical Decision Making, Big Data Analysis and Visualization, and Graphical Representation of Data. Microsoft Excel will be used to model and solve many of these problems. Class meetings will include extensive use of outside reading materials (no textbook is envisioned), group projects (during class meetings and outside class) as well as guest speakers. One or two classes will be in involved field visits to observe the ways businesses and government entities use data analytics to solve problems.

title: The UN & Emerging Institutions
description: This course is the MSFS Programâs foundational class on Global Institutions and Partnerships. Its premise is that the UN and multilateral institutions are animated by a particular form of âpolitics,â in which learning how âthe gameâ is played will benefit future professionals in international organizations themselves, governments, nonprofits, and business. The course begins with the primary locus of multilateral institutions â the United Nations â as a system any professional in international affairs should grasp. It addresses the role and reform of the UN Security Council and UN General Assembly; the politics of development, human rights and the Responsibility to Protect against atrocities, and of reform in the UN; and the UN's major agencies and programs, from UNDP to the WHO to UNICEF. It examines the impact of equal legitimacy for all governments, of blocs and caucuses in the UN, and of funding arrangements. The last 40 percent of the course addresses other institutions of global governance which are crucial partners and alternatives to the UN. Building critical thinking about the comparative advantages of various arrangements to solve particular problems, the course will treat emerging forums (e.g., the G-20), other economic, functional, and regional institutions (from the IMF and World Bank to the Arab League and OSCE), as well as hybrid and public-private partnerships (such as the UN-African Union peacekeeping operations or the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria). The course will intensively hone oral presentation and writing skills useful in professional settings. It will build familiarity with fundamental topics and concepts in global governance (treated in a take-home final exam), but allows focus in areas of student interest in UN and multilateral affairs, especially through short papers.

title: Trade Negotiations & Commercial Diplomacy
description: Trade Negotiations and Commercial Diplomacy (TNCD) builds practical analytical and negotiation skills for diplomacy that is aimed at influencing domestic and foreign government policy and at regulatory decisions affecting global trade and investment. Commercial diplomacy is the art of building consensus among domestic and international stakeholders when developing national policies that affect trade and when negotiating and implementing international trade agreements. Trade negotiations are no longer just about tariffs and quotas. Todayâs interdependent world demands skills and knowledge over a wide range of governmental activity affecting international commerce âin areas such as health, safety, and environment standards; consumer protection; competition policy; bribery and corruption laws: agricultural support programs; industrial subsidies; and regulations covering services such as banking, telecommunications and accounting. TNCD focuses on the analysis, advocacy, and negotiating in a public policy arena. These skills are built in the context of international agreements on investment and trade -related issues: in-depth analysis of the factors that bear on the policy process at home and abroad, political advocacy tools and techniques to assure support for desired outcomes, and the skills needed to complete successful negotiations.

title: Practical Evaluation
description: For each of the elements listed below, we will introduce the main concepts, discuss tools, and then engage the students in applying the tools through a âliveâ project. 1.What is evaluation? Vocabulary of evaluation; Challenges of evaluation 2.Evaluation design: quantitative and qualitative approaches 3.Evaluation tools and methods 4.Practical data collection procedures 5.Practical data analysis 6.Engaging stakeholders in the design and implementation 7.Planning and managing evaluation for maximum effectiveness 8.Budgeting the evaluation 9.Assessing the feasibility and usefulness of evaluation 10.Communication of findings By the end of this course students will be conversant in evaluation methodology and procedures, become discerning users of existing data, differentiate between types of methods and tools for practical evaluation, be able to discuss why evaluation techniques are appropriate to a country setting, apply the appropriate techniques to various projects, and be able to plan and carry out an evaluation of a project of their choice.

title: Economic Dimensions of International Security
description: This class will help international affairs professionals better understand and work within a traditional gap between international security and economic affairs, focusing on economic constraints and opportunities affecting security. While security and economics too often exist in separate worlds or are compartmentalizedâin both academia and governmentâwe will focus on their intersections, constitutive effects, and costs and benefits that economics has on international security. We will draw upon the alternative schools of thought to analyze recent historical and contemporary global cases, examining the motivations, ends, and means of international actors as well as the strategic environment shaping their choices. Designed primarily for those interested in economics but working in political or security fields, this course will also directly address tools and lessons for policymakers and practitioners. The course has four parts. First, we will scrutinize economic causes of conflict and opportunities for cooperation for states and societies, including globalization, resources, and tensions stemming from greed or grievance. Second, we will look at âendsâ: economic goals pursued through security policies of great powers and developing states, including from a regional perspective. Third, we will turn to economic âmeansâ of security policy, assessing the political and moral costs and benefits of aid, sanctions, and export controls, as well as âguns versus butterâ fiscal constraints on foreign policy. Finally, we will address economic players impacting international security, including international and non-government organizations, multinational corporations, contractors, and shadow market actors.

title: Communist China
description: This course covers the history of Communism in China, from the establishment of the Chinese Communist Party in 1921, until the present. It treats the major events in China during this period, including both domestic and foreign affairs, and draws on the major disciplines of social science, including politics, economics, and anthropology, as well as the humanities, history and literature. The course will be conducted in a seminar format, with required readings, discussions, and papers. The course is designed for Masterâs students in the School of Foreign Service and requires no prior knowledge of China or Chinese.

title: Business, Human Rights, & the Internet
description: This one semester course will look at the subject of business and human rights from a practitionerâs perspective and will focus on the information, communications and technology industry. Although we will view issues through the lens of the ICT sector, the goal is for students to draw practical lessons to apply to other global industries. We will cover the history of business and human rights, including a review of the relevant legal and normative frameworks. Weâll then turn to the ICT sector, focusing on relevant services and products and the potential intersection points between those offerings and freedom of expression and privacy, including through the tools of censorship and surveillance. Weâll also take an in-depth look at specific steps companies can take to build capacity to make responsible decisions on human rights issues, including through establishing human rights programs, conducting human rights impacts assessments and considering voluntary multi-stakeholder initiatives with credible accountability mechanisms. Although weâll discuss current business and human rights topics throughout the course â including the National Security Agency PRISM program â weâll reserve the final class session for a in-depth student-led discussion of emerging trends in business and human rights in the ICT sector, applying practical lessons learned during the semester. Class Format and Requirements This class is designed for students who may be involved in this field in the future as leaders in companies, civil society, academia, government or the investment community. Though designed as a seminar, the course will emphasize collaborative learning and realworld simulations. Weâll dedicate a full class to a case study where students will be assigned different corporate roles and will collaborate to make policy decisions based on a hypothetical business and human rights challenge introduced by the instructor. We will also hold a mock Congressional hearing on free expression and privacy where students will work in teams to prepare draft testimony for different stakeholders and then answer questions as if at a hearing. After both simulations, we will hold a debriefing and evaluate the learning and results of the exercise. Students will also complete three short writing assignments, preparing: a memo to a company executive; a short form Human Rights Impact Assessment; and, draft Congressional testimony. We expect to make liberal use of guest speakers across multiple disciplines.

title: Big Data & Global Affairs
description: This course will introduce and survey the entire realm of using âbig dataâ approaches to understand human society, applying a non-technical approach that emphasizes the methodologies, tools, thought processes, and mindsets behind these approaches, and how they differ from traditional small-data human-centered study of society and its application to global policy-making. Each week will focus on key methodologies, case studies, and real-world examples, but the high-level focus means students do not need any technical or statistical background for the course. While a focus will be on how to leverage big data for global policy-making, the core approaches and mindsets taught in the class are applicable to nearly every field and will help students in their future careers understand the nuances of the analytic products they consume, how to better leverage such analyses in their work, and how to understand the thought processes behind them to partner or communicate more closely with analysts. In essence, this class will teach the failures and showcases, pitfalls and promise, challenges and opportunities of the big data revolution and laying out a roadmap of how these methodologies and data streams can help inform and shape policy, diplomacy, and our global society.

title: Petroleum in the Global Economy
description: This course aims to present students with an introduction to the key issues and challenges for business leaders in the petroleum industry - and those who seek to regulate or interact with them. The oil and gas industry is arguably the most genuinely global in operation today. Companies in the industry span the full range from ânational championâ state owned companies and the largest multinationals to tiny independents. Its proponents argue its products are essential to modern life; yet companies in the industry are also often controversial and, at times, despised. The course will deal with major policy questions such as pricing, resource exploitation in less developed countries, climate change and energy security. It will approach these issues through the lens of the business participantsâ concerns and priorities. It does so via an in depth understanding of the business fundamentals of the industry and industry approaches to investment, portfolio design and risk management, as well as the external environment in which it operates. The first half of this program will explore the context of the industry, the business approach of those involved, and how it is grappling with the changes brought about by globalization, new technologies, environmental regulation and a volatile commercial environment. The second half will explore some major issues of geopolitics, environment and security as they impact the industry â and vice versa. The course is geared toward non-technical specialists, i.e., students are not required to have substantial knowledge of engineering, chemistry, economics, finance, etc. although aspects of these disciplines will be woven into the work program. However, understanding, developing and interpreting (relatively simple) financial models â and then deciding the best way to present the conclusions - is a key success factor for those interested in business related fields, and is a major learning objective of the course. At the end of this course students should understand how best to present usable advice to senior decision-makers and leaders in business and government on a variety of economic, policy and strategic questions related to the industry. They will have gained an understanding of decision-making processes and factors for major players in the industry and how they impact on (and are impacted by) the external environment.

title: Petroleum in the Global Economy
description: This course will introduce students to the basics of the international petroleum industry and its role of the international economy. The first part of the course will establish the basics and background of the industry. Students will be introduced to the economics of petroleum exploration and production, refining, marketing and use, including natural gas and alternatives forms of energy, and to the basic business models of the international petroleum industry. Drawing on this knowledge base, the second part of the course will examine the history of the petroleum industry, the global petroleum resource base, the structure and dynamics of the international petroleum market, the impact of petroleum on the environment and an overview of energy policies.

title: Emerging Issues in African Development
description: The course will survey the development of the Sub-Saharan African economies since independence. The focus will be on economic development, but aims to place the subject in its broader political, social and cultural context. The approach will be historical: starting with a brief review of the colonial inheritance, then covering the largely optimistic period to the early 1970s, moving on to the problems and stagnation of the 1980s, followed by the slow recovery of the 1990s which has accelerated in the first half of this decade. The survey will conclude by looking at where Africa is today and the prospects going forward. The course will explore key issues and themes that marked each period and try to piece together an overall story concerning where African economies have been and where they are going.

title: Cybersecurity: Implications for Business, Government & the International Community
description: Due to the evolving nature and increasingly high costs associated with sophisticated cyber-attacks, it is necessary for private sector businesses and government organizations to establish formal planning processes for preventing, mitigating, and responding to cyber-attacks. The threat of computer network attacks range from disruption of operations, theft of intellectual property, and physical damage to critical infrastructures resulting in major economic losses. It is increasingly important for students of international affairs to understand both the policy implications and technical aspects of cybersecurity. This class will take a comprehensive approach to understanding cybersecurity. The first phase of the course will concentrate on giving students a technical understanding of the methods attackers use to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to computer systems and electronic communications networks. The second phase of the course will focus on the impact of cyber attacks, the key actors and stakeholders of cyber threats, and the policies, laws and frameworks created by businesses, governments and the international community to mitigate and manage the damage caused by such attacks. The third and final phase of the course will give students hands-on exposure to professionals in the field tasked with managing cyber threats for government agencies and corporations where the stakes are highest. Students will do two or more case studies examining well-known instances of large-scale cyber attacks, visit a cyber command center for a major corporation, and will participate in a cybersecurity tabletop exercise.

title: Green Markets & Global Change
description: âGreenâ is now a modifier applied to a wide range of products, services, processes, and more recently, entire markets. A Green Market is one in which both consumers and producers derive benefits from a more eco-efficient utilization of natural resources and the global commons. In a world of increasing resource scarcity, widespread pollution, climate change and growing pressure on biodiversity, Green Markets can reassure consumers concerned about environmental impacts, while harnessing the innovation, efficiency gains and capital that private initiative can deliver. In the MSFS course, Green Markets and Global Change, we will critically inquire into the emergence of Green Markets, examining constituent concepts, taxonomies and practices. These include sustainability and sustainable development, natural capital, eco-efficiency, corporate social responsibility, shared value, triple bottom line accounting, among a host of related ideas. The course will assesses the role of policy and regulatory frameworks in shaping and defining the scope of Green Markets, and will examine the potential of green business models, new methods of valuing natural resources in production, and the growth of environmental products and services, among other areas. The class will ask when âgoing greenâ or âembracing sustainabilityâ makes business and environmental sense, and when such claims are primarily a form of marketing (also known as âgreen washingâ or âsustainababbleâ). Green Markets now play a role in state, development and multilateral strategies aimed at improving the stewardship of natural resources and the global commons. In addition, large non-state actors are embracing eco-efficiency as part of their growth strategies, driving greener practices across vast supply chains and influencing host countries and local communities. Green Markets can also contribute to economic and social development and help to reduce the causes of instability arising from threats to food, energy, and fresh water security. Market mechanisms are also being used to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, to foster ecosystem services and preserve biodiversity, and to spread the use of clean technologies and prevent deforestation. Massive urbanization across the developing world drives additional demand for Green Market solutions as part of the âsustainable citiesâ movement, by harnessing private initiative to increase energy efficiency in transportation, public infrastructure, and housing and other areas. This course will also focus on practical skills, real world applications and emerging opportunities related to Green Markets. We will examine relevant cases from some of the worldâs most successful firms, the emergence of clean and renewable technologies and related businesses, new developments in energy efficiency, and business opportunities arising from the systemic threat of global climate change, among other growth areas.

title: Public Diplomacy
description: Public diplomacy is an instrument used by states and non-state actors to understand others' cultures, attitudes, and behavior; build and manage relationships; and influence thoughts and actions to advance their interests and values. Drawing on the experiences of diplomats and a growing academic literature, this seminar-style course will how public diplomacy's changing actors, techniques and practice affects the issues, methods, and mediated environments of diplomacy in the 21st century. Students will build understanding of and skills in: -- public diplomacy concepts, institutions, processes, and tools; -- competing institutional mechanisms for advancing public diplomacy; -- how the Internet, social media, globalization, and non-state actors are shaping public diplomacy; -- evaluating public diplomacy strategies of countries; -- current challenges to public diplomacy, critically evaluating proposed reforms; -- drafting a public speech for senior officials suited to a chosen audience; -- evaluation actors' social media presence; and -- forming public diplomacy strategies' Graded exercises will include: a class presentation on the public diplomacy efforts of another country; a written critique of an organizationâs or individualâs use of social media, with recommendations for improvement; writing a policy speech for an Ambassador or other senior official to a designated target audience; and the development of a public diplomacy strategy, from mission statement to tactics.

title: Science, Technology & Global Politics
description: This seminar will expose students to some key contemporary international policy issues related to science, technology, environment and health while providing an overview of how technical information contributed to different policy developments and international structures. A focus of the seminar will be better understanding of and appreciation for the roles governments, international organizations, and the private sector in complex science-based issues, and how different governance structures developed to address the needs and involvement of these different stakeholders. The course will also look at emerging technical issues around health, energy and environment that have the potential to disrupt international and global politics while investigating the way such issues affect the public and private sectors. By the conclusion of this seminar, students will build the skills and competence to take advantage of the new paradigms of global governance of science and technology related issues, while learning to negotiate the array of complex technical issues involving numerous stakeholders that they will likely confront in careers in government, multilateral institutions, the private sector or NGOS. Grading for the course will be based on class participation (25%), developing and presenting a briefing paper on an issue at the interface of science technology and international relations (35%), a discussion of a press clipping (15%) and a group project to develop a science and foreign policy initiative (25%).

title: Crisis for Syria and Its Neighbors
description: Sustained violence in Syria has spillover effects for each of the countries surrounding it. These effects include an outflow of Syrian refugees, creating one of the largest humanitarian emergencies in the modern period. Flows of radical fighters into and out of Syria have sparked security concerns while the regimeâs fluctuating control of territory sparks fears that the violence within Syria could seep beyond its borders, calling into question a longstanding conflict-management approach that includes such UN PKOs as UNTSO, UNDOF, and perhaps UNIFIL. The sectarian dimension of the conflict within Syria has reverberated through the region, raising fears across the confessional spectrum. Economic effects of the crisis include disruption of traditional trade patterns, the displacement of Syrian industry and services, and the development of weapons supply lines. For each of Syriaâs neighbors, the conflict has resulted in a unique set of factors that test regional relationships and have strong implications for stability. This seminar will examine the spillover effects in thematic (humanitarian, security, sectarian/social, economic) terms as well as through a country-by-country (Lebanon, Turkey, Iraq, Jordan, Israel) review. In addition to analyzing the ramifications of the conflict for the region, the course will also identify how U.S. national and regional interests are at stake as a result of prolonged conflict within Syria. The seminar will offer students opportunities to develop public speaking and negotiating skills through role plays, presentations and debate. Short written assignments will focus on the art of writing for senior decision-makers. The instructor is a career U.S. Foreign Service Officer who has just completed an assignment as the U.S. Ambassador to Lebanon in addition to other assignments in Damascus, Amman, Jerusalem and elsewhere.

title: Preventing Deadly Conflict
description: Contrary to widespread public perceptions, the level of violent conflict in the world has declined markedly over the last sixty years. This has not been a spontaneous process, however, but rather the result of deliberate efforts by various international actors and in particular the United States. The prevalence of violent conflict in the world today is nevertheless still considerable with costly consequences to all affected. The desirability of averting such conflict on moral and strategic grounds remains high therefore. It is also conceivable, moreover, that the risk of violent conflict of various kinds could increase in the 21st century. The purpose of the course is to provide students with a theoretical grounding in the prevention of deadly conflict and offer policy relevant instruction on how to avert new threats to peace and stability in the future. The course will be divided into three parts. After an introductory session to review recent conflict trends and consider future threats to peace and stability, the first part of the course will discuss systemic, structural and operational forms of conflict prevention. Part two will apply these theoretical concepts to different kinds of conflict situationsâgreat power rivalry, political contests (succession and secession), economic conflict (resource disputes and organized criminal violence), terrorism and sectarian strife, environmental conflict and the special case of genocide and mass atrocities. Students will learn how to identify the related dynamics of these forms of violent conflict (their associated risk factors, drivers and triggers) and design comprehensive preventive strategies to avert their onset and mitigate their impact using specific contingencies. Part three of the course will assess the role of the principal categories of âpreventive actorsâ involved in conflict prevention todayâthe UN and major regional organizations, civil society, the corporate sector and finally the United States. Short and medium-length written exercises will include: a Risk Assessment of a potential conflict of a student's choice that dissects motives, means and opportunities of the parties, early warning indicators, likelihood, and potential impact; a Preventive Policy Memorandum offering a deliberate strategy for preventing a specific plausible conflict; and a Lessons Learned Paper on a prevention policy success or failure, deconstructing what happened and why.

title: Institutions, Development, and Growth
description: In addressing globalizationâs winners and losers of globalization and economic relations, this course takes an "institutional" approach. We will address the effects that domestic and international political institutions have on three central economic issues: trade, finance, and economic development. It will begin with the big three international institutions: the World Trade Organization (WTO), the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and the World Bank. These respectively create frameworks for international trade, lending large sums of international finance attempting to stave off economic crises, and lending more targeted finance attempting to promote economic development. Then it will consider domestic political regimes: democracy versus autocracy, briefly addressing other domestic institutions, such as presidentialism versus parliamentarism, political parties, legislatures, and labor unions. Domestic political institutions determine how governments survive in office, for example, by winning elections (under democracy) or through other means (as in authoritarian regimes).Next, it will consider the ways in which international and domestic institutions interact to influence patterns of trade, finance, and development. The course will conclude with a broader consideration of global governance, asking what needs to change, and addressing regional institutional solutions, as well as the connections across international institutions. This is a course in "international political economy." Economics is the study of the production, distribution, and consumption of scarce resources. Political economy considers the role that the state plays in such production, distribution, and consumption. International Political Economy considers the flows of such production, distribution, and consumption across national borders, recognizing that not just national governments play a role, but foreign governments and international institutions must also be taken into account. We will grapple with centralized versus decentralized mechanisms of allocation, where the two mechanisms often interact. The course is designed not just to familiarize students with institutional analysis, but also the equip students with analytical and quantitative tools to assess why institutions produce certain outcomes and their efficacy.

title: Political Risk Assessment
description: This course will look at the fundamentals of political risk analysisâthe exploration of the impact of politics on economic outcomesâthrough segments on theory, research and practice. It will investigate multiple levels of riskâ from the macro (international, cross-regional, and national) to micro (local), giving students the full overview of the range of political factors that impact business activity. It will focus, however, on macro geopolitical risksâtransnational issuesâand how they impact economic decision-making. The course will provide an introduction to the practice of political risk assessment, introducing active tools for forecasting, and articulating political risk. The skills gained will be professionally applicable to understanding the challenges facing governments, multilateral organizations, the financial sector, and businesses large and small. Throughout, we will engage the current literature on risk assessment to collectively bridge the gap between theory and practice. Students will be required to write a series of short papers, exploring each level of political risk analysis--transnational, country, and local risk using a case study approach. In-class exercises will include mock client presentations, where the student is the political risk analyst, and a final presentation. The course will tap into the experience of the instructorsâ roles as Director of Research and Director of Corporate Advisory Services at the Eurasia Group, respectively.

title: Economic Diplomacy and Trade Promotion
description: This course will familiarize students with the policy background, government infrastructure, and public-private partnership strategies related to how economic diplomacy advances international business development. It will provide students with an understanding of trade promotion within the context of U.S. foreign policy, explore the types of challenges that businesses face in conducting international business deals, and provide a practical overview of how to identify and navigate the types of government resources and services that support global business and investment. Additionally, the course will explore how this knowledge can be used in careers with government agencies that support international trade and investment, as well as how relevant jobs in the private sector with global companies and multi-client service firms can use this information to provide value-added strategies and services for their companies. The readings, case studies, discussions, and written assignments will be complemented by guest lectures from high-level public and private sector practitioners who will share their approaches to public-private strategies that address the complications and challenges of operating in global markets. Students will be assessed based on class participation and discussion, short memoranda addressing the readings and case studies, and a fully researched term paper.

title: International Business
description: TBD

title: Green Revolutions
description: While the term Green has many uses today, the original âGreen Revolutionâ was a revolution in agriculture. The Green Revolution dramatically increased global agriculture output in the second half of the 20th century with a combination of humanitarian concern, political mission, and good science. While the Revolution had negative side effects, it is often credited with saving the lives of tens of millions of people. However, the Green Revolution clearly did not solve the worldâs food problems. There are still nearly 1 billion hungry people, as many as in 1930. Increasing and increasingly wealthy populations continue to demand ever more food. New land and water resources to expand food production to meet this demand are simply not available. Demands for âgreenâ energy including biofuels and hydropower now compete with food for people. And the issue is also not just about more production. Hunger is as much related to lack of access to food as it is to the food itself. The root cause of malnutrition can be health, not diet. Obesity may now be a bigger health risk than hunger, even in developing countries such as India. Will we need another Green Revolution to meet our new and varied food challenges? Should it be a âgreenâ Green Revolution based on locally produced organics to ensure sustainability? Or a âgeneâ revolution of biotechnology to ensure that food prices remain low? Which Green Revolution(s) will work in a new world with new political priorities, new philanthropists and new technologies? Should we even worry about more food production and instead focus on income to buy the food and healthy bodies to absorb its nutrients? In this class, we will draw lessons from the last Green Revolution to help us consider what the next Green Revolution(s) could and should look like. As importantly, we will use the case of the original Green Revolution to consider how our world views influence our approaches to the problems we face, how science and international politics interact, and the roles of government, the private sector and philanthropy in addressing global challenges.

title: Corporate Responsibility in the 21st Century
description: Corporate Responsibility (CR) emerged as a major business driver over the past decade as businesses saw both the benefits and the necessity of a well-developed and relevant CR strategy. The motivations differ depending on the sector, the business and the communities in which they operate and can be a combination of the following: ensuring more acceptance in a community for the business, mitigation of risks and/or serving as a driver of a growth strategy. When it becomes integral to the business strategy it has the greatest potential to have a significant impact beyond commercial interests. Corporate responsibility emerged as a concept and a field of study as civil society groups became aware of the impact that businesses were having and could have on the challenges they were facing and how the civil society groups might shape the corporate response. Civil society organizations increased the spotlight they put on businesses and demanded changes in operations and policies but in some cases they also became partners and collaborators. It changed how business, non-profits, government and international organizations interacted. This course aims to give students an understanding of what Corporate Responsibility is (and related concepts such as sustainability, corporate citizenship, âgreenâ corporations), its origins, how it fits into a business strategy, steps in defining a CR strategy, criteria for evaluating different strategies and identifying the success components in a strong and effective CR strategy.

title: Identity and Conflict
description: This course will introduce students to the concept of identity and its various components â racial, ethnic, religious, national â and explore how and why issues of identity often are at the core of contemporary international conflicts. We also will investigate the unique challenges that face negotiators and mediators when trying to manage identity-based conflicts. We will define conflict very broadly, looking not only at the role of identity in violent or potentially violent conflicts (such as the Balkans, India-Pakistan, Cyprus, and Israeli-Palestinian situations) but also in other types of international interaction (such as the debate over Turkeyâs accession to the EU, immigration and migration, and even international commercial transactions). The goal will be for the student to come away with a better understanding of the role that issues of identity play, even if indirectly, in many contemporary international conflicts, and to be better equipped to recognize and assess identity issues when they are present. Although parts of the course will be theoretical, the thrust is practical, reflecting the fact that identity issues are real, volatile, and have a pronounced impact on the way in which international conflicts unfold. The role of identity in international interaction shows no signs of lessening.

title: International Trade Practicum
description: This course focuses on the practice of international trade and investment policy from the viewpoint of a practitioner (government, business, NGO, etc.). The course covers the basic elements of modern-day trade policy, including domestic policy mechanisms, regional or bilateral free trade agreements, and the World Trade Organization, as these address trade in goods, trade in services, private direct cross-border investment, the protection of intellectual property rights, and linkage between trade and social issues such as labor and environment. The course is designed to help the student develop practical skills in solving problems presented in international trade and investment policy; gain specific experience in collaborative problem solving through classroom simulations and other exercises; and refine oral and written briefing skills critical to the success of a practitioner in this area. The first part of the semester will focus on the rules that emerge from international trade and investment and that increasingly govern global trade policy making. The second part of the semester will address challenges and opportunities in policy making that arise in interagency and international negotiations as well as stakeholder relations with legislators, foreign government counterparts, international organizations, NGOs, and the media.

title: The Art of Advocacy Inside & Outside of Government
description: This course examines how advocacy influences political leaders and other stakeholders responsible for shaping international policy for managing global crisis and conflict. The course uses a case format to study examples of advocacy in five real world situations. The objective is to build a pragmatic understanding of the impact of advocacy on policy and the management of complex global problems, including famine, health pandemics war, and environmental disaster. The class will include interactive class discussions with policymakers and advocates who played principal roles in each case. The course will build practical skills, including: the ability to identify, evaluate, and formulate effective advocacy strategies and tactics; and the ability to develop common practitioner tools, such as concise briefing and messaging materials used in advocacy initiatives. Key requirements include a reading for each case, preparation of a discussion guide for one case study (to be completed in teams), a concise one page advocacy briefing memo for each case, and a short final paper.

title: Women, Peace and Security
description: Often treated as âperpetual victims,â women are relegated to the sidelines in formal and informal processes of peace-building, state-building and economic recovery. Yet womenâs participation, inclusion and leadership are integral components of building sustainable and peaceful societies. These issues are becoming central components of the diplomatic, development and military policies of the United States, as well as other nations, and critical for negotiations, grant writing, preparation of field manuals or military doctrine, humanitarian relief operations, international crisis management, statebuilding processes, etc. The syllabus will cover a wide range of topics related to womenâs experiences in conflict-affected and post-conflict settings and the impact of their participation in conflict prevention, peacemaking, peacekeeping, mitigating humanitarian emergencies, political transitions and economic recovery. The course will also examine different approaches and policies to gender mainstreaming in conflict and conflict resolution. Academic readings, multimedia tools and case studies from around the world will situate the topics covered in the syllabus in a national and international security context. Senior practitioner guest speakers from the diplomatic and practitioner communities will be invited to lead discussions and illustrate the practical implications of the issues covered. This course aims to equip students with a strong academic foundation and policy/practice-oriented toolkit for their careers, whether in the diplomatic, development, military or non-profit sectors, through team projects, case studies, memos, monitoring and evaluation exercises, and outreach to practitioners.

title: International Financial Institutions
description: The class will cover global economic governance and assess how it has evolved in the post war period that has seen growing trade and financial linkages between advanced, emerging market, and low income countries. The main focus will be on international financial institutions and how they have conducted economic surveillance, crisis prevention, and crisis management to improve economic security. Students will become conversant in the role and structure of these organizations, their cooperation with bilateral and other multilateral standing groups like the G-7, Paris Club, the Financial Stability Forum (FSF), and the G20 that steer international financial and economic policy, and the relationships between the main actors shaping international financial and macroeconomic policy. They will gain an understanding of how global governance is changing in response to the weaknesses triggered by the recent financial crisis, and the key challenges going forward. Although the main focus of the course will be on emerging market and industrial economies, the course will also review development issues facing low income countries.

title: Science, Technology and Development
description: This course introduces students to the transformational nature of innovation across different economic sectors, at the national and international level. Technology is the study and use of art, skills and craft to solve a problem. In that regard, technology is a means for innovation. Etymologically, innovation does not equate with improvement. However, innovation implies the creation of value added. To that extent, innovation is the main engine of development as it is mostly through innovation that a group of people would design new means to meet their needs and improve their standard of living. The first part of the course lays down the foundations to understand the emergence and potential transformative impact of innovation. The second part of the course focuses on sectoral analysis of technology (agriculture, manufacturing and services). The third part of the course studies technology in the context of globalization. It sheds light on how government policies could be impacted by innovation, the international diffusion of innovation, the global market for innovation, innovation as it relates to sustainable development and the ethical dimension of open source innovation. The teaching methodology of the class includes the use of case studies and discussions with seasons innovation practitioners.

title: Ethics in International Relations
description: Limited to and required for International Relations and Security Concentrators in the MSFS Program, this course addresses ethical dilemmas arising in international affairs careers in the public, private, and nonprofit sectors. It will offer frameworks as decision-making tools, including those drawn from natural, national, and international law, while emphasizing discussion of specific issues and cases. It will hone decision-making judgment on key problems as mass atrocities, dealing with illiberal governments, forms of slavery, gender justice and opportunity, poverty and hunger, corporate responsibility, and public health. It will explore choice of means, such as military force, counterterrorism measures, economic sanctions, use of contractors rather than public institutions, compromise, and nonpartisanship, as well as dissent and resignation in professional settings. The course will build skills of concise and effective written and oral argumentation based on ethical reasoning.

title: Development and Demography in Asia
description: In this class you will learn skills that are critical for specialists in a data-driven world: Be able to interpret regression results, and other statistical tables. Compute standard demographic measures of mortality, fertility, and migration. Prepare statistical and demographic graphs and tables, such as a population pyramid and time series data. â¢ Have a basic understanding of geographic information systems. â¢ Be able to locate and determine the reliability of demographic data. â¢ Develop research methods skills relating to questionnaire design and sampling. This course is a seminar and students should participate actively in class. That can be best accomplished by reading carefully each assignment prior to class, posting to the discussion site, and being respectful of your classmatesâ views and opinions. Students are expected to attend every class meeting and must read all of the required materials prior to class. Each student must post on a weekly basis to the on-line class site. Required readings are available on-line. Complete the five two-page policy briefs on the dates they are due. Each class will be in three parts: 1) a case study; 2) discussion of the assigned readings; 3) a lab relating on demographic data, statistical methods, and/or research methods. We will discuss demographic issues across Asia to give you breadth in the topics, and use country examples to give you depth in specific subareas/regions. Topics will include: Mortality and Fertility Transitions in Asia; Chinaâs Policy to Limit Births; Implications of Imbalanced Sex Ratios; Family Systems; Marriage and Marriage Markets; Ageing; Diet, Nutrition, and Famine; Smoking, public health and mortality; Immigration; Urbanization

title: GIS Applications in Development
description: Geospatial technologies include a suite of inter-connected technologies, such as geographic information systems (GIS) and remote sensing technologies (e.g. satellite imagery). These technologies have emerged in recent decades as cross-cutting, enabling, and prevalent in all aspects of life. This course primarily focuses on illustrating how GIS is enabling decision makers and professionals in understanding the needs of our rapidly changing, extremely diverse, yet ever more interconnected world. This course is designed primarily for graduate students who aspire to be leaders in the public, private, and non-profit sectors of international affairs and global development. Graduate students interested in international development, global politics and security, global human development, reducing poverty, global public health, and humanitarian/crisis management are welcome to join this course. Special attention will be put on the importance of ethics and best practices in applying GIS and other related technologies in these fields.

title: Comparative Regional Economic Development (CRED)
description: The main objective of this course is to help develop and hone the skills needed to diagnose a countryâs major development constraints or challenges and provide realistic policy advice. The course seeks to achieve this goal by providing students with an international comparative perspective on the key development issues facing countries throughout the developing world. The course is organized around five regional segments or modules corresponding to the worldâs major developing areas or regions: (i) East Asia; (ii) South Asia; (iii) Sub-Saharan Africa; (iv) Latin America; and (v) the Middle East and North Africa. Under each module, a set of two or three leading development issues are analyzed. These issues are selected with a view to analyzing a particular issue in the context of a region in which that issue is most prominent or challenging. The intention, therefore, is to present each major development issue in stark relief and encourage comparisons and contrasts across regions and countries where appropriate. For example, equity and distributional issues and macroeconomic stabilization are examined against the backdrop of Latin America where these issues have been particularly acute, while economic stagnation, how to initiate and sustain economic growth and aid effectiveness are analyzed in the context of Sub-Saharan Africa. Similarly, how to make growth more pro-poor is studied in South Asia, and the challenges of rebalancing growth, raising productivity and moving up the global value chain are examined in East Asia.
