Finally, resource management and governance issues related to changing the role and functions of the state are investigated in the oil-rich countries of the Middle East and North Africa.

title: Diplomatic and Military Statecraft
description: The course will examine the tough questions and dilemmas in the practice of contemporary statecraft and diplomacy. It will use a series of case studies to explore how states have successfully built integrated strategies to achieve their objectives and where and why they have failed. It will focus in particular on the changing nature of the tools available to states and the context in which they are used. The team of instructors, current (and former) diplomats and military officers from the SFS Institute for the Study of Diplomacy, will draw from their recent experience to round out the case studies and focus on the challenges of modern statecraft and diplomacy.

title: International Project Finance and Investment
description: The course will focus on design, development, structuring and execution of international project finance transactions. The semester will commence with the building blocks of structured finance including discussions of the differences between corporate and project financing and reviewing the interests of the various participants. We will then move quickly into the discussion of risk identification, allocation and mitigation, which is the foundation of creative financial solutions. The second half of the course will commence with a detailed review of the concepts and use of financial analysis and financial modeling followed by a discussion of the role of contracts in mitigating risk. The second half will also include a series of case studies as well as guest speakers that will discuss topics such as financial documentation, syndication, re-structuring troubled projects and the increasing emphasis on environmental and social issues in successful project implementation.

title: International Project Finance and Investment
description: This module will address the techniques of project finance across a range of project types, including public and private infrastructure, energy, power, extractive enterprises, and industrial projects. The course will cover methods of project feasibility evaluation, financial analysis and structuring, use of various financing instruments and markets, use of government and multilateral finance programs, and contractual documentation. The course will explore the contractual roles of various parties in a project, including owner/sponsor, design and technical engineers, equipment and other suppliers, government approval authorities, operations and maintenance contractors, bankers and capital markets, and government and multilateral finance authorities The course will make heavy use of case studies of real projects in a variety of venues with an emphasis on emerging markets.

title: International Project Finance and Investment
description: This module will address the techniques of project finance across a range of different infrastructure and resource management projects, including public and private infrastructure schemes, independent power plants, transportation, extractive enterprises, and industrial projects, The course is designed to introduce students to the analytical and conceptual tools used to appraise, evaluate, and structure complex, large infrastructure projects. A multi-disciplinary approach will be applied to show how project risks can be identified, quantified and mitigated. In addition, the course will involve one negotiation session where students will learn how to manage the legal, political, and financial instruments used in actual project finance transactions. At least one session will be devoted to derivatives, swaps, and financial modeling as these techniques apply to project finance risk analysis. The course will be useful for virtually any person interested in working in international finance and development.

title: Congressional Decision-Making and U.S. Foreign Policy
description: This course provides a practical understanding of how and why the U.S. Congress influences foreign policy. Prospective congressional staffers (or interns) -- as well as students who would like to find employment in Washingtonâs diplomatic, development, business, advocacy, or analytical communities -- should have a firm understanding of Congress and the intersect between the U.S. political process and foreign policy. Though Washington is experiencing political gridlock, there are meaningful opportunities for those working on â or with -- Capitol Hill to influence foreign policy. Part one of the course builds a constitutional, institutional, procedural, political and historical framework for understanding Congressâ role in international affairs. Part two looks at the specific tools used by Congress to influence foreign policy, such as the power of the purse, the authority to regulate foreign commerce, and the ability to reorganize the government. Part three examines the pressure brought to bear on Congress on international issues by the executive branch, foreign governments, lobbyists, advocacy groups and interest groups (in particular U.S.-citizen ethnic interest groups). The instructor is based at the SFS Institute for the Study of Diplomacy and is a former U.S. diplomat with extensive experience working for and with Congress on foreign policy.

title: Conflict Management and International Security
description: The seminar's central focus is the challenge of creating security and building peace in the 21st century. It will introduce students to the "intellectual map" of the peace-maker by exploring a wide range of literatures and cases in order to identify the roots and sources of conflict, and illustrate the varieties of third party intervention for conflict management. Drawing upon readings and discussion, students will examine changing patterns of conflict and explore a range of institutions, techniques and strategies for responding to the challenge of disorder. Course Requirements: There will be several short, written assignments accounting for 40% of the grade. Students will also prepare a research paper (40%) analyzing a specific conflict management issue and what can be done to address it. Students are expected to be prepared to participate in class discussion, and will present oral briefings to the seminar (20%).

title: Energy in a Low-Carbon World
description: This course is designed to present students with an introduction to the key issues and challenges confronting government policy-makers and business leaders in global energy. The course focuses on the impacts and intersection of emerging but potentially transformational events: 1.Forecasts of rising global energy demand, especially for fossil fuels 2.Growing concern about the dangers posed to US and global security by global climate change (GCC), especially the role and impact of CO2 emissions from fossil fuel use in the energy sector 3.Supply-side challenges in moving to low carbon sources for power generation and transportation 4.Demand-side challenges, especially in achieving greater energy efficiency 5.The changing dynamics of international oil and gas markets 6.The potential convergence of the transportation sector with electricity 7.Challenges posed by, and potential impacts of, critical geopolitical regional âhotspotsâ With a growing consensus on the science, causes and impacts of global climate change (GCC), especially since the issuance of the Fourth Assessment Report of the UN Inter-Governmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007, a lively policy debate has emerged on how to meet the worldâs energy and economic needs, while at the same time protecting the environment. A cornerstone of this debate centers on how to de-carbonize the power and transportation sectors. There has been renewed interest in low carbon technologies such as wind and solar, nuclear power, and clean coal technologies such as carbon capture and storage, as well as greater attention being paid to energy efficiency and natural gas. Nevertheless, despite a growing global consensus on the need to reduce carbon emissions, use of coal proceeds apace with India and China developing new coal-fired power generation at an alarming rate. Simultaneously, recent oil price volatility has fueled debate over the future direction of the oil industry, and its impact on economic performance and competitiveness of alternative fuels. In addition, a variety of regional energy issues are emerging to play a more critical role in the policy picture, for example: the importance of China and India and their energy policy to handle booming economic development; the role of Russia as an energy superpower, its impact on European energy security, and inter-relationship with Central Asian oil and gas infrastructure, and; the emergence of several other key countries and regions in the global energy industry.

title: Media & World Affairs
description: This course examines the relationship between the media and the conduct of international relations, beginning with Julius Caesar's self-serving account of the Gallic Wars and continuing through the media coverage of the Bush administration's case for pre-emptive war on Iraq, the global financial crisis and environmental issues. It also traces the evolution of the American news media from two developments in the 15th and 16th Centuries: the invention of moveable type and the translation of the Bible into English and German. The course includes a number of weekly writing assignments, and it places a heavy emphasis on the distillation and organization of large amounts of material into clear, concise prose, the kind that's valued not only in journalism, but also in industry, government and NGOs. In addition, there is a final paper of 2,000-2,500 words on a topic to be chosen by the student and approved by the professor.

title: Fragile States in Africa
description: This course is for students interested in some of the most important, challenging, complex, and difficult issues facing professionals working on diplomacy and economic development (including the role of the private sector) in Africa today. Focusing on the set of countries in Africa referred to as âweakâ or âfragileâ states, the course will analyze causes of state weakness, examining and critiquing various strategic approaches intended to help such states progress towards greater stability and development. From the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Ivory Coast, Somalia, Zimbabwe, and other countries, this course will first examine the concept of state fragility: what is it, why is the concept important, which countries can be considered fragile, how are they different from other developing countries, how do states move out of fragility? A fundamental course goal is ultimately to identify and discuss the most promising approaches to reduce conflict and fragility while fostering democratic development. The course will both examine specific country case studies and also discuss broad issues across countries, focusing on sectors and issues such as economic growth, education, health, justice, and democracy promotion. The course will compare strategies for fragile states with other approaches for more stable countries. Are strategies for fragile states radically different than those for other states? If so, why? Is this distinction regularly applied? If not, why not, and why does it matter? The point of view generally adopted will be of a person working in an Embassy, for a bilateral/multilateral donor, or for a UN operating unit in a developing country, but the course also will examine alternative points of view, including host country, business, and nongovernmental actors.

title: World Energy Security
description: The seminar discusses the following three related topics: (i) the present situation of World Energy Flows, focusing on both the largest world energy consumers and producers in Asia, Europe, Russia and Central Asia, the Middle East, Africa and LAC; (ii) the dynamics of the world petroleum market, by far the main source of primary energy in the World, and the most traded source of energy in the world and (iii) the potential for Hemispheric Energy Cooperation as a means of fostering regional energy security.

title: Global Business and Public Policy Practicum
description: Students aspiring to leadership in the public and private sectors need to understand the increasingly important role global companies play in shaping public policy on international trade and related issues. This course focuses on the practice of business-government relations, specifically the role of the global company as advocate on international economic issues. Last Fallâs course introduced students to the government relations programs of companies such as Proctor & Gamble, Coca Cola, Hewlett-Packard, Caterpillar, Bayer, McDonaldâs, Google, Boeing, Novartis and IBM. Students will gain a greater understanding of the ways in which corporations advocate in the executive and legislative branches of government. In addition, they will learn that advocacy takes many forms and that strategies differ from case to case. With reference to key issues, students will learn how major corporations identify priorities and develop and advocate policy positions. For 2012, students will explore government relations messaging and communications, including the shaping of public opinionânow a vital component of successful public policy programs. Students will evaluate the advent of social media in communications campaigns, studying the Capitol Hill debate over the Stop Online Piracy Act (SOPA) and its potential to redefine âgrassrootsâ advocacy. The course will also feature government relations crisis management, with particular reference to Googleâs decision to shut down its search engine in China rather than comply with Chinese law requiring self-censorship of Internet searches. Weâll contrast the business case for complying with Chinese law and Googleâs commitment to the principle of Internet freedom. To the extent practicable, the course will also consider what the 2012 elections might mean for global company government relations practices. To create a personal experience of the practice of government relations each student is teamed with a Washington-based corporate government relations executive involved in international business policy. A course highlight, the executive mentor assigns an issue project important to his/her company and the student has an opportunity to study and make a presentation on the issue for the companyâs Washington office. The course emphasizes developing internal and external business communication skills, including meeting-leadership skills, for effective Washington representation. It also focuses on advocacy, networking, decision-making processes and the respective roles of Capitol Hill and Executive Branch agencies. The course contributes to studentsâ ability to be effective participants in the development, implementation, and assessment of sound public policy, government affairs, and advocacy strategies for their current or future organizations.

title: International Law
description: This course will provide a detailed examination of the fundamentals of public international law. Taking a case-method approach, the course will discuss the sources of international law, the relationship of international law to domestic law, international personality, jurisdiction under international law-- including general principles of jurisdiction, extradition, the law of the sea, and air and space law, immunity from jurisdiction, state responsibility for injury to aliens, international human rights law, the law relating to the recourse to force, and the law concerning the conduct of hostilities. The primary text of the course will be: *International Law: Cases and Materials, 5th ed. 2009*, by Lori Damrosch, et al. Students will be evaluated based on class participation and *four* short legal memoranda.

title: Good Governance and Capable States
description: Over the last decade, the effectiveness of the state and how states are governed have emerged as central topics among donors and the aid policy community. There is now increased consensus that well functioning and accountable public sector institutions that make sound policies, regulate markets, mobilize public resources, and deliver services are the foundation for sustainable and broad-based socioeconomic development. Whether it is responding to natural disasters, curtailing humanitarian crises, mitigating conflict, or effectively implementing development policy â a capable state and its institutions of governance must lead the way. Increased interest in state capacity building and good governance has also been fueled by the on-going debate on aid effectiveness. After decades of post-war development assistance, there is growing concern that a lack of attention to state capacity and good governance has led to an unsustainable addiction to foreign assistance. The preferred prescription has been increased âcountry ownershipâ and use of host country systems. This presupposes capable state institutions that can effectively plan, design, execute and evaluate development programs in an accountable and transparent manner. Yet, daily headlines more often highlight governance failings than they do success, suggesting a continued need for reflection and improvements in how the foreign aid community promotes good governance and builds capable states. This class explores the relationship between good governance, capable states, and sustainable development. The emphasis is not on why the problem of governance exists, although we will establish conceptual foundations about the nature and scope of the state. Rather, our focus is on how the development community tackles the challenge, and what aspiring development managers can learn from collective experience in this field. Working both individually and in teams, students will gain a practical understanding of approaches, methods and solutions that are used today to tackle the problem of governance and state building. Students are encouraged to challenge assumptions, and openly examine the successes, failures and track-record of a unique type of development assistance. Class discussion will center on practical experience of development managers and real-world reading material â case studies, toolkits, operational handbooks, and program evaluations. Students will gain practical skills to analyze arguments, assess governance challenges, and make recommendations on program design, focus and priority.

title: Introduction to Microfinance
description: Microfinance is usually viewed as a development success in that it has demonstrated the capacity to improve standards of living among the worldâs poorest in a sustainable way. Microfinance development programs target their financial services to low-income micro entrepreneurs with fewer than 10 employees. The purpose of this class is to provide an introduction to best practices in the design, management, and evaluation of microfinance programs. At the end of the course, students will be able to describe the role of microfinance in the financial services sector; the profile of the average microfinance client; the range of providers of microfinance services; the role of government in developing microfinance; and the principles of microfinance performance management.

title: Econometrics for Policy-Makers
description: Econometrics is the quantitative analysis of economic phenomena using the tools of mathematics and statistics. This course is tailored for practitioners and policy makers who need to develop skills and stools to be effective in using economic analysis in policy formulation and analysis. The skills developed in this course allow policy makers not only to be critical consumers of policy research but also to understand the process that underlies policy recommendations. The course takes as given some basic familiarity with descriptive statistics, probability theory, random variables and probability distributions and inference statistics both in an univariate and multivariate setting. The first part of the course focuses on the practice of regression analysis for cross-sectional data. The second part builds on the first part to provide a solid treatment of regression analysis with panel data and the third part covers discrete modeling, experimental and quasi-experimental methods.

title: Foreign Policy Making in Developing Nations
description: This course is aimed at four objectives: (1) to give students a broad understanding of the foreign policy process in developing nations in the major developing areas, dealing specifically with each area both as a region, and with a closer examination of the foreign policy â outcomes and process â of several of the major states; (2) to present for criticism and discussion some of the major theoretical approaches to the analysis and comparison of foreign policy and the processes by which it is made; (3) to allow students to deepen their expertise in the region of their interest through the research and presentation of a foreign policy study of a state in this region; and (4) to sharpen students' analytic skills through creation of a common research design so that the students' research will fit together into a collaborative framework. This will facilitate both a specific understanding of the foreign policy in individual nations as well as an ability to make comparisons between states and generalizations about the topic as a whole.

title: China-US Great Power Rivalry
description: The bilateral relationship between the U.S. and China has emerged as the most important such interaction in current world affairs. While it is unique and complex, with an undefined trajectory and an unknown outcome, it has a context among previous relationships between great powers. This course will examine the U.S. â China relationship as a great power rivalry, following primarily a structural-historical approach. After exploring the concept of great power rivalry and examining several previous such relationships, the seminar will then turn to an examination of the U.S. and China, isolating such factors as the security, economic, cultural and ideological dimensions. The students will be expected to draw upon the discussion of these factors in the earlier case studies to establish analogies to their current and future roles in relations between Beijing and Washington. In addition to an appreciation of the phenomenon of Great Power rivalry and an in-depth understanding of the U.S. â China relationship in particular, students will be expected to develop and utilize presentation and briefing, interview, serving as a discussant for reviewing peer presentations, and policy-oriented writing skills.

title: Financial Regulations & Reform in Developing Countries
description: Financial markets have grown in size and sophistication over the last decade in the developing, newly industrializing, and post-Communist world. Financial liberalization in these countries has often involved contrasting polices. On the one hand, governments in capital-scarce economies have retreated from direct involvement in the allocation of financial resources. On the other hand, government authorities have also intervened in financial markets to protect consumers and investors, to promote fairness and transparency in financial transactions, and to maintain stability. Financial crises in emerging markets throughout the 1990s, however, raise several questions regarding the effectiveness of the financial reforms undertaken in those countries. Meanwhile, more recent challenges to the global financial system have required governments and international financial institutions to rethink regulatory standards. This seminar explores how and why governments intervene in financial markets. Four questions are of special importance: (1) What impact do financial markets have on the economic performance of developing countries? (2) What are the main dimensions along which financial systems vary across countries, and how do these differences matter? (3) Why are financial systems âfragileâ and what can governments do to ensure their stability? (4) How do financial markets recover, and what can governments do to speed restructuring? These questions are explored through a number of articles, books, reports, and case studies taken from different disciplinesâfinance, economics, public policy, and administrative studiesâand from different international settings.

title: Oil & World Power
description: Oil has been central to power and wealth since the early 20th century, and the history of oil provides important insights into the nature and dynamics of power and influence in the international system.

title: Human Rights and War
description: This course will examine the increasingly complex link between human rights and war. War has always posed a threat to human rights, but the threat has evolved as wars have evolved from traditional inter-state conflicts to ânew warsâ in countries like Bosnia, which deliberately target civilians. More recently,, we have seen the emergence of the âhybridâ war against terror. Looking ahead, we see new challenges in the form of cyber-warfare, new forms of weaponry like drones, and even climate change. The nature of war is changing before our eyes. What are the implications of these developments for human rights? Do they make it harder or easier to protect the vulnerable and disempowered? And is the traditional approach of international human rights â developing and implementing legal standards (conventions) through the UN and regional organizations - equal to the challenge? If not, how could the machinery of international human rights be adapted, to better respond? Is there still a role for traditional theories like Just War and even pacifism? How do we assess emerging doctrines, like R2P? The course will address these key questions through a series of major case studies: World War 2; Iraq; Afghanistan; Cambodia; Bosnia; Rwanda and the Great Lakes; Sierra Leone; Uganda/LRA; the Middle East (Gaza); and Post 9/11. The course will mix theory and practice, and focus in particular on the role of community organizations as agents of peace. Each participant will be assigned a case study and make a class presentation, working together or in teams. There will be one final paper, but no exams. A full syllabus and reading list will be posted in July. In the meantime, students will find a good overview in âHUMAN RIGHTS AND CONFLICT - Exploring the Links between Rights, Law, and Peace-building,â Edited by Julie Mertus and Jeffrey Helsing (US Institute of Peace).

title: Nuclear Policy & International Security
description: This seminar will be a real time study of the development of the overarching national security strategy of the Obama administration. There will be a heavy emphasis on nuclear weapons policy and nonproliferation strategy, and we will review the 2010 debate over Senate ratification of the new START treaty and track Senate consideration of the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty (CTBT). The course explores, in addition, a brief overview of the successes and failures of the previous administrationâs strategy. We will also discuss the practical aspects of government foreign policy decision-making, including the role of Congress and the national security bureaucracy. We will not spend much time discussing Libya, Iraq and Afghanistan, as these are amply covered by other courses, but there will be discussion of Pakistan, India, Iran and North Korea. There will be a heavy emphasis on learning the necessary skills of concise oral presentation and impact writing, such as the art of the one-page government memo and the two-minute oral presentation. Students will be expected to follow major diplomatic and regional developments in the daily and weekly media and specialized journals.

title: Workshop: Navigating U.S. Decision-making:  Political and Career Perspectives
description: This course will examine U.S. decision-making in both a global and regional context. Global issues assessed will include the environment, health, democracy/human rights and trafficking-in-persons with a focus on specific policy cases in Latin America, to see how they are addressed in a regional context. Consideration will also be given to differing perspectives of political and career service officials which often factor into decision-making and result in divergent policy outcomes. The instructors of this course will provide these contrasting views based on their respective careers. If you are interested in serving in the U.S. government, or in another government or institutional setting interacting with it, this course will provide you with an excellent foundation in and understanding of the myriad of challenges in decision-making and the broad range of policy instruments to tackle them. Focusing on techniques and the art of practice, some of the graded exercises in this course will include policy memoranda, briefings and oral presentations, team projects, and taking responsibility for consulting with current and recent government officials on a discrete project in global issues.

title: WKSHP: Managing Risk
description: Rationale International development is all about managed change; about self-conscious, deliberate, organized and purposeful efforts to change social, economic or political systems. Development theories and programs most commonly focus on the technical rationale for these changes, emphasizing the benefits that will be realized if they are implemented. Often missing from these strategies, however, is the inevitable concomitant aspect of change: risk. Development is not just about seizing new opportunities or securing new benefits. It is also about venturing into unknown territories, trying new approaches and going down unfamiliar paths. That means new risks â risks that are difficult to identify, to assess and to manage. The inevitability of significant, new and poorly understood risk helps explain why change â whether in the context of a development project or in âmodernâ societies -- is almost universally resisted. Resistance is ubiquitous and often deeply-held not simply because there are vested interests, or because the target population is ignorant and uneducated. Change is resisted because it brings new and often very different kinds of risks that are difficult to assess and to manage. In development, those risks are often faced both those with the fewest resources and the least capacity to absorb the negative consequences of risk. For these reasons, even change that has a strong technical rationale can mean greater and more threatening risks than the more familiar (if sub-optimal) status quo. Despite the fact that change is at the heart of international development, the academic and professional literature on risk management is largely focused on the challenges of the private sector, and it has most commonly been developed for the managers of private firms. Those engaged in international development must encounter and manage a range of risks every day, yet there is relatively little systematic examination of the problem of risk from the perspective of development managers, and few viable tools to manage risk actively. Course Objectives The core objective of this course is to further develop, test, and refine the analytic frameworks and practical tools to understand and manage risk as an integral part of the development process. This will include the ability to: â¢ Identify risk â understand the different kinds, modes, and varieties of risk; â¢ Assess risk â be able to examine risks relative to overall objective and other considerations; â¢ Manage risk â develop practical measures to mitigate and reduce risk Approach: Case Studies and On-Site Consulting Assignments The heart of the course is a series of consulting assignments or âcase studiesâ working directly with client organizations to illuminate, assess and respond to the problem of risk as it is encountered in a in a specific and practical part of their work. Small teams of students (approximately 3-4 persons) will be assigned to specific international development organization for a period of 6-8 weeks. The MSFS teams will in effect serve as unpaid consultants to these organizations, which could include nonprofit non-governmental organizations, for-profit consulting firms, think-tanks and multilateral financial institutions. Their task is to interview and work with the staff to assess and help strengthen risk management in the context of a specific development project or activity. Students will draft and negotiate a Scope of Work with the client organization. They will also formulate a work plan, manage and account for their time, report periodically to their client, produce a final report for and make a final presentation to the client. The final reports for and presentations to the clients will constitute a major part of the overall evaluation of the studentsâ performance in class. In addition the instructor will solicit feed-back from clients in a confidential client satisfaction survey. Note: Consulting assignments for the Case Studies are very demanding and labor-intensive. Students from prior years have advised that it is extremely difficult to complete the Case Studies for various client organizations and also to fulfill additional internship obligations. Students contemplating registering for this course should consider carefully all their other obligations before making a commitment. The final session of the course(December 6) will be a student-organized and led Mini Conference to which outside experts will be invited. Students will give brief oral presentation on some aspect of their key findings on Case Studies and on the advances made in developing a risk management framework and tools relevant to development mangers. Past Consulting Assignments and Case Studies Over the past three years, MSFS students have both interviewed and worked intensively with approximately 50 different international development organizations in the Washington DC area. These have ranged from small start-up firms to large established consulting companies, non-governmental organizations, bilateral aid agencies and multilateral development banks. Past consulting assignments and case studies â responding to specific needs and priorities identified by client organizations -- have included: â¢ Risk assessment tools and procedures to evaluate new business opportunities; â¢ Tools and frameworks to anticipate, identify and assess risks during project design; â¢ Frameworks for ongoing risk management for project implementation at the field level; â¢ Training modules for building risk management capacities in nongovernmental organizations; â¢ Risk assessment in cell phone applications in health surveillance systems; â¢ Analysis of client risk in cell phone application in microfinance lending and measures to mitigate those risks; â¢ Assessment of social media applications in promoting and strengthening civil society actors in the political process; â¢ Uses of ICT and social media in beneficiary monitoring and supervision of World Bank-funded projects

title: WKSHP: Engaging Change: Middle East and North Africa
description: In the Middle East and North Africa, international affairs professionals face regional upheaval, shifting regimes, non-state actors morphing into political parties, social unrest, increased demands from constituents for more accountable governments and more assertive foreign policies, as well as a 24-hour media cycle that includes new forms of communication. These dynamic factors have combined to make for the most profound transformations in the regionâs contemporary history. Professionals seeking to understand and adapt to these fast-paced developments will require appreciating the perspectives of the various regional and international actors; and require acquiring the skills necessary to gather and process information as well as assess and present policy options, whether in a government, an international organization, an NGO, or a business. This workshop will focus on short presentations based on written sources and interviews with practitioners, role-plays/simulation, and written work, to help students prepare for professional engagement with this specific regionâs dynamics, and with those of other regions as well.

title: WKSHP: Policy Innovation & Coalition Management
description: Regardless of which sector students enter after graduation, many will be responsible for the management of innovative policy initiatives and of complex diverse coalitions to advance such initiatives. This workshop will focus comprehensively on how to manage such multi-stakeholder policy innovations. Students will work in teams, each working for a specific private, public or NGO entity and each with a multifaceted policy issue, such as Pakistani madrassa reform, regulation of conflict minerals, or corporate social responsibility initiatives to end the use of slave labor in agricultural products. Students will learn key skills needed in navigating policy making, including issue analysis, risk and stakeholder assessment, coalition development and management, advocacy, and oral and written presentation skills. Each team will research and develop its policy initiative and strategies for working with key stakeholders in all sectors -- directly conferring with professionals in them, and present key proposals and an implementation plan to a panel of external experts.

title: Workshop: Global Finance
description: This workshop will cover a wide range of material designed to give students a solid background in global business and finance, particularly in the context of the global capital markets and the crises of the last few years. The workshop will develop existing functional skills as well as introducing new topics in order to enhance an overall understanding of the context, players, practice and relevant techniques of global business and finance. Sessions will largely be in-class work on cases in order to emphasize real world application and complexity and are intended to be inter-active sessions. At the end of the workshop students will have a critical understanding of the complexity and interconnectedness of many aspects of finance, and will have developed some key analytic tools in viewing these. The case process students will be employed to give students the opportunity of using this newly acquired knowledge and skill set. The workshop will benefit students who are intent upon obtaining basic corporate and capital market financial literacy and are interested in both the theory and practice of finance. Students should be curious as to the unfolding dramas contained in the financial press and wish to enhance their ability to understand and debate the salient issues. Students will become better equipped to understand these issues and the tradeoffs and uncertainties that different stakeholders might typically face. Students will also better understand the financial crisis and some of the lessons that must continue to emanate from it. The workshop will appeal to those who wish to develop careers that touch finance and the financial markets, be that in the public or private sectors as participants, regulators or observers. At the end of the workshop students will have enhanced their functional financial knowledge, and obtained a better perspective as to how the functional parts fit together. Finally students will have obtained sufficient tools and practice in applying them so as to be able to make intelligent judgments and contributions in real life situations.

title: WKSHP: Resource Management In International Relations
description: This workshop prepares students for dealing with the critical role of resources in international relations (IR). There is a vast global infrastructure that supports the conduct of IR. The requirements are infinite and expensive. The workshop will provide students with knowledge and skills that are necessary for effective IR resource management, including: budget formulation, analysis and evaluation; grant writing and evaluation; budget negotiations; program execution; the audit process; the mechanics of the U.S. foreign affairs budget, including the appropriations process and the U.S. defense budget, offered as an example of how a major world power funds its ambitious and sometimes controversial foreign affairs agenda; how multilateral institutions manage resources; and the often poorly-understood role that private corporations play as resource-mobilizing partners. The workshop utilizes the case method, in-class simulations, and practical exercises and will feature high-level guest speakers from the U.S. Government, NGOs, foundations, and private contractors.

title: Workshop: Strategy & Management Problem Solving
description: This workshop will provide students with working familiarity with the strategic, analytic and communications methods used by strategic planners, problem intervention teams, general managers/profit center managers and outside consultants. The first part of the course (weeks 1-6) will deal with high level strategy and will address issues and challenges of the large business firm that operates globally or is âglobalizingâ its scope. We will begin with a survey of general business strategy literature and concepts covering topics such as industry structure, competitive advantage, core competencies, and product differentiation. With this as a foundation, the course will engage a variety of strategic issues that accompany international or global business operations. These include country market selection, brand management and other marketing challenges, intellectual property protection, alliances and joint ventures, sourcing and supply chain management, organization design, and cross-cultural management. The second part of the course (weeks 7-14) will focus on industry analysis and corresponding corporate strategies for operating within these globally-organized industries. Industries to be covered include autos, consumer products, retailing, financial services, information technology, telecommunications, airlines, aerospace, energy, mining, pharmaceuticals and biotech, and media and entertainment. Among the practical skills to be practiced in the course is industry structure analysis. The entire course will operate at two levels reflecting, to some extent, the scope of work done by leading consulting firms for large corporates. The course will treat 1) high level strategyâglobal, regional and major market strategy. At the same time the course will provide experience in specific 2) problem diagnosis and solution strategies/tacticsâa more ground level kind of assignment. This aspect of the course will have four intersecting thrusts: business and organizational problemsâ¦across a variety of industriesâ¦in a range of geographic contextsâ¦requiring a spectrum of applicable diagnostic and analytical methods.

title: WKSHP Business of Development
description: This workshop focuses on practical skills relevant to the roles and job responsibilities of most development professionals - whether they work for multilateral organizations, government agencies, private sector firms, or NGOs. The course is designed to build knowledge and skills that match the complex, interdisciplinary reality of development management. Successful development practice depends on the capacity of program and project managers to integrate different disciplines and interact effectively with numerous stakeholders, both inside and outside their own organization. This capacity is likely to become more important, and more highly valued, as the global development ecosystem continues to evolve and the concept of âDevelopment 2.0â moves from rhetoric to reality. The workshop blends training in core skills and practices with exposure to the functions and characteristics of key actors in the ecosystem. The workshop is divided into three modules featuring exercises and presentations by student teams, and concludes with an individual project assignment that integrates material learned during the course. Module 1 will begin with an overview of the global development ecosystem, focusing on the major donor agencies and newer, nontraditional actors who are introducing important changes to the way that development projects are designed, financed, and implemented. Module 2 will examine the different types of organizations in the private and NGO sectors that implement development projects on behalf of project sponsors and funding agencies. Module 3 will focus on the skills and tools necessary to launch and implement a project, once a contract or grant agreement has been negotiated with the donor agency. The workshop will close with an individual written assignment. Students will respond to a case with a scenario describing a development problem in need of resolution, and will be asked to prepare a plan of action in response to this case.

title: Mathematics in Society
description: The course is concerned with mathematical approaches that can help resolve conflicts, make decisions under adverse or uncertain conditions, or extract social choices from the preferences of individuals. Topics will be chosen from elementary game theory (arms race, Prisoner's Dilemma), fair division schemes, probability models for chance events and risk (lottery, poker, life insurance), voting models (voting paradoxes, building coalitions), and statistical methods (opinion polls, clinical trials). Mathematical prerequisites are algebra, analytic geometry, and elementary probability as taught in high school. This course may be used toward completion of the College math/science requirement.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Modeling
description: This course if for students who want to see how the math they have previously learned can be applied to personal issues related to health and finance as well as in policy decisions from many different fields. In the first part of the course, personal issues, such as weight gain or loss, alcohol abuse, proper drug dosage, mortgages and annuities are primarily considered. In the second part of the course, we will consider more global models that give insight into sustainable management of the worlds resources or issues related to genetics and genetic engineering. Often the results will be counterintuitive, such as finding that an increase in the rate of wild-life harvesting may actually decrease the long-term harvest. Fall and Spring.

title: Calculus with Review
description: Math-029 is the first course of a year-long sequence that covers the material in a Calculus I course. This year-long sequence is appropriate for students who have little or no exposure to calculus and who have do not have a strong precalculus/algebra background. Students enroll in 029 in the fall semester and continue on to take a specially marked section of Calculus I (Math-035) in spring semester reserved only for those students enrolled in 029 in the Fall semester. This allows the students and the professor to pace through the Calculus I concepts over the period of a full year. The Math-029 course begins with a refresher of some important concepts related to functions, progressing into a study of limits, the conceptual underpinning of calculus, and then into differentiation. The year-long pace allows topics from algebra and precalculus to be reviewed, as-needed. Applications will be introduced to deepen the students' understanding of the concepts. NOTE: MATH 029 does not satisfy the College general education requirement in mathematics. Credit in 029 is regarded as a free elective towards the College degree. MATH 035 does satisfy the College general education requirement--see College Undergraduate Bulletin general education requirements section to see how MATH 035 can be combined in various ways to meet the math/science requirement in full.

title: Calculus with Review
description: Math-029 is the first course of a year-long sequence that covers the material in a Calculus I course. This year-long sequence is appropriate for students who have little or no exposure to calculus and who have do not have a strong precalculus/algebra background. Students enroll in 029 in the fall semester and continue on to take a specially marked section of Calculus I (Math-035) in spring semester reserved only for those students enrolled in 029 in the Fall semester. This allows the students and the professor to pace through the Calculus I concepts over the period of a full year. The Math-029 course begins with a refresher of some important concepts related to functions, progressing into a study of limits, the conceptual underpinning of calculus, and then into differentiation. The year-long pace allows topics from algebra and precalculus to be reviewed, as-needed. Applications will be introduced to deepen the students' understanding of the concepts. NOTE: MATH 029 does not satisfy the College general education requirement in mathematics. Credit in 029 is regarded as a free elective towards the College degree. MATH 035 does satisfy the College general education requirement--see College Undergraduate Bulletin general education requirements section to see how MATH 035 can be combined in various ways to meet the math/science requirement in full.

title: Calculus I
description: This is the first part of the four semester calculus sequence (Math-035-036 and 137-150) for mathematics and science majors. Students do not need to have any familiarity with calculus, but do need good algebra/precalculus preparation. Topics include limits, derivatives, techniques of differentiation, applications of the derivative, the Riemann integral, the trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions, and the logarithmic and exponential functions. Fall and Spring.

title: Calculus II
description: A continuation of MATH-035. Topics include techniques of integration, applications of the definite integral, improper integrals, Newton's method and numerical integration, sequences and series including Taylor's theorem and power series, and elementary separable and first and second order linear differential equations. Fall and Spring.

title: Probability and Statistics
description: This course will introduce students to the basic concepts, logic, and issues involved in statistical reasoning, as well as basic statistical methods used to analyze data and evaluate studies. The major topics to be covered include methods for exploratory data analysis, an introduction to sampling and experimental design, elementary probability theory and random variables, and methods for statistical inference including simple linear regression. The objectives of this course are to help students develop a critical approach to the evaluation of study designs, data and results, and to develop skills in the application of basic statistical methods in empirical research. An important feature of the course will be the use of statistical software to facilitate the understanding of important statistical ideas and for the implementation of data analysis. The course has two lectures and one lab section. Cannot be taken for credit if the student has already taken ECON 121, OPIM 173, IPOL 320 or MATH 140. College Economics and Political Economy majors should enroll in ECON 121, rather than MATH 040. This course does NOT satisfy the Mathematics minor or majors requirement for a Statistics class--these students should enroll in MATH 140. Seniors and Post Baccalaureate Pre-Medical students must get special permission to enroll in this course.

title: Multivariable Calculus
description: This is a first course in differential and integral calculus of functions of several variables. After the introduction of vectors and the 2 and 3-dimensional Euclidean space, functions of several variables are discussed. Functions of two variables will be visualized by surfaces in the three-dimensional space. Further topics include partial derivatives and the total derivative of real-valued and vector-valued functions, the chain rule, directional derivatives, extrema of real-valued functions, constrained extrema and Lagrange multipliers, double and triple integrals, and the change of variables formula in multiple integrals. This course consists of two 75-minute lectures and one 50-minute recitation section.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Statistics
description: This course provides an introduction to basic concepts in probability theory and applied statistics. Topics to be covered include methods of enumeration, properties of random variables, common discrete and continuous distributions, expectations, the central limit theorem, parameter estimation, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and linear regression. Concepts will be illustrated and applied to data using the statistical package Minitab. This course should be taken by students planning on being a math major or minor.

title: Linear Algebra
description: Normally taken after MATH-137. This course presents the basic theory and methods of finite dimensional vector spaces and linear transformations on them. Topics include: matrices and systems of linear equations; vector spaces, bases, and dimension; linear transformations, kernel, image, matrix representation, basis change, and rank; scalar products and orthogonality; determinants; eigenvalues, eigenvectors, diagonalization of symmetric matrices, positive definite matrices. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Proof and Problem Solving
description: Formerly called Foundations of Mathematics, this course will introduce different methods for constructing simple proofs, including forwards/backwards proofs, contradiction, contraposition, and induction. The students will apply these methods to a variety of areas of mathematics, including simple number theory, relations, calculus concepts, and a study of infinity. This course is required for Math Majors and is a prerequisite for many upper level courses. Fall and Spring.

title: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: This course provides an introduction to the theory, techniques, and applications of ordinary differential equations. Topics include first order equations, second order linear equations, series solutions, the method of Laplace transforms, systems of equations, and an introduction to nonlinear equations and stability theory. Fall.

title: Vector Analysis
description: This course is a continuation of multivariable calculus. In Calculus you learned how to integrate along straight lines and on two-dimensional or three-dimensional regions. In this course you will learn how to integrate along curves and on surfaces. In this context some sweeping generalizations of the fundamental theorem of calculus are obtained. We will discuss line integrals, Green's Theorem, surface integrals, the definition of divergence and curl, and the theorems by Gauss and Stokes. Furthermore, some geometric concepts such as surface area and curvature will be introduced. Vector analysis has many applications, in particular to engineering and physics. As an application we may visit Maxwell's equations which are the basic equations of electrodynamics. This course is a capstone to the calculus sequence and students should gain some appreciation of the interplay of calculus and geometry in three-dimensional space.

title: Number Theory
description: Number theory is one of the oldest branches of mathematics and as the name suggests studies the properties of, and the relationships between, particular types of numbers. Of the sets of numbers studied in number theory, the most important is the set of positive integers. In this course we will study divisibility questions among integers, elementary properties of prime numbers, the unique factorization theorem of positive integers, congruences, the Chinese remainder theorem, Fermat's Little theorem and Wilson's theorem, multiplicative functions, Diophantine equations, primitive roots of unity and quadratic reciprocity. We shall also discuss applications of number theory to cryptography, in particular the RSA cryptosystem which is the most commonly used public key cryptosystem, whose security is based on the difficulty of factoring integers. This course is recommended for students with an interest in problem solving and learning proofs.

title: Abstract Algebra
description: This is a rigorous introduction to algebraic structures and their homomorphisms with emphasis on proofs. Topics from group theory will include permutation groups and Sylow theory. Topics from ring theory will include integral domains, unique factorization domains, and polynomial rings. Spring. Prerequisite: MATH-150 and MATH-200.

title: Graph Theory
description: This course treats the basic concepts of graph theory, including graphs and digraphs, trees, networks, Eulerian and Hamiltonian graphs, and crossing numbers. Applications will be given to VLSI chips, RNA folding, and traffic signal design. Map colorings (including the famous four color theorem) will also be considered.

title: Applied Statistical Methods
description: This course, builds on the statistical principles and methods developed in Math-040 or Math-140 and exposes the students to more advanced data analysis methods. After a brief review of exploratory data analysis and principles of statistical inference, the course will cover in detail simple linear regression, multiple regression (including inference and variable selection), logistic regression, one-way and multi-factor ANOVA, and non-parametric methods. Classroom material will be accompanied by hands-on experience using statistical software.

title: Scientific Computing
description: Computers are a central tool in modern science. This course covers some of the main ideas and skills through which computers are used to solve scientific problems. The class will focus on algorithms and theory related to numerical optimization and Monte Carlo methods, two pillars of scientific computing. Ideas from numerical analysis that are important in application will be discussed. Topics will be presented in the context of problems arising in computer science, physical sciences, and economics. Programming is an essential component of this class. The R programming language will be introduced and used throughout the semester. Students need not have any programming background. prerequisites: Linear Algebra and Calculus III

title: Mathematical Biology
description: Biology is becoming more quantitative by the minute. This course will examine applications of mathematics in biological contexts including genetics, ecology, physiology, neuroscience and epidemiology. It will draw on diverse mathematical tools and introduce students to principles of modeling that apply in diverse scientific and technical domains.

title: Tutorials
description: Tutorials on special topics are offered at the discretion of the instructor and with the permission of the Department Curriculum Committee.

title: Analysis I
description: This is the first part of the two semester advanced calculus sequence (Math-310-311) which provides a rigorous treatment of topics in calculus with the emphasis on proofs of major theorems. Topics include the basic properties of the real numbers and n-dimensional Euclidean space, the basic topology of metric spaces including compactness and connectedness, the theory of numerical sequences and series, and the properties of continuous functions on metric spaces. Fall.

title: Analysis II
description: This course is about analysis in several dimension. Topics include the Euclidean spaces, metric spaces, functions of several variables, differentiation and integration of functions of several variables, and the theorems of vector calculus. Most of the topics will be familiar from Multivariable Calculus (math-137). Many proofs will be presented and discussed. Students will be made familiar with some concepts of topology, the basic theory of metric spaces as well as the importance of compact sets in analysis. This course is expected to result in an appreciation for some of the central theorems of analysis such as the Inverse Function Theorem, Taylor's formula, the Change of Variable Formula in multiple integrals, and the Integral Theorems by Green, Gauss, and Stokes.

title: Complex Variables
description: Complex numbers. Analytic functions including exponential, logarithmic and trigonometric functions of a complex variable. Geometric and mapping properties of analytic functions. Contour integration, Cauchy's theorem, the Cauchy integral formula. Power series representations. Residues and poles, with applications to the evaluation of integrals. Conformal mapping and applications as time permits.

title: Applied Statistical Methods II
description: This course introduces the student to various upper-level statistical concepts and methods. Topics include regression modeling for count data (e.g. Poisson regression, alternatives for over-dispersed data, etc.); survival analysis; multivariate analysis topics such as principal components, factor analysis, and discrimination and classification; bootstrapping; and time series analysis. Statistical computing is performed using the statistical freeware tool, R.

title: Applied Time Series Analysis
description: This course will focus on the theory and application of regression methods for statistical modeling and data analysis. Emphasis will be in the following areas: This course discusses the modeling and forecasting of univariate and multivariate time series. Course topics include exploratory methods, white noise and random walk models, smoothing techniques, time series regression, autoregressive moving average (ARMA) models, spectral methods, and advanced topics in multivariate time series (vector autoregressive models, State-space models, and Kalman filtering and smoothing). Theory and applications using R and/or SAS. Examples are drawn from a variety of areas including business, economics and ecology. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: MATH-137 and MATH-140 (undergraduate); Math-503 (graduate)

title: Mathematics and Climate
description: In this course, mathematics and statistics will used to answer current questions of interest in climate science and sustainability, and climate science will be used to motivate and explain techniques from applied mathematics and statistics. The course will emphasize conceptual models that capture important aspects of the Earth's climate system: Energy balance and temperature distribution, ocean circulation patterns such as the Gulf Stream and El NiÃ±o â Southern Oscillation, ice caps and glaciation periods, the carbon cycle, and the biological pump. Mathematical and statistical topics will be selected from theories and methodologies for dynamical systems, bifurcation theory, ordinary and partial differential equations, signal processing, regression analysis, extreme value theory, and data assimilation. Some knowledge of Matlab or R is needed.

title: Mathematics and Climate
description: In this course, mathematics and statistics will used to answer current questions of interest in climate science and sustainability, and climate science will be used to motivate and explain techniques from applied mathematics and statistics. The course will emphasize conceptual models that capture important aspects of the Earth's climate system: Energy balance and temperature distribution, ocean circulation patterns such as the Gulf Stream and El NiÃ±o â Southern Oscillation, ice caps and glaciation periods, the carbon cycle, and the biological pump. Mathematical and statistical topics will be selected from theories and methodologies for dynamical systems, bifurcation theory, ordinary and partial differential equations, signal processing, regression analysis, extreme value theory, and data assimilation. 3 Credits Prerequisites: Linear Algebra (Math-150 or equivalent), Differential Equation (Math-201 or equivalent), Introductory Statistics (Math-040 or equivalent), familiarity with Matlab or R.

title: Probability Theory and Applications
description: Probability Theory and Applications. This is a graduate level introduction to probability theory. Topics include probability measures, independence and conditional probability, discrete and continuous random variables and their properties, joint distributions, moment generating functions, elements of Poisson processes, notions of convergence, Laws of Large Numbers, and the Central Limit Theorem. A working knowledge of multiple integrals and partial derivatives is essential for this course. Some previous exposure to elementary probability and statistics, at least at the level of Math 040, is recommended. This course is not based on measure theory. Textbook: A Course in Probability, Neil A. Weiss (Addison Wesley, 2005) Prerequisites: Math 137, Math 150

title: Deterministic Mathematical Models
description: A graduate level course in basic mathematical modeling with emphasis on derivation and analysis of models via differential equation methods including variational analysis. Includes both ordinary and partial differential equations of first order. Applications of ordinary differential equations include age-structured populations; partial differential studies will be directed towards conservation laws and traffic flow. Fall and spring. Prerequisites: multivariable calculus and linear algebra.

title: Mathematical Statistics
description: This is a first course in the mathematical theory of statistical inference. The emphasis is on classical methods, with appropriate attention also to Bayesian methods. Topics include principles of data reduction (sufficiency and sufficient statistics, likelihood, invariance); point estimation (method of moments, maximum likelihood, Bayes estimators) and associated criteria (mean squared error, unbiasedness, consistency); some asymptotic properties of point estimators; construction of and criteria for hypothesis tests (error probabilities and power, most powerful tests, bias); asymptotics of some large sample tests; construction of and criteria for interval estimate; and elements of decision theory and applications to statistical inference (Bayes rules, minimax), analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA, F-test, contrasts), linear regression (least squares, tests for model parameters, pointwise and simultaneous estimation and prediction), and nonparametric methods (e.g., sign test, rank sum test, Wilcoxin test, Mann-Whitney test).

title: Numerical Methods
description: This course introduces numerical algorithms for solving nonlinear equations; systems of linear equations; approximation and interpolation; numerical integration; fast Fourier transform and least squares methods, and ordinary differential equations. We will also discuss issues associated with computer arithmetic. Error analysis and convergence of numerical schemes will be examined as well. This course requires knowledge of multivariable calculus, linear algebra and differential equations. Some knowledge in one computer programming language is required. Spring semester; fall and spring starting in 2010.

title: Mathematical and Statistical Computing
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with programming background sufficient for graduate level study in mathematics and statistics. The course gives an introduction to SAS, R and Matlab, in this order. R and SAS are the statistical packages most widely used by practicing statisticians. This portion of the course will be structured around statistical methods and examples will be worked out using both computing environments. Statistical topics to be covered include data management, simulation, descriptive statistics, graphical displays, hypothesis testing, correlation, regression models, and simple multivariate analysis methods. The introduction to Matlab will cover the basic structure of the language, commands, M-files, function, loops, and graphing. Mathematical topics will be drawn from numerical linear algebra and selected topics in analysis as time permits.

title: Introduction to Financial Mathematics
description: This is a course on mathematical finance, emphasizing mathematical models and techniques for pricing financial derivative instruments. Financial markets of stocks, bonds, futures and options; present value analysis; Brownian motion and Ito's formula; option pricing with random walk models, partial differential equations (Black-Scholes), and binomial trees. Both the constant and stochastic volatility cases will be considered. Spring semester.

title: Stochastic Simulation
description: This course is an introduction to Monte Carlo methods and computer modeling of stochastic systems. Monte Carlo topics that we will cover include random variable generation, expectation estimation with confidence interval formation, importance sampling, stochastic optimization, MCMC algorithms and sampling of Brownian motion. In developing these topics we will emphasize their role both in statistical inference and modeling. This course will also introduce the students to some fundamental stochastic processes such as discrete state Markov chains, Poisson processes, and Brownian motion; as well as an array of important stochastic models. Computer programming will be a central part of this course.

title: Regression Methods and Generalized Linear Models
description: This course will focus on the theory and application of regression methods for statistical modeling and data analysis. Emphasis will be in the following areas: simple and multiple regression, inference and prediction, model building and diagnostics, model selection and validation, analysis of variance (ANOVA), analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), generalized linear models, logistic and Poisson regression and other extensions as time permits (e.g. mixed models, nonlinear regression). Practical issues involved in implementation of these methods will be presented using statistical software packages SAS and R based on example problems from a wide range of applications.

title: Data Mining
description: This course presents an introduction to computational and statistical methods for exploring large data sets and discovering patterns in them. Visualization and other exploratory methods will be used throughout the course. The course surveys methods in predictive modeling (classification) including decision trees, NaÃ¯ve Bayes and nearest neighbor methods. In the process, we will study discretization, data normalization and attribute selection as well as sampling methods like cross-validation, bagging and boosting. Other topics will include cluster analysis, association analysis, anomaly detection and text mining. For all topics studied, students will work with various real and constructed data sets to see the impact of different distributions on the performance of the algorithms. A variety of performance metrics will be studied. The software Weka, R and Excel will be used in the course, although only basic knowledge of R and Excel will be assumed. Fall semester.

title: Categorical Data Analysis
description: This course deals with statistical models for the analysis of categorical data. Topics to be covered include inference for contingency tables, generalized linear models with emphasis on logistic regression, Poisson regression, and models for clustered/repeated measures.. The goal of the course is not to memorize formulae, but to understand and apply statistical concepts and techniques to real data. Prerequisites: Background in maximum likelihood theory and linear models are required (at the level of Math-651).

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study in graduate level mathematics and statistics. Topics will be determined by individual needs. Course is restricted to graduate students in the mathematics and statistics graduate program.

title: Knights of Old & Harry Potter
description: This course explores the medievalism of J.K. Rowlingâs Harry Potter novels. To do this we go back to medieval antecedents from the 12th-15th centuries, which allow us to contrast and compare the old and the new. You will read masterpieces of imaginative storytelling from French, English, and Latin medieval literature as well as selected volumes of J.K. Rowlingâs Harry Potter novels and her associated smaller works. The old and the new are linked thematically in that they are all narratives about growing up and finding oneâs identity: a complex, mysterious, and sometimes arduous process that the hero experiences as a magical world where the natural laws governing human existence are suspended, the unexpected is bound to occur, and marvels are reserved for the chosen few. The choices to be made, dangers to be faced, and discoveries to be made, all help the hero to relate to his own world and the public world outside. Friendship, love, good and evil, authority and power, success and failure, responsibility, belief and knowledge, self-knowledge and inexplicable mysteryâthese are essential experiences that structure and develop identity, in real life and in literature, today as well as yesteryear. All readings and discussion are in English. The course fulfills the Humanities & Writing II requirement. It also counts toward the Medieval Studies Major and Minor, and the SFS Certificate in Medieval Studies. The course is also offered as FREN-040-01.

title: History and legend in medieval Britain
description: Focuses on contrasting modern views of British medieval history with medieval perceptions. The historical evidence and the legends for figures such as Arthur, King Alfred, Thomas Becket, Robin Hood, and Richard II are compared. Prerequisites: None

title: Senior Seminar: Research
description: This research and reading seminar is required for senior Medieval Studies majors. Minors and SFS certificate students wishing to enroll and write a thesis are welcome to contact the Program Director, Prof. David Goldfrank at goldfrad@georgetown.edu.

title: Medieval Studies: Thesis Seminar
description: This course constitutes the second half of the year-long thesis process for seniors in the Medieval Studies Program. After taking MVST 348 in the fall, the students taking MVST 349 write their theses, c. 50 pages, in the spring term, to be completed by early April in consultation with their thesis advisors and the Director of the Medieval Studies Program. Spring.

title: Medieval Studies: Thesis Seminar
description: This course constitutes the second half of the year-long thesis process for seniors in the Medieval Studies Program. After taking MVST 348 in the fall, the students taking MVST 349 write their theses, c. 50 pages, in the spring term, to be completed by early April in consultation with their thesis advisors and the Director of the Medieval Studies Program. Spring.

title: Social Propagation of Global Infectious Disease
description: In this course we will study the hypothesis that the world has become a global system for the propagation of infectious disease. We will examine three general questions throughout the course. First, we will examine how social inequality helps structure the global distribution of contracting and recovering from infectious diseases in the developed and developing world. While long considered a conquered disease in the Western, middle class world, tuberculosis smoldered among the poor throughout the world and has âre-emergedâ in virulent, drug-resistant forms that threaten rich and poor alike. In studies of the United States and Brazil, we will focus in detail on how the experience of poverty affects peoplesâ sense of risk, particularly for sexually transmitted diseases such as AIDS and cervical cancer. Second, we will critically examine the role of âcultureâ in the spread and treatment of infectious disease. For example, what is the role of the cultural subordination of women in the spread of sexually transmitted disease? How do the cultural presuppositions of bureaucratic public health affect and potentially distort the epidemiological portrayal of infectious disease? Third, we will examine how government policies, procedures and practices affect local expressions of global epidemics. For example, we will critically assess the accusation that the Mbeki government exacerbated the HIV/AIDS in South Africa through âunscientificâ beliefs and practices banning antiretroviral mediations. We will close the course by examining the controversy about the âeradicationâ of smallpox.

title: Interdisciplinary Global Infectious Diseases
description: This course will present a comprehensive view of the global infectious disease landscape through a series of lectures that will first introduce the student to the biology and pathogenic manifestations of specific infectious agents followed by a corresponding lecture on a relevant health policy or program implementation example by an expert with direct experience in the subject. These presentations will cover âhigh impactâ as well as neglected tropical diseases and potential pandemic agents. This course is designed for graduate and undergraduate students from diverse backgrounds that intend to work in an interdisciplinary environment and will benefit from a thorough understanding of all aspects surrounding global health issues.

title: Microbiology of Biological Threat Agents & Emerging Infectious Diseases
description: This course will cover NIH bioterrorism agents (categories A-C), which can be utilized as biological weapons. The microbiology of these agents will focus on structure, pathology, and virulence factors. The immune response to these agents will be presented. Viral agents will include Variola and hemorrhagic fevers (Ebola and Lassa). Bacterial agents will include B. anthracis, Yersinia pestis (plague), and Francisella tularensis (tularemia). Emerging infectious disease threats such as Nipah, Hantavirus, and SARS will also be covered.

title: Biological and Radiological Safety
description: This course will cover the biological safety levels (BSL) for containment working with biological agents - both recombinant DNA and emerging infectious diseases. Radiological safety - will discuss the issues of dirty bombs, containment, and health issues associated with radioactive materials that bioterrorists might employ.

title: Bioterrorism
description: This course will examine the use of biological weapons. Terrorism in its modern form will be discussed as well as which biological agents are most likely to be used, their techniques for deployment, and their prevention and control. Specific disease agents to be covered will include anthrax, plague, botulism, smallpox, tularemia, and viral hemorrhagic fevers.

title: Sociological Perspectives on Biodefense
description: This course will critically examine political and organizational controversies about defending against terrorism including bioterrorism. The course opens by studying the debate about terrorism as a type of asymmetrical warfare. What accounts for terrorist attacks against the United States and our allies? What is the relationship between foreign policy, law enforcement and warfare in our response to terrorism? What constraints, if any, should we apply to ourselves in responding to terrorism?
