And e-business and social networking are rapidly changing many features of the way we do business today. It is important for managers to understand how to use e-business and social technology to improve how their companies set up supply chains, sell products and services, and present themselves to current and potential customers. We will explore this fascinating field through readings, class discussions, and hands-on instruction in some important tools for e-commerce development. The course will culminate with presentations of business plans, web sites, and social media plans for new e-commerce companies, developed in groups.

title: Intensive First Level Persian I
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive First Level Persian II
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive Second Level Persian I
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Intensive Second Level Persian II
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Advanced Persian I
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: Advanced Persian II
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: Translating Persian Media
description: Translating Persian Media In this class, students will learn both how to translate Persian media from Persian to English, and how to analyze and interpret Persian media in its political, social and economic context in the target language. This means that in addition to translation, we will also focus on each of the four main skills of foreign language use: reading, writing, speaking and listening. Students enrolling in this class should have taken five semesters of university-level Persian courses, or have equivalent experience with the language. Please contact the professor if you are unsure whether or not your current skill level is appropriate for the class. This course will prepare students to take the Oral Language Proficiency Exam required by various departments and programs within the university. Contact: Farima S.Mostowfi fsm7@georgetown.edu

title: Films of the Persian-Speaking World
description: TBA

title: Persian Language Media
description: The purpose of this seminar-style course is to follow current affairs by looking at Persian-language newspapers, websites, weblogs, and TV and radio channels both inside and outside of Iran. The class meets once a week to examine politics, culture, and society in Iran and the wider "Persianate" world (including Central Asia and Afghanistan) through the prism of the media. We will compare the "old" and the "new" media to discuss how Twitter, YouTube, and Facebook have given voiceless young Iranians a new tool to speak about social, religious and political issues. We will also analyze the Iranian government's extensive use of media technology. Students will focus on topics such as Iran's foreign policy, nuclear ambitions, love-hate relationship with the United States, economy, and youth culture. They will acquire basic knowledge of Iran's governmental institutions, factional politics, public life and intellectual trends. Students will learn how to write the news reports while improving their reading, speaking, and comprehension skills. Two years of studying Persian or the instructor's permission is required.

title: General Principles of Pharmacology
description: This course consists of that part of PHAR-501 which deals with the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, and elimination. This course begins in mid-August. Permission of instructor required.

title: Graduate Pharmacology
description: This course includes both a broad survey of pharmacology and detailed discussions of experimental approaches used in pharmacological research. Discussions of primary literature articles related to the major lecture topics are included. PBIO501 and PHAR611 are prerequisites.

title: Advanced General Pharmacology
description: This course consists of an in-depth analysis of selected major units presented in PHAR-504. PHAR504 is a prerequisite.

title: Modern Methods in Molecular Biology
description: Introduction and evaluation of most of the commonly used methods in molecular biology.

title: Ethical Issues in Scientific Research
description: Discussions of ethical questions and dilemmas facing scientists today.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology
description: This minicourse presents an overview of some aspects of pharmacology. It begins with basic principles of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics. Neuropharmacology, including the autonomic nervous system and the central nervous system are then covered. Cardiovascular pharmacology and its central control is discussed. Finally, there is a specialized discussion of the role of voltage and ligand-gated channels in controlling cellular function.

title: Drugs, the Brain, and Behavior I
description: This course uses a interdisciplinary approach to study function and dysfunction of the brain. We emphasize basic concepts in neuroscience, examine the cellular and molecular underpinnings of various abnormalities of the nervous system, and explore neuropharmacology as therapy and as a tool to study brain function. Special emphasis is placed on 1) bridging basic neural mechanisms (neurotransmitters, circuits, systems) and higher brain processes (emotion, cognition, memory) and 2) the utility and limits of animals models of human behavior and disease. The course will involve lectures, student presentations, an discussion of primary literature. A term paper focusing on the pharmacology of a disorder covered in class will be a requirement of the course. Fall: stress and homeostasis, reward and addiction (drugs, eating disorders), mood regulation (anxiety and depression) and attention, social behavior, and cognitive control (schizophrenia, autism, and ADHD).

title: Drugs, the Brain, and Behavior II
description: This course uses a interdisciplinary approach to study function and dysfunction of the brain. We emphasize basic concepts in neuroscience, examine the cellular and molecular underpinnings of various abnormalities of the nervous system, and explore neuropharmacology as therapy and as a tool to study brain function. Special emphasis is placed on 1) bridging basic neural mechanisms (neurotransmitters, circuits, systems) and higher brain processes (emotion, cognition, memory) and 2) the utility and limits of animals models of human behavior and disease. The course will involve lectures, student presentations, an discussion of primary literature. A term paper focusing on the pharmacology of a disorder covered in class will be a requirement of the course. Spring: memory systems (Alzheimer's), motor systems (Parkinson's, Huntington's, Multiple Sclerosis, Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis), Neural injury, prion disease, HIV-dementia, and epilepsy. Fall is *not *a prerequisite for spring.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Medicinal Plants and Pharmacognosy
description: Plants have provided drug models for more than a quarter of pharmaceuticals in use today, including digoxin, warfarin, morphine, and Taxol. This course will cover the traditional and modern uses of medicinal plants, and compare the pharmacocologic effects of herbal medicinal products and synthetic drugs. The risks and benefits of St. John's wort, echinacea, ginkgo, ginger, and other popular phytomedicines will be discussed.

title: Advanced Topics in Nutrition
description: Description: This course examines a range of topics on human nutrition, including macronutrients, micronutrients, the effects of diet on health, and claims and evidence for use of megadoses of vitamins, minerals and amino acids for treating specific health conditions. The emphasis is on evidence-based nutritional information, utilizing the medical and epidemiological literature to analyze the impact of food, diets and nutritional supplements on human health. The class will also address food preparation and cooking to facilitate the adoption of nutritional principles into your diet. Special topics include breastfeeding, exercise nutrition and sports supplements, the nutrition of beverages, genetically modified foods, prebiotics & probiotics, , the science of dieting, the evolutionary origins of the human diet, and the endocrinology of adipose tissue and obesity. Grading is based on the performance of several assignments, a paper and a final exam.

title: Nutraceuticals, Herbs and Health Care
description: This interactive and interdisciplinary course covers the use of botanical medicines, dietary supplements, and nutraceuticals within and outside of conventional medicine. The course combines faculty and guest expert presentations with class research projects on the regulation and marketing of herbs, vitamins, and nutraceuticals and their potential impact on public health and public policy.

title: Pharmacology I - basic principles
description: Fundamental concepts in pharmacology such as drug-receptor interactions via radioligand binding, functional agonisim, antagonism, allosteric modulation as well as drug metablolism and pharmacokinetics will be covered. The course also includes an overview of experimental design and statistical analyses of scientific data. Meets MW 3-5. Quizzes every day. Final at end. Recommended (but not required) text is: Rang and Dale's Pharmacology Either 6th or 7th ed (used 6th is cheap on Amazon) Syllabus on website: //pharmacology.georgetown.edu/courses/phar611.html

title: Introduction to Regulatory Science
description: Regulatory science is an applied scientific discipline that generates and makes use of evidence-based knowledge to inform decision-making about the safety and benefits of medical products. This survey course will examine the myriad issues that arise in the field of regulatory science, and will introduce students to fundamental principles of regulatory science, describe the differences between regulatory science and regulatory affairs, and provide an overview of the innovative clinical research tools of regulatory science. Building upon the Food and Drug Administrationâs (FDA) Strategic Plan: Advancing Regulatory Science at FDA (August 2011), individual classes will focus on the eight priority areas defined by the FDA as necessary for advancing regulatory science. Expert lecturers from Georgetown University and the FDA will present case studies, best practice examples, and guide discussion of key issues. EIGHT PRIORITY ISSUES DEFINED BY THE FDA 1. Modernize Toxicology to Enhance Product Safety 2. Stimulate Innovation in Clinical Evaluations and Personalized Medicine to Improve Product Development and Patient Outcomes 3. Support New Approaches to Improve Product Manufacturing and Quality 4. Ensure FDA Readiness to Evaluate Innovative Emerging Technologies 5. Harness Diverse Data through Information Sciences to Improve Health Outcomes 6. Implement a New Prevention-Focused Food Safety System to Protect Public Health 7. Facilitate Development of Medical Countermeasures to Protect Against Threats to U.S. and Global Health and Security 8. Strengthen Social and Behavioral Science to Help Consumers and Professionals Make Informed Decisions about Regulated Products COURSE TOPICS 1. Fundamentals of Regulatory Science 2. Regulatory Affairs and the Law 3. Regulatory Science and Bioethics 4. Clinical Research Tools of Regulatory Science 5. Toxicology and Product Safety 6. Personalized Medicine and Patient-Reported Outcomes 7. Product Manufacturing and Quality 8. Genetics and Product Development 9. Novel Technologies for Innovative Medical Products 10. Bioinformatics 11. Food Safety 12. Biosurveillance, Medical Countermeasures, and Global Health (Part I) 13. Biosurveillance, Medical Countermeasures, and Global Health (Part II) Meets Weds 6:00-8:00

title: Research
description: Individualized research tutorial with laboratory component. Open only to MS students in the Pharmacology program. Graded.

title: Research II
description: Individualized research tutorial with laboratory component. Open only to MS students in the Pharmacology program. Requires permission of instructor. Graded.

title: Continuing Registration in Pharmacology
description: Continuing Registration is for students who have completed all requirements for the Ph.D. degree but who have not yet graduated. M.S. students can use this mechanism to maintain their student status while finishing needed requirements.

description: Research for Doctoral Dissertation

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Thesis research leading to a PhD dissertation. This section 03 is for the student who is still in coursework, but also in thesis research.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: An Introduction to Ethics is taught in the FALL, SPRING, and SUMMER semesters. Please scroll down page to view individual section course descriptions: SPRING 2014 Sections 1 - 10 Pinkard, Terry In this course, we will be concerned with how we orient and guide our lives in terms of making judgments about what is best for ourselves, for others, and our communities and what role moral judgments play in that kind of orientation. Among the basic ideas we will be covering will be those concerning the role of happiness, freedom, and being true to oneself. We will be asking ourselves questions such as, âWhat is happiness?â and âWhat is the right conception of happiness?â This will require us also to ask some very general questions about how â or even whether â we can justify any particular answer that we might give, either to ourselves or to other people and why we might think (or might not think) that we should be called upon to justify such things. We will also be asking ourselves why any of us should even care about being moral. To do this, we will be examining four classic texts from Aristotle, Hume, Kant, and Nietzsche; and we will be looking at the work of a contemporary philosopher, Charles Taylor. These texts do not, of course, exhaust the scope of moral philosophy. The selection of texts is intended to give you an introduction into the way that philosophers have talked and argued about the topic of morality and to help you in thinking your way through these issues on your own. We will be seeking to develop our understanding of these texts both historically and philosophically. Section 11 Carse, Alisa Section 12 Keown, John. This course provides an introduction, predominantly from a ânatural lawâ perspective, to some of the most interesting ethical questions concerning life and death. The course is divided into three parts. Part I comprises a short introduction to natural law, comparing and contrasting it with a number of alternative ethical approaches. Part II examines several controversial questions concerning life and death in the bioethical context, including abortion and euthanasia. Part III considers issues of life and death beyond the bioethical context, including capital punishment, war, and the possession of nuclear weapons. Classroom discussion, which will often revolve around real-life cases, will be encouraged. Assessment: three papers (90%) and class participation (10%) Section 13 Withy, Kate In this course, we will explore both the foundations of Western ethical thought and the contemporary ethical imperative of authenticity. In the first part, we will cover the major normative theories in the Western tradition, including virtue ethics (Aristotle), deontology (Kant), utilitarianism (Mill) and the ethics of care. In the second part, we will critically examine the nature and history of the ideal of âbeing authenticâ. We will identify early expressions of the imperative of authenticity, read several contemporary analyses of it, and assess the picture of human nature on which it is based. The course concludes with a discussion of power and freedom, using ideas from Foucault to question the way in which we are constituted as ethical subjects. Students will learn to read difficult texts closely and to analyse the logical structure of arguments and concepts. There will be frequent tests and short writing assignments. FALL 2013 Sections 1 - 11 Lichtenberg, Judy Here are the main things we will do in this course. We will ask, and try to answer, some basic ethical questions. Are people fundamentally self-interested? Why should a person act morally? Does morality require a religious foundation? (Or, as Dostoevsky put it, is everything permitted if God is dead?) Is there a universal morality that applies across cultures? We will read some of the great philosophers, including Plato, Aristotle, Immanuel Kant, and John Stuart Mill, who articulated theories and ideas that continue to inform thinking about ethics today. We will also read work of some contemporary philosophers. Utilizing these writings, we will investigate some contemporary moral problems facing us as individuals and as members of societies. Possible topics include punishment and the criminal justice system; abortion; the moral status of nonhuman animals; poverty and inequality (domestic and global); how to treat yourself and others. Section 13 Withy, Kate In this course, we will explore both the foundations of Western ethical thought and the contemporary ethical imperative of authenticity. In the first part, we will cover the major normative theories in the Western tradition, including virtue ethics (Aristotle), deontology (Kant), utilitarianism (Mill) and the ethics of care. In the second part, we will critically examine the nature and history of the ideal of âbeing authenticâ. We will identify early expressions of the imperative of authenticity, read several contemporary analyses of it, and assess the picture of human nature on which it is based. The course concludes with a discussion of power and freedom, using ideas from Foucault to question the way in which we are constituted as ethical subjects. Students will learn to read difficult texts closely and to analyse the logical structure of arguments and concepts. There will be frequent tests and short writing assignments. Section 14 Murphy, Mark what is morally right or morally wrong is a sort of skepticism: either there is no such thing as moral rightness and wrongness or, at the very least, we cannot have any confidence in any of our answers to these questions. There is somewhat less skepticism, though, about the idea of what people have good reason to do. In this course, we are going to think together about the various factors that are relevant to whether one has good reason to do something. We will then consider how reflection on these questions about good reasons is relevant to thinking clearly about morality. No textbook; all readings will be placed on reserve. Four short papers, two exams, and prepared and active attendance are required.

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: An Introduction to Philosophy is taught in the FALL, SPRING, and SUMMER semesters. Please scroll down page to view individual section course descriptions: SPRING 2014 Sections 1-11 Wetzel, Linda This course is a general introduction to philosophy, in which we read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. We will also watch some film selections and use the situations depicted therein as a springboard for discussion. For example, we will see situations in which it is pretty clear that the right thing to do is whatever leads to the best outcome for all concerned. This suggests that it is always the case that the right thing to do is whatever leads to the best outcome for all concerned (utilitarianism). But we will also witness apparent counterexamples. So our first question is whether utilitarianism is true. Next we will see examples which lead us to wonder whether we have sufficient grounds for thinking there is even a physical world. You think, you reason, you have plenty of experiences, but are they experiences of anything? Is there any reason to believe reality is as you suppose? Third, we will assume you do have a body, and are not just a disembodied mind, and will try to figure out what the relation is between your mind and your body. And fourth, we will ask whether you act freely sometimes or are merely a cog in the relentless causal machine that is the universe. Requirements: 3 or 4 short papers; 2 exams; participation in class discussions. Text: Richard Fumerton and Diane Jeske (editors): Introducing Philosophy through Film: Key Texts, Discussion, and Film Selections. ISBN: 978-1-4051-7101-4. (Paperback). Â©2009, Wiley-Blackwell. Section 12 Kukla, Rebecca This course will introduce students to some central issues in philosophy, central figures in the history of philosophy, and techniques of philosophical argument through a consideration of the question of what it is to be a person. More specifically, we will focus on questions such as: what is to have a mind, or a body? How do we know what goes on in our own minds or those of other people? Does free will exist, and what is the relationship between freedom, determinism, and responsibility? Are persons essentially social - that is, does being a person depend on being a person intertwined with other persons? Section 13 Mulherin, Thomas Philosophy's living history makes it nearly unique among academic disciplines. Methods, arguments, and ideas from throughout the history of philosophy continue to inform contemporary approaches to philosophical problems. Likewise, many contemporary philosophical questions are recognizable descendants of historical questions. This complicates the project of giving an introduction to philosophy, for in addition to introducing the tools and methods of philosophy, one may also introduce its history. Simply put, an introduction to philosophy may show both how to do philosophy and how it has been done. This course folds these tasks together, teaching students how to do philosophy by seeing how it has been done. To this end, we will undertake close readings of methodologically diverse texts from the history of philosophy by authors such as Plato, Descartes, and Nietzsche, as well as some thematically relevant contemporary texts by authors such as Peter Singer and John Perry. These readings converge on a single questionââWhat kind of thing am I?ââand its implications for issues including personal identity, survival after death, and ethics. FALL 2013 Sections 1-10 Lance, Mark This course will introduce you to some central issues in philosophy, central figures in the history of philosophy, and techniques of philosophical argument through a consideration of the question of what it is to be a person. More specifically, we will look at two questions connected to essential features of personhood: what is it to have a mind? and What is it to be free? Course assignments will consist of active participation in discussion in recitation sections, 2 papers, and 2 take-home exams. This course presupposes no prior philosophy training. Section 11 Bronstein, David This course is an introduction to philosophy through a close study of some of the great works in the history of Western philosophy. Our focus will be on works by Plato, Descartes, and Hume, among others. We will examine and compare these authors' answers to a number of questions that still occupy the interest of philosophers today, including: What is knowledge and how do we acquire it? What is the fundamental nature of reality? Does God exist? Is there an answer to skepticism? What is the relationship between the mind and the body? Section 12 Lu-Adler, Huaping This course provides a general introduction to the theoretical (as opposed to practical) philosophy. We will read important historical and contemporary texts on these topics: mind and body, knowledge and certainty, science and method, personal identity and freedom, existence of God. We will recognize the historical continuity of philosophical inquiries. We aim to learn how to do philosophy by discussing and analyzing how it has been done.

title: Political and Social Thought
description: This course is taught in both the FALL and SPRING semesters. See below for individual sections. FALL 2013 Sections 1-6 Richardson, Henry The ideal of democracy is quite a complex one. As children, we get introduced to it via the idea of majority rule; yet no contemporary government operates on the basis of simple majority rule. The scale of modern governments introduces a need for representative government, which is already a serious complication. Further, the potential for the powerful to dominate, whether absent institutions of majority rule or despite them, must be addressed. Deciding policies or picking rulers by lottery would be fair and impartial in a way, and yet in democracies, we think, citizens should debate policies. Why? Individual rights, as often enshrined in constitutions, do delimit the scope for majority rule as expressed via elected legislatures; but are these rights (such as the rights of free speech and assembly) hindrances to democracy or necessary props of it? Governments also have come to rely on large bureaucracies to work out and implement the policies that legislatures decide upon. Does this reliance destroy the possibility of rule by the people? And what does rule by the people have to do, anyway, with majority rule? In this course, we will read some of the main texts of Western political philosophy. They have been chosen so as to put you in a position, by the end of the semester, to understand democracy in a deeper and more nuanced way. This deeper understanding will enable you better to understand why it is important that governments be democratic and what is required for them fully to live up to the ideal of democracy. Each of the authors we will read makes an essential and distinctive contribution to that ideal. Whether all of their contributions should be incorporated into that ideal is a controversial question; but at least you will be equipped, by the end of the course, to address those questions about the democratic ideal in a thoughtful way. Assigned texts: Machiavelli, The Prince and the Discourses (McGraw-Hill). Hobbes, Leviathan, ed. Curley (Hackett). Locke, Political Writings, ed. D. Wootton (Mentor). Rousseau, The Social Contract and Discourses (BN Publishing) Kant, Perpetual Peace and Other Essays (Hackett) Rawls, Justice as Fairness: A Restatement (Harvard). Sections 7-12 Douglass, Robert This course is designed as an undergraduate introduction to political and social theory. It is a required course in the undergraduate program of the School of Foreign Service, and enrollment in the course is limited to SFS students. The course focuses on selected major figures in the history of the Western political tradition. The reading assignments are drawn entirely from primary texts, such as Plato's Republic, Hobbes' Leviathan, and J.S.Mill's On Liberty. Students are expected to spend most of the time they devote to this course in a careful reading of the assigned texts. Most of the time in the meetings of the full class is devoted to lectures. These sessions are supplemented by weekly meetings of discussion groups led by teaching assistants, which are meant to facilitate discussion of the assigned readings. Each student enrolled in the class is assigned to a discussion group and is expected to attend its meetings on a regular basis. Written work in the course includes two short papers and a final examination.

title: Ethics: Bioethics/Beginning of Life/The Law
description: 1. Introduction to ethics 2. Introduction to ethics 3. Ethics and law 4. Human embryo research in the US and UK 5. Cloning and public policy 6. Cloning and public policy 7. Abortion law in the US, Germany and the UK 8. The Abortion debate 9. The âmorning afterâ pill and âpartial birthâ abortion 10. Eugenic and gender abortion 11.Infanticide 12.Infanticide 13. Non-treatment of disabled neonates: the Arthur case 14. Separating conjoined twins: the case of Re A 15. Separating conjoined twins: the case of Re A Required texts: C Kaczor, The Ethics of Abortion (Routledge, 2011) Human Cloning & Human Dignity (PCB, 2002) (Online at: www.bioethics.gov/reports/cloningreport) Required initial reading: Alfonso Gomez-Lobo, Morality and the Human Goods (GUP, 2002) Peter Singer, Rethinking Life and Death (OUP,1995) Raanan Gillon, Philosophical Medical Ethics (Wiley, 1986) Recommended initial reading: Helen Watt, Life and Death in Healthcare Ethics (Routledge, 2000) John Harris, The Value of Life (Routledge, 1985) Beauchamp and Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (OUP, 7th ed, 2012) Assessment: 45% in-class written test; 45% take-home paper; 10% class participation. After an introduction to three different approaches to ethics, this course analyses several topical, controversial ethical questions at the beginning of life, including embryonic stem cell research; cloning; abortion; infanticide; court-ordered Caesarians; non-treatment of disabled neonates; and the separation of conjoined twins. Classroom discussion, often around real-life cases which have confronted the courts, is encouraged.

title: Ethics: Bioethics
description: Ethics: Bioethics is taught in the FALL, SPRING, and SUMMER semesters. Please scroll down page to view individual section course descriptions: SPRING 2014 Section 1 Stohr, Karen Bioethics is a fundamentally interdisciplinary subject, encompassing humanistic, scientific, and legal perspectives. It is also highly practical insofar it aims at helping us resolve pressing real-life ethical problems. In this course, we will bring the theoretical resources of philosophical ethics to bear on complex problems in medical practice, scientific research, and health care policy. We will also employ narratives from literature, journalism, and film as a way of improving our ethical understanding and remaining aware of the implications of these discussions for real human lives. Specific topics to be covered include genetic enhancement, reproductive technology, abortion, end of life care, physician-assisted suicide, truth-telling in medicine, distribution of health care resources, and clinical research in domestic and international contexts. The course will incorporate ongoing discussion of Catholic perspectives on bioethical issues and health care practice. Section 2 Sachs, Carl Bioethics is a deeply interdisciplinary exploration of medicine and health-care, environmental ethics, and emergent technologies. In this course, we will see what happens when the sublime purity of abstract theory interacts with the complex ambiguity of concrete situations that arise in medical practice, scientific research, and health care policy. Discussion will focus on real life case studies and contemporary debates. Specific topics to cover include the doctor-patient relationship, genetic engineering and neuroenhancement, reproductive technology and abortion, end of life care and physician-assisted suicide, environmental ethics, the ethics of research, and the social justice of global healthcare. Section 3 Hickey, Colin This course serves as an introduction to contemporary bioethics, an interdisciplinary subject that investigates and analyzes moral issues arising in medicine, research, and health policy. The course has two main orientations. First, we will need some grounding in theory and methodology, so we will discuss some of the best developed moral theories coming out of philosophy (Utilitarianism, Kantianism, Virtue Theory) and different ways to approach bioethical problems (principlism vs. casuistry). Second, we will use a wide array of case studies (both real and fictional) to look at a selection of modern bioethical issues, including: beginning of life and reproductive technology, human enhancement, animal research, allocation of healthcare resources, end of life care, etc. Weâll read work from Maggie Little, Peter Singer, James Rachels, Nick Bostrom, Thomas Pogge, Tom Beauchamp, Tina Rulli, among others. Students will be responsible for three (approx. 5 pg.) papers and active class participation. Sections 4 & 6 Herbert, Cassie Bioethics is a deeply interdisciplinary subject that permeates our lives. In this course, weâll bring the theoretical resources of philosophical ethics to bear on complex problems in medical practice, scientific research, and health care policy. Discussion will focus on real life case studies and contemporary debates. Specific topics weâll cover include genetic enhancement and body modification, reproductive technology and abortion, end of life care and physician-assisted suicide, and clinical research. This course will be heavily discussion driven and grades will be based on class participation, four short writing assignments, and a group presentation. FALL 2013 Section 1 Stohr, Karen Bioethics is a fundamentally interdisciplinary subject, encompassing humanistic, scientific, and legal perspectives. It is also highly practical insofar it aims at helping us resolve pressing real-life ethical problems. In this course, we will bring the theoretical resources of philosophical ethics to bear on complex problems in medical practice, scientific research, and health care policy. We will also employ narratives from literature, journalism, and film as a way of improving our ethical understanding and remaining aware of the implications of these discussions for real human lives. Specific topics to be covered include genetic enhancement, reproductive technology, abortion, end of life care, physician-assisted suicide, truth-telling in medicine, distribution of health care resources, and clinical research in domestic and international contexts. The course will incorporate ongoing discussion of Catholic perspectives on bioethical issues and health care practice. Section 2 Cudney, Paul The course will begin with a general introduction to ethical theory, especially as it relates to bioethics. This will consist of a quick overview of deontological and consequentialist ethical theories and a more detailed examination of two prevalent bioethical methodologies, principlism and casuistry. Once we have a general understanding of these theories and methods, we will jump into thinking about some contemporary issues. The issues on which we will put the most focus are: abortion, health resource allocation, medical paternalism, patient autonomy, human enhancement, end of life care, and physician-assisted death. The goal of the course is to provide us with the opportunity to develop both our ability to think clearly about these crucial, contemporary issues and our own positions with respect to them. Students will be evaluated according to: class participation (10%), five pop quizzes (10%), two four page papers that summarize and explain an issue discussed in class (20% each), and one eight page critical response paper that both explains the relevant details of an issue and goes on to defend a position on that issue (40%). The only required text for the course will be Beauchamp and Childress's Principles of Biomedical Ethics 7th Ed. Section 3 Rieder, Travis In its broadest sense, bioethics is the field that investigates ethical issues arising in medicine, biological research and the life sciences. The umbrella term âbioethicsâ, then, includes: medical ethics, research ethics; environmental ethics; population ethics; ethics of biotechnologies; and much more. Bioethics is also intimately intertwined with politics, public policy and the law. The goal of this course is to provide a broad introduction to the study of bioethics. Our time will be divided into roughly two sections: first, methodology and moral theory; and second, specific bioethical issues and case studies. In the first

title: Ethics: Autonomy and Health
description: âAutonomy' refers to the ability of persons to live their lives according to their own values and life plans. To respect autonomy, then, is to protect individuals against unfair coercion, deception, and paternalism. In many cases it is morally justifiable (and even praiseworthy or obligatory) to interfere with a person against her will when it will benefit her or society at large; for example, forced drug rehabilitation for an addict and laws against smoking in public are considered morally legitimate by many who would otherwise champion peopleâs right to choose for themselves how to live their lives. This class will focus on the moral importance and limitations of autonomous decision-making in medical settings. We will begin by looking at autonomy through the lens of different ethical theories in order to understand how one can conceptualize and balance this value against others. We will read positions from John Stuart Mill and Immanuel Kant, but we will focus most of the semester on writings from contemporary philosophers and bioethicists. We will study different formulations of the principle to respect autonomy coming from bioethicists such as Tom Beauchamp, Onora OâNeill, and Adrienne Asch. By deconstructing and analyzing cases, we will also discuss some of the core ethical considerations when clinicians trust families, surrogates, and advance directives for medical decision-making. Students will need to purchase The Theory and Practice of Autonomy by Gerald Dworkin (all other materials will be provided digitally). Assignments will include in-class debates, two short papers, and one research project. The course website, which will be continually updated, can be found here: .

title: Ethics: Gender and Feminism
description: TBA

title: Ethics: Just Wars
description: When is it right for a nation to go to war? Once at war, what sort of behavior becomes permissible for soldiers and commanders? And what obligations do soldiers, sovereigns and states have in the aftermath of wars? Throughout the semester, we will tackle these questions (and many tangential and subsidiary ones) through the lens of contemporary just wary theory. We'll begin with a brief unit situating the just war tradition among other historically prominent perspectives on warârealism, pacifism, militarism and crusadism. We will subsequently turn our attention to issues of jus ad bellum, or moral issues surrounding the initiation of war. Subtopics will include aggression, preemptions, preventive wars, interventions, secessions, reprisals. We shall then turn our focus to conduct within war (jus in bello), and take a close look at the Doctrine of Double Effect, noncombatant immunity, the moral responsibility of soldiers fighting unjust wars, and conditions of "supreme emergency". Finally, weâll discuss a recently popular movement in just war theory that focus on justice in the conclusion of war (jus post bellum). While our readings will come primarily from contemporary western just war theory, we will also examine the historical roots of the western just war tradition, as well as the ethics of war in other cultures. In our final unit, we will briefly explore the Islamic, Jewish and Chinese traditions of just war.

title: Ethics: Oppression & Justice
description: What does it mean to be oppressed, and what forces coalesce to create, reinforce, and maintain oppression? Do all experiences of oppression look the same? How can an oppressed individual break free from societal shackles? How should those in the privileged class respond to these injustices? In this course, we will explore philosophical conceptions of oppression, taking into account its manifestations in both explicit and implicit policies, practices, and behaviors. Specifically, we will look at discrimination against persons based on race, sex, gender, sexual orientation, disability, and mental illness. We will look at historical and contemporary discussions about the types of obstacles facing these groups. Additionally, we will study different models of personal responsibility and consider to what extent it makes sense to hold a perpetrator or bystander responsible for oppressive conditions. Readings for the course will include contemporary philosophical texts as well as commentaries written by activists. Requirements include three short papers, an in-class debate, and a final research project. For more information, please visit the course website: //oppression-and-justice.weebly.com

title: Ethics: Business Responsibility
description: What are the moral obligations of businesses? Are for-profit companies solely obligated to maximize profit or do they have responsibilities beyond this? Many top companies today have policies regarding "Corporate Social Responsibility", "Global Citizenship", "Sustainability", and other nice-sounding buzz words. And vocal groups around the world similarly call for "corporate accountability" and for businesses to "give back" to their communities. But what do these terms mean and what should businesses do about them, if anything? We will consider topics including the philosophical nature of business, the nature of money, whether and how businesses can have moral obligations, means of defining the responsibilities of a business, whether businesses can have virtuous character, and the role that personal responsibilities play in business, amongst others. By drawing on classic and contemporary philosophy, this course will work to clarify the meaning of corporate responsibility and the origins of business ethics. Readings will focus on contemporary literature in business ethics, but also incorporate Aristotle, Plato, John Locke, Adam Smith, Immanuel Kant, John Stuart Mill, and John Rawls. Students will be assessed based on class participation, an in-class debate, and two papers (a 5-pager and an 8-pager).

title: Ethics: Love and Sex
description: "The poet and short story writer Raymond Carver once wrote: âIt ought to make us feel ashamed when we talk like we know what weâre talking about when we talk about love.â This may seem like an odd admonition. After all, it is often said that love is one of the things that make a life most worthwhile. How can we really fail to understand something most of us assume to be so important and to which we devote so much time and effort? But a little reflection suggests that love is perplexing. The affection between a parent and child is quite different from the bonds existing between two romantic lovers and between friends. And these differ from love of oneâs country, the love of an ideal like justice, liberty or equality, or the love of the game in a competitive sport. Can love really come in so many forms? What should we do if they conflict? What do we owe to our family, friends and romantic partners? What obligations do we have to them that we do not have towards strangers or acquaintances? Sexuality is a battleground in our culture. It is often seen as the terrain where love can become perverse or unethical. Do celibates sacrifice something important? Can one love multiple romantic partners simultaneously? Is it wrong to? Does gender determine what forms of love and sex are natural, healthy or otherwise good? Is homosexuality or bisexuality unethical? We often suppose that romantic love is properly a private affair. But is sex? Should we object to pornography? Is it obscene? Does it oppress women? In any case, should it be protected as free speech? What sort of control or oversightâif anyâshould public authorities with coercive powers have over our sex lives? Should some sexual media be censored? Who should make these decisions, and how? In this course, we will explore such questions in order to clarify and deepen our engagement with contemporary problems surrounding love and sexuality. We will primarily rely on philosophical texts in doing so, but we will explore the themes of the course with the help of some appropriate literature and film. Some authors whose work we will examine include Plato, Catharine MacKinnon, Bell Hooks and Audre Lorde. The goal of the course is to help us become more thoughtful about the problems and possibilities of love and sexuality in our lives and our society. Its graded content includes two short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, which consists of group presentations and individual contributions to in-class and online discussions of the assigned material.

title: Ethics: Liberty
description: âLibertyâ is a concept pregnant with meaning and historical significance. From its role as an ideal of the American and French Revolutions, to its radical reconception by socialists and Marxists, to its fundamental place in the anti-slavery and Civil Rights movements in America, to its present centrality to the American â and increasingly global â libertarian movement, liberty has motivated humans to fight and die, to oppose oppression of all kinds, and to overthrown the greatest dictatorships the world has ever known. The goal of this course is to examine this concept primarily from the libertarian perspective and to subject this perspective to substantial critical evaluation. To do this, we shall spend the first 4 weeks of the semester examining a number of prominent texts in the history of liberty: John Lockeâs Second Treatise, Jean-Jacques Rousseauâs Social Contract, and Karl Marxâs The Communist Manifesto. We shall then spend the rest of the semester reading and discussing texts by prominent exponents of 20th century libertarianism, including Ludwig von Mises, Friedrich Hayek, Ayn Rand, and Murray Rothbard. Throughout, our goal will be to understand these authors in their own terms and to subject their philosophical framework and substantive conceptions of liberty to close scrutiny and critical evaluation. Students will be graded on the basis of one-page reading responses to be submitted every 2 weeks, 3 short papers, and 1 long paper.

title: Ethics: Relationships
description: In this class, we will approach ethics by investigating the nature of personal relationships. We are in many different kinds of personal relationships â family relationships, friendships, romantic relationships, sexual relationships, and collegial relationships. We are also tied to others on the basis of ethnicity, nationality, religion or gender. First, we will ask about the natures of our various relationships. Among other topics, we will consider how gender and sexuality structure our relationships, whether any kind of relationship is more valuable than another, and whether any are intrinsically immoral. Is there anything wrong with promiscuity, incest, polygamy, prostitution, surrogacy, homosexuality? Then, we will explore the norms that govern our personal relationships: What do we owe to our partners? Do relationships give rise to special obligations? Do some relationships make otherwise immoral actions permissible? Is there a tension between the impartial perspective of morality and the partiality involved in personal relationships? Finally, we are going to ask whether the government can legitimately privilege some personal relationships and prohibit or regulate others. Readings will include texts from classical authors (among others Plato, Aristotle, Kant) and from contemporary authors (among them Elizabeth Brake, John Corvino, Andrea Dworkin, Harry Frankfurt, Catherine MacKinnon, Martha Nussbaum). We will also look at literature and movies. The course will be heavily discussion-driven. Grades will be assigned on the basis of weekly reading responses, two course papers, and class participation.

title: Ethics and International Affairs
description: With increased globalization, the issue of what moral obligations exist in the international arena arises not only with respect to states, but also NGOs and multi-national corporations. What moral principles ought to guide these entities in an increasingly globalized world? In this course, we will address (among others) the following questions: â¢ Can humanitarian military interventions be morally justified? â¢ Is there a viable international definition of human rights? Is there an implicit Western bias (as some philosophers have charged) in the demand for universal standards and the corresponding criticism of illiberal cultures and their practices around the world? â¢ What (if any) obligation do wealthy states have to rectify global economic inequality or serious poverty? â¢ What obligations do multi-national corporations have to workers in countries with weak protections for workers? Should we export âfirst worldâ moral standards to developing nations? â¢ Can moral principles even govern relations among states, or should states adopt a realist attitude in international relations?

title: Ethics: Environmental
description: When Rachel Carson published Silent Spring in 1962, the environmental movement was itself relatively silent. Today, however, environmentalism is among the most pressing and prominent issues in public discourseâboth local and global. Greenlandâs lightning fast melt, air pollution in Beijing going literally off the chart, and the consumption practices of ordinary folks like you and me receive sustained public attention. Businesses scramble to cash in on the new appeal of being green. Mainstream religions compete with the new eco-centric religions to captivate our spiritual sensitivities and re-enchant our religious experience. The global community is caught up in the debates that inevitably precede significant social change as we wrestle with complex moral, scientific, economic, and political issues revolving around humanityâs relationship with the environment. This course will focus on the moral dimension. We will read authors foundational to the environmental movement including John Muir and Aldo Leopold, as well as provocative contemporary moral philosophers such as Peter Singer and John Rolston Holmes. In each of these, we will explore the questions, whose interests are at stake? Is âvalueâ something that belongs only within the human sphere? What responsibilitiesâon both the individual and collective levelâdo humans have to one another and to plants, animals, and ecosystems? What is ânatureâ? Who is most wronged by environmental degradation, and how are we to address such issues in the face of limited cooperation and other problems of collective action?

title: Ethics: God & Godlessness
description: From an ethical standpoint, the most salient religious question is not simply whether God exists, but what impact the existence or non-existence of God should have on our lives. What would Godâs existence mean for how we should treat one another? Would sharing the world with God affect how we should view nature and inhabit the Earth? In a godless world, can anything be ultimately good or bad, right or wrong? If so, who is to say what is? If not, should we care about this, or about anything at all? In this course, we will explore the ethical and existential stakes of God and godlessness, using what we learn to clarify and deepen our engagement with contemporary problems surrounding the relationship between religious and secular values, which some have dubbed a âCulture War.â Specifically, we will examine the controversial âNew Atheismâ of writers like Christopher Hitchens and Richard Dawkins, as well as ongoing political contests surrounding same sex marriage and abortion. In doing so, we will rely primarily on philosophical texts. Our basic readings come from the work of such writers as Plato, Thomas Aquinas, David Hume, the American Transcendentalists, Friedrich Nietzsche, Sandra Lipsitz Bem, John Finnis and Emmanuel Levinas. However, we will also draw upon moral and theological reflection from the Abrahamic tradition of monotheism encompassing Judaism, Christianity and Islam. And we will explore the themes of the course with the help of some appropriate films. The overall goal of the course is to help us to develop thoughtful, critical relationships with the possible presence and absence of God. Its graded content includes two short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, which consists of group presentations and individual contributions to in-class and online discussions of the assigned material.

title: Ethics: Animal Rights Origins
description: Do animals deserve any sort of moral consideration? Ought we to kill them, eat them, torture them? Are we even allowed to do any of these? What features of animals (or humans) bear on these questions? In the span from 1635 to 1865 we find thinkers engaging with these questions in recognizably modern ways for the first time. Some give reasons to think that animals lack minds, and therefore merit no moral consideration whatsoever. Some think that animals deserve moral protection only as they relate to human interests. Others think that suffering, in itself, is a brute bad and is to be avoided. By investigating these views in their historical context, we will ask what the salient philosophical considerations ought to be in our moral thinking about animals. In doing so, we will try to determine how the views these thinkers held lead to the kinds of views commonly held today. We will read works by RenÃ© Descartes, Michel de Montaigne, Pierre Bayle, Thomas Hobbes, John Locke, G.W.V. Leibniz, David Hume, Immanuel Kant, Jeremy Bentham and J.S. Mill. There will be several 1-2pg writing assignments and one long paper (10+ pgs).

title: Ethics: Fundamentals of Market Society
description: Are markets good or bad? How free should markets be? What good are economic and commercial liberties, and how strong should those liberties be? Is capitalism an impediment to social justice, or could capitalism be a way of achieving social justice? Is it possible for a society to be free, prosperous, and fair? Is market society cooperative and peaceful or competitive and exploitative? Do markets promote or undermine community and virtue? This course is meant to introduce you to moral and political philosophy by focusing on a particular set of questions about capitalism, market institutions, and the ethics of wealth creation. We will start by asking what moral reasoning is, examine some common moral and political theories, and ask what counts as a sound ethical reasoning and what it takes to justify moral beliefs. We will evaluate the institutions of the market using the tools of ethics, political philosophy, economics, history, and political science. We will investigate issues concerning the nature of money and prices, the role of the division of labor, business ethics, commerce and entrepreneurship, overconsumption and overpopulation, exploitation and alienation, the relationship between wealth and happiness, the motivations of market actors, the rule of law, liberty and market society, rent-seeking and corporate welfare, and more. We will read and discuss evaluations of markets from multiple ideological perspectives: socialist, communitarian, left-liberal, classical liberal, libertarian, and conservative.

title: Ethics: Global Justice
description: Whether discussing the Arab Spring, the rise of China, the Euro crisis, or global climate change, issues of poverty, inequality, and humanitarian aid are at the forefront today. Hand in hand with the questions of policy are the ethical questions concerning global justice: What is justice in a globally interconnected world? How ought we as individuals, societies, and governments to respond to 1.5 billion people living in absolute poverty? Do we have different moral duties toward our neighbors or fellow citizens than we do toward foreigners? Is global inequalityâwhether defined in terms of income, capability, or healthâa matter of justice? What is the role of human rights in securing global justice? Are our current institutions sufficient to address global challenges such as environmental degradation, health care, and immigration? In this course we will examine these and other questions as we read, discuss, and write on contemporary moral philosophers focused on global justice.

title: Ethics: Technological Challenges
description: We are awash in the benefits of technology. But in its wake as much as ahead of its break, technology often complicates already complicated ethical questions, and not infrequently it sets us adrift in utterly new moral territory. It is all too easy to be swept along in the tide, but moral complacency is hardly an admirable (or safe) option. This course will be an attempt to navigate some of the pertinent ethical challenges new technology brings. Weâll examine technological incursions into at least the following major areas of moral concern: property and ownership, privacy, the environment, and reproduction. Student interest will determine where else we direct our attention. The principle goals for the course are to equip and motivate participants to grapple with the ethical challenges technology presents both in personal life and as a matter of social choice. Among the principle skills weâll be keen to enhance will be those that are relevant to crafting inquiries and arguments. We will also make extensive use of our classroom community, so another important set of goals for the course will focus on productive collaboration. Readings will include canonical authors such as Mill and Locke, but will in greater proportion reflect more recent contributions to the relevant fields. In addition to a short paper, an exam and participation, assessment will include a long-term project with presentation, research and writing phases.

title: Ethics: Rights
description: Talk of rights has become ubiquitous to our understanding of interpersonal obligation. If one wants to know what one owes another person, what duties one has to them, it is commonly thought that one should consult their rights as a guide. Do we have a duty to provide healthcare? Consult everybodyâs rights. Do we have a duty to help the global poor? Again, consult their rights. However, must we understand interpersonal obligation in terms of rights? What does rights-talk add to our descriptions of duty? Why canât we just dispense with rights-talk? In order to answer these and related questions, we begin by reading several important texts which express duties in terms of justice rather than rights, including Platoâs Apology and Gorgias, excerpts from Aristotleâs Ethics and Politics. We then consider why rights are perhaps a better way of understanding interpersonal obligation than mere justice, reading Hobbes, Locke, and Kant. We conclude by considering several important critiques of rights-talk, including Mill, Marx, and Rawls. The course will be conversation-driven, and grades will be assigned according to participation and three short papers.

title: Ethics: Public Policy
description: This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, from a theoretical and practical perspective, as it relates to public policy. The course will begin with a close reading of classic texts including works of Aristotle, Kant, and Mill, which try to make sense of what makes human lives and actions good, happy, and meaningful. We will subject these views to contemporary reflections on what it means to be a moral actor, considering pointed critiques of traditional western thinking about ethics. Throughout, we will be asking ourselves about the constraints that ethical considerations might ask us to impose on our own freedom, and whether there are elements of the ethical life that are not within our control. Finally, we will apply these ethical frameworks to public policy asking how ethical considerations inform (or fail to inform) our public policy through close study of specific issues of contemporary national, political and global significance such as international migration, poverty, and corporate globalization. Throughout the course we will explore the question of how and when morally evaluative judgments are possible concerning the practices of individuals, communities, corporations, and nations. Requirements of the course will include two papers, a mid-term and final examination, and class participation.

title: Beginning Logic
description: This course is a general introduction to formal logic. In it, you will learn how to translate sentences from ordinary English into sentences of symbolic logic, and how to construct valid arguments using basic rules of inference. We will begin by considering basic arguments of truth-functional logic, in which some propositions are regarded as simple, while others are negations, conjunctions, disjunctions, or conditionals of these simple ones. Once we have a handle on how to symbolize English sentences and how to construct valid proofs, we will move on to the first-order predicate calculus, in which simple propositions will be further analyzed in terms of their parts. Again, we will see how to symbolize ordinary English sentences, and how to prove arguments in a rule-governed system. Students will be evaluated on the basis of four exams. We will be using Gary Hardegree's Symbolic Logic: A First Course.

title: Beginning Logic
description: "We gain much of the knowledge we possess through reasoning, and logic is the study of how we ought to reason. Mastery of logic is useful not only to philosophers, but to all those who go on to engage in any kind of persuasive enterprise. The goal of this course will be to familiarize students with the standards and common mistakes of reasoning. After a brief overview of arguments in general, the first half of the semester will be devoted to deductive logicâsyllogistic and propositional logic. These two units will give students a firm working knowledge of the concepts of validity and soundness. In the second half of the semester, we will turn our attention to the more widely applicable inductive logic, through which we will broaden our understanding of the standards of reason. Students will be assessed by three examinations over the course of the semesterâtwo in class, and one cumulative exam during the week of final exams. NOTE: this course will not satisfy the logic requirement for Philosophy majors."

title: Introduction to Logic
description: Logic is the study of arguments, more specifically, of what distinguishes sound arguments from fallacious ones. We will study inductive as well as deductive logic, but the bulk of the course concerns formal deductive logic. We begin by studying the main patterns of inductive reasoning: the argument from analogy, Millâs method, and Hypothetical Induction. Then we will study the traditional Aristotelian logic of syllogisms, the Boolean approach characterized by the use of Venn Diagrams, and finally modern symbolic logic which utilizes truth tables and the method of deduction. A high degree of technical or mathematical ability is not required; this is the most elementary course in the logic sequence and does not presuppose any previous knowledge of logic or philosophy. There will be one short paper, three quizzes, and a final exam. PHIL-151 will satisfy the logic requirement for philosophy majors, but is open to all students with one prior philosophy course. Required Readings: Davis. An Introduction to Logic, 2nd. Edition (Kunos Press).

title: Introduction to Logic
description: This course is a general introduction to formal logic. In it, you will learn (1) how to translate sentences from ordinary English into sentences of symbolic logic, and (2) how to construct valid arguments using basic rules of inference. We will begin by considering basic arguments of truth-functional logic, in which some propositions are regarded as simple, while others are negations, conjunctions, disjunctions, or conditionals of these simple ones. Once we have a handle on how to symbolize English sentences and how to construct valid proofs, we will move on to the first-order predicate calculus, in which simple propositions will be further analyzed in terms of their parts. Again, we will see how to symbolize ordinary English sentences, and how to prove arguments in a rule-governed system. We will be using Gary M. Hardegree's Symbolic Logic: A First Course. Students will be evaluated on the basis of 4 exams spaced throughout the semester. This course satisfies the logic requirement for philosophy majors.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: This course is a general introduction to formal logic. In it, you will learn (1) how to translate sentences from ordinary English into sentences of symbolic logic, and (2) how to construct valid arguments using basic rules of inference. We will begin by considering basic arguments of truth-functional logic, in which some propositions are regarded as simple, while others are negations, conjunctions, disjunctions, or conditionals of these simple ones. Once we have a handle on how to symbolize English sentences and how to construct valid proofs, we will move on to the first-order predicate calculus, in which simple propositions will be further analyzed in terms of their parts. Again, we will see how to symbolize ordinary English sentences, and how to prove arguments in a rule-governed system. We will be using Gary M. Hardegree's Symbolic Logic: A First Course. Students will be evaluated on the basis of 4 exams spaced throughout the semester. This course satisfies the logic requirement for philosophy majors.

title: Intro to Logic:Critical Thinking
description: This is course is an introduction to informal logic. That is, critical thinking. Student will learn how to make good arguments and recognize bad ones. This course is foundational not only for all philosophy, not only for all other academic topics, but also for all human thought. Logic examines the reasons we have for believing something. It is how we determine whether we have sufficient reasons to believe that any statement is true. Of course, being able to determine what is true is important to almost everything we do. Students will read the textbook and work through the assigned exercises to improve their ability make and recognize logical arguments. There will be no lectures. I will spend all of the class time going over the students' assignments and answering their questions. Texts & Readings 1. Richard L. Epstein, Critical Thinking (4th Edition). Advanced Reasoning Forum 2. Critical Thinking: work book (4th Edition): Advanced Reasoning Forum. The workbook is a free download. Assignments & Expectations of Students Students will have daily home works and regular quizzes and tests.

title: Perception
description: Right now youâre staring at a computer, though probably not paying attention to it. In fact, until just now, itâs not likely that you attentively noticed the computer at all, let alone the blurry, indeterminate visual field surrounding the computer screen. Youâre not even likely to have paid attention to the fact that youâre eyes are scanning over black squiggly lines. Physically, what is happening is that light is reflecting off of the objects in in your immediate vicinity, striking your cornea, and reflecting two small, fuzzy, upside-down images on your retina. Your experience, however, is radically different. Youâre perceiving a rich and vivid world, familiar and meaningful, detailed and complex. Immediatelyâat least, before I got you to start reflecting on it, but probably even nowâyouâre experience is not even one of seeing words, but rather one of reading or taking in meanings. Perception is transparent to us, normally we donât see it; but it somehow opens us up onto the meaningful world that we do see. How does that happen? And what is it that is happening? Are you actively doing something or are you a passive bystander, perceptually assaulted by the world? How does perception relate to beliefs and knowledge, thought and language? How do we distinguish and how do our different senses relate? In what ways is our perceptual experience similar to or different from that of non-human animals? And how do we theoretically account for what takes place when we perceive (or when we misperceive by hallucinating or being âtrickedâ by an illusion)? By quickly scanning your eyes over this paragraph, not only have we somehow entered into a conversation, but this conversation has led you to both reflect on and experience perception in a different way than you otherwise would (at least normally). The continuation of this conversation will make up the content of our semester together.

title: Existentialism
description: Many of the philosophers we'll discuss under the label 'existentialism' either predated the term or rejected it outright. Nonetheless, we can think of existentialism as a trajectory of modern thought driven by a set of roughly overlapping concerns â e.g. human freedom and its basic threats in modernization; the place of rationality in human life; meaning; commitment; anxiety; the absurd; authenticity. We will start the semester by exploring the historical situation that gave birth to existentialism in order to get a feel for what existentialists are responding to. We will then explore the philosophical responses to this historical situation offered by Friedrich Nietzsche, Albert Camus, Jean-Paul Sartre, and Martin Heidegger. Students will be assessed based on participation in class discussion, two short (3 page) papers, and a two or three quizzes.

title: Existentialism
description: Existentialism is more than anything else a revolt against excessive abstraction in philosophy and in human life in general. Existentialists see abstraction in the "leveling down" of human life by diverse forces of homogenization, such as universal morality and rationality, mass society, and modern science. In each of these forces existentialists see a threat to human freedom, a threat to the individualâs distinctive identity and her ability to commit herself in her own voice (rather than in the voice of reason or morality or custom) to a life-defining commitment. Although existentialism is just as much literary and cultural as philosophical, in Philosophy 159 we will focus mostly on the philosophical aspect of existentialism. Existentialism is, moreover, a loose philosophical movement, rather than a school of thought. For this reason, we will be looking at several approaches to a group of problems or worries, rather than at the development of a set of doctrines. We will start with an exploration of some of the basic threats to human freedom felt by the existentialists. We will then explore the philosophical responses to these threats offered by Jean-Paul Sartre, SÃ¸ren Kierkegaard, and Friedrich Nietzsche. We will conclude the semester with a look at the ethical sources and implications of existentialism.
