We will conclude the semester with a look at the ethical sources and implications of existentialism.

title: Philosophical Reflection on the Christian Creeds
description: In this course, we will use the tools of philosophy to try to make sense of various ideas central to the Christian creeds: belief, God, creation, Trinity, incarnation, atonement, resurrection of the body, and heaven and hell. We are not trying to use philosophy to prove that the Christian creeds are true; we are asking, rather, whether and how philosophical investigation help make sense of these doctrines. Everyone, of whatever faith or none at all, is invited to work through some of these really interesting problems. Course requirements include two medium-length papers, a final comprehensive exam, various unannounced reading quizzes, and prepared and active attendance.

title: Contemporary Political Thought
description: This course picks up where Political and Social Thought (PST) left off. While my version of PST presents an overview of political thought from 400BCE to the 1800s, Contemporary Political Thought (CPT) takes a slightly more in-depth look at the political thought of the last one or two hundred years, examining the most influential contemporary schools of political thought. Political thought is about argument. It is about putting forth an idea and giving reasons to support it. It is about examining other peopleâs ideas and giving reasons either to support or reject them. That is what all political theorists do, and that is also what students of political theory do. The students in this class will learn about the contemporary political debate through reading and lectures, and they will participate in the contemporary political theory debate by writing about what they read, reading each otherâs writing, and taking issue with each others positions both in written homeworks and in in-class discussions.

title: Language and Power
description: Language plays a central role in our interactions with others in the world: it helps us to convey our thoughts and to create important connections with people. It can also be a powerful mechanism through which to derogate, marginalize, or subordinate persons. This course will examine how language draws on, exerts, and reinforces social power. Weâll make use of classic texts from philosophy of language to address contemporary concerns about social discourse. Weâll start by examining John Stuart Millâs famous arguments in support of freedom of speech. One of the central questions of the course is whether these arguments hold up when we understand speech as not merely a way to convey information, but, as J. L. Austin argues, as an action which itself can have a significant impact on others. Weâll cover topics ranging from hate speech and the use of derogatory terms to whether there is a distinction between serious and humorous speech. Then, we'll look at how social features impact a speaker's ability to deploy language successfully. We'll read texts from Miranda Fricker about how we rate the credibility of speakers, Rebecca Kukla on how social factors affect what a speaker is able to do with her speech, and Rae Langton on the role of pornography on how we take up language in a gendered society. This course will be heavily discussion driven and grades will be based on class participation and several writing assignments.

title: Philosophy/Science/Pseudoscience
description: Should public schools include astrology in their science curricula? Should the government fund research into parapsychology or Intelligent Design? Should homeopathy be covered by public health insurance? Many of us would answer ânoâ to these questions, but why? A promising answer is that astrology, parapsychology, and the like are pseudosciences, such that their products have no place in our schools, budgets, or pharmacies. But isn't this controversial? Given that modern parapsychologists, homeopaths, and Intelligent Design theorists are well-trained, publish in peer-reviewed journals, and use rigorous science methods, isn't it unfair to label their research âpseudoscientificâ? And if it is unfair, then what of our original claim? Could it be that Intelligent Design, parapsychology, and astrology deserve the sort of respect we normally reserve for disciplines like evolutionary biology, psychology, and astronomy? The questions we've been asking are all special cases of a more general question concerning the boundaries of respectable science. In a word: âWhat distinguishes or demarcates science from pseudoscience?â In this course, we will explore this question of demarcation at two levels of abstraction. At a relatively low level of abstraction, we'll consider some of the canonical approaches to scientific demarcation in the philosophy of science. There is a vast literature on this topic, but our focus will be on four main figures: Karl Popper, Thomas Kuhn, Imre Lakatos, and Paul Feyerabend. At a lower level of abstraction, we'll study debates about the scientific status of particular research programs, including Intelligent Design, parapsychology, MKULTRA, psychoanalysis, and cold fusion. In this part of the course, we'll hear from at least two scientistsâa physicist and a clinical pharmacologistâas guest speakers, and read works by Philip Kitcher, Michael Ruse, Ian Hacking, and Anthony Flew. Assessment: Two take-home tests, bi-weekly reflection papers, a group project. Required Texts (please order them online): - Feyerabend, P. Against Method [4th Edition]. New York: Verso Books, 2010. - Godfrey-Smith, P. Theory and Reality: An Introduction to the Philosophy of Science. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2003. - Kuhn, T. The Structure of Scientific Revolutions: 50th Anniversary Edition. Preface by Ian Hacking. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2012. - Popper, K. Conjectures and Refutations: The Growth of Scientific Knowledge [Routledge Classics]. London: Routledge, 2002.

title: Disputes in Early Modern Philosophy
description: Within the philosophical communities that flourished from 1650 to 1800, thinkers debated issues both old and new. For instance, they disputed the status of the new experimental scienceâcould it really ground knowledge?â and the place of human beings within that new vision of the world. In service of their positions, they offered clever arguments and compelling criticisms of their friends and rivals. They differed over arguments claiming to show that God exists, too, some claiming that they couldâand didâconclusively show that there is a supreme being, others holding that the arguments were flawed. They argued about whether non-human animals have souls, among many other topics. In this class, weâll look at how thinkers like Thomas Hobbes, Isaac Newton, Margaret Cavendish, G.W.V. Leibniz, Samuel Clarke, and Anne Conway contributed to these debates by enacting them for ourselves. One class meeting every week will feature a debate, and two members of the class will serve as the lead for their debate teams. Every student will be expected to provide the lead argument for one debate and to sit second chair on another. In addition, each student will be expected to provide a write-up of her/his debate (6-10pp) at the end of the semester. Modern Philosophy: An Anthology of Primary Sources (Second Edition) ed. Roger Ariew and Eric Watkins ISBN: 978-0-87220-978-7 Women Philosophers of the Early Modern Period ed. Margaret Atherton ISBN: 978-0-87220-259-7

title: Hellenistic Philosophy
description: The Hellenistic era-- the period in Greek history inaugurated by Alexander's conquest-- represents perhaps the first golden age of western philosophy. In this course, we will explore the major schools of philosophical thought that arose during this golden age. After a brief introductory unit on philosophy generally, and western philosophy before the Hellenistic era, we will read the surviving primary and secondary texts left by the Stoics, the Epicureans, the Skeptics, the Cynics, the Neoplatonists, and several smaller Hellenistic schools of thought. We will focus especially on the ethical, epistemological and metaphysical thought of each of these schools. Students will learn to recognize, analyze and critique the arguments put forward by the prominent thinkers within each school. Assessment will consist of three written assignments and an in-class midterm exam.

title: What Am I?
description: This course is an introduction to what is sometimes called the metaphysics of persons. We'll be concerned with four interrelated questions: â¢ What is required for our continued existence over time? â¢ What sort of thing are we? (a mind, soul, animal, or what?) â¢ What is the nature of mental states? â¢ What is free will and do we have it? Assessment will be based on a mix of short assignments on the reading and short essays.

title: Philosophy of Language
description: This course explores the major philosophical attempts to make sense of meaning and communication. After surveying the "greatest hits" of 20th Century philosophy of languageâincluding the classic accounts of reference, meaning, speech acts, and metaphorâwe will shift gears, and focus on several more contemporary lines of inquiry. Assessment: This class will emphasize discussion; participation will account for a significant portion of your final grade. The remainder of your grade will be determined your performance on (i) biweekly reflection papers, (ii) a 2,000 word essay, and (iii) two take-home exams.

title: Philosophy: East/West
description: A simple goal motivates this class: to sort out what people mean when they refer to âEastern thinkingâ as opposed to âWestern thinking.â This will, of course, turn out to be quite slippery. But we can try to get a foothold by investigating some of the most influential texts in the Eastern tradition: Confuciusâ Analects (and some material by his followers Mengzi and Xunzi), the two chief works of Taoism (the Chuang Tzu and the Tao Te Ching), the Dhammapada, and the Bhagavad Gita. We will situate these works and their main claims against excerpts of some classics of Western philosophy (e.g., Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, the Stoics, Parmenides, Heraclitus, Descartes). This will reveal, and allow us to evaluate, some crucial differences between the traditions, manifested everywhere from metaphysics and epistemology to ethics. We will find competing answers about how we ought to live (including famous analyses of suffering and reasonâs role in the good life); divergent ideas about deathâs badness; rival conceptions of the self (or no-selfâ¦); and disagreement over the value and use of reason/argument/language/definition as the method to âget to the heart of thingsâ (as contrasted with mysticism, where the Zen koan, yoga, meditation, etc., are used). We will supplement the primary texts with some contemporary secondary sources, including Alan Watts, Huston Smith, Mark Siderits, David Hall, and Roger Ames. Finally, we will study how some key Eastern insights have been taken up and/or criticized by contemporary philosophers (e.g., Parfit and Korsgaard). The course will require active participation and three (approx. 6-7 pg.) papers.

title: Sex and Death
description: In this course, we will examine the importance of evolutionary theory to ongoing debates in philosophy. We will attempt to develop a solid foundation for exploring questions about human behavior, especially as regards issues of adaptation, genetic determinism, and the nature/nurture controversy. In addressing these issues, we will adopt an interdisciplinary perspective, reading philosophers, evolutionary biologists, and cognitive anthropologists. Over the course of the semester, you will write 3 short papers, and you will be expected to contribute to in-class discussion. No previous background in philosophy or evolutionary biology will be presupposed.

title: Philosophy of Music
description: This bridge course will be designed especially for students who are engaged in music production, but is open to all. We will ask a range of fundamental questions about music: what is music? What is an aesthetic response to a musical or other artwork? Is there a fact of the matter about what music is good? How does music evoke emotion, and how central is that to its nature? How does music function in forming social groups, especially political movements? Is it legitimate to judge music by it's political value? The texts for the course are Aesthetics: A comprehensive anthology (Blackwell), Steve Cahn, and Aaron Meskin (ed) Dancing In The Streets - Ehrenreich The Imaginary Museum of Musical Works - Goehr Students will actively participate in discussion, write 3 short papers, and develop a final project in consultation with the professor.

title: Pursuing the Truth
description: How does the pursuit of truth play out in our lives? In many of Platoâs dialogues, Socrates argues that this pursuit is not a solitary endeavour. We should pursue the truth in conversation, or âdialogueâ, with others. Similarly, once we think we have the truth, we should not attempt to persuade others (by, say, giving a rousing speech (ârhetoricâ), but should instead converse with them in a cooperative examination of the reasons for our beliefs. To explore this view, we will read some of Platoâs key dialogues â the Gorgias, the Phaedrus, and parts of the Sophist. These will also illuminate many of Platoâs views on knowledge, human nature, ethics and other philosophical topics. We will also look to contemporary media, culture and politics to ask questions such as whether contemporary political discourse counts as pursuing the truth through dialogue.Students will be assessed based on participation in class discussion, two short (3 page) papers, and a longer (5-6 page) final paper.

title: Philosophy and Star Trek
description: Star Trek is very philosophical. What better way, then, to do philosophy, but to watch Star Trek, read philosophy and hash it all out in class (and on Blackboard)? Thatâs the plan. This course will center on topics in metaphysics that come up again and again in Star Trek. In conjunction with watching Star Trek, we will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. Questions we will wrestle with include: I. Is time travel possible? Could you go back and kill your grandmother? What is time? II. What is the relation between your mind and your brain--are they separate items or identical? Can persons survive death? Could a machine someday think? Is Data a person? III. What is a person? Must you have the same body to be you? Same memories? When do we have one person, and when do we have two (think of the episodes where people "split" or "fuse"). IV. Do you have free will, or are you determined by the laws of nature to do exactly what you wind up doing (while believing you have free will)? Or both? What is freewill? Text: Metaphysics: Classic and Contemporary Readings, 2nd ed., Ed. by Hoy & Oaklander (Wadsworth, 2005). Four short papers.

title: Philosophy and Free Expression
description: This course will examine the philosophical dimension of free speech and apply these insights to contemporary free speech issues. The course starts with a discussion of some philosophical perspectives that can put free speech issues into a larger context of political theory, views on rights, freedom, and justice and the relationship of individuals to their communities. Readings from Locke, Kant, Bentham, Mill and Aristotle illuminate the contrasting perspectives of human rights, utilitarianism and communitarianism. We will then move on to various justifications for a regime of free expression. One justification treats free speech as a necessary element of identity and autonomy, an approach that treats free speech as a fundamental aspect of human dignity and can be traced back to the human rights tradition in political philosophy. An alternative justification focuses less on the rights of speakers and more on the social harms that result from censorship and social gains from free expression. This outcomes-based framework approach to free speech can be traced back to the utilitarian perspective in political philosophy. A third justification is the role of free expression in providing the information, discussion and debate required for effective democratic self-governance, a perspective that is based on the necessary conditions for the legitimacy of political authority. The communitarian approach provides a critical perspective by emphasizing how free speech can result in damage to the integrity and structure and reputation of religious, ethnic or national communities. We will also review some of the critiques of free speech as an abstract value. These views think of free speech as an element in political struggle or more broadly as part of socially constructed reality that cannot be divorced from the aims and goals of political agents acting in specific social and historical circumstances. The question of how to balance free speech against other values has to be addressed in national legal systems. We examine this balancing question in the courseâs final section discussing several contemporary policy issues: hate speech, free speech on the Internet, free speech and privacy, and the regulation of political speech and commercial speech.

title: Aesthetics
description: At the end of the Republic, Plato has Socrates banish the poets from his ideal city. Plato fears that the poets will corrupt the citizens, encouraging them to let their passions, as opposed to their reason, rule them. In exiling the poets, Plato inaugurated a conflict between art and philosophy that continues even today. During this time philosophical aesthetics has lived in genteel poverty on the outskirts of the discipline, trying to refute, diminish, or reverse Platoâs charges while occasionally admitting artâs guilt. This course will take Plato's worry about the danger of art seriously. It will begin with a survey of various definitions of art (e.g. as representation, expression, form), considering the extent to which these answers relieve or deepen such concerns. With these definitions in hand, we will then turn our attention to issues that address the danger of art more explicitly, including the relationship between the aesthetic and the ethical, mass/popular art and kitsch, public art, and the aesthetics of the political. Given our focus upon the philosophical critique and defense of art, the majority of readings will come from philosophy, although these will be supplemented by selections by artists and critics. Authors will include Plato, Aristotle, Schopenhauer, Danto, Greenberg, Hanslick, and Tolstoy. Of course, any course in the philosophy of art would be incomplete without engagement with actual art. This will include scheduled film screenings (Banksyâs Exit Through the Gift Shop and Lech Majewskiâs The Mill and the Cross), visits to exhibitions at the National Gallery of Art and the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden, and trips to contemplate some of Washington D.C.âs public art, such as Maya Linâs Vietnam Veterans Memorial. Texts: All texts will be available on Blackboard. Assessment: Students will be required to write three 4-6 page papers (20% each) and give a 10-15 minute, philosophically-informed presentation on an artistic movement or artist (20%). The remaining 20% of the grade will be based upon participation, broadly construed to include not only in-class discussion, but also participation in course-related activities and the maintenance of an electronic reflection journal.

title: Classical Chinese Philosophy
description: In this course we read and discuss texts on the following topics in classical Chinese philosophy: Confucianism and its developments (including neo-Confucianism); Daoism and its developments (including neo-Daoism); Mozi and Mohism; Han Feizi and Legalism; language and paradox (e.g. âWhite horse is not horse.â); women in Chinese thought. Special attention will be given to the controversy over human nature, with an eye toâbut without expanding onâits implication for ethical and political views. Students in this class should focus on cultivating the following skills and understanding: (i) interpreting complex and often obscure texts; (ii) identifying, analyzing and critically assessing the arguments contained therein; (iii) appreciating the distinctive teachings of the various schools of classical Chinese philosophy; (iv) reflecting on and recognizing how the various branches of Chinese philosophical tradition may (or may not) be at the root of any notable differences (or commonalities) between Chinese and Western cultures. Graded requirements include the following: (1) participation (30%), which involves writing brief commentaries on all reading assignments; (2) one term paper, 1,500-2,000 words (40%); (3) Comprehensive, open-book final exam (40%).

title: Other (Kinds of) Minds
description: How do we know what other people are thinking, given that we seem to have only indirect access to their beliefs and desires? Do we have any reason for thinking that other people have minds that are fundamentally like our own? Philosophers have often asked what it takes to have a mind, and this turns out to be a very hard question. We often use terms like âbeliefâ, âdesireâ, and âintentionâ to explain the behavior of multinational corporations and governments, people and household pets, laptop computers and automobiles, but some of these appeals are intended in a metaphorical or figurative tone of voice. In this course, we will examine a variety of philosophical and psychological attempts to explain what it means to have a mind. We will inquire into the kinds of minds that other entities possess. We will also ask whether it is possible to understand the minds of persons with serious mental illnesses or cognitive disabilities; we will ask about the presuppositions that underlie the study of non-human minds (including apes, monkeys, cats, rats, snakes, spiders, and cockroaches); and we will ask whether it is possible to build an intelligent machine.

title: Hallucinating
description: SPRING 2014 We all make mistakes. We all sometimes remember things that didnât really happen and sometimes forget things that really did. We all get confused by optical illusions, and phantom pains and itches. We all sometimes believe false things other people tell us. We all sometimes confuse our dreams for reality. We all sometimes believe things because we really want them to be, even though we have good reasons for thinking they arenât. And sometimes weâre just plain hallucinating. How can we be sure that weâre not mistaken about everything? What kinds of thing can we know for sure? What is knowledge anyway? Weâll look at some old, not so old, and even less old answers to and ways of thinking about these questions. Some of these will come from philosophers. But some will come as well from science, science fiction, literature, film, art, etc. The views we come across will be very different, and weâll spend a lot of time trying to sort out our own reactions to these questions. Weâll all probably end up with different answers ourselvesâbut at least theyâll be our own answers, and weâll have good reasons for them. (Thatâs the philosophy part.) Here are just a few of the things we may be looking at: â¢ Existenz, ?, Rashomon â¢ Writing by Descartes, Hume, Russell, Dennett ... â¢ Recent research in cognitive science and psychology (of individuals and groups) â¢ Capgras and other delusions â¢ Maybe some selections from Stanislaw Lem (the good stuff) â¢ Stories by Borges, Langford, others â¢ Religion and other mass delusions FALL 2013 We all make mistakes. We all sometimes remember things that didn't really happen and sometimes forget things that really did. We all get confused by optical illusions, and phantom pains and itches. We all sometimes believe false things other people tell us. We all sometimes confuse our dreams for reality. We all sometimes believe things because we really want them to be, even though we have good reasons for thinking they aren't. And sometimes we're just plain hallucinating. How can we be sure that we're not mistaken about everything? What kinds of thing can we know for sure? What is knowledge anyway? We'll look at some old, not so old, and even less old answers to and ways of thinking about these questions. Some of these will come from philosophers. But some will come as well from science, science fiction, literature, film, art, etc. The views we come across will be very different, and we'll spend a lot of time trying to sort out our own reactions to these questions. We'll all probably end up with different answers ourselves---but at least they'll be our own answers, and we'll have good reasons for them. (That's the philosophy part.) Here are just a few of the things we'll be looking at: * The films The Matrix and Existenz * Kafka's ``The Metamorphosis"; Thompson's Fear and Loathing in Los Vegas; Burroughs' Naked Lunch; Ellison's The Invisible Man; etc. * Hume's A Treatise of Human Nature (parts) * Recent research in cognitive science and psychology (of individuals and groups) * Some selections from Stanislaw Lem (the good stuff) * The Star Trek pilot episode, ``The Cage" * Some more philosophy stuff * Parts of Don Quixote * Religion and other mass delusions.

title: Metaphysics
description: In contemporary philosophy metaphysics means philosophical inquiry into the most fundamental features of reality and ourselves. This course is an introduction to some classic issues in metaphysics as they have been treated in Anglo-American philosophy in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. We will focus on the following issues: (1) Past, present and future. Time seems to flow: it seems that what is now present was once future and will be past. But does it really flow or is the appearance of flow reallly an illusion to which we are subject? (2) Parts and wholes. The everyday things around are complexes, having parts, and it seems they continue to exist even as they change in their parts, as my body seems to continue to exist as its atoms are continually replaced. But is this appearance of continued existence correct, or does a change of parts literally result in a new thing? (3) The self. What sort of thing am I? An animal, a mind, or perhaps a soul? And what conditions must be met for me to continue to exist over time? (4) Free will. What is it? Could we have it if everything that happens is the ineluctable consequence of what went before it? Although these four topics may seem quite different, they in fact are closely related in many respects.

title: Ethical Theory
description: Spring 2014

title: Dante and the Christian Imagination
description: Jorge Luis Borges said that no one should deny themselves the pleasure of reading Danteâs Commedia. This course is intended to help the reader discover why this is so. More specifically, this course will consider the explicitly Christian and uniquely contemporary intellectual relevance of Dante's Divina Commedia in the context of the questions of human freedom and identity, as well as the role of the imagination in the formation of culture and worldviews. The basis and substance of the study will be Dante's Divina Commedia. The approach of the course to this theme will be interdisciplinary with significant consideration being given to the function of imagination as it operates in poetry, psychology, philosophy, and theology. The unifying element in this approach would be the role of metaphor in all these disciplines, with special attention to both the similarities and the specific difference of that role in each as it appears in the Comedy. We will read in translation and discuss substantial portions of all three of the Cantiche of the Commedia, Inferno, Purgatorio, and Paradiso. Students will be asked to make regular use of a website designed specifically for this course incorporating the text of the Commedia in Italian and English translation, as well as images from the rich history of the illustration of the poem by great artists and commentary on the text from a variety of sources. No prior expertise in web technology is required; students will, however, be asked to become familiar with and use a few basic techniques of interactive, web-based learning. Attention will be given to the poetic art of Dante as it is manifest in the original Italian text, but reading knowledge of Italian is not required.

title: Bioethics: Theory and Practice
description: This course is a philosophical and historical treatment of issues in bioethics. The following major areas are covered: human research ethics, clinical ethics, the place of principles and rights in biomedical ethics, and animal research ethics. The issues and controversies to be discussed include the moral significance of research scandals throughout the 20th century (principally in Germany and the United States); the nature of, and problems of, informed consent and informed refusal; problems of âdeath with dignity,â âthe right to die,â and physician aid in dying; whether unregulated innovative medical âpracticeâ and off-label use of pharmaceutical products are acceptable in medicine; whether the pervasive distinction between clinical medical ethics and clinical research ethics is tenable; the quality of federal regulations governing research and the lack of regulations governing medical practice; the nature and role (if any) in bioethics of common morality theory; and whether a moral revolution in government regulations to protect animal research subjects is now needed. So-called âtheories of biomedical ethicsâ and the role and importance of moral justification will also be featured. The format in class will be brief lectures, focused class discussion, and papers presented by students. Discussion will involve a minimum of interpretation of articles and books and a maximum of discussion of the issues.

title: Tutorial:  Philosophy
description: Permission Required.

title: Text Seminar
description: There are multiple Text Seminar sections. Please scroll down page to view individual section course descriptions: SPRING 2014 Section 1 Murphy, Mark Derek Parfitâs 1984 Reasons and Persons is a tremendously influential and powerful work of philosophical ethics. The central question of the book is: What is the best theory of what we have most reason to do? Parfit attacks this question from various angles, asking whether some such theories are self-defeating, whether some theories are inherently unstable, whether some theories depend on false metaphysical beliefs about ourselves, whether some theories have the power to solve obviously important and vexing moral problems. We will read the whole book (except for some of the material in its Appendices), working together on understanding and evaluating its many arguments. Requirements include a lot of short writing assignments, a presentation of a draft of oneâs final paper, a comment on another studentâs final paper, and a final paper. A warning and an encouragement: This is a hard book. Reading will average only 25 pages per meeting or so, but they are hard pages. If you are willing to struggle with difficult material, you will get a lot out of doing the reading, doing the writing, and discussing this material in seminar. Section 2 Lu-Adler, Huaping Descartesâs Meditations on First Philosophy is revolutionary in the history of philosophy. The metaphysical and epistemological issues it raised have exercised many great philosophical minds ever since its publication. Following the most recent Descartes scholarship, we will sort out and discuss Descartesâs own views on these issues in light of his interest in the scientific revolution. To facilitate such discussions, we will read John Carrieroâs Between Two Worlds: A Reading of Descartesâs Meditations (2009)âas well as other relevant secondary literatureâalong with the Meditations itself. Students in this course should focus on cultivating and improving on the following skills: (1) reading complex and often difficult primary and secondary texts, and being able to recognize and interpret main points and arguments in the readings; (2) making well-informed critical comments, and being able to effectively present and support them in public; (3) identifying important philosophical issues; (4) appreciating the cultural and historical context, philosophical traditions and philosophical problems in connection with which a philosopherâs views were developed; (5) becoming proficient at writing a critical essay, which may involve various drafts and revisions based on feedbacks from the peers as well as the professor. Graded requirements include the following: (1) participation (30%), which involves writing brief commentaries on all reading assignments and one peer-executed and peer-evaluated oral exam (around mid-term); (2) one term paper, 1,500-2,000 words (45%), which involves an initial draft, two peer reviews and a final version that reflect improvements based on such reviews; (3) final oral exam on selected topics, given by the professor (25%). FALL 2013 Lewis, Neil In this seminar we'll be studying the treatise on free will written by the medieval thinker Peter John Olivi (1248-1298). Olivi is described on the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy as "one of the most original and interesting philosophers of the later Middle Ages. Although not as clear and systematic as Thomas Aquinas, and not as brilliantly analytical as John Duns Scotus, Olivi's ideas are equally original and provocative, and scarcely known even to specialists in medieval philosophy." Olivi's treatise on free will, in which he defends a libertarian account of free will, is one of the most substantial works on free will written in or even after the middle ages. This treatise has not been translated into English, but you won't need to know Latin to read it since we'll be reading it as the instructor translates it. We may well be the first group to read it in its entirety since the middle ages. The treatise is clearly written and contains an extensive presentation and rebuttal of deterministic arguments against free will, original and very modern-looking defenses of the existence of free will, an account of the will as an active self-moving power, and a discussion of the senses in which infants, madmen and sleepers do and do not have free will.

title: 20th Century German Philosophy
description: The course is a survey of some but not all of the main streams of German philosophy in the 20th century. In particular, it is focused on the development of phenomenology by Edmund Husserl; on Martin Heideggerâs attempt to extend and ultimately replace phenomenology with something called fundamental ontology (which got the nickname, âexistentialismâ); on the movement of neo-Kantianism which formed an important couterweight both to phenomenology and Heideggerâs thought (represented here by one figure, Ernst Cassirer); the movement that came to be called âWestern Marxismâ with its neo-Hegelian and humanist elements, as seen in the work of the Hungarian philosopher, Georg LukÃ¡cs (who wrote in German) and in Ernst Bloch (who rethought Marxism as a kind of non-theistic endorsement of biblical themes); Heideggerâs post-war âturnâ from his earlier work to his later thought on âbeingâ; Hans-Georg Gadamerâs reworking of Heideggerâs early work into the movement called âhermeneuticsâ; Dieter Henrichâs new beginning with a metaphysics of self-knowledge; finally, a quick overview of Frankfurt Critical Theory, an attempt to mate philosophy and empirical social science into a left-leaning emancipatory comprehensive social theory (Theodor Adorno, JÃ¼rgen Habermas, Axel Honneth). It is a survey course, and like all such courses, it gains in breadth what it loses in depth. The tension between breadth and depth will be one of the ongoing issues to be dealt with during the course of the semester.

title: 19th Century German Philosophy
description: The purpose of this course is to provide a historical and philosophical overview of the development of German philosophy from Kant to Nietzsche. The goals of the class involve getting a good historical overview of the development of German philosophy in that period, developing a good philosophical grasp of what were some of the key issues in the development of that tradition and grappling with the philosophical issues about why and how the development took the shape it did. A list of key topics to be considered would include but not be exhausted by terms such as âspontaneity,â âautonomy,â ârecognition,â âalienation,â âmodernity,â and âsubjectivity.â The focus all along will be on themes having to do with theories of knowledge, philosophy of mind, and the connection between ethics and political thought in all these thinkers. Aesthetics will also figure prominently. Among the philosophers to be considered will be: Kant, Fichte, Schleiermacher, Schelling, Hegel, Schopenhauer and Nietzsche. We will also look very briefly at the poets Novalis, HÃ¶lderlin, and Heine.

title: 20th Century European Philosophy
description: This course is a guided, collaborative excursion into important thinkers and themes in 20th C. European philosophy. Students will spend the semester researching and producing a substantial e-portfolio, and class sessions will be designed to further this work. In some sessions, students will informally present their own research and follow up on topics of interest that arise over the term. Other sessions will resemble traditional lectures, covering works by significant thinkers (such as Simone de Beauvoir, Maurice Merleau-Ponty, Emmanuel Levinas, Walter Benjamin, Roland Barthes, Martin Heidegger, Michel Foucault, Ferdinand de Saussure, Jacques Derrida and Giorgio Agamben) and significant themes (such as the influence of Hegel, recognition and interpersonal relationships, the body, art and authorship, technology and modernity, and the structure of language). We will also aim to read short works of literature that influenced or were influenced by 20th C. European philosophy (such as works by Primo Levi, Italo Calvino, and Franz Kafka). The course will be fast-paced, eclectic, philosophically sophisticated, and writing-, research- and reading-intensive. Students can expect to accomplish much of their learning outside of the classroom and will be required to actively work on their e-portfolios and to actively participate in class sessions throughout the term.

title: Ethics and Economics
description: This course is an effort at interdisciplinary thinking about the possible conflicting demands of efficiency and justice. In a first section we will look at the history of economic thought We will start by studying Adam SmithâS arguments for efficiency while not overlooking Smithâs concern for the common good. Then we will read some crucial publications by neo-liberal economists who defend the role of the government so as to keep the free market efficient (anti-trust laws, banking regulations, etc.). Next we will read a couple of chapters in Marxâs Capital illustrating great injustices in the capitalist system. In a second section we consult current economic theory. We will begin by summarizing the requirements for economic efficiency, as defined by the economic profession (welfare economics). We will then present the economic argument for market failure, particularly in the case of public goods. With the concept of merit good we encounter the explicit ethical dimension in economic thought. This concept will allow us to articulate the problems of property rights, anti-trust legislation, subsidies for education and social legislation. In a third section we will look at philosophical reflections upon economics as done by either philosophers or philosophically inclined economists (e.g., Hegel, Rawls, Sen, Baier, Buchanan)The implementation of ethical principles takes place within the context of existing ethos pattern (Olson or Briefs). We will also study how religious ethics plays an important role in the implementation of social justice. In a fourth section we will look how the financial crisis of 2007/08 can be clarified by the ideas developed in the previous chapters. Readings: Ver Eecke, W. (2013). Ethical Reflections on thr Fiancial Crisis 2007/08. Making Use of Smith, Musgrave and Rajan. Heidelberg, New York: Springer Verlag. Ver Eecke, W. (Editor).(2007). An Anthology regarding Merit Goods. The Unfinished Ethical Revolution in Economic Theory [Compiled with Commentary by Wilfried Ver Eecke]. West Lafayette: Purdue University Press. (This book is available at very reduced price in Amazon.com). A number of articles by economists, philosophers, and social scientists will be put on reserve in the library or made available electronically. There will be three take home exams, blackboard discussions and a research paper. For undergraduates the research paper is expected to be 5 pages. For graduates the research paper is expected to be between 15 and 20 pages.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: Logic examines the principles of correct reasoning. In Symbolic Logic we use and investigate one theory that seems to have successfully captured a variety of such principlesâclassical first order logic. The notions of formal language, proof and derivation, and truth under an interpretation will be considered in detail. For the most part we will be working within classical first order logic, but some time will be devoted to proving things about that theory and to possible alternatives to it. One of the main tools for this kind of âmetatheoreticâ investigation is mathematical induction. For this reason, and since this mathematical induction is itself a useful method of reasoning, we will spend some time on this. There are no pre-requisites for this course, but those who wish a slower-paced course that omits the metatheory and mathematical induction and aims more to improve reasoning ability should consider taking Introduction to Logic instead. This course is more suitable for those with theoretical interests in philosophy, mathematics, linguistics, computer science, or cognitive science. There will be no papers and little reading, but there will be problem sets.

title: Intermediate Logic
description: This course is designed to fill the gap between symbolic logic and advanced graduate courses in mathematical and philosophical logic. Symbolic logic emphasizes truth and proof within a particular formal system, usually some version of classical first order logic. The advanced courses emphasize metatheoretical results about a variety of such systems. In this course the plan will be to quickly review the material in the first thirteen chapters of the innovative text by Barwise and Etchemendy and then to work through Chapters 14-19 in greater depth and at a more leisurely pace. Those chapters introduce a variety of more advanced topics of interest in philosophy, computer science and linguistics. Topics to be covered include generalized quantifiers, naive and axiomatic set theory, resolution theorem proving, completeness proofs for propositional and first order logic, the compactness and Lowenheim Skolem theorems, and Godelâs theorem on the incompleteness of arithmetic. If enrollment is sufficiently small the class will be conducted in modified seminar format, with discussion and problem-solving playing as large a role as lectures.

title: History of Ancient and Medieval Philosophy
description: In the first half of the course (taught by David Bronstein) we will focus on the two giants of Ancient Greek Philosophy, Plato and Aristotle. Our main texts will be Platoâs Apology and Phaedo and selections from Aristotleâs Metaphysics, Categories, Physics, On the Soul, and Nicomachean Ethics. We will examine and compare Platoâs and Aristotleâs answers to such questions as: What is the fundamental nature of reality? What is the good life? What is the relationship between the mind and the body? What is knowledge and how do we acquire it? What is philosophy? The second part of the course (taught by Neil Lewis) takes up medieval philosophy, with a focus on philosophy in the Latin West. Philosophy is closely intertwined with theology in the middle ages and done within a Christian framework, so we will structure our examination of philosophical developments through the great theme of the departure and return of things to God, considering along the way how Greek thought is appropriated, transformed and, when necessary, rejected. Topics to be considered include the medieval understanding of the nature of philosophy, the existence and nature of God, Godâs relation to creatures (creation, freedom and foreknowledge), freedom of the will, natural law, and human fulfillment and the afterlife.

title: History of Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: In the first half of the course we will focus on the two giants of Ancient Greek Philosophy, Plato and Aristotle. Our main texts will be Platoâs Apology and Phaedo and selections from Aristotleâs Metaphysics, Categories, Physics, On the Soul, and Nicomachean Ethics. We will examine and compare Platoâs and Aristotleâs answers to such questions as: What is the fundamental nature of reality? What is the good life? What is the relationship between the mind and the body? What is knowledge and how do we acquire it? In the second half of the course, we will look at a number of significant figures in medieval philosophy (c. 400-1400 AD), as the Greek heritage was adapted by three religious traditions, the Jewish, Muslim and Christian faiths. Figures will include Augustine, Anselm, Abelard, Isaac Israeli, Saadia, Avicenna, Averroes, Maimonides, Aquinas and Duns Scotus. We also will look at questions such as the fundamental nature of reality and the good life, but also questions such as the nature of possibility, universals, determinism and the freedom of the will, along with the relation of faith and reason.

title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: Classic philosophical texts of the 16th through the 18th centuries (from the scientific revolution through Kant) will be examined in the context of historical developments in the philosophy of the period. The emphasis in lectures will be on textual analysis of major texts and the historical development of modern philosophy, often with discussion of the adequacy of the various competing philosophical theories. The major texts of major figures in the history of philosophy will be carefully examined, but the writings of several less renowned, yet historically significant, figures will also be examined (e.g. Newton, Malebranche, Pascal, and Reid). The assigned material falls into the following general areas of philosophy: Metaphysics and Epistemology (M&E) Moral and Political Philosophy (MPP) The history of modern philosophy covered in this course dictates that most of the assigned reading be in M&E, but the student will be given ample opportunity to study assignments in MPP and to write papers in this area. A part of the design of the course is to allow students to work on philosophical problems that interest them the most.

title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: We will examine some of the central questions of early modern philosophy (17th-18th European philosophy). We will trace the origins of these questions in the relevant cultural, religious and scientific contexts and especially in the Scientific Revolution. The main topics to be discussed include a) the possibility and extent of human knowledge and scientific explanation, b) the possibility of a philosophical understanding of God and c) the possibility of human freedom. We will examine a variety of views on these topics both by the canonical figures including Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz, Locke, Berkeley, Hume and Kant and by thinkersâespecially women philosophers such as Margaret Cavendish, Emilie du ChÃ¢telet, Anne Conwayâwho are not normally included in the canon of modern philosophy. In this course the students should aim at (i) becoming familiar with important themes and figures in early modern philosophy, (ii) learning how to approach and analyze a historical text, (iii) becoming proficient at writing short essays on historical topics and (iv) developing skills for effectively presenting and supporting views in public.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Today, people are raising many philosophical questions about religion, and this course aims to address a number of the more central ones. Most of these questions have been treated by prominent thinkers of high philosophical quality, and we will be examining thinkers from both the past and the contemporary philosophical scene. These latter are particularly important since there has been much recent work in the philosophy of religion. In the past forty years there has been a robust resurgence of interest in applying contemporary philosophical methods to perennial issues pertaining to religion, and new perspectives have opened up, yielding new approaches to old problems and new areas for investigation. We will be discussing some of the following topics: God and evil, religious experience, faith and reason, who God is, proofs for Godâs existence and whether belief in God needs proof, language about God, miracles, life after death, religion and science, religious diversity, religion and science, religion and morality. By conversation and confrontation with the philosophers and their writings on these topics, you will be in an informed position to develop your own views. Syllabus Required text: Philosophy of Religion: Selected Readings, 4th edition, 2010. Edited by Michael Peterson, William Hasker, Bruce Reichenbach and David Basinger. This 4th edition is available at Amazon, etc., and there are various used copies. In addition, it will be at the bookstore. 1. What are the goals of this course? One primary goal is to sharpen and widen your set of analytical skills, both verbal and written. By continual practice, you will grow in your ability to analyze arguments found in texts, display them clearly in premise and conclusion form, and evaluate them both for truth and validity. The second primary goal is achieved in tandem with the first, that is, by using your analytical skills on texts dealing with the philosophy of religion, you will arrive at your own informed opinions on a wide range of issues concerning religion. The texts are of high philosophical quality by prominent thinkers, both from the past and the contemporary philosophical scene. Some topics are: God and evil, religious experience, faith and reason, who God is, proofs for Godâs existence and whether belief in God needs proof, language about God, miracles, life after death, religion and science, religious diversity, religion and science, religion and morality. By conversation and confrontation with the philosophers and their writings on these topics, you will form your own positions. A third goal is simply to appreciate recent work in the philosophy of religion. In the past thirty years there has been a robust resurgence of interest in applying contemporary philosophical methods to perennial issues pertaining to religion, and new perspectives have opened up, yielding new approaches to old problems and new areas for investigation. 2. How will the goals of this course be achieved? A. The sharpening of verbal analytical abilities will take place through class discussion. This is reflected in the percentage of the final grade that depends on that participation. B. The sharpening of written analytical abilities will take place through the writing of a short, 1-2 page paper each week. Each student should be prepared to present the paper for discussion to the class on the day the paper is due, either a Monday or Wednesday. On two occasions during the semester, each student will be allowed (but not required) to skip a week and write a 3-4 page paper the next week. C. You will have the opportunity to form your own informed position on a topic that interests you by writing a 5-10 page final paper. 2.1 Expectations 2.1.1 Expectations regarding Attendance and Participation Since attendance is a necessary condition for participation in class discussion, my expectation is that every student attends every class and arrives on time for class. Absenteeism and lateness to class count heavily against the class participation grade since they adversely impact the learning of the class as a whole. (If you have a legitimate reason for missing class or coming late, such as a personal or family emergency, please contact me as soon as possible, preferably prior to missing class. I can be reached by telephone (leaving a message on voicemail if I am not in) or by email. If you miss a class, it is your responsibility to find out what you missed, including any changes in the assignment for the next class.) Participation: Active and thoughtful class participation is critical to your learning and the learning of your classmates, and so class participation accounts for a significant portion of the total grade of the course (30%). In evaluating class participation, I will consider both quality and frequency of contributions, with emphasis on the former. In assessing quality I consider the following types of issues: â¢ Does the comment simply repeat parts of the reading or does it provide analysis that adds to our understanding of the issue and its broader implications? â¢ Does the comment fit well into the flow of the discussion? Is it linked to the comments of others? â¢ Is the comment presented in a clear, compelling manner or is it confused, repetitive or contradictory? 2.1.2 Expectations for Short Written Assignments All short written assignments must be turned in by the start of the class of the day designated for it to be turned in. No extensions will be granted. A late penalty will be automatically assessed should you not obtain prior permission under exceptional circumstances. Students should bring two copies of the assignment to class, one for the professor and one for themselves in case they are called upon OR you may submit written assignments ahead of time electronically (Word.DOC or Adobe. PDF) through Blackboard. The time stamp of electronic submission would determine if an assignment was submitted on time. All written work must be printed out with generous margins and double spacing, with pages stapled together. Proofread carefully. Failure to follow these instructions will diminish the quality of your work and affect your grade. 2.1.3 Expectations for 5-10 Page Paper I expect you to choose a topic of real interest to you. By the end of March you should come into my office and talk to me briefly about your paper. I can help you choose current articles or a book on the topic and also help you to focus your paper. The typical fault of studentsâ papers is trying to do too much with a consequent lack of focus. So, pick a topic, find one or two articles or a short book, analyze the key argument(s) and come up with your own reasoned position. The paper is due May 9.

title: Just Wars in Theory and Practice
description: The course presents the major principles of just war thinking and examines the problems of justification and application which arise when this tradition of moral thought is used to guide and criticize policy decisions in the contemporary world. Key issues are: noncombatant immunity, nuclear deterrence, humanitarian intervention, guerrilla warfare, terrorism. Students are expected to prepare reports on particular cases, which will also be addressed by visiting speakers. Spring.

title: Just War Theory
description: The course presents the major principles of just war thinking and examines the problems of justification and application which arise when this tradition of moral thought is used to guide and criticize policy decisions in the contemporary world. Key issues are: noncombatant immunity, nuclear deterrence, humanitarian intervention, guerrilla warfare, terrorism. Students are expected to prepare reports on particular cases, which will also be addressed by visiting speakers. Spring.

title: Social and Political Philosophy
description: TBA.

title: Epistemology
description: This course will be a survey of contemporary issues in epistemology. The course is designed primarily for philosophy majors, though it is open to others, and it will both introduce students to the field and prepare them for advanced work. Assignments will include class discussion and four 5-7 page papers.

title: Global Justice/The Environment
description: The central topics examined in this course pertain to the global use and distribution of vital, but dwindling resources of food, energy, and water (the FEW Problem). Policy choices regarding any one of these resources are intertwined with resource implications for the other two. Beyond the policy challenges are deeper, often unrecognized issues of global justice. Access to and control over these resources raise fundamental questions about the paths to global development, poverty alleviation, and the capacity of individuals and nations to secure the basic requirements for decent human lives and to preserve a sustainable human habitat. The relevant issues of global justice arise primarily out of the ways that major political institutions and patterns of economic organization shape food, energy, and water policies, and as a consequence exert profound and pervasive impact on the life prospects of everyone, especially the most vulnerable, least powerful people on the planet. For more on the FEW Problem, see my website fewresources.org. Reading assignments and background resources for projects will be drawn from the FEW website, along with reading assignments posted on Blackboard or made available as electronic reserves. There is no required text. The course requirements include vigorous class participation, student responsibility for introducing the main ideas and issues presented by the assigned readings, and a class presentation on a topic that will be the subject of the final term paper.

title: Set Theory
description: Set theory is where mathematics and philosophy overlap. Understanding it is critical to philosophical studies such as the foundations of mathematics and semantics, as well as certain mathematical studies, such as topology. This course is a standard, not very hard, introduction to axiomatic set theory. We will begin with the intuitive ânaive set theory,â as Frege and Russell did, and see how that leads to paradox, as Frege and Russell did. (There is a town with a barber who shaves all and only those who donât shave themselves. Why shaves the Barber?) Then unlike F & R weâll study an elegant and popular axiomatic set theory that gets round the paradoxes: itâs called ZF set theory after its founders Zermelo and Fraenkel. Weâll see some of the lovely things that can be done in ZF (such as ordinal and cardinal arithmetic), and some of the things that are independent of ZF (such as the Axiom of Choice and the Continuum Hypothesis)âalthough their independence from ZF can usually be proved in ZF itself! Along the way weâll reflect on some of the philosophical implications of these results in connection with the foundation of mathematics and more general epistemological and metaphysical questions. Two easy exams, seven problem sets. Text: Keith Devlin: The Joy of Sets: Fundamentals of Contemporary Set Theory.

title: Philosophy of Mind and Cognitive Science
description: In this course we will examine some of the core issues in the philosophy of mind and cognitive science. We will look at a number of issues concerning the methodology that ought to be adopted in studying the mind. We will ask whether the mind is best understood in computational terms; we will ask whether the mind is a system of dedicated modules; we will ask how important it is that the mind is embodied; and we will ask whether the mind extends beyond the boundaries of skin-and-skull. Over the course of the semester you will write two short papers, and you will be expected to contribute to in-class discussion. Previous knowledge of psychology and cognitive science will be helpful, but this background is not required; similarly, prior work in the philosophy of science, philosophy of language, or metaphysics will be incredibly helpful.

title: Ethics of Death and Dying
description: The ethical problems of the care of the terminally ill have been among the most controversial issues in medical ethics over the past decade: the definition of death, intentional killing or mercy, forgoing life-support, living wills, surrogate decision-making, ethics committees, neonatal death, and demands for futile care. This course will explore these issues combining lecture, discussion, and film. The course will not emphasize the sociological and psychological aspects of death and dying, concentrating instead on philosophical, ethical, and public policy issues. There will be a mid-term paper and a final exam. The final exam will be a take-home final distributed the last day of class and due on the scheduled examination date.

title: Paradoxes and Legacy of J.S. Mill
description: J. S. Mill was the most important and influential English-speaking philosopher of the nineteenth century. He made significant contributions across every area of philosophy as well as in practical politics, and wrote one of the seminal works of feminism. Yet his life and work are riddled with paradoxes. Was Mill a libertarian or a socialist? An elitist or an egalitarian? Was he even a utilitarian? We will approach these questions through a study some of Mill's most important moral and political writings. In addition to Utilitarianism, On Liberty, The Subjection of Women, and (parts of) Considerations on Representative Government, readings will likely include the essays on Bentham, Coleridge, and Tocqueville; "A Few Words on Nonintervention"; âChapters on Socialismâ; as well as some secondary sources and contemporary writings about Mill.

title: Aristotle and Virtue Ethics
description: This course will be an exploration of Aristotleâs Nicomachean Ethics as a text in ancient philosophy and as the inspiration for contemporary accounts of virtue ethics. The topics for the course will center on the idea of virtuous action, but will range widely across important themes in both Aristotleâs ethics and contemporary neo-Aristotelian ethics. Because the course fulfills one of the graduate programâs history requirements, students can expect to work closely with the Nicomachean Ethics and relevant secondary literature. They can also expect to become well-versed in the current literature in virtue ethics. Required reading for the course includes the Nicomachean Ethics (translation TBA), Rosalind Hursthouseâs On Virtue Ethics, and a number of articles that will be made available through Blackboard.

title: Phenomenology
description: "Phenomenology" is a name both for a movement within 20th century philosophy and for a method of doing philosophy. The movement is meant to be unified by the method. These days self-identified phenomenologists are typically scholars who work on the texts and doctrines of the major figures of the movement. In this seminar we will explore the question whether we can identify a (or perhaps several) phenomenological method(s). We will examine some of the texts of leading figures in the movement (Husserl, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty) with an eye to the questions, What is phenomenological method? Can it be properly distinguished from conceptual/linguistic analysis, ordinary language philosophy, and traditional philosophical dialectic? What is the distinction supposed to be between transcendental phenomenology (Husserl after about 1905) and existential phenomenology (Heidegger before about 1933 and Merleau-Ponty)?

title: Hannah Arendt
description: Hannah Arendt was one of the twentieth century's greatest and most influential political philosophers. But her philosophical contributions included moral psychology and ethics, as well as legal and constitutional theory, philosophy of history, and the analysis of violence and power. In ethics she developed a view akin to contemporary particularism, and her moral psychology produced the famous (and much-misunderstood) concept of the "banality of evil." In law she had important ideas about international tribunals, criminal responsibility, civil disobedience, statelessness, and constitutionalism. We will also take a sidelong glance at Arendt in connection with the philosophers who most influenced her (Augustine, Kant, Heidegger). Readings will be drawn from Eichmann in Jerusalem, The Human Condition, and portions of several other books (Between Past and Future, On Revolution, Men in Dark Times, Crises of the Republic, her Kant lectures); the important essays "Thinking and Moral Considerations" and "Organized Guilt and Universal Responsibility"; excerpts from her correspondences with Heidegger, Karl Jaspers, and Gershom Scholem; and related philosophical material by other authors.

title: Human Nature
description: What kinds of things are human beings? Are they competitive or cooperative by nature? Are they naturally disposed toward hatred and violence, love and compassion, or do they simply strive to increase their power? What roles do âbiologicalâ and âculturalâ factors play in the production of constraints on human excellence and human well-being? These types of questions have long been the bread-and-butter of philosophy, and they are once again coming to prominence in philosophical and scientific contexts. In this course we will examine the extent to which investigations in the cognitive and biological sciences can tell us something interesting about human nature; we will also consider the possibility that such investigations are hopelessly misguided.

title: Epistemology Proseminar
description: This is a graduate level introduction to epistemology. We will begin by surveying some of the classic and enduring problems of modern epistemology, such as the problem of skepticism, the nature of knowledge and justification, knowing how and knowing that, the question of whether knowledge has a foundation, perceptual knowledge and the âgivenâ, and the possibility of a priori knowledge. We will then turn to social epistemology, and briefly examine the epistemology of testimony, the nature of expertise, and the possibility of collective knowledge. Finally we will focus in more depth on two currently âhotâ topics in epistemology: standpoint epistemology and pragmatic encroachment. Throughout, we will make integral use of feminist and anti-oppression lenses and the tools of philosophy of science. The final exam serves as the Departmentâs comprehensive exam in epistemology.

title: Deontic Pluralism
description: Many moral theorists have tended to focus on the thinnest possible assessments of action âon what is permissible or impermissible, required or forbidden. While such categories are important, exclusive focus on them tends to obscure some of the most important categories we use to navigate the moral life: what does it mean to wrong another, rather than just do wrong with respect to them? what does it mean to act badly rather than wrongly? how do we understand regulative ideals and imperfectly structured duties? can we have a moral right to do wrong? Many theorists of practical rationality, in turn, have tended to focus on a similarly spare approach to the rational intelligibility of an action -- such intelligibility is a function of the balance of reasons, and reasons can be fully analyzed in terms of domain, direction, and strength. While such categories are important, building a theory of rational intelligibility and agency out of them may be problematic: is agency impossible when the reasons run out? do reasons always direct us deontically, or are they sometimes content to commend? are rules reducible to reasons? can speech acts, such as requests and demands, also serve as intelligible bases for action? This seminar will explore these questions with an eye to asking whether a rich, rather than spare, theory of morality and again practical rationality does better justice to our lives as agents. Readings will include selections from a wide variety of contemporary authors, including Joseph Raz, Carl Wellman, Onora Oneil, Steve Darwall, David Owens, Patricia Greenspan, Jay Wallace, Julia Driver, and Jonathan Dancy.

title: Later Heidegger
description: In this seminar, we will attempt to understand and intelligibly express the claims about being that Heidegger made in his âlaterâ works. The first part of the semester will be dedicated to reading Introduction to Metaphysics (1935), in which Heidegger initiates us into the questioning of being and shows us how to think the human being as Dasein and being as phusis. We will go on to read various essays, covering (i) the history of being and Heideggerâs critiques of modern science and technology, (ii) the role of art and poetry in the granting of being and in offering a way out of modernity, (iii) Heideggerâs deployment of the notion of the fourfold and (iv) Heideggerâs announcement of the end of philosophy and his sketch of a new kind of thinking. Our goal throughout will be to break through the barrier of Heideggerâs terminology and determine how to express his ideas in plain language. Students can expect to produce regular written assignments and to give in-class presentations. Prior familiarity with Heideggerâs thought is neither required or expected. The course is open to graduate students in the Philosophy Department; others only with permission. No auditors. Texts: Martin Heidegger, Introduction to Metaphysics, trans. Gregory Fried and Richard Polt. Yale, 2000. 0300083289 Martin Heidegger, Basic Writings, ed. David Farrell Krell. Revised, expanded edition. Harper Perennial Modern Classics, 2008. 0061627011
