The objectives of the course are two-fold: First, to understand the basic principles of tax policy, and second, to gain a deeper understanding of some of the major tax policy issues and choices facing the United States today.

title: GLOBALIZATION: ISSUES, ACTORS & CHOICES FOR THE 21ST CENTURY
description: This course examines the nature and impact of globalization. The class begins with an attempt to define, measure, and trace the path of globalization over the last three decades. We then look at how trade theory fits into the world of globalization (and its limitations). We also explore exciting recent work that describes the globalization of production processes in terms of value added and global supply chains. We examine how the mismeasurement of globalization has led to possibly serious biases in the measurement of real GDP and labor productivity growth rate. We then turn to an examination of the aspects of globalization in a variety of areas, including, the question of whether globalization has help alleviate or exacerbated poverty and its impact on income inequality throughout the world; a perspective on the impact of the global financial crisis on globalization; and the role of immigration and labor in a global economy, among others.

title: COST-BENEFIT ANALYSIS & COST EFFECTIVENESS ANALYSIS
description: This course is designed to provide the student with the analytic frameworks and tools for conducting cost-effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses. The course emphasizes the application of these analyses to public policy with special attention to education. The student will study the allocation of resources and tradeoffs involved in the production of education, and estimate the involved productivity relationships. The learning objectives also include the study of the elements that compose cost-effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses. The student will be trained in the techniques necessary to conduct these analyses. The course relies on a combination of theoretic and practical work that will ensure the right preparation for those students interested in education, evaluation, administration and public policy.

title: POVERTY AND THE SOCIAL SAFETY NET
description: This seminar course examines the nature and extent of poverty in the U.S., and looks at public policies aimed at creating a social safety net as protection against the effects of poverty. Anti-poverty policies - social insurance, income maintenance and other programs- are discussed with an emphasis on the behavioral impacts of the policies and their ultimate effectiveness in reducing poverty. Education and labor market policies for the disadvantaged are considered as well.

title: MANAGEMENT OF PROGRAM EVALUATION
description: The course objective is to develop studentsâ abilities in the crucial executive functions of managing the evaluation of policy programs. Although there will be significant devotion to academic consideration, the course is designed to focus principally on the executive practitionerâless so on the scholar. For example, although there will be serious treatment of statistical concepts, the successful student will not become a statistician; he or she will become comfortable and confident with the most important professional usages of statistics. Perhaps more importantly, the course will attempt to âinoculateâ the student from inappropriate useâintentional or otherwiseâof quantitative techniques. This graduate level class will stress (1) conceptualization, (2) design methodology (qualitative as well as quantitative), (3) conduct (collection of evidence) and oversight of the evaluation process, (4) critical analysis, including a focus on oft-underestimated implementation issues, and (5) interpretation and apolitical communication of findings.

title: U.S. DEFENSE POLICY MAKING
description: The foremost responsibility of the United States government is to provide for the common defense. This seminar style course examines the context for U.S. national security, the making of defense policy, and the implementation of that policy. Part I surveys the context of American defense and national security. It will emphasize the post 9/11 security environment. Part II studies the defense policymaking process, with emphasis on who participates and how. Part III examines the implementation of defense policy within the Department of Defense. It will stress current activities and processes. Part IV looks at contemporary issues in U.S. national security and defense policy.

title: NATURAL RESOURCES POLICY
description: This course provides an overview of the key elements of natural resources policy. Students will study the relevant statutes, legal interpretations, and development of underlying policies relating to natural resources in the United States. During the course, students will draw on pertinent and practical legislative and administrative materials, such as bills and testimony, regulations, budget materials, and environmental analyses. The course will address some of the pressing natural resources topics of today, such as hydraulic fracturing and climate change adaptation, as well as providing an overview of federal land use management planning for our public lands and forests, wilderness and protected lands, mining, oil and gas development on Federal lands and the Outer Continental Shelf, the policy and law impacting the availability of water resources, and innovative approaches to addressing natural resources issues on an ecosystem scale. Throughout the course, we will consider the successes and failures of natural resources policy, the trade-offs between preservation and development, and future prospects for effective resource management and conservation. Students will be expected to participate in class discussions and in a mock administrative or congressional proceeding, as well as to complete a final project (research paper and oral presentation).

title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF DEVELOPMENT
description: This class explores some of the central debates in the application of political economy to development policy. We will examine how political institutions influence economic development and how economic factors, in turn, impact politics in developing countries. We will also explore why governments employ policies that may actually hinder development. The goal of this course is for students to think critically about development both in theory and in practice. As such, we will study the main theories of development policy from both political and economic perspectives. And, students will become well acquainted with development policy by studying real world cases and developing practical policy recommendations of their own. ** MPP/MPM ELECTIVE ONLY

title: INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS
description: This course will provide an overview of the international financial institutions (the World Bank, regional development banks, and the International Monetary Fund) from a policymaker's perspective. Students will gain a better understanding of the diversity of roles the international financial institutions (IFIs) play in the global economy, ranging from bailouts in Europe to conditional cash transfer programs in Honduras to hotel construction in Ghana. The course will explore the history of the IFIs, their governance structures, and their instruments of engagement as a basis for understanding their approaches to today's international policy issues. Students will be encouraged to examine the IFIs from a policymaker's perspective through policy-oriented writing assignments and simulations of IFI-related policymaking exercises.

title: INTERNATIONAL TRADE POLICY & NEGOTIATIONS
description: International trade agreements have evolved from relatively simple agreements to comprehensive treaties that cover all aspects of economic relations between countriesâsuch as trade in goods, services, investment, intellectual property, food standards, labor, and the environment. Consequently, trade agreements are often politically contentious and have become lighting rods for a wide-range of concerns about globalization. Understanding how trade agreements work and how they have been critiqued is timely as the United States is engaged in negotiations to create the two largest free trade agreements in the world: the Trans-Pacific and Trans-Atlantic partnerships. International Trade Policy and Negotiations will expose students to a comprehensive understanding of modern international trade policy-making, trade agreements and trade negotiations. The class will explore countriesâ motives for negotiating trade agreements; the evolution of these agreements at a multilateral, regional and bilateral level; how trade agreements are typically structured; the range of diverse areas they cover; the domestic policy debates around trade; and critiques of free trade and globalization, particularly from civil society and developing countries. The class will also discuss practical skills in negotiating international treaties.

title: NONPROFITS IN HEALTHCARE: LEADERSHIP & EFFECTIVENESS
description: Nonprofit organizations play a large role in the health sector. Nonprofits in Washington are particularly focused on health policy. There are several challenges facing these organizations in the current environment which demand skilled leadership. Students who take this class will get an introduction to the range of issues facing nonprofit leaders, including membership, finance, and effectiveness. They will also personally engage with leaders in the field, both in the classroom and in field interviews. The class will employ case studies of DC based nonprofits. Students will research an organization of their choice, perform a situational analysis, and recommend a course of action for the organizationâs leaders. These recommendations will then be presented to the class for group evaluation. Students who take this class will have a fuller appreciation of the range of issues facing organizational leaders, and will be prepared to be effective participants in this sector themselves.

title: FINANCE IN DEVELOPMENT
description: The aim of the course is to introduce students to the trade-off between the growth promoting and the volatility creating role of financial markets in developing and emerging market economies. On the one hand, a broad strand of the policy debate has dealt with the so-called finance-growth-nexus. In particular, financial liberalization and the implementation of growth enhancing financial policies and accompanying instruments such as microfinance (e.g. microcredits, microinsurance, Islamic finance) have achieved great prominence in the debate on financial development. On the other hand, even before the outbreak of the current global financial crisis, the so-called finance-volatility-nexus has been discussed intensively as a potential down-side risk arising from liberal financial market governance practices. Insofar, the course will introduce students to underlying mechanisms behind phenomena such as lending booms, international over-borrowing, and financial crisis with a special focus on the role of political economy aspects. In doing so, the course will consist of a combination of case studies, as well as empirical and theoretical approaches to address these multifaceted aspects of financial development.

title: RISK MANAGEMENT
description: Resources are finite, and policy-making commands trade-offs. For example, an agency or department might focus on the prevention of nuclear terrorism at the expense of other priorities, such as violent crime policing or pandemic planning and response. But such a decision might not represent the best use of scarce resources, depending on the risk associated with each priority. This course examines risk management in the public policy context. It introduces students to the concepts and methods of risk, risk assessment, and risk management; and it presents examples of applications of these concepts and methods in policy settings including national/homeland security, foreign policy, financial regulation, public health, and the environment.

title: BRICS & THE GLOBAL ECONOMY
description: Three out of every seven people in the world today live in BRICS countries of Brazil, Russia, India, China, and recently added South Africa. BRICS today account for over a third of the global foreign exchange reserves and its output is projected to surpass that of the USA by 2018. This course will provide, using workable in- class case studies, an in depth analysis of comparative economic development across BRICS economies from a policy-oriented perspective. The course will examine how these countries became the âdriving economiesâ, marshaling substantial resources and large populations to industrialize, growing at rapid rates, and maintaining central positions in their respective regions and the international political economy. Current growth strategies of BRICS economies also offer us the opportunity to look into the larger questions of global order and national development. How can states and markets work together to generate equitable and participatory growth? How should the BRICS economies be factored into the 21st centuryâs policy challenges, especially in reworking international financial institutions after the recent economic crisis? What do the experiences of the BRICS mean for the many economies that still lack such rapid growth? The course will evaluate if BRICS are the next drivers of economic growth for the global economy?

title: INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL  ECONOMY
description: The key objective of the course is to introduce students to key concepts of modern international political economy. Starting from a closed economy perspective, the course will address topics related to the question how political economy forces shape economic policies in industrial, transition and developing economies. In this respect, the emphasis will be on modern theories of political economy and recent empirical findings thereof. In particular, the course will focus on the role of special interest groups, popular electoral pressure and institutional design in driving economic policies and outcomes. Although a minor share of course material will be based on formal models, the aim is to assist students in developing strong analytical skills and a sound intuition concerning the identification of political motives in driving primarily macroeconomic outcomes, rather than to engaging in excessive economic formal modeling exercises. Starting with some basic tools for analyzing driving forces behind political decision-making processes, the course will cover a variety of policy areas such as institution building, fiscal and monetary policy, as well as government responses to financial crises episodes in industrial, transition and developing economies. In order to get a deeper understanding of certain international political economy concepts, selected case studies, for instance on the Arab Spring, the Eurozone crises and the reform of the International Monetary Fund will complement the course.

title: HISTORY OF DEVELOPMENT
description: The History and Theory of Development is a survey of the economic histories of a number of societies and regions such as South America, Africa, Japan and Russia. Designing development policy today requires a careful understanding of socio-political and economic contexts. The aim of this course is to discuss a few contexts in some detail that will inform a term project describing the economic histories of a country of the student's choice.

title: HISTORY OF DEVELOPMENT
description: The History and Theory of Development is a survey of the economic histories of a number of societies and regions such as South America, Africa, Japan and Russia. Designing development policy today requires a careful understanding of socio-political and economic contexts. The aim of this course is to discuss a few contexts in some detail that will inform a term project describing the economic histories of a country of the student's choice.

title: INTERNATIONAL ECONOMICS
description: This course is divided into two parts. The first deals with the essential issues of globalization: Why nations trade with each other, the importance of trade, the factors that determine trade and production, the gains that accrue from trade, the effect of trade on the distribution of national income, the formation of regional trade blocs, and the nature and costs of government policies that impede trade. The second part deals with issues of international finance: the balance of payments, the determination of exchange rates, alternative exchange rate regimes and their implications for domestic monetary and fiscal policies, the macroeconomic effects of international capital movements, and dollarization.

title: HOMELAND SECURITY
description: This course is intended to provide a broad and deep understanding of homeland security policy. The September 11th terrorist attacks put homeland security at the very top of the national public policy agenda. Securing the homeland is an extremely complex mission that requires a coordinated and integrated effort from all levels of society: the federal government, state and local governments, the private and nonprofit sectors, and the American people. The concept of homeland security focuses on protecting the nation from terrorist attacks but it also encompasses planning and preparing for natural disasters. The goal is to provide students a thorough understanding of the policy, strategy, legal and organizational issues and challenges associated with the defense of the U.S. homeland, the efforts underway to meet those challenges, and the range of means and methods that are or could be brought to bear on this subject.

title: POST-CONFLICT RECONSTRUCTION
description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the components and challenges of post-conflict reconstruction. Students will gain familiarity with planning, the Essential Tasks Matrix, the interagency and multinational process, civil-military relations in PCR, and project monitoring and evaluation. Topics include the benefits of sequenced vs simultaneous approaches; phases of reconstruction; and operating in a complex, unstable, unsustainable post-conflict environment. Case studies will serve to highlight âlessons learnedâ from previous and on-going reconstruction efforts.

title: CLIENTS IN THE POLICY ANALYSIS PROCESS
description: This course is designed for MPP students in their last semester and students who receive the consent of the instructor. It focuses on the role of clients in the policy analysis process. One core aspect of policy analysis involves the relationship between the analyst and the individual he or she is advising â the client. This seminar includes examination of the literature on clients in policy analysis as well as exposure to the range of clients and their relationship to analysts. Students will be asked to examine their own analytic work and to think about the clients who would be involved in that topic. This course provides an opportunity for students to develop an understanding of: - The differences between policy research and policy analysis - Identifying and dealing with clients - Defining the expectations of clients - Defining a policy problem - Changing relationships between analysts and clients - Mapping the policy environment - Writing a policy analysis memo to a specific client

title: SURVEY RESEARCH METHODS
description: Surveys are the most commonly used data collection tool in the social sciences, providing for efficient collection of data over broad populations, amenable to administration in person, by telephone, and over the Internet. The course teaches state of the art methods for designing, conducting, and interpreting the results of surveys. The emphasis is on the development of practical skills that will be valuable additions to the MSPP studentâs analytic toolkit. The course integrates perspectives from sociology, economics and cognitive psychology and methodologies commonly applied within policy evaluation, social science and public opinion research settings. Topics include questionnaire development and testing, judging the quality of a survey, the use and misuse of survey methods, sources of bias/error in survey design and analysis, techniques for dealing with limitations in surveys, data collection techniques, coding and data processing, and ethical issues in survey research.

title: PUBLIC OPINION & POLITICAL BEHAVIOR
description: In this course, we will survey the major literature on public opinion. We will seek to understand how citizens come to develop attitudes towards politics and policies and what they do about their political attitudes, be it to debate, organize, vote, advocate for policy, run for office, or nothing at all. Theories of political and social psychology, economics, cognition and collective action will provide different perspectives on the topic. Given the important role that opinion polling has played in contemporary politics, we will also study how public opinion is measured by designing, fielding, and analyzing our own public opinion survey. Though the focus of this course will be on American politics, much of the literature has comparative implications and parallels. Research in this field is largely quantitative so it is recommended that students have at least one semester of statistics education (undergraduate or graduate) prior to taking this course.

title: THE POWER and INFLUENCE OF PHILANTHROPY:  LOCAL, NATIONAL, GLOBAL
description: How does the power and influence of philanthropy have an impact on public policy? This course takes a critical look at the practices of local, national, and global philanthropy, how the field deploys its resources to influence public policies, and how to tap into these resources. It examines the diverse strategies of foundations and individual donors to advance their missions -- from developing cross-sector partnerships to fostering innovation and social entrepreneurship; from building community capacity to supporting policy research, education and advocacy; from raising up community ideas to adopting grant making and other approaches that address the root causes of social problems. It explores how philanthropic organizations assess the effectiveness of their own work and that of the programs they fund, and whether these efforts are sufficient. Recognizing its strengths and deficiencies, students will re-imagine philanthropyâs role in meeting the complex challenges of a global society, starting with the communities in which we live. Students will interact with guest speakers and present their ideas and findings to leading practitioners in the field.

title: THESIS WORKSHOP II
description: The thesis is a two-semester project required of all public policy students for graduation. For the thesis, students conduct original research on a government or public program or issue of their choosing, and summarize their findings in a thesis to be completed during the second semester. GPPI faculty have developed The Thesis Guide on the Web as a reference and aid for students who are completing their thesis projects.

title: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: PPOL 702: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. The main objectives of this module are to familiarize students with the most important economic development issues confronting and challenging African countries today and to demonstrate how economic analysis may be used to better understand and address these development issues. The module begins with an analysis of Africaâs post-independence development experience, often characterized as a growth tragedy, and the factors, both exogenous and policy-induced, that help to explain that performance. The module then turns to current African development issues, including: the transition to more market-friendly policies and the effectiveness of structural adjustment programs; Africaâs unfinished reform agenda, particularly downsizing and changing the role and functions of the state in the economy, including the establishment of new regulatory systems and the divestiture and privatization of state-owned enterprises; macroeconomic stabilization, including the role of Africaâs central banks and exchange rate regimes; the persistence of poverty and income inequality in African countries; the capacity and credibility of public sector institutions and the difficulties of governance and economic policy making in ethnically divided and war-torn African states; Africaâs marginalization in world trade and limited access to private capital markets; Africaâs investment in human capital and the fight against HIV/Aids; the constraints imposed by Africaâs limited physical infrastructure; how to deepen Africaâs financial sector; the agricultural sectorâs role in supporting broad-based economic growth; the effectiveness of official development aid (ODA); the burden of Africaâs external debt and the highly indebted poor country (HIPC) initiative; and finally the scope for regional economic cooperation among African countries. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF FOREIGN AID
description: This module will cover official development assistance (ODA) by major bilateral and multilateral donors (e.g., USAID and the World Bank) as well as assistance from new donors (e.g., China) and new forms of aid delivery such as global funds (e.g., Global Fund for AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria), large NGOs and foundations (e.g., Save the Children; Gates Foundation). We will review trends in aid flowsâcontrasting aid with other ways to finance development (FDI, trade, debt relief, remittances). We will adopt a historical perspective and consider how foreign aid has changed since the end of World War II, starting with old-style foreign aid and conditionality during the Cold War period (from the 1950s to the late 1980s) and examine changes in the âaid paradigmâ adopted by rich countries. Our emphasis will be on whether aid is effective and achieves measurable results. We will consider the "macro-micro paradox" in the effectiveness of foreign aid, namely that aid achieves micro successes but that they are not matched by visible macro growth effects. Macro growth effects are hard to achieve because the magnitude of aid is not sufficient to affect recipient countries macro variables, and harder to observe because statistical causality is difficult to establish. Micro effects are easier to observe and can be detected through rigorous impact evaluation methods. However, micro successes must meet the challenges of scalability and sustainability. We will consider the economic and social impact of aid, using empirical evidence, asking whether and how aid contributes to growth and poverty reduction in developing countries. We will also discuss project and program evaluation methods, with a few case studies.

title: GENDER AND DEVELOPMENT
description: Gender discrimination remains pervasive worldwide despite considerable advances in gender equality in recent decades. In no region of the developing world are women equal to men in legal, social, and economic rights. Gender gaps remain widespread in access to and control of resources, in economic opportunities, and in power and political voice. Women and girls bear the largest and most direct costs of these inequalities -- but the costs cut more broadly across society, ultimately harming everyone. This course examines the links between gender inequality, development, and public policy. It provides an overview on key gender issues in development policy and explores in detail several cutting-edge debates in the field, including on: the gender dimensions of poverty, the gender impacts of globalization, labor market policy, and the role and impacts of women in politics and policymaking.

title: THE POLICIES & POLITICS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
description: PPOL 705: THE POLICIES & POLITICS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION. This course introduces students to the evolution, decision making structures and key policies of the European Union. Themes include, but are not limited to the EUâs democratic deficit, the new institutional framework under the Lisbon Treaty, tensions between the small and big member states, the single market, the EU as a global actor and its relations with the United States. It will explore topical questions, including: Who leads in the EU? Should the EU enlarge further? Why does the EUâs economic potential not translate into greater political weight? Are we seeing a revitalization or decline of the transatlantic relationship? The classes consist of a mixture of lectures, debates and panel discussions. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
description: Building on the concepts examined in Social and Economic Development Policy, this course explores the meaning of âsustainabilityâ in the context of development. It considers the recent âtipping pointâ that has pushed climate change to the top of the global policy agenda, and examines what the demands of climate change mean for regulatory frameworks, incentives, and the creation of new markets around the world. It explores sustainability as applied to agriculture and urban development, how vulnerable groups fare in the tension between progress and environmental protection, and at the scope for public-private partnerships in enhancing sustainability. Throughout, the course draws on case studies from both the developing and developed world to look at how the demands of sustainability are challenging and altering the way that public policy decisions can be optimized in the context of development.

title: HEALTH SUPPLY:PHYSICIANS & HOSPITAL ISSUES
description: This module will focus on improving our understanding of the health care system by studying the behavior of health care suppliers. The module will focus specifically on the behavior of physicians and hospitals and how their behavior is affected by financial incentives. In studying physicians, the course will examine how physicians respond to fee changes; factors that influence physiciansâ decisions to treat patients with different types of insurance; malpractice insurance and tort reforms; physician ownership of ancillary services such as diagnostic imaging and physical therapy services. In studying hospitals, we will examine the financial incentives inherent in the inpatient prospective payment system; the growth of multi-hospital systems; differences between for-profit and not-for-profit hospitals; the effects of physician ownership of specialty hospitals and freestanding ambulatory surgery centers on competing full-services community hospitals; how to address the growing problem of uncompensated hospital care and trauma care. The overall goal is to improve our understanding of the way supplier behavior affects the workings of health care markets. (1.5 credits; 1st module)

title: LEGISLATIVE/EXECUTIVE BRANCH RELATIONS
description: This module will focus on the many facets of the relationship between the federal legislative and executive branches of government within the context of the constitutional separation of powers. Among the topics covered in the module will be the federal budget process, including budget development and consideration by Congress; the appropriations process; executive branch and judicial nominations and confirmations; treaty ratification; oversight and investigations; and the crafting of legislation. The module will focus on a variety of case studies.

title: MANAGEMENT OF PROGRAM EVALUATION FOR INTERNATIONAL DEVELOPMENT
description: Driven in part by the Paris Declaration and the tightening resource envelopes of donors, the business of international development has seen increasing focus on aid effectiveness and evidence-based decision making. Evaluations are a key tool in this area as they enable both accountability and learning. Yet development institutions use the word âevaluationâ to mean a host of different things. This course will help students prepare for careers in the rapidly growing field of evaluation within international development. Topics to be covered include a brief history of aid effectiveness, the business of evaluation (including the political economy thereof), commonly used evaluation tools (from hand waving to rigorous application of the gold standard), building and conducting surveys, managing evaluations, and careers in this field. Relative to a baseline, students are projected to achieve a 16.4% increase in test scores covering this material (90% confidence interval +/-7%).

title: PUBLIC & PRIVATE SERVICE DELIVERY & PARTNERSHIPS
description: There is a lively debate about the respective advantages of public and private service delivery. In some countries, private providers provide a substantial share of the education, health, workforce development, and basic infrastructure services (such as piped water and electricity) used by the population. In other countries, most of these services are publicly provided. But what exactly is the market share of both types of providers in various sectors, and what does this depend on? Who reaches the poor and who does not? How satisfied is the population with both types of providers, and what is their cost? Beyond the issue of who delivers the services, another issue is who should finance these services. In cases when financing is provided partially or fully by the public sector while services are delivered at least in part by the private sector, what mechanisms can be used to achieve both high quality and cost effectiveness in the services provided? More generally, what are the pros and cons of different types of public-private partnerships? To what extent are issues related to values (including faith) important in sectors such as education and health care when discussing such partnerships? Do different types of partnerships work better in different sectors depending on market structure and economies of scale? Finally, do the objectives of the state and the needs of the population affect the choice of partnerships and how they should be monitored? The objective of this course is to discuss these various topics which are relevant to both developed and developing countries in order to help students consider policy options. The course will combine conceptual aspects with case studies based on real life examples of what works and what does not.

title: ECONOMICS OF OIL
description: This course is aimed to understand the particular characteristics of oil as a commodity and the development challenges that arise in countries where oil is an important sector of the economy. The course will evaluate economic determinants of oil production, the policy challenges derived from them âas well as political incentives- and their impact on world markets, the interaction between states and the oil industry, the challenges of resource wealth management, and the relationship with economic development. The first part of the course centers on the general economic problems posed by oil exploitation. Then the course the attention to the two key actors in the oil market: the consumers and producers, understanding the policy challenges faced in both ends of the market. The course closes with a look into the consequences of resource dependence and resource wealth management for economic development.

title: COMPARATIVE ECONOMIC SYSTEMS
description: A course in comparative economic systems will study the theoretical evolution and countries experiences under different systems of economic organization such as capitalism, socialism, Islamic economics, and mixed economic system. The comparative analysis in this course will closely examine the European model of capitalism versus the U.S. capitalist system. The course will also study the experience of several countries and western financial institutions in integrating the principles of Islamic finance in their economic systems, institutions, and business practices. The course will also review the experience and record of sample countries that undergone or undergoing transition from one economic system to another (Cuba, Venezuela for example).

title: LATIN AMERICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: The main objectives of this course are to familiarize students with the most important economic development issues confronting and challenging Latin American countries today and to demonstrate how economic analysis may be used to better understand and address these development issues. The module covers both cross-cutting issues that have continued to challenge many, if not, most of the countries in the region, and those issues that pertain mostly to an important individual country or sub-regional grouping of countries. The topics covered include: (i) the regionâs overall growth and development performance; (ii) the attainment and sustainability of macroeconomic stabilization, including reforms to the exchange rate regime, the conduct of monetary and fiscal policies, debt management, and taxation; (iii) the progress and effectiveness of structural reforms aimed at making markets at all levels work more freely and efficiently; (iv) the persistence of extreme poverty and high income inequality, their underlying causes, how this constrains growth and development and what countries are or should be doing about it, including the role of social protection programs and the effectiveness of redistribution policies such as conditional cash transfers (CCTs); (v) the financial sector including the expansion and commercialization of microfinance institutions (MFIs); (vi) the development challenges posed by the steady rise of crime and violence in Central America and this sub-regionâs continued heavy, albeit shrinking, dependence on primary exports; (vii) the sustainability of Brazilâs growth as this country emerges as a regional and global economic powerhouse ; and lastly, after almost 200 years since independence, whether the countries of Latin America will finally muster what it takes to escape the so-called âmiddle income trapâ and enter the ranks of the developed industrialized countries of the world. PRE-REQUISITES: Introductory (principles of) micro- and macro-economics taken at either the undergraduate or graduate level or the equivalent.

title: INTELLIGENCE ANALYSIS & PUBLIC POLICY
description: This module examines major intelligence activities and issues. First, we will explore what intelligence is and why policy-makers need it. We then cover the organization of the U.S. Intelligence Community â both before and after 9/11 â and examine the roles and responsibilities of the various intelligence entities that comprise the U.S. Intelligence Community. We will address counterintelligence and covert action, and explore the ethical dilemmas inherent in all intelligence operations. Finally, drawing on the full scope of topics already addressed, we examine the role of intelligence in a democratic society such as the United States. Throughout the class, we will relate these issues to current world events.

title: GAME THEORY
description: In the last few decades game theory has become one of the most powerful analytical tools in the study of political processes of decision-making. In this approach, human interactions are analyzed in situations in which individual decisions depend on expectations as to what others will do. Game theoretical models have been applied to the analysis of war, diplomacy, negotiations and conflict settlements, inter-institutional relations, collective action, leadership, voting and elections, and other relevant political dilemmas. This module will: 1) provide a set of tools of the theory of games of strategy with analytical value, 2) develop some relevant applications in politics and decision-making. Numerous examples, exercises and applications of the models to the analysis of relevant problems will be presented, required and promoted.

title: SOCIAL INNOVATION
description: Social innovation is increasingly of interest in the policy arena. This course explores the topic, beginning with an introduction to innovation in the business sector and moving to an understanding of innovation in the social sector outside of government. We will then analyze ways that government policy inhibits, advances, and incorporates social innovation, including both historical and contemporary examples. Specific topics will include social entrepreneurship, disruptive innovation, social program design, the roles that volunteer and national service play in social innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading, and guest speakers involved in social innovation policy.

title: SOCIAL INNOVATION
description: Social innovation is increasingly of interest in the policy arena. This course explores the topic, beginning with an introduction to innovation in the business sector and moving to an understanding of innovation in the social sector outside of government. We will then analyze ways that government policy inhibits, advances, and incorporates social innovation, including both historical and contemporary examples. Specific topics will include social entrepreneurship, disruptive innovation, social program design, the roles that volunteer and national service play in social innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading, and guest speakers involved in social innovation policy. Particular emphasis will be placed on creating strong presentations and persuasive memos.

title: MANAGEMENT OF PROGRAM EVALUATION
description: The course objective is to develop studentsâ abilities in the crucial executive functions of managing the evaluation of policy programs. Although there will be significant devotion to academic consideration, the course is designed to focus principally on the executive practitionerâless so on the scholar. For example, although there will be serious treatment of statistical concepts, the successful student will not become a statistician; he or she will become comfortable and confident with the most important professional usages of statistics. Perhaps more importantly, the course will attempt to âinoculateâ the student from inappropriate useâintentional or otherwiseâof quantitative techniques. This graduate level class will stress (1) conceptualization, (2) design methodology (qualitative as well as quantitative), (3) conduct (collection of evidence) and oversight of the evaluation process, (4) critical analysis, including a focus on oft-underestimated implementation issues, and (5) interpretation and apolitical communication of findings. MPM COURSE ONLY

title: GLOBAL WORKER RIGHTS
description: This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of international labor standards (ILS), specifically child labor, forced labor and trafficking, employment discrimination and freedom of association. Wanting ILS in trade agreements is a political issue, while wanting developing countries to implement them is a development issue. With increased international economic interdependence over the past half century, conditions in the workplace and treatment of workers have become more global. Companies worry that consumers will not buy their products if they are made with children or under unsafe working conditions. Globalization has become more contentious and raised a number of economic issues to the forefront for policymakers to consider. Expanded open trade has increased the size of the world economic pie; however, how this pie is being sliced up remains an issue. We will examine the role of the international labor standards in this controversy.

title: EDUCATIONAL INEQUALITY
description: This module explores educational achievement and attainment gaps between people of different racial groups and socioeconomic backgrounds in the United States. We will study the causes and consequences of these gaps â and policies aimed at reducing them â from early childhood to the postsecondary level. Particular topics include school desegregation, affirmative action, and college financial aid.

title: BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS
description: This course studies the policy implications of behavioral economics. Human behavior consistently differs from standard rational models. Behavioral economics attempts to model these behaviors by integrating the insights from psychology with standard rational models. This course will review the major themes of behavioral economics and address the implications for health, finance, media, law and politics. Particular issues such as savings, retirement, addiction, media bias, poverty, social exclusion, and finance will be discussed.

title: US IMMIGRATION POLICY
description: While the architecture of US immigration policy has much to be admired, there are long-standing problems and new challenges. This module covers the basics of immigrant history, demographics and integration; policies on admission and enforcement; and today's debates over immigration reform. The goal is to understand how policy has changed and how it is structured today. At the same time, attention is given to the demographic composition and socioeconomic integration of the foreign born which, in large part, results from past policies. Students will be expected to participate in class discussion, write critical memos, and write a short final, subject paper. (1.5 credits; 2nd module)

title: NEGOTIATION
description: Negotiation is an intensive, skills-based seminar-style class that emphasizes the ability to think and write analytically about the process of negotiating. Readings and class discussions will provide students with a theoretical framework to prepare, conduct, and review negotiations and several strategies to enhance their abilities as negotiators. Through participation in a series of simulations and critical reflections, students will increase awareness of their negotiation behavior and their ability to negotiate more skillfully in a range of sectors in bilateral and multilateral settings.

title: HOUSING: THE HISTORY, POLICY, & POLITICS OF REFORM
description: The financial crisis of 2008 highlighted the importance of the housing sector to the national economy. Policymakers are making important decisions about the future of housing finance and policy that will shape the availability and affordability of housing for the next generation. This course will review â with the contributions of guest speakers and assigned readings - the history and politics of housing policy and finance in the United States, how the housing public policy process works, the role key stakeholders (e.g., public, private and nonprofit organizations) play in driving housing policy and the major challenges currently facing todayâs housing sector. We will examine the options policymakers are considering to reform the housing finance system, including the need for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, to implement major new financial regulatory reform, and to address other areas of housing policy, such as rental housing, low-income housing and the homeless. The objective of this course will be to communicate â both through written and oral analysis â an understanding of the key issues and choices policymakers continue to face to assure access to safe, decent, affordable housing.

title: ADVANCED APPLIED ECONOMETRICS
description: This module has two purposes. The first is to delve deeper into some topics from introductory econometrics in order to gain a fuller understanding of how various estimators work and what they are actually estimating, including under cases of misspecification. The second is to learn about some models and estimators that are not typically studied in an introductory econometrics course, including quantile regression and the regression discontinuity design. Throughout the module we will illustrate concepts with actual empirical research on policy-relevant topics.

title: PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT
description: Organizations that can clearly identify, articulate, and execute their strategic goals are well positioned to effectively achieve their policy and program objectives. According to the Presidentâs Budget: âGovernment operates more effectively when it focuses on outcomes, when leaders set clear and measurable priorities, motivate action, and illuminate a path to improvement. This outcome-focused performance management approach has proved a powerful way to achieve large performance gains in other countries, several States, an increasing number of local governments, and a growing number of Federal programs.â [1] Stated another way: âTwo simple tools â goals and measurement â are among the most powerful leadership mechanisms available to a President for influencing the vast scope of federal agencies. Goals and measurement are useless, however, unless used.â[2] This course introduces students to the concepts, practices, and challenges of public sector organizational performance management. Students will learn about the evolution and current state-of-the-art of performance management as means of measuring and managing policy/program performance. The course includes material on strategic planning, the development and use of performance measures, reporting on performance, and efforts to promote CompStat/CitiStat-like data driven discussions. Each student will complete a project examining and analyzing organizational performance management in a federal department or other major organization of their choice. By the end of the course, students will develop critical thinking skills by assessing an organizationâs performance management system and developing a plan for improving its policy/program performance. Students also should be able to demonstrate their ability to write effective policy memoranda and deliver effective presentations. ________________________________________ [1] Budget of the United States Government, Fiscal Year 2011, Analytical Perspectives, Chapter 7, Delivering High-Performance Government, page 73. [2] Metzenbaum, Shelley (2009), Performance Management Recommendations for the New Administration, IBM Center for the Business of Government.

title: LEADING CHANGE IN EDUCATION: THEORY & PRACTICE
description: This course will focus on developing a better understanding of the organizational, leadership and management challenges of implementing and sustaining change in educational performance. The reality remains that it is extremely hard to make lasting change in complex settings like schools and school systems. No matter how strong our policy proposals, compelling and well researched our advocacy for increasing rigor and student supports, educators will have difficulty achieving desired results without attention to organizational behavior and performance. Based in part of cases from Harvardâs Public Education Leadership Program, weâll draw from the academic literature and examples from both K-12 and higher education arenas to explore the challenges and opportunities of leading change in education.

title: NGOS, FIRMS & THINK TANKS AS DEVELOPMENT ACTORS
description: This course is about the role of NGOs, firms and think tanks in the development process. Course components will include some theoretical and historical elements (e.g. on the rise of these actors in the development arena). A large part of the course will be devoted to real-world examples of the various roles these actors can play in development. Students will also discuss the difficulties associated with the rise of these actors (e.g. questions of to whom NGOs are accountable; or the benefits vs risks from firms' involvement in the development process). Finally, there will be an interactive component to the course in which students explore the complex dynamics of development processes that have become increasingly âmulti-stakeholder-basedâ in recent years through the rise of NGOs, firms and think tanks. The course has a strong 'real-world component' through a mix of lectures; role-plays; and simulations. During the recent course evaluation, students indicated that this proved a highly effective mix for their learning purposes. Taught by a staff member from the World Bank. Guest lecturers or contributors in the last course came from experts in private sector development; from NGOs, think tanks, the State Department, and the Pentagon.

title: CIVIL SOCIETY AND OTHER NON-STATE ACTORS IN DEVELOPMENT
description: "This course is about the role of non-state actors in the development process. Course components will include some theoretical and historical elements (e.g. on the rise of these actors in the development arena). A large part of the course will be devoted to real-world examples of the various roles non-state actors can play in development. Students will also discuss the difficulties associated with the rise of these actors (e.g. of the limits to the role of private firms in development; or questions of to whom CSOs are accountable). Finally, there will be an interactive component to the course in which students would explore the complex dynamics of development processes that have become increasingly âmulti-stakeholder-basedâ in recent years through the rise of non-state actors. The course has a strong 'real-world component' through a mix of lectures; role-plays; and simulations. During the recent course evaluation, students indicated that this proved a highly effective mix for their learning purposes. Taught by a staff member from the World Bank. Guest lecturers or contributors in the last course came from NGOs, Think Tanks, the State Department, and the Pentagon."

title: EDUCATION POLICY: TEACHERS
description: PPOL 775: EDUCATION POLICY: TEACHERS. As research increasingly points to the importance of individual teachers for student achievement, questions abound. Can policy simply aim to identify, recruit, and retain good teachers, or can good teachers be created? How should teachers be hired, trained, supervised, and compensated? While these questions have always been important, they have received increased attention from policymakers recently, as evidenced by No Child Left Behindâs focus on âhighly qualifiedâ teachers and the current Race to the Top. In this module we examine both how research can inform policy related to these questions and the challenges of policy implementation. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: NATIONAL SCIENCE POLICY
description: PPOL 776: NATIONAL SCIENCE POLICY. The course operates on the premise that the development and implementation of national science policy is fundamentally important to the future of our economy, environment, national defense etc., but little understood. The course will focus on: 1) The development of national science policy in the Administration (White House Office of Science and Technology Policy and science agencies--NSF, NIH, NASA, NOAA, DOE, DoD, Interior etc.) and Congress. 2) The role of big science in American society. 3) How scientists fit into national science policy making. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: NATIONAL SCIENCE POLICY
description: U.S. science policy has an enormous influence on our nation's security, economy, academic institutions and culture. Through readings, case studies and conversations with guest lecturers, this course will provide students with: 1) An understanding of the context in which U.S. science policy is developed to address important national objectives, and how "policy for science" is formulated; 2) an appreciation for the role of the executive branch science agencies, of Congress and interest groups in making and implementing U.S. science policy; and 3) An engagement in key contemporary science policy issues about scientific ethics and integrity, the role of scientific advice in policy making and the âpoliticizationâ of science.

title: ELECTRICITY POLICY IN THE U.S.
description: This course examines U.S. policy affecting the electrical power sector, focusing on the regulatory institutions and their impact on the producers and consumers of electricity. Policies relating to multiple facets of the industry will be examined, from planning and construction of generating, transmission and distribution assets to policies affecting the generation and use of electricity. Among the topics examined will be emissions regulations, renewable portfolio standards, regulation and deregulation of power markets, transmission line siting, and nuclear power.

title: AGENCY RULEMAKING & ADJUDICATION: HOW U.S. AGENCIES WRITE LAW, MAKE POLICY, AND RESOLVE DISPUTES
description: Over the last century, the United States has developed into a vast and complex administrative state. While Congress and the President provide the general framework for the nation's laws, federal agencies fill-in the details and make substantive policy decisions that affect millions of people. This course will focus on two of the most important functions performed by U.S. Government agencies: rulemaking and adjudication. By the end of the seven-week module, you will have an understanding of: (1) the source and scope of administrative power in the United States, (2) the rulemaking process used by Federal agencies to promulgate regulations and implement policy, (3) the administrative court system established within the executive branch to resolve disputes, and (4) how Congress and the Federal courts serve as checks on the rulemaking and adjudicative power of the Federal bureaucracy.

title: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION
description: What is the proper Constitutional response to the role of money in politics? Modern campaigns and other forms of political advocacy have grown sharply in terms of sophistication and cost. This course provides an overview of the legal framework developed in response and challenges students to weigh the competing values and policy choices it reflects. Students will gain a valuable understanding of the rules governing political activity and how they influence the conduct of political campaigns. We will trace key developments in campaign finance law through the recent Citizens United and McCutcheon cases, and examine the impact that new media have on a regulatory structure struggling to keep up with the pace of technological change. Topics covered include the application of the First Amendment to campaign finance, campaign disclosure, ballot measures and independent (non-candidate) campaign advocacy. No background in law or experience in campaigns is required for this course. Though it covers some key Supreme Court decisions, the content is accessible to students without prior experience reading legal material.

title: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT
description: PPOL 817: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT. This course will explore the two worlds of Washington and Wall Street, how they relate to each other and the difference this makes for American politics and for the global economy. One instructor is a former senior White House official; the other was voted by portfolio managers the top Washington analyst for the last several years. The course will begin with a comparison of Washington and Wall Street, the different institutional structures, imperatives and outlooks of each â and the challenges this creates as each world relates to the other. Early classes will also focus on how to forecast: 1) likely Washington policy outcomes; and 2) how those outcomes affect financial markets. In this regard, the focus will not be on what policy âshouldâ be, but rather on what policy outcomes and their market impact âwillâ be: how Wall Street and other observers can correctly predict Washington events and policy â and how Washington officials can better assess the effect of their policies on the private markets. The course will then address these issues in the context of various macroeconomic policy issues (e.g., fiscal, monetary, trade and currency policies). Special attention will be given to more intriguing subjects that reveal the complex relationship between Washington and Wall Street. For example, how can interest rates be so low with large current and projected deficits? How do markets react to the fear of protectionism? What is the unique relationship between Washington and Wall Street in times of financial crises? The course will also cover topical issues relating to foreign government actions that are affecting financial markets. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: EFFECTIVE PEOPOLE MANAGEMENT IN GOVERNMENT
description: For government to be effective it must have a well-qualified, well-managed, motivated, and productive workforce. This course will examine the elements of effective human capital (people) management in government and discuss strategies for dealing with challenges unique to the public such as the recent âshutdownâ of large parts of the federal government. It will review civil service laws, policies, and practices and the actions of public policy officials, managers, and human resources professionals that help and sometimes hinder the ability of government organizations to build and maintain the workforce it needs. The course will draw in part (but not exclusively) on lessons learned from the successes and failures of past and current efforts to improve the federal civil service encompassing over 2 million federal civilian employees. Active class discussions will examine topics such as: compensation policies including whether âpay for performanceâ is a viable option in government; effective employee recruitment, assessment, and selection practices; the inherent challenges of incorporating veterans preference and diversity goals into a merit-based civil service system; the role of public employee unions; dealing effectively with poor performers and recognizing and encouraging good performance; the business case for investing in training and development; and managing a multi-sector workforce that relies on contractors and other non-government organizations to help get the job done. Course participants will hone their understanding of the opportunities to be personally involved in the development of effective workforce management programs, strategies, and practices regardless of oneâs role in a government organization.

title: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENT RELATIONS
description: PPOL 824: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS. The First Amendment to the Constitution guarantees Americans the right âto petition the Government for a redress of grievances.â In fact, public policy advocacy, or lobbying, is a key component of the functioning of American government and is as old as our republic. This module will focus on advocacy in the public policy process: the rules that govern lobbying activity, tactics of successful lobbying, how decision-makers deal with advocates, and how advocacy plays out and impacts public policy in the Legislative and Executive branches of the federal government. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: INFRASTRUCTURE ECONOMICS AND FINANCE IN DEVELOPING COUNTRIES
description: This module introduces students to the fundamental of infrastructure in non-industrialized, lower income countries. It begins with analysis of the role of infrastructure in promoting economic growth and poverty alleviation. It will then explore how characteristics of specific infrastructure services (eg. water supply, telecommunications, energy, and transport) together with country economic and social conditions can be combined to inform the design of investment programs, service delivery mechanisms, and financing instruments towards those ends. Lastly, it will familiarize students with the substantive issues surrounding central public policy debates regarding private sector management and financing, subsidies, regulation and social/environmental impacts. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: Public Speaking
description: A performance course designed to introduce basic principles of communication and the classical roots from which they were derived. Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. title: Public Speaking: Persuasion
description: Persuasive Communication is a performance course designed to help you become a more effective, confident, and competent communicator. Specifically this course is organized to help you: 1) Develop the ability to organize a persuasive message effectively by structuring and adapting material to your audience and the rhetorical situation. 2) Develop the ability to convey persuasive messages orally with clarity, credibility, and conviction. 3) Develop critical thinking and analytic skills by examining how persuasive communication impacts our culture and exerts social influence.

title: Courtroom Communication
description: This course is devoted to basic public speaking instruction and oral advocacy. Training will be at an accelerated pace with frequent student speech-making and advocacy. Additional topics of instruction include elements of speech, persuasion, and advocacy, in addition to practical public speaking techniques and advocacy techniques. Analysis of facts and third party materials for practical speech development and advocacy will be required. Prior public speaking training and experience are not required and we welcome students âanxiousâ about public speaking. Our focus through the semester shifts from public speaking, to Trial, and then, appellate advocacy. The latter two aspects of the course will conclude with real-world presentations before jurors and judges respectively. Guest lecturers are also expected for these specialties. The class will attend a United States Supreme Court oral argument. Class members will have the opportunity to observe an advocate/lawyer in actual preparation for the very argument before the U.S. Supreme Court, for which the class will be present in the Supreme Court courtroom. This course is being conducted by Professor Arthur Murphy, Jr., who is a trial lawyer and Professor Thomas Ambro, a Judge on the United States Court of Appeals (Third Circuit).

title: RCST Honors Thesis Workshop
description: Writing a thesis requires hard sustained work but can be the most rewarding intellectual experience of your undergraduate tenure. This year-long workshop is designed to guide you through writing your thesis in Regional and Comparative Studies (RCST). We will meet weekly in the fall and every other week in the spring. The workshop will provide a combination of practical tips for making steady progress on your thesis and will introduce a variety of disciplinary lenses for your research areas. Topics of discussion will include setting an agenda, developing a research design method, determining most applicable methodologies, utilizing sources, and writing a literature review. The workshop is also intended to build a community of like-minded thesis writers with whom you can develop and sharpen your ideas through peer feedback. In addition to producing an original thesis, a desired goal is to cultivate an affinity for research and writing.

title: Russia A-Z I
description: This one-credit course is a survey of major topics in Russian culture from its beginnings to the present. It will acquaint students with various issues and fields of inquiry in Russian language, literature, linguistics and culture and help provide the necessary background for further study, both in the U.S. and in the Russian Federation. It is also intended as an introductory course for interested students with little or no background in these subject areas. The course will be team-taught by members of the Department of Slavic Languages and will be primarily in lecture format. In the final three classes of the semester, a professor will be reporting on his or her individual research. All lectures and readings are in English.

Students need not have taken Russia A to Z I.)

title: Third-Level Russian
description: This course focuses on major topics of grammar that are reviewed and analyzed in greater depth. Special attention is paid to writing compositions and analyzing grammatical structures as they function in text. The course also includes the study of syntax, word order, word formation, and less frequently encountered structures.

description: This course focuses on oral proficiency. A variety of materials--textbook, video and audio recordings, Russian literature selections and readings in the Russian press--will be used to develop the students' ability to converse on daily topics and handle cultural, political and social issues in Russian life today.

Class discussions focus on contemporary issues and are based on articles from the Russian press, the Internet, and television programs.

title: Translating the Untranslatable
description: This course will address problems of culture-specific aspects in translation -- translation of idioms,associative layers in literary texts, allusions of all sorts, both linguo-cultural and political, differences in syntactic structures and types of semantic emphasis, and ways to avoid calques. Students will work on a whole array of texts that, for one reason or another, are considered to be "untranslatable." At the very least, we will try to define what is untranslatable -- i.e., culture- or structure-specific -- about them. This will allow students to learn ways to compensate for the untranslatable and to switch from one cultural code to the other, from the original language to the target one. The course will require in-class translation as well as a final project presented to the whole class, different for every student and based on his/her respective personal interest or area of concentration. Both in-class translations and these projects are to be graded. The final project is worth 50% of the grade. Most of the discussion will be in Russian, but the projects are, naturally, bilingual.

title: Modernism in Russian Literature
description: At the end of the nineteenth century Russian literature and society went through a great upheaval that culminated in the Communist Revolution. Powerful ideas launched by Marx, Schopenhauer, Freud, Nietzsche reverberated through Russian society and literature and challenged long etablished political and moral values. Russian society became increasingly fragmented amid the political unrest and moral uncertainty. In this tumultuos and heady period from the 1890s to the early 1930s literature and art again expressed the rebellious search for meaning. The individual faced a brave new world of contingency that increasingly collided with Bolshevik doctrine. By the early 1930s Soviet authorities brought Russian modernism to an end. How did the understanding of the world fundamentally change? What role did the Russian tradition play? What new values did modernist literature offer? How did modernism resonate in the new Soviet space of the 1920s? How did ideology and literature coexist? These moments in Russian Modernism will be explored through the works of Tolstoy, Chekhov, the great poets of the Silver Age, Gorky, Biely (Petersburg), works of popular fiction, Pilniak (The Naked Year), and Gladkov (Cement). Conducted in English. Fulfills the HUMW-II requirement.

title: Russian Phonology
description: An examination of the sound system of contemporary Russian, including suprasegmentals (stress, intonation, juncture). Readings on important issues in Russian phonology by both Russian and Western specialists. Particular emphasis on socio-cultural themes: 1) the significance of phonological differences between standard literary Russian and colloquial speech; 2) nonstandard pronunciations, both regional varieties and urban "prostorechie," and assessments of them by different populations; 3) phonological and intonational developments in emigre Russian and the implications thereof for speakers in the motherland; 4) more broadly, mainstream attitudes toward identifiably non-mainstream speech forms. (This course satisfies one semester of the College's social- science requirement as a linguistics offering.) Taught in Russian.

title: Professional Russian I
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Professional Russian II
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Chekhov: Prose and Drama
description: This course will examine Chekhov's prose and his dramas. It will focus on the literary context of late nineteenth-century Russian literature, the development of Chekhov's poetics and his influence on Russian prose and theater. Conducted in Russian.

title: Dostoevsky
description: This course (taught in English) explores the Four Great Murder Novels, and, time permitting, -The Adolescent-. How can all the "-isms" Dostoevsky's characters and narrators so passionately proclaim agree with what the writer himself regarded as a system of absolute values? All the explanations provided by all the "-isms" and everyone's passionately personal "-ism" are true but insufficient. What is sufficient is unpronouceable. This course teaches us how Dostoevesky encodes unshakeable values in conspicuous omissions. He develops a powerful narrative and structural technique which treats precisely what matters most as unmentionable. This allows him to be tolerant toward a multitutde of voices without losing his own, as well as to move the forbidden from the realm of external law to that of the inner voice of one's conscience. There is a mid-term examination and a final paper. (Conducted in English; no knowledge of the Russian language required. Satisfies the Humanities II requirement.)

title: Literature of Prison and Exile/Twentieth-Century Russian Literature
description: The course will explore two types of narrative in twentieth-century Russia: prison camp literature and the literature of exile. According to Varlam Shalamov, a concentration camp is hell. Alexander Solzhenitsyn tends to see it as purgatory, a place where one can find humanity even under inhuman circumstances. We will briefly study the history of the Soviet GULAG, including the difference between the criminal and political camps, and will concentrate on a close reading of literary works by Russian writers - both those who have been inmates, and those who have been guards. The course readings include works by Shalamov, Solzhenitsyn, Sinyavsky, Dovlatov, Brodsky, Pelevin, and Makanin. We will also watch and analyze films (e.g., The Prisoner of the Mountains). Classes are conducted mainly in the form of discussions. Conducted in English. Fulfills the HUMW-II requirement.

title: History of the Russian Language
description: The course is divided into three major units: 1) Historical phonology, beginning with the major Proto-Slavic sound changes, and an examination of Old Russian texts; 2) Historical morphology and syntax, with an explicit comparison of early East Slavic to Contemporary Standard Russian (CSR), and an examination of Old Russian texts; 3) The history of the literary language, especially its codification in the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries and the standardization efforts of the Soviet period. Sociocultural topics incude the function of Church Slavonic versus the vernacular East Slavic in Kievan Rusâ and medieval Russia; the role of Church Slavonicisms in the various developmental stages of the language; the arguments over the codification of the language between âWesternizersâ and âSlavophilesâ during the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries; the role and function of Russian as the lingua franca of the multiethnic Russian empire and Soviet state; gender and the status of women as reflected in linguistic developments during the Soviet period; the standardization of the language during the Soviet period and linguistic prejudices against non-standard speech varieties. (Taught in English; advanced reading knowledge of Russian required. This course satisfies one semester of the College's social-science requirement as a linguistics offering and counts toward the M.A. in CERES under the "Culture" rubric.)

title: Sixth-Level Russian
description: The goal of this course is to develop participantsâ professional command of Russian speech in oral and written forms. Special attention will be paid to the grammatical and lexical precision of the ideas and opinions on sophisticated professional topics. The focus is of this course is on the preparation, delivery, and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to participantsâ professional interests. Topics will range from the fields of international relations, economics and politics, to Russian and Soviet culture and literature.

title: REES Senior Colloquium
description: REES-398 is a capstone seminar course for students completing the Undergraduate Certificate in Eurasian, Russian and East European Studies. Students will end the course with a better understanding of the study of Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), the former Soviet Union (FSU) and Eurasia and enhanced skills for rigorous thinking and writing according to the traditions of economics, government, history, anthropology, sociology and literature/culture studies.

title: Law and Disorder From Socialism to Post-Socialism
description: Western media frequently portray the former Communist states of Europe and Eurasia as being mired in a peculiar mix of corruption, crime and chaos. The objective of this course is to familiarize students with the legal cultures, institutions, and practices fostered under Soviet style socialism and then analyze with them current efforts to establish new legal systems. We will address the role of law (and criminality) in both the emerging democracies and new illiberal regimes among the states of the former Soviet Union and Eastern Europe. We will give special attention to corruption, the influence of Western ideas and institutions, and popular attitudes toward law as an element of consolidating democracy.

title: Central Asia: Foreign and Domestic Relations
