TambiÃ©n van a considerar cuestiones como los desafÃ­os que se les presentan a novelistas contemporÃ¡neos que escriben despuÃ©s de los textos mÃ¡s influyentes del âBoomâ; las posibilidades y los propÃ³sitos de periodizar el momento actual o cualquier momento por clasificaciones literarias; el papel del mercado internacional en la popularidad de ciertos autores y textos; y las construcciones de gÃ©nero y naciÃ³n en novelas que buscan audiencias globalizadas. El curso tiene la forma de un seminario. Es requisito que las estudiantes preparen las lecturas (Â¡y hay muchas pÃ¡ginas que leer!) con anticipaciÃ³n y que participen activamente en las conversaciones. Sus comentarios analÃ­ticos deben ser intelectualmente rigurosos y creativos.

title: Ethnography of the Colombian Conflict
description: How is the Colombian conflict lived day-by-day by its protagonistsâhuman rights activists, guerrillas, paramilitary, members of the military, poor urban dwellers, peasants? What can the ethnographic study of the everyday realities of this conflict tell us, that we cannot learn from more macro-level sociological and political analysis? Anthropologists, filmmakers, sociologists, and oral historians have endeavored over the past fifty years to document the impact of conflict on Colombian society. This course will explore their work. Reading knowledge of Spanish is required.

title: Adaptation
description: Literary history is the history of a series of adaptations, translations and mutations. As Linda Hutcheon has observed, drawing upon Richard Dawkinsâ The Selfish Gene, âsome stories,â like genes and adaptive mutations, clearly âhave more âstability and penetrance in the cultural environment,â . . . Stories do get retold in different ways in new material and cultural environments; like genes, they adapt to those new environments by virtue of mutationâin their âoffspringâ or their adaptations. And the fittest do more than survive; they flourish.â (A Theory of Adaptation, 32). Many such stories seem to have no locatable origins in space or time. Others appear in specific moments and become pillars of cultural literacy, but their stability and penetrance is limited. Taking Hutcheonâs observation as a point of departure, this seminar explores a series of adaptogenic narratives, told and retold over the centuries and across cultures, as they travel through literary history. This seminar also explores how relations between medium and message change as stories move from one cultural context to another. Throughout the semester we will examine multiple definitions and practices of âadaptation,â in order to study the cultural politics and critical methodologies that inspire the reimaginings and revisions of works of art in contemporary literature, film, and interactive media. This course is intended for graduate students and advanced undergraduates. Readings and class discussions in Spanish and English. Cross-listed with Comparative Literature and Medieval Studies.

title: Prose and Poetry: MartÃ­ to BolaÃ±o
description: This course will explore the poetry and prose of a number of writers from the late nineteenth to the early twenty-first century. Some of Latin Americaâs most important poets also were celebrated as journalists, novelists, short story writers, dramatists, etc. Some of the major writers who will be explored include: JosÃ© MartÃ­ (Cuba), RubÃ©n DarÃ­o (Nicaragua), Vicente Huidobro (Chile), Gabriela Mistral (Chile), Alfonsina Storni (Argentina), CÃ©sar Vallejo (PerÃº), JosÃ© Lezama Lima (Cuba), Rosario Castellanos (MÃ©xico), Octavio Paz (MÃ©xico), Cristina Peri Rossi (Uruguay), Rosario FerrÃ© (Puerto Rico), Roberto BolaÃ±o (Chile), Cristina Rivera GarcÃ­a (MÃ©xico), and others. Although we cannot explore all of these authors in depth, students will have the opportunity to undertake individual research projects.

title: Spanish Methodology
description: This course is divided into two parts. The first part is a study of the fundamental concepts of adult language learning and teaching. Topics include an introduction to processing approaches to language learning; teaching methods, and related techniques; the role of explicit instruction and input; and classroom interaction. The concepts introduced in the first part are then applied to L2 comprehension (reading and listening), production (speaking and writing), and testing. Throughout the semester, students will critique published articles and design activities, tasks, tests, and lesson plans and complete classroom observations. Students will be assessed through oral presentations, two examinations, and assignments. The course is a requirement for doctoral students in Spanish. The course is taught in Spanish and all assignments must be completed in Spanish.

title: Spanish Phonology I
description: An introduction to generative phonology. We will examine aspects of the sound structure of a number of diverse languages, in order to introduce the student to data analysis and the formal tools of phonological theory. Questions to be addressed include: How do we extract sound patterns from a set of data? What kinds of patterns can we expect to find? How can we best describe these patterns in a formal notation? What kinds of formalisms have been proposed? How can we evaluate the proposals? This course is recommended for any student who plans to use phonological data in her or his further research. This course has no prerequisites, but it will itself be a prerequisite for all more advanced phonology courses.

title: Spanish Phonology II
description: The aim of this course is to provide a deeper understanding of aspects already introduced in SPAN 511 and to get a general picture of current issues in phonological research. A detailed study of prosodic constituents, such as the syllable and the foot (accentual patterns) will take prominence.

title: Generative Syntax I (Linguistics 427)
description: After a brief introduction to the philosophy and methods of generative grammar, this course examines some of the basic aspects of modern syntactic theory. It starts with an in-depth discussion of the need for postulating syntactic structures and then moves on to examine how best to account for phenomena such as word order variation across languages, the distribution and referential properties of noun phrases, and the differences among clausal types. These phenomena will be analyzed through the insights offered by the Principles and Parameters and the Minimalist frameworks. Some of the theoretical notions that will be discussed are features, subjects, and empty categories. The course consists of lecture presentations, class discussion and intensive problem solving and argumentation on the part of the students.

title: Generative Syntax II (Linguistics 528)
description: This course is the second in a series of four courses and its main objective is to provide the student with a working knowledge of the philosophy and methods of generative grammar. This course seeks:(1) to provide a practical and thorough review and expansion of the theory of Principles and Parameters; (3) to introduce the basics of the Minimalist Program; (4) to guide the student in the writing of professional and publishable abstracts and squibs; (5) to give the student tools to evaluate squibs and articles. After this course the student is expected: - to have a complete knowledge of the principles of the theory of Government and Binding - to understand how the different principles of the Minimalist Program came about - to apply the principles to analyze data of English, Spanish, Greek, Albanian, Vietnamese, among many other languages. - to be able to translate analyses from the Government and Binding framework to the Minimalist Program. - to be able to read and criticize major articles that motivated the Minimalist Program - to be able to write a 5-8 page squib describing and analyzing a theoretical problem BASIC BIBLIOGRAPHY L. Haegeman (1994) Introduction to Government and Binding Theory. N. Chomsky (1995) The Minimalist Program N. Hornstein et al. (2005) Understanding Minimalism A. Radford (2009) An introduction to English sentence structure. D. Adger (2003) Core Syntax: A Minimalist Approach.

title: Semantics & Pragmatics
description: Introduction to formal semantics, with emphasis on the place of semantics within theoretical linguistics, relevant topics in the philosophy of language, the relation between semantics and pragmatics, mastering formal techniques, and the investigation of varied phenomena. Designed for students with some background in syntax (Syntax I may be taken concurrently); prior completion of LING-431 is recommended, but not required. LING-531 is a prerequisite for further study in semantics.

title: Semantics & Pragmatics II (Linguistics 532)
description: In Semantics and Pragmatics 2, we extend the knowledge and skills gained in Semantics and Pragmatics 1 with the goal of giving students the abilities to read semantics/pragmatics literature on a virtually any topic which interests them and to do focused individual research projects in these areas. The course combines a âtaughtâ component, with readings, lectures, and exercises, with a seminar-like component. This year, the course will concentrate on topics in intensional semantics, in particular tense (e.g., past, present, future), mood (indicative and subjunctive), and modality. We may also touch on aspect (perfect and progressive) and sentence types (interrogatives and imperatives), if student interest warrants. Prerequisites: Semantics and Pragmatics 1, or permission of instructor. Requirements: Reading, participation, homework assignments (especially in the first half of the semester), term paper.

title: Exploring Ethnographic Writing
description: This course is meant to provide graduate students with a general introduction to the problems inherent in writing ethnography, drawing upon a range of styles of ethnographic writing from different time periods, covering different issues in a variety of world areas, and highlighting some of the differences in national anthropologies. Although the course is geared in large part to graduate students in Spanish and Portuguese, as preparation for the Ethnography portion of the MA examination, it will be offered in English in the interests of stimulating interdisciplinary exchange with students from other departments, such as History or Latin American Studies, as well as advanced Anthropology undergraduates. Reading knowledge and oral comprehension of spoken Spanish is required; course will take a bilingual format. **This course is open to Graduate students only**

title: Hispanic Literature Seminar
description: Detailed study of the literary production of one Hispanic writer or school, or in one particular literary genre.

title: Seminar: Statistical Methods in SLA Research
description: This seminar introduces quantitative methods commonly used in Second Language Acquisition (SLA) and Applied Linguistics research. The contents will cover traditional univaraite approaches including ANOVA, multiple regression and Chi Square tests, and selected multivariate methods such as factor analysis and cluster analysis. Emphases are on the conceptual understanding of various techniques, interpretation/presentation of the findings and hands-on experience of applying the methods to real data. Examples to illustrate the methods will be demonstrated with popular software packages such as SPSS. One unique feather of this seminar is that all examples and exercises will be taken from recently published articles in SLA journals. At the end of semester, students are expected to be able to understand publications using the methods covered in this course and to analyze their own data for publication. This course is reading and analysis intensive.

title: Technology & L2 Learning
description: Undoubtedly, the use of technology in the second/foreign language (L2) classroom has had a tremendous impact on both experimental research and pedagogy. Empirically and pedagogically, the findings of many of these technology-based studies appear to indicate that employing technology in the L2 classroom is more advantageous for L2 exposure and instruction in the classroom setting. However, two recent methodological studies addressed the robustness of research designs employed in technology-based empirical studies and, more specifically from a psycholinguistic perspective, the effects of computer-assisted language learning (CALL) and synchronous computer-mediated communication (SCMC) versus traditional instruction or face-to-face (FTF) in relation to L2 grammatical development (Cerezo, Baralt, Suh, & Leow, 2013), and vocabulary learning, reading and listening comprehension (Leow & Suh, forthcoming) and raised the important question of whether the medium really matters. In this seminar, the theoretical underpinnings driving CALL studies will first be discussed, students will then identify the strengths and weaknesses of selected published studies on the role of technology in second/foreign language learning, attempt to improve the robustness of the research designs (i.e., their internal and external validity), and finally conduct a data-based project that addresses empirically the effects of the electronic medium by probing deeper into how learners cognitively process online L2 data. Students will also be assessed by oral presentations and written assignments.

title: Moral Foundations of Market Society
description: Are markets good or bad? How free should markets be? What good are economic and commercial liberties, and how strong should those liberties be? Is capitalism an impediment to social justice, or could capitalism be a way of achieving social justice? Is it possible for a society to be free, prosperous, and fair? Is market society cooperative and peaceful or competitive and exploitative? Do markets promote or undermine community and virtue? This course is meant to introduce you to moral and political philosophy by focusing on a particular set of questions about capitalism, market institutions, and the ethics of wealth creation. We will start by asking what moral reasoning is, examine some common moral and political theories, and ask what counts as a sound ethical reasoning and what it takes to justify moral beliefs. We will evaluate the institutions of the market using the tools of ethics, political philosophy, economics, history, and political science. We will investigate issues concerning the nature of money and prices, the role of the division of labor, business ethics, commerce and entrepreneurship, overconsumption and overpopulation, exploitation and alienation, the relationship between wealth and happiness, the motivations of market actors, the rule of law, liberty and market society, rent-seeking and corporate welfare, and more. We will read and discuss evaluations of markets from multiple ideological perspectives: socialist, communitarian, left-liberal, classical liberal, libertarian, and conservative.

title: International Business
description: A first course in the theory and practice of international business. After building a foundation of international trade, foreign direct investment, exchange rates, and government policy, the course emphasis is on the application of concepts to the solution of international business problems. It focuses on areas such as international market entry, the internationalization of the marketing, finance and management functions within the firm, and the development of global business strategies.

title: Business Government Relations
description: The "Business and Government Relations" is an alternative to Business Law in satisfying the "legal and political environment of business" requirement. The course is designed to prepare you for the time when you, as a senior manager of a business firm, will find your business activities being partly determined by government policies. Over the course of the semester, you will learn the Skills: to analyze normative arguments about what government and corporate policies ought to be; to analyze positive arguments regarding claims made about the effects of government policies on business; to understand the how and why of government policy-making; and to learn to "forecast" changes in government policy. You will develop Substantive Knowledge: of political institutions, here and abroad; of the effects of differing government policies on national industrial organization; and of the effects of business on government, in the United States and abroad. You will Reflect: on the appropriate role of corporations in the democratic political process; and on how your civic obligations should affect you in your role as a manager. The course readings will compare American political and business institutions with those of other democratic capitalist countries. Formerly offered as MGMT 265.

title: Advanced International Business
description: This course analyzes the international business environment (IBE), defined as the economic, political, and social factors that shape firmsâ global strategies. We seek to explain the ways in whichâand the reasons whyâcountries differ in macroeconomic and regulatory policies relevant to the firm. The course places special emphasis on the political economy of the IBE, which means that we study how international and domestic political forces influence continuity and change in the rules governing international economic transactions.

title: Entrepreneurship
description: This course covers the fundamental requirements and processes of new business formation. Students will work in teams to identify an idea for a new venture, explore market and conditions that will surround the new business, and finally plan start-up operations and project financials. In addition, the course examines requirements and characteristics of leadership for organizational start-up, sources of capital, methods of negotiation for capital or strategic alliance, and the fundamentals of good selling. Formerly offered as MGMT 280.

title: Social Responsibilities of Business
description: This purpose of this course is to enhance your appreciation for, and ability to deal with, the ethical and legal problems you will face in your careers as managers. The course is designed to teach you how to deal with the ethical and legal dilemmas that can arise in the business context. Please note the emphasis on the word âhow.â This indicates that you will be required to master a method for solving problems rather than simply learn and remember a stock set of answers. It is important for you to appreciate this distinction. In many of your courses you are required to understand a conceptually complex set of materials and demonstrate your understanding on examinations or in papers. This is not such a course. In this course, your job is to master a skill; specifically, the skill of normative problem-solving. More precisely, you will be required to develop three closely related abilities: 1) the ability to identify and analyze the ethical and legal problems that can confront you in business situations, 2) the ability to derive a solution to these problems, and 3) the ability to communicate the justification for your solution to others. The above implies that you should not expect to be provided with answers to the problems we will be examining. In fact, we will often close our discussion of a problem without achieving any definitive resolution. The success of this course should not be measured by the number of ethical or legal problems that we resolve during the semester, but by the degree of confidence you feel at its conclusion in your ability to deal with the unexpected and unexamined normative dilemmas that may confront you in your professional life.

title: Strategic Management
description: Strategic management deals fundamentally with the ways in which firms build and sustain superior competitive positions and profitability. Successful strategy design and implementation requires a holistic understanding of the firmâs external environment and its internal resources and capabilities. Although we will draw on your prior studies in finance, marketing, accounting, and operations for insights to analysis of firms and their environments, strategy is a stage-setting activity that should driveârather than be driven byâfunctional area decisions and actions. The primary objectives of the course are to improve your ability to: â¢ Evaluate strategic opportunities in a firmâs external environment and assess the resources and capabilities that a firm can bring to taking advantage of opportunities. â¢ Develop superior strategies that will promote sustainable profitability. Determine the appropriate scope of a firmâs activities for expansion and growth.

title: Launching the Venture
description: Overview The course is designed for students who are serious about trying an entrepreneurial path (whether or not they plan to start a new venture in the near future. The heart of the course involves students working in teams to develop a new venture idea from "inspiration" to formal business plan. Lectures and guest speakers will expand and enrich learning. Grades There are no exams. Grades depend on the teamâs developing and presenting the business plan and each studentâs individual commitment evaluated by his/her teammates. Class Participation 20% Business Plan and Presentation 80% Paul Centenari He taught this course five times, ten years ago. Many of his students remember him as âthe best teacher they had at Georgetownâ. He is the CEO of Atlas, a manufacturer of corrugated packaging products. The business started as an acquisition using equity from angels including fathers of old girlfriends. Growth has been fueled by a dozen LBOâs. The operating philosophy of the company is a unique combination of an open-book culture with democracy in the workplace. See the attached Inc article for more details.

title: Consulting Frameworks, Methods & Contexts
description: The overarching objective of this course is to provide multi-disciplinary conceptual foundations and analytical tools that sharpen students' consultive problem solving skills and prepare them for fast starts in their internships, Global Experiences and full-time careers. The central premise of the course is that consulting is, at its core, structured problem solving and that problem solving skills can be developed by learning frameworks (i.e. recurring conceptual structures) and methods (i.e. analytical protocols for diagnosing problems and prescribing solutions) and applying them in a variety of contexts (i.e. different types of problems in diverse industries and settings.

title: Price/Value/Profitability
description: The overarching objective of the course is to provide the conceptual underpinnings and analytical tools required to make reasoned pricing decisions in highly competitive, dynamic environments. PVP will explore topics including understanding the link between marketing and economics; understanding the behavioral and psychological aspects of pricing; developing and interpreting value maps; assessing the profitability of pricing alternatives; customizing prices for diverse market segments; understanding revenue and price yield management; and managing in price war environments.

title: Health Care Organization, Financing, and Delivery
description: This introductory course focuses on health care organization, financing and delivery in the United States, to include the role of business management, public policy and industry regulation. Core constructs for analysis include national health expenditures and cost containment strategies, health care quality and performance improvement, patient access and health disparities, health worker labor projections and workforce management, life sciences research and industry perspectives, hospital management and health professionals, and business models and incentives for insurers and employers.

title: Investing for Impact
description: The future of successful business leaders will be defined by the ability to create, build and sustain organizations with and for mission and finance related returns. In light of changing organizational structures (from C corp. to B corp.), the infusion of private capital for social good, and the increased integration of responsibility and ethics into the corporate workplace, emerging leaders should be prepared to understand the core concepts of investing for impact to retain a competitive advantage as a leader and organization. The course will engage students in understanding the core principles for investing for impact. For students interested in pursuing careers in social finance, philanthropy/nonprofit, investment management, or responsible banking, this course is a must have. With such little formal text and theory on the topic of social responsible investing and related sectors, this class will provide a unique overview of the market and provide a reflective and practical application of core principles of the sector. The course will provide insight as an âimpact investorâ and explore the due diligence and deal structuring required for investing in social enterprises. The course will introduce students to an investment continuum and provide related skills and theory on how to apply diverse capital layers to emerging and growth enterprises. The course format will include class discussion, case studies and guest speakers. Each class will include discussing the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Lead Mgmt Consulting Firm
description: This course provides a broad overview of the management consulting industry. Specific course objectives include (1) To give students a deep understanding of the management consulting industry, the major firms and how they work, and what separates winning and losing strategies, (2) to prepare students for a successful early career in management consulting and (3) to arm students with frameworks and analytic tools they can use in consulting or in other business problem-solving contexts

title: Mergers and Acquisitions
description: This course provides a broad overview of mergers and acquisitions. This course will provide you with a framework to help you understand the merger and acquisition environment, to analyze M&A transactions, to develop opinions about them, to design M&A proposals, and to think about M&A events critically. The course examines a variety of M&A transactions, including friendly deals, hostiles, leveraged buyouts, mergers of equals, and cross border situations. The course is primarily case based with some lecture. There are two simulation exercises, one involving a friendly merger negotiation, the other a hostile takeover. A major focus is exploring what makes some deals successful and others failures.

title: Small Business Clinic
description: Students will develop business plans and help with legal, accounting and finance issues to small businesses and non-profits in DC. Targeted business clients will be socially responsible, often women-or minority owned, and committed to stay locally owned.

title: Entrepreneurship Practicum
description: The âEntrepreneurial Practicumâ aligns teams of two students each with a venture capitalist or angel investor or with a CXO officer of a high growth company, with a specific task to achieve during the course of the six-week module. The two-student teams will work hand-in-hand with either the investor or the company CXO to develop a specific deliverable to benefit the sponsor. Professors will work with the teams and their sponsors to orchestrate a project plan that will provide the students with the insights into how either the investors (half of the class) or the executive (other half) performs their day-to-day tasks.

title: Entrepreneurship Practicum
description: The âEntrepreneurial Practicumâ aligns teams of two students each with a venture capitalist or angel investor or with a CXO officer of a high growth company, with a specific task to achieve during the course of the six-week module. The two-student teams will work hand-in-hand with either the investor or the company CXO to develop a specific deliverable to benefit the sponsor. Professors will work with the teams and their sponsors to orchestrate a project plan that will provide the students with the insights into how either the investors (half of the class) or the executive (other half) performs their day-to-day tasks.

title: Understanding Enterprise
description: This course is a gateway course for studetns seeking answers to the following questions: -- What makes an entrepreneurial venture successful and sustainable? -- What does it mean to be an entrepreneur and where do entrepreneurs fit in the larger, more important dynamic of entrepreneurship? -- How and where can entrepreneurship take place and what is the nature of the value it can create? The course questions fundamental assumptions about entrepreneurs to get to truth behind what leads to successful, sustainable ventures. The course begins by establishing critical theory but emphasizes application of that theory throughout. This course will challenge students to explore and adopt an entrepreneurial mindset, forming a skill and a baseline critical to not just entrepreneurial pursuits but any career path. This is not a "how to start a business" course, but instead has more emphasis on understanding the overall concept of entrepreneurship and entrepreneurs, with a heavy emphasis on reading and personal reflection.

title: Corporate Strategy
description: Corporate Strategy explores the question: In what set of businesses should a firm compete? Organizations routinely grow and change through merging with or acquiring other organizations and also through the internal creation of new divisions or subsidiaries. Through such activities, organizations can extend their core capabilities into new lines of business, expand their existing lines of business into new geographic areas, and improve the efficiency of existing businesses. Firms acquire, create, or merge with other organizations in order to improve the profitability of the overall corporation. This course is designed to introduce you to the central challenge of corporate strategy, which is the creation of a corporate advantage through investments in a set of businesses that are better off, individually or collectively, for being commonly owned.

title: Economics of Strategic Behavior
description: Key topics of This course will focus on the dynamics of entry and the impact of global competition, the strategic dynamics of competitive markets, the primary sources of competitive advantage (local and global), managing competitive interactions through cooperation and preemption, bargaining situations, the impact of information distribution, financial implications of strategic economics. \ The emphasis of the course is on the ability to apply a small number of principles effectively and creatively, not on the mastery of detailed aspects of theory. The course is particularly relevant to those pursuing careers in strategy consulting, equity and investment research, asset management, investment banking, mergers and acquisitions, private equity and venture capital, entrepreneurship, marketing and brand management, international business development, public sector management and non-profit management.

title: Organizational Design Strategy
description: It has been observed that the effectiveness of a firmâs strategy is often influenced by the limitations of the organization that is empowered to carry it out. However well-designed a company strategy is, such a strategy will fail if it is not well-aligned with the organization and its people. This course will provide students a set of frameworks that will enable the appropriate design of an organization that is aligned with and fits well with a given strategy. In addition, direction will be provided for creating structures and accountability systems that influence how people do their work, where they focus their attention and how their activities can be aligned to contribute to overall strategic goals. The course is well-suited for those who wish to engage in general management roles, strategy consulting, human resource management, not for profit and public sector management and entrepreneurship.

title: Organizational Design Strategy
description: It has been observed that the effectiveness of a firmâs strategy is often influenced by the limitations of the organization that is empowered to carry it out. However well-designed a company strategy is, such a strategy will fail if it is not well-aligned with the organization and its people. This course will provide students a set of frameworks that will enable the appropriate design of an organization that is aligned with and fits well with a given strategy. In addition, direction will be provided for creating structures and accountability systems that influence how people do their work, where they focus their attention and how their activities can be aligned to contribute to overall strategic goals. The course is well-suited for those who wish to engage in general management roles, strategy consulting, human resource management, not for profit and public sector management and entrepreneurship.

title: Developing the Concept
description: Students develop the first stage of a high-growth startup business. This is an applied learning course, with particular emphasis on learning the real-world perils and rewards of starting a business. Students form teams around business concepts and then work to develop them into viable businesses that investors judge for viability. Several teams have gone on to build the businesses started in this class. The course combines short lectures, guest speakers, student presentations and fieldwork, culminating in an investor presentation to a panel of judges. The first half of the course focuses of building/testing the business model against the Business Model Canvas. The second half focuses on preparing the Minimum Viable Product and the investor pitch. Throughout, students are encouraged to engage in lively discussions on their business concepts and to support concepts of fellow students with ideas and feedback. This course is for you if you have a business idea youâd like to explore or begin to build; if you donât have a business idea but want to see if working within a start-up environment might be the right path for you; if you would like to know how to add innovation to business models, principles that can be applied within a start-up or corporate setting; or if you are considering a venture capital career and would like to know what it looks like from the other side of the table and how to evaluate business plans.

title: Preparing to Launch
description: A follow-on to 596, this ocurse takes thes business plan even deeper. Extremely practical focus on how to write a business plan. Project-focused.

title: Doing Business in the European Union
description: The purpose of the course is to enable students to become more knowledgeable about the European Union as a place to do business â whether as a firm based in an EU country or as a firm based outside the EU. Examples of specific topics covered in the course include: competition policy, including several high-profile cases involving US-based firms; common external trade policies, shared responsibilities of the EU and national governments for policies concerning financial services and investment; the euro zone and its relationship to the Danish, Swedish and UK currencies, and energy industry issues. The course uses case studies and lectures. Depending on availability and scheduling constraints, there are guest lecturers. Student teams present cases analyses in class, and each student prepares an in-depth study of a topic of his/her choosing.

title: Advanced Competitive Strategy
description: The goal of the course is to expand your understanding of strategy by learning a proven methodology for successful strategy development and practicing the application of this methodology to real-world business situations. In this course we will explore different ways that companies identify opportunities and develop strategies to capture sustainable, profitable business growth. Businesses survive and prosper when they understand the ways their business environment is changing, identify the implications this has for the way they do business, and take action to shape their business future. Too often, managers focus on the idea of creating competitive advantage versus peers. In this course you will learn that business advantage is often best achieved through non-competitive thinking in which a company places strategic focus on the ways it, and sometimes it alone, can create value for customers and capture this value in the form of profit. Each company has a distinct set of assets, capabilities, customers, business partners, and product offerings, which present distinctive opportunities, threats and strategy options. Companies achieve success not by emulating what competitors are doing, but by making the right choices for their business. Companies that choose to serve customers for whom they can deliver a truly valuable product or service experience and then engineer their delivery approaches to be able to do this profitably will succeed regardless of what competitors are doing. Moreover, the more a company tunes its strategy to profitably deliver the specific experience its target customers want, the more distinctive its customer offerings and operational processes become, making them harder for competitors to copy. In case and class discussions we will explore both the theory and practice of strategy. Each class is designed to introduce you to a proven methodology for business strategy development, expose you to useful analytical tools, and then exercise your skills by applying newly learned concepts to actual business situations. Coursework is intended to benefit professionals involved in strategy development, whether as general manager, business manager, management consultant, strategic planning manager, business development specialist, entrepreneur, or functional specialist.

title: Short Course in Game Theory
description: The objective of this class is to give you the tools to develop your own game theoretic models. Students will learn about important game theoretic ideas such as rationality, rational expectations, asymmetric information, credibility of threats and promises, signaling and commitment. These ideas will be illustrated using mini-cases that cover a wide array of issues and situations such as inter-personal conflict, market entry, perjury, trust in the workplace, employee promotion and firm commitments, and competition over markets.

title: Technology Strategy
description: This course examines a variety of problems in the strategic management of innovation and technology. It asks how and why innovation occurs in an industry, why some innovations rise to dominate others, and how firms develop and implement strategies for innovation and technology. Among the key topics to be explored are how the same technical innovation can either sustain or disrupt existing businesses. We will highlight the specific opportunities and challenges disruptive innovation brings to all aspects of a business, from the strategy making process to organizational structure and from marketing to finance. Case studies from a variety of ânew economyâ and âold economyâ industries serve as the basis for much of the course.

title: Strategic Pricing
description: This is an advanced course in the economics of pricing, strategy and policy of pricing by firms. It is intended to provide a keener analytical basis for managers to understand and make pricing decisions than is possible from the tools provided either in other core classes or electives. As an advanced course, this class will be based on highly interactive lectures in which students and the professor jointly explore topic areas. Second, each class will have a âcaseletâ discussion of 5-15 minutes in which students discuss a ârealâ pricing case/issue. Third, students will participate in teams to develop and present a substantive research paper that provides insight into a pricing issue in a specific industry. Finally, each team will participate in a competitive pricing game that will require substantive preparation and contributions to pricing decisions in a simulation exercise.

title: Entrepreneurship: Implementing the Plan
description: This course emphasizes pragmatic skills in entrepreneurship, including setting up the company, how to raise capital, practical legal issues, systems for day-to-day management, crisis management, corporate strategic partnerships, low budget sales and marketing, IT infrastructure, and "harvesting"/ exit strategies. We will have several outside speakers (entrepreneurial CEOâs).

title: Managing Corporate Change
description: Technology change has been a constant for four decades, and its effects have hit almost every industry. The quickest to exploit change often have been entrepreneurial start-up firms founded by individuals able to envision years ahead how this change would unfold and the resulting discontinuities. In turn this has given rise to a robust venture capital industry. Larger organizations in general have not been leaders but adopters, some earlier than others, using deals with the innovators to capture the benefits for themselves. Some corporations have used their own venture funds with widely varying success rates. What is the right strategy? Innovate yourself? Invest actively in technology change to be an early adopter? Do deals with winners after they emerge? The instructor has spent thirty years living these options, and this course will examine what has worked and not worked, where and why. For this reason the selection of cases is a bit eclectic, but the goal is to evolve a general understanding of the limits of deals as a way of managing technology change.

title: Global Strategy
description: The emergence of the global economy and the multinational enterprise is one of the most significant developments of the past three decades. This course is designed to offer future leaders an overview of the changing character of comÂ¬petÂ¬ing on a worldwide scale. The course builds upon the concepts and theories covered in the core courses in Strategic Management, International Business and International MacroecoÂ¬nomics (as well as drawing upon the core courses in Marketing, Operations Management and Accounting and Finance). A distinguishing feature of this course is that it addresses the management of highly complex situationsâintegrating different cultural, geopolitical and economic issues. As a result, Global Strategy will force us to address issues that are at the cutting edge of managerial practice.

title: Production Practicum
description: These production labs involve experiential learning in scenic, costume, props, and run crew areas, as supervised by TPST faculty and staff. Majors must complete three graded single credit assignments of this class taken over a series of semesters once declared as a major, including one run crew assignment, whereas minors are required to take a single credit semester of the practicum. These supervised production labs, linked to the TPST home season in the Davis Center, help to strengthen embodied learning in the field, enabling a more well-rounded understanding of making theater. While students may specialize in one production area most intensively, every major will participate in at least 2-3 production areas over the course of fulfilling this requirement to gain breadth of exposure. In addition to registering for the class, ALL majors and minors must fill out a scheduling form kept with Susan Hougen in 108 Davis by the first week of the new semester to secure placement. Students are expected to commit to regular weekly scheduled meetings, typically for three hours, with faculty/staff for production labs or for more intensive run-crew assignments (which happen in consolidated timeframes). The zero credit option may be taken once a student has fulfilled the requirement or for no credit; however majors must take the 091 class three times to fulfill the requirement. TPST 092-01 may be clustered with TPST 091-01 for practicum requirement.

title: Production Practicum

These production labs involve experiential learning in scenic, costume, props, and run crew areas, as supervised by TPST faculty and staff. The zero credit option may be taken once a student has fulfilled the requirement or for no credit; however majors must take the 091 class three times to fulfill the requirement.

title: Cross-Cultural Performance Studies
description: This gateway TPST course introduces students to myriad approaches to performance and its role/s, understood variously as spectacle, community ritual, creative expression, civic activism, cultural transmission, and/or religious enactment, for example. We will focus on the varied sensory worlds (e.g. the sounds, sights, spatio-kinetics, even smells and tastes) generated in different living performance traditions and cultures-particularly those with deep local and global reach. Through embodied research, direct performance encounters in the DC area, and analytic as well as artistic assignments, we will also consider the different demands on performers and audiences in distinct socio-cultural webs and circumstances of staging. An experiential entree to the field, this class is structured to ease students into a respect for performance, its cultural embeddedness and range, and students' own emergent relationships to performance. In different weeks, we may give primary focus to at least three of the following: Native American dance theater, South Asian Dance, Theater Psycho-Physical Realism, Theater of the African Diaspora, intercultural Balinese performance, Theater as Social Change, Queer Theater, transatlantic Solo Performance, and/or Euro- American Avant-Garde Performance. Reading shall draw on works by authors such as Eugenio Barba (The Secret Art of the Performer), Victor Turner (From Ritual to Theater), Joseph Roach (Cities of the Dead), David Roman, Suzan Lori Parks, Anne Bogart, Augusto Boal, John Emigh, Constantin Stanislavski, and others, with generous exposure to multimedia and performance materials. Lab Fees:

title: Acting I
description: A studio class, Acting I is an experiential introduction to the study of acting for the stage with a basis in psychological and physical realism. Emphasis is placed on the critical and creative theories and techniques to cultivate imagination, focus, embodied creativity, self-awareness, vocal range, and script analysis of predominantly modern and contemporary drama. Acting projects include scenes, monologues, and acting exercises. Readings, writing assignments, and performance projects required. Suitable for students with considerable performance experience but without college coursework in acting, and for complete beginners. Must attend first and second class to retain spot. Student may only add after the first class with instructor approval.

title: Improvisation for Social Change
description: Since theatre began it has served to spark community dialogue about issues both personal and global. From this position, the theatre artist has a responsibility as a citizen to creatively investigate her/his knowledge and understanding of current social and political realities. The course is a participation lab study in improvisational acting based on the techniques articulated by Viola Spolin and further expanded by the Living Stage Theatre Company. Living Stage was the community outreach program of Arena Stage, dedicated to working with underserved audiences. Students will engage in group and individual theatre âgamesâ designed to provide forms of expression in response to queries about various social issues. The goals of this course are to explore the student's potential to work in a creative group; to enhance the student's ability to identify and articulate in theatrical formats issues related to social justice; and to expand the student's awareness of current trends in socially conscious theatre. This may include creating group theatrical presentations based on personal ideas, writings, and adaptations of material related to researched problem themes. Class sessions will involve solo and collective voice and movement exercises, some reading and outside research. Theater Performance Fees.

title: Play Analysis
description: This gateway course introduces students to reading and writing about plays in the context of theatrical production and performance. Engaging with plays from a variety of periods, cultures, genres and styles, we will develop studentsâ ability to read plays closely while analyzing the host of production choices - inspired by structure, theme, context and interpretive lens, for example. Plays studied range from classics to contemporary realism, farce to solo performance. Required for all TPST majors, this course also satisfies the College Humanities and Writing II requirement, with writing assignments designed to hone studentsâ emergent critical and creative voices. Cross listed with Comp Lit. Prerequisites: None

title: Play Analysis

Engaging with plays from a variety of periods, cultures, genres and styles, we will develop studentsâ ability to read plays closely while analyzing the host of production choices - inspired by structure, theme, context and interpretive lens, for example. Plays studied range from classics to contemporary realism, farce to solo performance. title: Intro to Production Techniques
description: This introductory gateway course covers all aspects of technical theatrical production. Students learn the basic materials, tools, terminology and techniques used in construction of scenery, scenic painting, costumes, props, as well as basic familiarity with lighting and sound equipment. In addition to individual projects, practical group labs link to the actual mounting of productions in the Davis Center. Emphasis is placed on the roles and responsibilities of production team. Attendance at campus theater productions, as well as at least one professional theater production, leading to critical review of the technical aspects is required. Lab Fees.

title: Principles of Theatrical Design
description: This is an intensive introductory course that explores visual and spatial creativity through artistic composition, script analysis, and theatrical design. This overview course acquaints students with design elements and techniques as non-verbal communication tools to express the creative imagination in theatrical contexts. Lecture, discussion, reading, and project work in a variety of media will place emphasis on imagining the theatrical world of the play. The course will examine traditional areas of design including scenery, lighting, and costumes, and will also focus on the collaborative process with directors and other theater artists. If possible, project work may be linked to work in the production season or directing curriculum. Weekly lab required as posted. Fulfills a TPST major requirement. Theater Performance Fees.

title: Performance Lab: Things/Go
description: Registration by Audition and Permission of instructor only. These immersive studio courses manifest as rehearsal and performance in the TPST home season.

title: Performance Lab: Hamlet
description: Registration by Audition and Permission of instructor only. These immersive studio courses manifest as rehearsal and performance in the TPST home season.

title: Adaptation and Performance of Literature
description: This interdisciplinary intermediate course engages students in an experiential and experimental approach to the adaptation, staging, and performance of literature (poetry, fiction, non-fiction), working across a variety of cultures, genres and styles. Students will develop original performances, acting as the adapter, director, and performer in their works. Students will work collaboratively, as well as independently, as they pursue the intersection of critical and creative values. Cross-listed with Comparative Literature, this course is required for the TPST major. It also fulfills the Humanities and Writing II requirement. Theater Performance Fees

title: Performing Black Citizenship
description: An interdisciplinary seminar, âPerforming Black Citizenshipâ will consider how artists and activists deploy embodied practices to simultaneously refashion blackness and citizenship. Realizing how race and its representations in politics has always been fluid, enigmatic, constructed and historically informed (from Plessy v. Ferguson to the inauguration of Barack Hussein Obama), the class will explore how performance contributes to and disallows the production of the racialized citizen. In the 21st century race remains a powerful aspect of political identity, but what factors contribute to that power? Using performance theory as our critical lens, this class will examine how black artists and activists perform citizenship in drama (Mulatto, Les Blancs, Topdog/Underdog), film (When the Levees Broke, Glory) literature (The Parable of the Sower, The Parable of the Talents) and spontaneous and choreographed performances (flash mobs, the March on Washington, sit-ins) using theories of governmentality, biopolitics and speech acts. We will begin our inquiry by asking the following interlocking questions: can race be performed, and under what circumstance? What actions, gestures or movements signify race? What actions, gestures or movements signify citizenship? How does language and speech contribute to and participate in the performance of race and of citizenship? What histories allow certain performances to be read as racial and/or patriotic? Cross-listed with African-American Studies and American Studies, this TPST course also fulfills the Humanities and Writing II requirement. Taught by new Georgetown Professor Soyica Colbert, Performing Black Citizenship is intended for intermediate and advanced students; first year students may request faculty approval, pending readiness.

title: Comedy
description: This course offers a critical exploration of practices, theories, and examples of comedy across a wide variety of forms and genres - performative/theatrical, cinematic, and literary. In this course we will explore the psychological, philosophical, political, cross-cultural and aesthetic roots of comedy, investigating why and how we laugh, as well as the deep roots of our varied forms of laughter. Beginning with the assumption that "comedy is serious business," we will engage with many different forms and genres of comic texts, including the plays of Aristophanes, Shakespeare, Samuel Beckett, Moliere, Pakistan's Shahid Nadeem, Robert O' Hara, and Oscar Wilde; the films of Charlie Chaplin, Buster Keaton, Jacques Tati, the Marx Brothers, and more; the stand-up work and comic personae of Lucille Ball, Lenny Bruce, Richard Pryor, Moms Mabley; approaches to improv and sketch comedy (Monty Python); the political influence of Jon Stewart and Stephen Colbert and others, and the emergent form of the "mockumentary." We will explore silent comedy, slapstick, absurdism, the comedy of manners, camp, satire, parody, ethnic humor, jokes, and political humor. In so doing we will examine how comedy intersects with questions of identity, gender, the body, censorship, sexuality, race, obscenity and the law. Cross-listed: Comparative Literature and Film Studies.

title: Writing Stage Adaptations
description: This workshop-based seminar introduces students to the art of adaptation for the stage from a playwriting perspective. Each student will adapt a literary, historical or fact-based work as a one-act play, developed in the context of reading and discussion of the theory and practice of adaptation. Together, the class will develop critical and creative fluency by considering disparate theatrical texts adapted for the stage (including from other media, times, cultures). The semester will culminate in staged readings of studentsâ adapted texts. This course satisfies the Humanities and Writing II requirement. Cross-listed with English and Comparative Literature. Prerequisites: TPST 130, or TPST 250 or TPST 280. Students without prerequisites may ask for faculty approval.

title: Theater and Writing Communities
description: This intermediate and advanced-level course heralds the inaugural partnership between Georgetownâs Department of Performing Arts and the Young Playwrightsâ Theater (YPT), a group that works with children in schools across the DC area to develop and perform the childrenâs original plays. We will explore theater writing in the community through a combination of critical reading and research, YPT class visits, and centrally, through immersion-- students will work closely with YPT through the semester as they develop new plays by children from inception to full performance by adult actors. We will learn about YPTâs methodology and approach both through working closely together in a particular school, and through studying their history and mission in the context of the history, theory and practices of community-engaged theatres (Boal and others). We will consider the wider implications for social justice and transformation of childrenâs playwriting projects such as YPT, New Yorkâs 59th Street Projects and Providenceâs Manton Avenue Project, and examine the ways in which specific communities themselves shape the work. Students will be asked to reflect on their own experiential learning in working with YPT, and should plan on allocating some of their planned study time for this course to semi-regular school visits. Cross listed with EDIJ (Education, Inquiry, & Justice), and AMST (American Studies)

title: Performing Madness
description: This multidisciplinary course examines the relationship between madness/mental illness and performance. Using texts from history, dramatic literature, philosophy, medical science and film, we will investigate how the idea of mental illness is represented and/or performed (in theatrical or non-theatrical context). We will take case studies from a variety of cultures and historical moments, exploring how the notion of what is "normal" and "abnormal" transform through time and context. In addition to discussions and presentations, we will engage in a variety of performance and other creative workshops throughout the semester.

title: Spectacle of Eating
description: In this high-brain-calorie intermediate course, we will explore intersections between food/eating and performance/theatre. Our concerns range from the theoretical (when is eating"performative"?) to the artistic (how do we represent the act of "eating" on stage without actually consuming food?), from the historical (how do we stage the radish-chopping scene in Nozakimura?) to the contemporary (how do we stage food journalism?), from the local (how is food "performed" in Washington DC?) to the global (how does a culture construct its identity through food preparation and eating?), and more. Cross-listed with Comparative Literature and American Studies. Theater Performance Fees. Students must attend first week to reserve priority.

title: Ensemble Workshop: Hamlet, The Course
description: Intended for intermediate and advanced students of theater, this immersive studio course (tied to the TPST Season production) utilizes a research to performance model to explore and examine Shakespeare's classic play, Hamlet, with special focus on drawing out the play's connections to todayâs world and participating students. Often called âthe greatest story ever told,â Hamlet has been the subject of more interpretations and adaptations than virtually any other work in Western literature. Critically and creatively, we will explore the playâs themes of loneliness/ alienation, madness, the relationship between private and public worlds (the "personal and the political"), and the relationship between language and identity, framed now in a world of social networking profiles, tweets, and status updates. The class will work collaboratively to connect our Hamlet production as deeply as possible to the emotional and material lives of students in 2013, and more specifically to the Georgetown students working on the play. Class assignments will explore many versions of the play (films, texts, adaptations etc.), and a range of theoretical, historical texts from a range of disciplinary perspectives (philosophy, psychology, theology etc.), as well as pursuing personal performative explorations to probe the world of the play. Offered in conjunction with the Theater and Performance Studies Programâs Fall mainstage production of Shakespeareâs Hamlet, this performance workshop course is an ideal workshop class for students cast, via auditions at the beginning of the semester, in the play, yet TPST 300 is not required to participate in the production. Those working on the production in other capacities (dramaturgy, design, stage management etc.) are also most welcome to best cultivate an ensemble ethic. Students who cannot participate in the production must have permission of the instructor and will be admitted based on their expression of their own interests in the material, and based on prior experience. Theater Performance Fees. Lab TBD

title: Advanced Acting Workshop
description: Designed for advanced students this studio course is taught by one of DCâs leading actors. Students will develop skills to deepen their creative and critical understanding of given circumstances, moment-to-moment stimulus response, objectives/obstacles/tactics, transitions, decisions, discoveries, interplay, subtext and character development. Class projects will include scenes from the works of Chekov, Ibsen, Inge, Williams, Miller, Hellman, O'Neill, Wilson and others deeply rooted in the realist tradition. Students will participate in a rigorous approach to the critical analysis of acting through character research, through evaluating performances of their own and fellow studentsâ work and through written analysis of plays attended on and off campus. Pre-requisites: TPST-120. Majors and Minors receive priority. Faculty approval needed if adding after 1st class. Students who have not taken a second acting class may request faculty permission. Lab Fee.

title: US Latino/a Drama and Performance
description: This intermediate, interdisciplinary course involves close reading of US Latino/a plays written in English and also provides an overview of the major social and political forces impacting the development of Latino/a cultural production in the past fifty years. It critically addresses the complex intersection of race, ethnicity and gender in Latino/a drama and the struggle for representation in the broader US theatrical landscape. Beginning with the Chicano movement in the 1960s, we will read, view and write about plays across several generations of the main Latino/a groups in the US informed by their production history, theoretical and historical articles, performance videos, polemics and manifestos. Cross-listed with American Studies, English, Comparative Literature. Intended for students from a range of majors. Includes Juniors and Seniors.

title: Making History: African American  Drama/Film
description: This course will examine works by African American playwrights and directors between 1984 and 2012. Considering the distinctive elements of African American drama in the late twentieth century and early twenty first century, we will explore how social, political, and artistic histories inform the work of Lee Daniels, Katori Hall, Spike Lee, Robert OâHara, Lynn Nottage, Suzan-Lori Parks, and August Wilson. At the same time, we will analyze how the aforementioned artists create historical narratives and how their work comments on the shape of history. The course will examine the relationship between the historical landscape of post-Civil Rights Movement Era as depicted, for example, in references to Martin Luther King, Jr. and Malcolm X and the aesthetic influences of hip hop and spoken word poetry. In addition to the primary texts, we will read critical works that theorize history and memory in African American culture. The course will investigate such questions as: What is an African American play? What is an African American film? Are there distinctly African American aesthetics? How does the political and social climate impact the form and content of contemporary African American drama? Cross-listed with African American Studies, American Studies, Comparative Literature. First years may be admitted by permission of instructor.

Each student will adapt a literary, historical or fact-based work as a one-act play, developed in the context of reading and discussion of the theory and practice of adaptation. Together, the class will develop critical and creative fluency by considering disparate theatrical texts adapted for the stage (including from other media, times, cultures). The semester will culminate in staged readings of studentsâ adapted texts. This course satisfies the Humanities and Writing II requirement. Cross-listed with English and Comparative Literature. Prerequisites: TPST 130, or TPST 250 or TPST 280. Students without prerequisites may ask for faculty approval.

title: Colloquium for TPST Majors
description: This one-credit capstone course is intended as a colloquium for advanced theater degree students considering a future in theater/performance studies, scholarship, education, or production. This class aims to provide a forum to support studentsâ emergent self-definitions in relationship to the field while demystifying its professional terms. Faculty, staff and guest artists will introduce dedicated students to diverse sites of professional engagement in relation to the disciplineâs distinctive methodology. Students, in turn, will present to their colleagues in the cohort regarding their forays in the discipline, and in relation to a self-chosen project, approved and mentored by the professor. As this class is a workshop/colloquium, lively student participation and respectful critique is urged. Required for TPST majors (recommended for graduating seniors).

title: Directed Study: Costume Design
description: This design apprenticeship is for advanced or exceptional intermediate students and will be mentored by Professor Debra Sivigny and is tied to work in the TPST season. Instructor approval requested.

title: Design Apprenticeship
description: This apprenticeship course for costume, lighting or scenic design is tied to the TPST Season and/or a Professional Apprenticeship, advised by Georgetown faculty. It is only taken Junior or Senior year and for students who have previously taken design coursework. Instructor approval is requested.

title: Sr. Thesis Ind- New Work Development
description: This directed research study supports the development of new plays for the honors thesis project. Instructor approval is required.

title: The Problem of God
description: An examination of the religious dimension of human experience and consciousness in relation to a number of problems and challenges: the problem of knowledge; the relation of faith and reason; various historical, social and existential determinants of belief; the challenge of atheism and humanism; the impact of secularization on religion.

title: The Problem of God
description: An examination of the religious dimension of human experience and consciousness in relation to a number of problems and challenges: the problem of knowledge; the relation of faith and reason; various historical, social and existential determinants of belief; the challenge of atheism and humanism; the impact of secularization on religion.

title: Problem of God
description: This is a course that grapples with deep and difficult questions about life, meaning purpose and fulfillment. In other words, it explores the notion of God and fundamental aspects of belief in such a being. The course explores questions pertaining to the concept, nature, existence and efficacy of God. We will look at some essential foundations of theistic belief, the nature and purpose of theological language and also explore some of the traditional arguments that both support and challenge belief in the existence of a loving, all-good, all-powerful and all-knowing deity. To do so we will explore not just philosophical and theological sources but also particular existential questions and approaches. What many believe to be the core challenge to belief in God â the problem and extent of suffering and evil in the world will be the focus of a number of classes, including with reference to classic explorations of such problems from religious and philosophical texts, as well as from literature and film. We will look at accounts of experiences of crises of faith as well accounts of extraordinary religious experience. We will also encounter varieties of atheistic argumentation including the so-called ânew atheistsâ such as Richard Dawkins.

title: The Problem of God
description: This course examines the religious dimension of human experience. It considers arguments for and against the existence of God as well as questions relating to the nature of God, human suffering and joy, and religious pluralism and culture.

title: The Problem of God
description: Drawing on theology, philosophy, literature, visual art and film, this course explores the ethical and political implications of the question W.E.B. Du Bois raised in The Souls of Black Folk: âHow does it feel to be a problem?â The question underscores what Lewis R. Gordon calls the âhermeneutical turnâ in Du Boisâs search to find meaning in the world behind the veil of blackness. The course will explore the varying degrees to which Du Boisâs question shapes the epistemic terrain of twentieth-century African American religion and sheds light on contemporary existential concerns facing human flourishing."

This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Introduction to Biblical Literature
description: An introductory study of 1) the books of the Bible, 2) the history of Ancient Israel and first-century Christianity, and 3) the process of formation and transmission of the faith traditions coming to literary expression in the biblical literature.

title: The Bible and Social Justice
description: A study of the various ways in which Christians try to apply the Bible to questions of social justice in contemporary society. Biblical texts will be studied which relate to the major issues of social justice, such as world hunger, the poor and oppressed, revolution, pacifism, the role of government, etc.

title: The Bible and Social Justice
description: A study of the various ways in which Christians try to apply the Bible to questions of social justice in contemporary society. Biblical texts will be studied which relate to the major issues of social justice, such as world hunger, the poor and oppressed, revolution, pacifism, the role of government, etc.

title: The Book of Genesis
description: To quote the flyleaf from Robert Alterâs translation of Genesis, which we will use in class: âGenesis begins with the making of heaven and earth and all life, and ends with the image of a mummy â Josephâs â in a coffin. In between come many of the primal stories in Western culture: Adam and Eveâs expulsion from the Garden of Eden, Cainâs murder of Abel, Noah and the Flood, the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Abrahamâs binding of Isaac, the covenant of God and Abraham, the saga of Joseph and his brothers. These are stories we attend to throughout our lives, for their beauty, their emotional resonance, their philosophical weight, and their sacredness. They connect us with one another and with generations to come.â In this course we will explore together the stories from the book of Genesis, with special attention to their literary quality, their ethical content, and their theological implications.

title: Jesus in Paul's Writings
description: An exploration of how Saul the Pharisee came to be Paul the Apostle, enlarging the scope of his Judaism through faith in what God had done through the Jew, Jesus, crucified and risen. The course will inquire into what St. Paul may or may not have known of the earthly Jesus whom he always wrote of as the glorified "Lord" and "Christ."

title: Jesus in Paul's Writings
description: An exploration of how Saul the Pharisee came to be Saul the Apostle, enlarging the scope of his Judaism through faith in what God had done through the Jew Jesus, crucified and risen. The course will inquire into what St. Paul may or may not have known of the earthly Jesus whom he always wrote of as the glorified "Lord" and "Christ."

title: The First Christians & Christianity Today
description: A study of the Acts of the Apostles and the Gospel of Luke to see how the first Christians developed the legacy left by Jesus; their response to certain issues which face Christians today: living in a secular culture, attitude toward the poor and socially alienated, diversity of theological opinion, charismatic and institutional aspects of the Church, etc.

title: The First Christians & Christianity Today
description: A study of the Acts of the Apostles and the Gospel of Luke to see how the first Christians developed the legacy left by Jesus; their response to certain issues which face Christians today: living in a secular culture, attitude toward the poor and socially alienated, diversity of theological opinion, charismatic and institutional aspects of the Church, etc.

title: Introduction to Catholic Theology
description: This course undertakes a study of the major themes of the Christian faith from the perspective of the Catholic tradition. It investigates both how various doctrines cohere into a world view, and the tensions that exist among them. Each of the themes - revelation, creation, sin and redemption, Christology, ecclesiology, eschatology - is viewed in its development from story, image, and symbol to dogmatic formulation and contemporary interpretation.

title: Introduction to Catholic Theology
description: This course undertakes a study of the major themes of the Christian faith from the perspective of the Catholic tradition. It investigates both how various doctrines cohere into a world view and the tensions that exist among them. Each of the themes--revelation, creation, sin and redemption, Christology, ecclesiology, eschatology--is viewed in its development from story, image, and symbol to dogmatic formulation and contemporary interpretation.

title: Christian Initiation
description: The objectives of this course are to consider various aspects of Christian initiation: (1) conversion; (2) the scriptural roots and historical evolution of Christian initiation rites â both in terms of practices and theology and across communions, with an emphasis on the developments in the sacramental churches (e.g., Catholic, Orthodox, Anglican, churches of the Reformation); (3) an in-depth look at the contemporary symbols/ritual practices and theological understandings of Baptism, Confirmation/Chrismation, and Eucharist; (4) an examination of the process of initiation of adults/children of catechetical age (the RCIA in the Catholic Church and the similar processes evolving in other sacramental churches) and of the reception of a baptized person into full communion of the Catholic Church/transfer from one Christian communion to another; and (5) the contours of a baptismal spirituality and the implications of Christian initiation for everyday living.

title: Asian Philosophies
description: This course provides a general introduction to some of the most influential philosophical traditions of India and China and serves as a philosophical and historical foundation for students interested in Asian philosophies. The course concentrates on the theories of human nature that were associated with the early Confucian, Daoist, Hindu, and Buddhist traditions and the ways in which these theories served as the foundation for their ethical, religious, and political views. The accounts of selected early Confucian philosophers are examined, followed by the classical Daoist tradition and the texts of the Daodejing and the Zhuangzi. The course then focuses on the early Indian philosophies associated with the Upanishads and the Bhagavad-Gita, as well as early Buddhist philosophy, which went on to influence the philosophical climate of East Asiaâincluding the development of Confucian and Daoist thoughtâin remarkable ways.

title: Buddhism & Poetry
description: This course will survey the poetic traditions of Buddhist cultures spanning from ancient India to contemporary North America. Lectures, discussions, and writing assignments will focus on the philosophical, ritual, and social contexts in which these poems were written, read, and performed. Through close readings of individual poems, students will develop an appreciation for a variety of classical poetic forms employed by Buddhist poets in Asia. The first section of the course will focus on the poetic traditions of South Asia, including narrative representations of the life of ??kyamuni, the records of verses attributed to the earliest monastic community, the philosophical poetry of the Sanskrit Mah?y?na tradition, and the vernacular songs of the tantric siddhas. The second section of the course will shift the focus to Central and East Asia with a focus on the Chan/Zen tradition and the âlove songsâ of the Sixth Dalai Lama. We will also explore the encounter of Asian Buddhists with modernity through the writings of the Tibetan poet Gendun ChÃ¶pel (1903â1951) and the Korean poet Manhae (1879â1944). The final section of the course focuses on the development of Buddhist poetry in North America from the Beat movement to the present day.

title: Struggle and Transcendence
description: The course will apply the theological method of Bernard Lonergan, S.J. to four phases of the African-American struggle in order to discern and describe the transcendent presence of God. We will examine how, when, and where God shows up in the experience of African-Americans. We will look at four periods: (1) Contemporary Black Culture, (2) The Civil Rights Movement, (3) Reconstruction and Turn of the Century, and (4) Slavery. We will understand the theological method of Lonergan and apply it to these four phases of the struggle in an effort to understand faith, and its impact on the struggle of African-Americans. Lonergan's notions of cognition, history, dialect, doctrine, conversion, and bias will be treated.

We will examine how, when, and where God shows up in the experience of African-Americans. We will look at four periods: (1) Contemporary Black Culture, (2) The Civil Rights Movement, (3) Reconstruction and Turn of the Century, and (4) Slavery.
