Lonergan's notions of cognition, history, dialect, doctrine, conversion, and bias will be treated.

title: Exploring the Qur'an
description: So many questions swirl around the Qur'an these days -- in religious studies, in theology and in politics -- that it seems essential to come to know this text and the way in which a community of believers has been formed around it and by it. This course will explore the themes and content of the Qur'an, its characteristic styles and structures, and the way in which it functions as an authority for Muslims. The Qur'an tells us it forms part of a long tradition of scripture that includes Biblical and post-biblical material, and it addresses its listeners as though they are familiar with that tradition. Therefore, it is important to read the text in the context of the world of late Antiquity that it is so clearly addressing. The course does not require previous study of Islam. However, students who are new to the field will need to do some extra reading at the beginning of the course.

title: The Church in the 21st Century
description: Jesuit theologian Erich Przywara (1889-1972) asserted that a Jesuit university lives âon the borderline where the Church meets the world and the world meets the Church.â In this course, we stand courageously at this vantage point, and strive, in Przywaraâs words, âto interpret the Church to the world and the world to the Church.â We begin by situating ourselves in the Church and the world, exploring the challenges and opportunities of our time and the spiritual and religious aspirations of the millennial generation. We review basic principles of Catholic and Christian theology: faith, revelation, sin, grace, Incarnation and salvation. We then bring the context of the Churchâs tradition to our discussion, exploring the foundations of the early Church and examining how the Catholic Church in Vatican II defined its mission in the modern world. In the course of the semester, students critically engage some of the following topics: Catholic social teaching; conscience, loyalty and dissent; ecumenical and interfaith dialog; the sacraments; spirituality and prayer; ministry of the laity and ordained; political participation and voting; the role of Catholic universities in the Church; and pastoral care for divorced and remarried persons, women, young adults, and LGBTQ persons.

title: Theology and Literature
description: A series of literary pieces, both ancient and modern, will be studied from the point of view of the theological horizon of their authors and the faith that is being imaged in them.

title: Theology After Freud
description: Originating with and still profoundly influenced by Sigmund Freud, contemporary psychotherapeutic theory and practice give rise to new images of the good human life. Some of these images challenge older images maintained for centuries by religious traditions of the West, both Christian and Jewish. The course examines these images and the extent to which a traditional religious understanding can assimilate positively some of the new psychotherapeutic perspectives and evolve to a fresh, but still genuinely religious, view of human life.

title: Theology After Freud
description: Originating with and still profoundly influenced by Sigmund Freud, contemporary psychotherapeutic theory and practice give rise to new images of the good human life. title: Islamic Religious Thought and Practice
description: Course Goals & Description The three main goals of this course: â¢To give the student a familiarity with Islam as a religious choice and vision, not simply a political issue â¢To provide an understanding of the way this vision, as well as the societies that have based themselves on it, have developed and historically and continue to do so â¢To offer a more nuanced, less polarized view of our contemporary world situation Texts & Readings Required Books: Daniel Brown, A New Introduction to Islam, Second edition (Wiley - Blackwell, 2009) F. E. Peters, A Reader on Classical Islam (Princeton, 1994) Available on Blackboard. Recommended Books (on reserve): Sachiko Murata and William C. Chittick, The Vision of Islam (Paragon, 1994) David Waines, An Introduction to Islam (Cambridge, 2003) Goldziher, Ignaz, Introduction to Islamic Theology & Law (Princeton, 1981) Students should have a printed copy of the Qur'Ã¢n in translation. Those by Arberry, Pickthall and Abd al-Haleem are recommended. Several translations are available online for use outside of class time. Assignments & Expectations of Students Assessment: Participation (including full and punctual attendance): 10% Mid-term test: 20% 2 short quizzes mostly on terminology: 5% each 2 Short writing exercises: 10% each Final paper (3,500 â 4,000 words): 40%

title: Religion and Aesthetics
description: This course explores the relationship between religious and aesthetic experience. "Aesthetics" can be defined as the experience of the beautiful or the sublime, and its mystical dimensions create a connection to religion. The course will take a comparative approach by looking at the evolution of these ideas in Western philosophy and religion, on the one hand, and at examples of Buddhist inspired fiction, poetry and film in China, Korea, and Japan, on the other. The comparison is designed to enhance our perspectives on both the Western and East Asian contexts, relative to each other.

The comparison is designed to enhance our perspectives on both the Western and East Asian contexts, relative to each other.

title: Comparative Mythology
description: A wide variety of myths will be discussed as symbolic narratives which reveal the cultural, psychological, and religious dimensions of human life. The myths of Africa, Asia, and the Ancient Mediterranean will be compared, and conclusions will be drawn about the nature of myth and its continuing presence in the modern world.

title: The Hindu Religious Tradition
description: Hinduism is a vast world of ideas and practices - often paradoxical and bewildering - that have spanned millennia. A host of gods attracts the devotions of worshipers, but a single Cosmic Principle draws the unwavering concentration of the mystic. Popular rituals and magical practices reside alongside mind-splitting subtleties of philosophical speculation. This introductory course will focus on a few basic themes that lend a sense of unity to the puzzle of Hinduism. We shall look at the ancient ritual and philosophical texts of the Vedas and Upanishads, survey medieval reformist movements and their foundation in devotional religion, observe the practice of religion in rural India, and uncover the religious origins of Gandhi's political philosophy, among other themes.

title: The Hindu Religious Tradition
description: Hinduism is a vast world of ideas and practices--often paradoxical and bewildering--that have spanned millennia. A host of gods attracts the devotions of worshipers, but a single Cosmic Principle draws the unwavering concentration of the mystic. Popular rituals and magical practices reside alongside mind-splitting subtleties of philosophical speculation. We shall look at the ancient ritual and philosophical texts of the Vedas and Upanishads, survey medieval reformist movements and their foundation in devotional religion, observe the practice of religion in rural India, and uncover the religious origins of Gandhi's political philosophy, among other themes.

title: The Philosophies of India
description: What is the nature of the soul and what happens to the soul after death? Do we perceive reality accurately, or are we steeped in a distorted--or even illusionary--sense of reality? How can one escape the endless cycle of misery, which is life-death-rebirth, in order to attain absolute Being, Knowledge, and Bliss? These are some of the philosophical issues that have occupied individuals and entire communities in India for over three thousand years. We shall look at the questions, and numerous competing answers, offered by a wide range of literary sources. Indian religious wisdom finds an eloquent voice in the poetry of the Rg Veda and the Bhagavadgita, as well as the speculative Upanishads and the brilliant commentaries by Shankara. We shall also look at the medieval theologies of devotional sects and Tantra, and the modern syntheses of Sri Aurobind, Vivekananda, and Radhakrishnan. This course is neither strictly historical nor philosophical, but it combines both disciplines in order to help students understand the driving ideas of Hinduism in their timeless universality as well as cultural context.

title: Judaism in America
description: Before Ellis Island, before the Lower East Side, Jewish immigrants had already spent over 200 years working to establish a uniquely American form of Judaism. This course will examine the transformation of American Jewish identity from its beginnings in the Colonial period to the present day. Topics will include the development of the various religious movements (Reform, Conservative, etc.), the rise of the American synagogue and mutual aid societies, approaches to social justice, and reactions to anti-Semitism.

title: Judaism in America
description: Before Ellis Island, before the Lower East Side, Jewish immigrants had already spent over 200 years working to establish a uniquely American form of Judaism. This course will examine the transformation of American Jewish identity from its beginnings in the Colonial period to the present day. Topics will include the development of the various religious movements (Reform, Conservative, etc.), the rise of the American synagogue and mutual aid societies, approaches to social justice, and reactions to anti-Semitism.

title: Judaism: Ancient Tradition and Contemporary Practice
description: A general introduction to Judaism as a whole, with a focus on the ethical emphasis of biblical religion. Special stress will be placed on the evolutionary nature of Judaism, its diversity and flexibility, its reactions to other religious systems, and its contemporary meaning. Topics addressed will include the nature and condition of man; evil, suffering, and death; and the origins and significance of Zionism and modern Israel.

title: Buddhism and Social Action
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the ethical teachings of Buddhism, their philosophical and spiritual foundations, and their application to contemporary issues. The first half of the course will focus on the nature and development of Buddhist ethics, from the early Pali and Sanskrit texts to the writings of contemporary Buddhists. Special attention will be given to the ethical teachings the XIV Dalai Lama and Thich Nath Hanh. The second half of the course will examine social teachings of Buddhism and the way contemporary Buddhists are expanding classical Buddhist ethics to address a number of issues such as human rights, obligations to animals and to the environment, social justice, corporate responsibility, just war, death penalty, abortion, euthanasia, same sex marriages, gender inequality.

title: Monasticism: Buddhist and Christian
description: In recent decades there have been increasing contacts and dialogues between Buddhist and Christian monastics. This course will explore the interreligious explorations of monastics practices and values by Buddhist and Christian monastics. It will begin with Buddhist reflections on the Rule of St. Benedict and Thomas Mertonâs reflections on Christian practice in light of Zen Buddhism. It will explore Thich Nhat Hanhâs development of Buddhist monasticism for the contemporary world. It will discuss the dialogue of Brother David Steindl-Rast and Roshi Robert Aitken. It will conclude by exploring two of the Gethsemani Encounters at Gethsemani Abbey, Kentucky, where Buddhist and Catholic monastics lived together and explored the meaning of the spiritual life and responses to environmental catastrophe.

title: Judaism and Gender
description: This course will be taught by Julia Watts Belser, Ph.D., who formerly taught at Missouri State University. From contemporary controversies over womenâs prayer at the Western Wall to the dramatic growth of new rituals and practices inspired by Jewish feminism, gender plays a critical role in shaping Jewish religiosity and political life today. This course examines how gender has shaped Jewish culture and religious practice throughout history. We trace the construction of gender in the rabbinic period, examining formative traditions of law, practice, and theology that shaped Jewish ideas of womanhood, masculinity, and androgyny. We explore modern transformations of Jewish life: studying new feminist rituals and religious practices, diverse expressions of gender in contemporary Jewish theology, the rise of queer Jewish culture, and the way transgender Jews are shaping new conceptions of Jewish gender.

title: Jewish Sages and Sinners
description: This course will be taught by Julia Watts Belser, Ph.D., who formerly taught at Missouri State University. This course introduces students to early Jewish culture through the study of legends and stories from the Babylonian Talmud, arguably the most important post-biblical text in Jewish life. We will study some of the most famous characters in early Jewish thought: gripping tales of Jewish rain-makers who bargained with God to rescue their communities from crushing drought, stories of rabbinic heroism and cowardice during the destruction of Jerusalem, an ill-fated rabbinic attempt to make a mystical ascent to paradise, and the eventual redemption of Judaismâs most famous heretic. Through these stories, we will grapple with key religious controversies and theological challenges in early Jewish thought.

title: Theologies of Social Justice
description: The course will explore philosophical and Christian perspectives on justice, with a particular focus on the Catholic social ethics tradition.

title: Religion/Ethics/International Relations
description: Four case studies in the intersection of ethical concerns and international politics are reviewed to uncover and apply the relevant ethical principles. The cases are: the atomic bombing of Hiroshima; the uneven ledger of collaboration and resistance to the Nazi effort to eliminate the Jews of Europe; the war in Vietnam, a memory "that haunts us still"; and the unfolding horror of war in the Balkans. The methodology combines close reading and discussion of the texts with viewing of documentaries on these events. Ethical models utilized are the natural law and just war theories found in the philosophy and law of armed conflict.

title: Ethics and International Relations

title: Death & Afterlife
description: The course investigates two issues: What happens to a person when she or he dies? Is there something that can be called âThe Afterlifeâ? The perspectives from which these two questions are examined are those of world religions, with an emphasis on Christianity. Among the many issues studied are: death as âseparation of body and soulâ; death as the peak of human freedom; death as the end of earthly pilgrimage; immortality of the soul; reincarnation; hell, âpurgatoryâ; heaven; resurrection; end of the world.

title: Religious Ethics and Moral Issues
description: An exploration of fundamental concepts in ethical discourse, and of positions taken by religious moralists on particular issues such as human sexuality, abortion, revolution and war, and euthanasia. Readings from ethical theory, contemporary religious and secular ethicists, and case studies.

title: Saints and Sinners
description: The central issue for this course is the relationship between ethics and Christian faith. Among the issues we will study are the following: how Christian beliefs shape one's ethical views; how different Christian theologies give rise to different understandings of the moral life; the tie between spirituality and ethics; and how the universal call to holiness gives rise to certain moral ways of being. We will use classical and contemporary theological works (including those of Thomas Aquinas, Martin Luther, and John Calvin) and spiritual writings (such as Jean-Pierre de Caussade's Abandonment to Divine Providence and Dietrich Bonhoeffer's The Cost of Discipleship).

title: Saints and Sinners
description: The central issue for this course is the relationship between ethics and Christian faith. Among the issues we will study are the following: how Christian beliefs shape one's ethical views; how different Christian theologies give rise to different understandings of the moral life; the tie between spirituality and ethics; and how the universal call to holiness gives rise to certain moral ways of being. We will use classical and contemporary theological works (including those of Thomas Aquinas, Martin Luther, and John Calvin) and spiritual writings (such as Jean-Pierre de Caussade's Abandonment to Divine Providence and Dietrich Bonhoeffer's The Cost of Discipleship).

title: Theology and Sexuality
description: Discussion of recent developments in understanding the nature of human sexuality and the process of sexual unfolding as providing a new context for theological and ethical reflection on sexual issues and calling for personal appropriation of religious and ethical values. Theology and Sexuality Spring 2013 Professor McFadden 114 New North 687-6230 Office hours: Mon 4:15 - 5:30; Wed 2:15-3:30 pm mcfaddew@georgetown.edu Reading List: Todd Salzman and Michael Lawler, The Sexual Person Readings on Blackboard Lorna J. Sarrel & Philip M. Sarrel, Sexual Turning Points (selections) William Stayton, âGender Identity and Expressionâ Mary Pellauer, âThe Moral Significance of Female Orgasmâ James B. Nelson, âEmbracing Masculinityâ James B. Nelson, âSources for Body Theology: Homosexuality as a Test Caseâ Margaret A. Farley, âAn Ethic for Same-Sex Relationsâ Lisa Sowle Cahill, âFeminist Theology and Sexual Ethicsâ Eugene Borowitz, âWhen Is It Moral to Have Intercourse? A Personal Summaryâ Audio-visual Materials "The Body Human: The Sexes" "Rape is . . ." "Have a Healthy Baby" "The Secret of Life" "Killing Us Softly III" "On Being Gay" âSilent Pioneersâ "Sexuality and Aging" Assignments -two typed pages in response to selected reading assignments -take-home examination -oral examination

title: Political Theology
description: What is the relationship between politics and theology? This course invites students to explore and to consider a variety of responses to the question of the relationship between "church and state.â Materials are drawn from a range of disciplines, from revealed religion and theology to philosophy, political science and law. Selected readings include excerpts from the Gospels, Plato, Aquinas, Maimonides, Farabi, Hobbes, Spinoza, Schmitt, Strauss, liberation theology and Supreme Court rulings. Emphasis will be given to introducing students to short, key passages from these sources, in a way that will be accessible for discussion without prior background.

title: The Bible and Contemporary Politics
description: Thursday, 3:15-5:05PM, REYN 130 Starting from the premise that the Bible has always played a salient role in the nationâs political life this course seeks to examine the ways in which Scripture is invoked, discussed and debated in contemporary American public discourse. Close attention is paid to the process of biblical interpretation--a process that leads political actors to confidently conclude that âthe Bible says this!â Of course, the very multivalence of sacred Scripture and the flexibility of the interpretive process lead other actors to come to strikingly different conclusions about what the Good Book really says. Political controversies in which the testimony of Scripture is brought into play will be studied, such as homosexuality, abortion, the death penalty, stem-cell research, the pedagogical value of creationism, the ethics of warfare, proper sexual behavior, the limits of artistic freedom, and so on. Students will be familiarized with the interpretations and interpretive strategies of religious conservatives, liberals and radicals, as well as the views of those who seek to eliminate Scripture from the sphere of political disputation altogether.

title: Development of Islamic Thought in Contemporary Turkey
description: The political upheavals that surrounded the fall of the Ottoman State and the establishment of the Turkish Republic produced serious challenges for Muslims, not only in Turkey but also throughout the international Islamic community. The abolition of the sultanate (1922) and the caliphate (1924) and the establishment of the Republic (1923), were events that catalyzed the thought of Muslims in the new nation. No Muslim thinker in Turkey was more active and creative in reimagining the role of faith in the modern context than Said Nursi (1878-1960). Progressing from early military and political activism and Kurdish nationalism, Nursi emerged as a leading advocate of Islamic pacifism and proponent of Muslim-Christian unity. His views, expressed in the 6000-page commentary on the Qurâan entitled the Risale-i Nur (Message of Light) have made Nursi the most influential scholar in contemporary Turkey. His influence on a new generation of reformers, characterized by Fethullah GÃ¼len and the Hizmet movement, is being felt today among modern Muslims. This course intends to study the intellectual developments among Muslims starting from the establishment of the Turkish Republic.

title: Jesus Christ in a Pluralist Age
description: This course will examine contemporary interpretations of Jesus in light of religious and cultural diversity. Jewish, Muslim, Hindu, and Buddhist views of Jesus will be explored, as well as Christian understandings of Jesus informed by interreligious perspectives. The course will explore Asian Christian perspectives on Jesus.

title: The Latino Church Doing Justice
description: A study of and a reflection on major influences â cultural, socio-economic, racial, spiritual, theological â which shape the Latino Church in the United States today and which will affect its future identities and roles in this country. Analysis of personal stories combined with related readings and written student reflections will be our approach. There will be one written mid-term exam and a final oral exam. A weekend visit to Camden, New Jersey, one of âAmericaâs most dangerous cities,â will be available to interested students.

title: Religion in American Political Life
description: Since its founding, the citizens of the United States have continually clashed over what they deem to be the appropriate role for religion in American political life. James Madison, on one hand, believed that government was not to be cognizant of religion and that religious diversity and competition of sects would protect religious liberty. George Washington, on the other hand, believed that government should encourage religion and that non-sectarian religious language, symbolism, and celebrations would serve as vehicles for moralizing and uniting the nation. These varying positions held by two of the nationâs most esteemed founders are mere harbingers for the diversity of positions that have emerged and been debated throughout American history. This course is designed to introduce students to the foundations of these debates as well as the various theories held by religionists, theologians, and sociologists regarding the role of religion in political life. Class readings and discussions on the place of religious discourse in politics, public education, marriage, and health care will help students appreciate the complexity and precariousness of drawing sharp distinctions between what is deemed to be secular and religious.

title: Introduction to Christian Ethics
description: This course explores the sources, methods, disputes and insights of Christian ethics. Central themes and issues include freedom, conscience and the passions, the relation of God and morality, love and justice, virtue and law, and the question whether there is a distinctive Christian ethics. Because Christian ethics is a form of reflection which arises out of moral conflict, the course utilizes specific moral problems as a way of exploring these themes. Ultimately, these explorations will provide the student with a theoretical basis for understanding the nature of moral thinking itself and the form which it takes within a Christian context.

title: Introduction to Christian Ethics
description: This course explores the sources, methods, disputes and insights of Christian ethics. Central themes and issues include freedom, conscience and the passions, the relation of God and morality, love and justice, virtue and law, and the question whether there is a distinctive Christian ethics. Ultimately, these explorations will provide the student with a theoretical basis for understanding the nature of moral thinking itself and the form which it takes within a Christian context.

title: Is There A Unity of Religions?
description: This class engages students in a study and discussion of some major aspects of the diversity of world religions, and the debated question of their unity. Although some background information about major religions will be provided by the instructor and/or researched by students, this is not an introductory course to contemporary world religions but an examination of contemporary discourses on the problem of their diversity, and the ways in which their unity and theological and moral differences have been theorized by major religious thinkers of the 20th and 21st centuries. The problem of religious diversity, and its consequences in the form of religious conflicts and civilizational clashes between Islam and the West, among others, have been at the center-stage of international affairs. On the other hand, globalization and the information technology have made widely available an unprecedented amount of primary and secondary sources about world religions and wisdom traditions from Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Buddhism, Taoism, Hinduism and the world Shamanistic traditions. This information has suggested to some the possibility of the emergence of a trans-religious language of spirituality, as testified by the so-called New Age movement. By contrast, a number of contemporary collective identities have sedimented around religious concepts, and sometimes hardened into aggressively exclusivist, and even intolerant, movements. Hence the very pressing relevance of an examination of commonalities among world faiths, as well as the need for a clear understanding of the foundations of their differences. The dialectics of religious unity and diversity will be envisaged on several levels and in several realms through a multifaceted approach to the religious phenomenon. Metaphysics, theology, spirituality, symbolism and the arts will be called to bear in our studies and discussions. We will pay particular attention to the specificity of Buddhism as a non-theistic religion, and the challenges posed by its concepts to the idea of a unity of religions. We will define, examine and discuss the relevance of concepts such as monotheism, henotheism, polytheism, pantheism, panentheism, shamanism, and animism to our primary question. As a major contributor to the theological debate over religious unity, the perennialist concept of a Â«transcendent unity of religions Â» will be explained, examined and debated, as will contemporary objections to that concept in academic, Christian and Muslim theological circles. Finally the concept of universality as applied to the diversity of faiths will be envisaged within the theological confines of Islam and Christianity, and various interpretations of this concept in each tradition will be examined and discussed.

title: Religion and Science
description: This course explores the complex and sometimes contentious relationship between religious belief and modern science. After examining some of the larger historical and conceptual issues surrounding the modern debate between religion and science, we will then briefly survey the religion-science relationship across several major religious traditions. Having done so, we will focus in greater detail on one of the most important and heated areas of the current debate over religion and science: namely, the potential conflict between traditional forms of Christian faith and evolutionary theory. Authors to be studied include Richard Dawkins, Daniel Dennett, Owen Flanagan, John Haught, Kenneth Miller, Alvin Plantinga, John Polkinghorne, and Michael Ruse.

title: God in the Brain
description: This course will explore the nexus between God and the mind/brain. Students will have the opportunity to analyze and critically assess several proposals that claim to explain the relationship between the mind/brain and the belief or experience of God. The proposals that will be explored fall under the two general headings of Cognitive Science of Religion (CSR) and Neuro/Theology (NT). CSR locates belief in God and other spiritual and religious phenomena in the hyperfunctioning of the manifold cognitive devices through which the mind knows its environment. NT focuses on brain regions and neural circuits that can account for the experience and belief in God as well as other religious and spiritual phenomena. The final part of the course will be devoted to reflecting on the implications of those proposals for actual religious faith and practice.

title: Catholic Ritual, Spirituality, and Justice
description: This course will introduce students to the history and theology of the liturgy of the Roman Catholic Church. We will explore various elements of worship including symbol, ritual, sacred space, language, and music. Attention will also be given to the relationship between liturgy and justice, liturgy and Catholic identity and spirituality, and liturgy and personal/devotional prayer. A few classes will explore ritual performance experientially.

title: The Church and the Poor
description: This course explores the rich and varied tradition of Christian responses, over the centuries, to the perennial challenge of poverty as both an evangelical virtue and a sinful social structure. Resources include sacred Scripture, patristic teaching, official pronouncements and spiritual works, as well as the activity of religious orders and Church-related groups seeking to eradicate the causes of poverty, as well as to alleviate its symptoms. Attention will also be paid to liberation theology and current Church involvement in the struggle for social justice and integral development at local, national, and international levels.

title: The Jesuits: History and Spirituality
description: Following his own religious awakening in 1521, Ignatius of Loyola developed a way to awaken and direct the spirit, and he founded a group to bring his message to the world. The Jesuits influenced the science, arts, and politics of Europe--as well as India, China, Japan, Africa, and the New World. Students will develop and present a research project.

title: The Jesuits: History and Spirituality
description: Following his own religious awakening in 1521, Ignatius of Loyola developed a way to awaken and direct the spirit, and he founded a group to bring his message to the world. The Jesuits influenced the science, arts, and politics of Europe--as well as India, China, Japan, Africa, and the New World. Students will develop and present a research project.

title: Islamic Mystical Tradition: Sufism
description: The course aims to introduce students to one of the central intellectual and institutional dimensions of the Islamic religious tradition: Sufism. Sufism is a discipline, an experience, an intellectual tradition, a social phenomenon, a reform movement, a literary heritage, and much more. Themes include: the Sufi mystical experience; its relation to scripture, communal orthodoxy, the revealed law, and philosophy; the role of desire, the will and imagination in the Sufi experience, as well as the role of poverty and sexuality; institutional and popular forms of Sufism; Sufism as a force for social and religious reform and as a moral and economic model for society; Sufism and the modern world.

title: Theological Implications of the Holocaust
description: This course examines the implications of the holocaust from a theological perspective focusing primarily on rethinking the agenda of Jewish theology in contemporary times but also on exploring Christian theological encounters with Judaism and antisemitism. The following issues will be discussed: God's role in history, the problem of evil, the nature of covenant, and the struggle for meaning and human dignity in the kingdom of night.

title: Theological Implications of the Holocaust
description: The Holocaust is recognized as one of the traumatic moments in human history. The uniquely systematic depths of human-human interaction it revealed, paired with daring acts of heroism which the period yielded, have raised a range of questions which challenge long-held assumptions about what humanity is, if and what God is, and how to understand the concepts of good and evil. This course will have as its goal to assess the Holocaust as it has been approached by a range of thinkers, and to place it within the larger context of theology, history, art and thought. While our primary backdrop will be theological questions provoked by its narrative -- from both Jewish and Christian perspectives -- we will inevitably encompass the larger historical picture of Jewish-Christian, Jewish-Jewish, Christian-Christian and human-divine relations. We will also consider the importance, in the later part of the twentieth century, of visual (and other) art as a means of response -- both in the expression of anger and in seeking healing -- to this trauma.

title: Jews and Modernity
description: This course examines the way in which Jews met the religious, social, and cultural challenges posed by the onset of modernity. We will trace the central transformations in Jewish life from the 18th â 20th centuries in Europe, the United States, and the Ottoman Empire. Topics will include the quest for political emancipation, the Jewish Enlightenment, Hasidism, Jewish nationalism, and the Jewish response to new forms of Anti-Semitism.

title: Jews and Modernity
description: This course examines the way in which Jews met the religious, social, and cultural challenges posed by the onset of modernity. We will trace the central transformations in Jewish life from the 18th â 20th centuries in Europe, the United States, and the Ottoman Empire. Topics will include the quest for political emancipation, the Jewish Enlightenment, Hasidism, Jewish nationalism, and the Jewish response to new forms of Anti-Semitism.

title: Adam and Eve
description: This course looks at the story of Adam and Eve, especially as found in the Bibleâs Book of Genesis. It examines the history of Muslim, Jewish, Christian and non-religious interpretations of the story, as well as contemporary issues related to it. These issues include humansâ role in the natural world, the meaning of death, evolution/creationism, moral decision-making, the origins of evil, gender relations, race relations, and sexuality.

title: Religion and Violence
description: This course will explore the intertwining of religion and violence in an interreligious perspective. Attention will be given to religiously motivated violence and to religious resources for overcoming violence and fostering reconciliation. The course will discuss theories of religious violence and practical strategies of response.

title: Portraits of Jesus in the New Testament
description: While the New Testament, in general, affirms that "there is one Lord," its writers have left us several different pictures of Jesus. This course will consider how various New Testament authors have nuanced their images of Jesus to present him as the Christ to Christian communities of their day. Time will be devoted to studying the Jesus of the Gospels and Acts, as well as the Letters of Paul.

title: Relativism, Truth, and the Natural Law
description: This course will examine the following pressing question of our times: Can the liberal state survive the conflict between truth and contemporary relativism? We will examine some major theories of relativism, inquire into how they can ground the liberal state, ask whether classic notions of truth can rebut them, and then study whether the ânatural lawâ can provide a viable ground for liberalism. This will be the first time I have taught this course, so the students and I will be co-partners in understanding. I will be graciously assisted in the course by guest lectures from a distinguished colleague of another department of the University.

title: Church in Our Times
description: âChurch in Our Times: Ecclesiology in Modern and Contemporary Perspectiveâ Ecclesiology embraces the study of the story, nature, self-understanding, hopes, achievements and failings of the church. This course explores the Christian Church in modern and especially contemporary times. What have been the key moments, achievements and aspirations of the church in these periods? What have been its key challenges and malaises? What are among the most significant cultural, intellectual, social, ethical and organizational developments pertaining to the church in these times? Topics that may be explored include: Exploring the story and self-understanding of the church in differing periods; how the church has interacted with the wider world at different stages in these periods; the understanding of the church in differing Christian traditions; historical and contemporary relations between Christians from differing churches and between Christians and members of other faiths (including questions of ecumenism and interfaith dialogue), along with the related challenges of forms of social and religious exclusion and exclusivism; different forms of and significant transformations in ecclesiology itself (differing methods, concepts and themes, contributions from interdisciplinary perspectives); questions of renewal and reform; changes in institutional and organizational structures and practice; transformations in the understanding and exercise of ecclesial teaching authority; the contribution, place and role of women in the church; major movements and global, regional and local perspectives and developments in the church; ecclesiological approaches, methods and debates such as liberation theology and religious pluralism; the social role, mission and impact of the church in these times; crises in the churches such as the modernist crisis and the religious and clerical abuse crisis; developments in how the church has responded to particular moral challenges; debates and developments in theological ethics; relations between scholars, particularly theologians and central church authorities; models and practices of ecclesial service and leadership; concepts such as authority, magisterium, sensus fidelium and participation in the church in these times; declining involvement and practice in church and religious activities; hope in the churchâs future; depictions and conceptions of the church in literature and film.

title: Introduction to Buddhism
description: This course provides an introduction to the teachings and practices of Buddhism, from its origins in India to its traditional manifestations in Southeast Asia, Tibet, China, Korea, Japan, and its latest developments in Europe and North America. Special attention will be given to the figure of the Buddha (his life, cultural context, spirituality and philosophy), and to the study of traditional as well as contemporary forms of Buddhism (Zen, Tibetan, Theravada, Pure Land). We will read several classical sources from different schools and selected writings from representatives of contemporary Buddhism (Dalai Lama, Thich Nath Hanh, Walpola Rahula, Shunryu Suzuki, D.T. Suzuki, Bhikkhu Gunaratana, Bhikkhu Bodhi, Joanna Macy, etc).

title: Introduction to Buddhism
description: This course provides an introduction to the teachings and practices of Buddhism, from its origins in India to its traditional manifestations in Southeast Asia, Tibet, China, Korea, Japan, and its latest developments in Europe and North America. Special attention will be given to the figure of the Buddha (his life, cultural context, spirituality and philosophy), and to the study of traditional as well as contemporary forms of Buddhism (Zen, Tibetan, Theravada, Pure Land). We will read several classical sources from different schools and selected writings from representatives of contemporary Buddhism (Dalai Lama, Thich Nath Hanh, Walpola Rahula, Shunryu Suzuki, D.T. Suzuki, Bhikkhu Gunaratana, Bhikkhu Bodhi, Joanna Macy, etc).

title: Religions of the African Diaspora
description: This course is an examination and appreciation of the extent and depth of the African religious heritage of the Americas: from Brazil to the Caribbean to the United States. Students will be required to carry out extensive library research on a particular people of African descent in order to discuss such topics as the ideas of God and Spirit; spiritual growth through divination, initiation, sacrifice and trance; the magical and medical uses of spiritual forces; and the role of religion in the struggle for freedom and justice.

title: Early Chinese Political Thought
description: This course explores the history of early Chinese political thought with a special focus on the close relationship between ethics and politics in early Chinese thought and the contemporary relevance of these views. Texts from the four major schools of thought in classical China are examined, with special attention given to the way in which political philosophy was informed by a diverse range of competing accounts of human nature and morality in the Confucian, Mohist, Daoist, and Legalist traditions. The course also examines the greatest Chinese military classic of all time: Sunziâs Art of War. In addition to a close reading of these primary texts, students will read and discuss contemporary sources that constructively apply traditional Chinese views to questions of public policy and governance in both East Asia and the U.S. today.

title: Martyrdom: Dying for God
description: A readiness to die for God is a phenomenon that stretches over the centuries and continues to be known and accepted today across a variety of religious and secular traditions. This course, focusing on martyrdom in the three so-called monotheistic faiths (Judaism, Christianity, and Islam), examines a number of case studies of martyrdom across the centuries with special attention to firsthand accounts. Among the themes to be considered: the various kinds of martyrdom (e.g. active/passive, lethal, suicidal, etc.); the motives of the martyr (spiritual, political, etc.); the way martyrdom is remembered and celebrated by religious communities; and the ways in which religious leaders/scholars define the parameters of acceptable martyrdom. Finally, we will consider the factors driving the phenomenon of martyrdom on the global stage today.

title: Buddhism and Film
description: This course will explore some classic Asian Buddhist literary themes as they are expressed through films from Tibet, Korea, Japan and Vietnam, as well as some of Western origins. The films will be supplemented with literary sources such as fiction and aesthetic theory in order to trace the movement of these themes from classic Asian sources into the modern medium of film. A major theoretical focus of the course will be on the unique features of film as an aesthetic medium and how it enables us to "see" the world in a way that conceptual thinking cannot.

title: Magic and Religion in the Greco-Roman World: The Beginnings of Judaism and Christianity
description: The world of the Greeks and Roman was one of endlessly multi-aspected paganism, with its consciousness of a range of gods and goddesses, daimons and spirits moving between their realm and ours. It was also one in which the Hebrew-Israelite-Judaean competed with paganism and continued to struggle to define itselfâand ultimately split into what became Judaism and Christianity. This course will focus on the times, places and literatures that reflect the interface between paganism as it has evolved within the Greco-Roman world and nascent Judaism and Christianity. It pushes toward an understanding of how Judaism and Christianity emerged out of the Hebrew-Israelite-Judaean tradition as two forms of faith each claiming to be the proper continuation of that tradition. It considers how their theological relationshipâtheir competition regarding the Truth regarding divinity and its relationship to humanityâis not only affected by their mutual interface and their theological relationships with paganism but by the political context of the pagan Roman Imperium in which they both develop. This is a world of meeting, divergence, convergence, synthesis, embrace and rejection of religious principles and ideas. It is a world in which verbal distinctions that we take for grantedâsuch as those between magic and religion, myth and theology, superstition and true belief, astronomy and astrologyâhave not yet assumed the place to which they arrived within our vocabulary, over time. Our goal is largely to come to understand what comprise the key elements that distinguish and join these traditions, why and how this vocabulary emerges and evolves, and how the shaping of that vocabulary has affected and continues to affect our sense of what Judaism and Christianity are. Course requirements include being in class, doing the readings, participating when it is appropriateâand writing two 10-12-page tale-home exams/papers, one toward midterm and the other at semesterâs end. Readings will be drawn from a combination of Blackboard and library reserve materials.

title: Christian Social Ethics
description: This course explores the responses of the Christian Churches to social challenges and dilemmas. It will explore the background and emergence of traditions in Christian Social Ethics as well as a range of historical, thematic and methodological approaches. Contributions from Catholic and Protestant thinkers and communities will feature, including examples of the radical Christian tradition. Methods and approaches encountered will include, for example, Catholic Social Teaching, liberation theology and public theology. Throughout the course particular moral dilemmas and case studies will be engaged with â both historical and contemporary. The course will also introduce students to a range of issues and challenges concerning the relationship between Christian churches and the wider âworldâ, including civil, secular and pluralist societies. Social issues explored may include wealth and poverty, war and peace, bioethical dilemmas (such as beginning and end of life issues), race and social exclusion, globalization, migration, and advances in genetic technology.

title: Theo-Humanism: Great Books about God
description: What in the world is theo-humanism? We usually speak of secular humanism. Doesnât that mean that theology and humanism are at odds with each other? Or is the idea that God and human nature stand in mutual opposition but a product of a time now past? This course looks at the human being as object of theological reflection. What can the human being (as a creature who thinks, imagines, experiences, even doubts) tell us about Godâs way of guiding the world? We will proceed by reading a selection of literary classics from Judaism, Christianity, and Islam that look to the human being as a creature possessed of transcendent aspiration: works of history, political treatises, belles-lettres, epic poems, novels, autobiography, etc. What are believers doing when they look to the human venture and take up the humanistic enterprise to explain Godâs ways? The course is designed to equip students with the concepts needed to approach the perennial questions about the human condition as essential part of the theological journey.

title: The Qur'an and Islamic Origins
description: For about a fifth of the people alive in the world today, the Qurâan is the voice of God on earth, giving instructions, warnings, and comfort to the human race. For many more people besides these, the Qurâan influences their lives through a multitude of direct and indirect ways. This course is an introduction to the world of the Qurâan and the late antique context into which it and Islam first appeared. The semester will be divided into uneven thirds. First, we will look at the cultural setting of early Islam in the 6-7th centuries. We will examine the text of the Qurâan itself in detail, noting structures, styles, themes, and patterns. Second, we will turn to classical Islamic scholarship of the Qurâan which belongs to the middle ages, and what this can and cannot tell us about the first Muslims and the Qurâan itself. Finally, we will look at modern critical scholarship of the Qurâan in search of new questions about the Qurâan, its place in history, and its ancient and medieval readers. No one student will agree with all of these classical and modern readings, nor should she. Many of works we will look at are factually incorrect, biased, mere supposition, or even perhaps Islamophobic. Yet everything we will study will ask important questions - whether or not we agree with the conclusions provided. In the end the student will walk away informed with the ability to speak from multiple points of view about the Qurâan and the origins of Islam, whether they are the studentâs own or not. Students of all backgrounds are welcome, but no previous experience with the Qurâan, Arabic, or Islamic studies is required.

title: One Land, Two Faiths: Turkey
description: Dr. Lauve H. Steenhuisen Turkey has been home to several global religious traditions, and has served as the background to some of the most significant religious events in Christian and Islamic history. This course will research theological questions such as: Why did the Eastern Orthodox church split from the Roman Catholic in 1054?; What political problems with Islam were the Christian Crusades addressing?; In what ways are mystical movements reactions to an over-politicization in religion?; What pluralistic infighting did the Christian Nicean Creed resolve?. Turkeyâs rich cultural, geographic, and religious heritage will surround us as we ask--Why is there no longer a global Islamic caliph? Through site visits and course assignments, students will be encouraged to connect theology to geopolitics in fresh ways.

title: Peacemaker and Peacemaking
description: This course studies the personal and social character of the religious commitment to nonviolence in relation to concrete struggles for social justice. We will take up a trajectory of reflection and practice that extends from M.K. Gandhi through Martin Luther King, Jr., and then, with important modifications, onto to Roman Catholic prophetic witness in the works and life of Dorothy Day, Thomas Merton, and Daniel Berrigan. Buddhist and Muslim approaches to nonviolence and political life follow--with and without overlap. The course concludes with a considered look at Gene Sharp's "secular" vision of nonviolent resistance to tyranny and its reputed influence for the Arab Spring.

title: State & The Veil: Policies
description: Dr. Lauve H. Steenhuisen Turkey has experienced two political revolutions with regard to the veil in the last century--Ataturkâs secular revolution and the rise of Islamic piety in the 1990s. This course will examine the politics of the veil in three nation states, with the primary focus on our host site Turkey. As we visit Istanbul, Ankara, etc. we will discuss how state actors prohibit or promote veiling, and how such actions spark countercultural revolutions. We will analyze how religion can serve as both a liberalizing and conservatizing force, and the role of gender in mobilizing social groups. France as a European case study will form the background for examinations of how colonial history in Algeria and historical religious wars combined to create a public policy inadvertently removing educational access for many Muslim girls. As we travel around Turkey as a learning community, we will examine if veiling is more about religion or politics from the perspective of the state.

title: Judaism Under Crescent & Cross
description: Many of the defining characteristics of contemporary Jewish civilization were formed during the long Middle Ages when Jews lived under Christian and Muslim rule. This course is will explore some of the central themes in the social and religious history of the Jewish people during this period, with special attention given to the complex relationship that Jews had with their host societies. Topics will include the primary points of conflict and cooperation between the three monotheistic religions, the development of Jewish self-government and communal organization, and the major currents in Jewish intellectual culture.

title: The Poetry of the Bible
description: The Bible contains a distinctive and highly crafted body of poetry that has, for most of its history, gone unrecognized as poetry. Because the Bible has been read primarily as a religious text, questions of literary form and poetic qualities have been largely ignored, as interpreters have focused on more obviously religious content such as theology and ethics. But we now know that the Bible is not only a foundational source for Jewish and Christian religion, but is also a work of literature in which literary form is as important as content. The course will focus on the poetry of the Bible (most especially the Hebrew Bible or Old Testament), including the book of Job, the prophets, the love poetry of the Song of Songs, and the psalms. These are endlessly fascinating works, in which poetic form is used to explore large questions of suffering, love, justice, etc. The course will be run as a genuine seminar, in which all members of the class will contribute equally to our analysis and discussions of the texts.

title: New Testament Seminar: History and Apocalyptic
description: Three seminars, offered in different semesters, will focus on different portions of the New Testament but will address similar issues; (1) the social and historical contexts of the writings, (2) the methodology necessary for uncovering their theological messages, and (3) these messages themselves.

title: New Testament Seminar: History and Apocalyptic
description: Three seminars, offered in different semesters, will focus on different portions of the New Testament but will address similar issues; (1) the social and historical contexts of the writings, (2) the methodology necessary for uncovering their theological messages, and (3) these messages themselves.

title: Systematic Theology
description: The course in systematic theology explores both the major doctrines of Christianity and issues in contemporary theological method. Through a consideration of Christian doctrine, it seeks to clarify the interconnection and the coherence of the various dimensions of Christian belief. The systematic approach of the course provides 1) a framework for comparing and evaluating different theological opinions and methods, and 2) a means of addressing pressing contemporary issues in light of the wisdom of the Christian tradition.

title: Approaches to Religion
description: An examination of theories about the nature of religion: exploring how religion may be identified, interpreted, and understood. We will survey the history of the interpretation of religion, and apply theories to a variety of human beliefs and actions in order to appreciate and criticize their cogency.

title: Comparative Mysticism
description: This course is designed to introduce students to major aspects of mystical teachings and practices from a comparative perspective. Our most general task will be to reflect on the definition of âmysticismâ in contradistinction with kin concepts such as âreligionâ, âspiritualityâ, and âesoterism.â This will lead us to explore the various facets and dimensions of mysticism in world religions, its expressions in the ways of knowledge, love and action, and some of its main manifestations in Semitic monotheistic and Indic religions. We will engage a few classical key-texts within the context of an inter-religious âdialogueâ among some of the major representatives from the Hindu, Buddhist, Christian and Islamic mystical traditions.

title: Faith, Social Justice, and Public Life
description: How does faith inform public debates on social justice in American politics? How should religious leaders and politicians engage the political process while maintaining their moral witness? This course, taught by Rev. Jim Wallis, founder of Sojourners, will address the intersection of religion, society, and politics in the United States, across a series of critical issue areas. Topics to be addressed include poverty, education, healthcare, race, and gender. Frequent guest presentations will expose students to practitionerâs perspectives on these and other issues. The course will be videotaped, edited, and presented for wider distribution. Admitted students will be required to sign a release form. For information, email Randolph Pelzer at jrp52@georgetown.edu.

title: Christian Thinkers: Karl Rahner
description: This course will study the theology of Karl Rahner, commonly considered the preeminent Catholic systematic theologian of our time, and influential in the development of Christian theology generally. Rahner articulates the structure and dynamics of faith in a way that shows the interconnection of the various dimensions of faith. In doing so he aims to explain why it makes sense to believe in God, in Christ, and in the Church; to understand oneself to be called by God; to pray, and in making life choices to seek to discern Godâs will. So a spirituality is built into his theology, which also, because systematic, provides a framework for situating various developments in theology. It should be noted that reading Rahner, whose writing is dense, makes for a demanding exercise.

title: Christian Thinkers: Karl Rahner
description: Karl Rahner, S.J., called âThe Church Father of Roman Catholic Theology in the Twentieth Century,â has had a profound influence in the development of Christian Theology in our time. As did that of Thomas Aquinas for many generations, Rahnerâs theology now commonly serves as an authoritative point of reference for theologians in the Roman Catholic community and, as well, an important reference for some theologians of other Christian denominations. The course will begin with Rahnerâs fundamental analysis of the human personâs relation to God and proceed through his treatment of revelation, faith, Christ, church, sacrament, spirituality and fundamental ethics. It should be noted that Rahnerâs writing makes serious demands and would not please a facile or faint hearted reader.

title: Senior Seminar: Religious Pluralism
description: This course will study the relationship between Christianity and other major world religions. Offered from the Christian perspective, it will address the philosophical and theological issues and conflicts that arise in a religiously pluralistic environment. This will include an historical examination of the Christian disposition towards the other religions, the problem of conflicting truth claims, the nature of salvation in the religions, the role of religious language, and the process of interreligious dialogue. The course will critically assess current Christian theological thinking about the other major traditions and offer possibilities for interreligious understanding. (Madigan) This seminar for senior theology majors in the Religion and Culture track will study the contemporary issue of "pluralism,â that is, how we are to think of and respond to a plurality of religious claims and experiences. This particular section of the seminar will concentrate on theories of plurality and otherness that are not from the explicitly theological tradition, and on instances of the encounter with religious otherness that are not explicitly religious. This semester we will examine the theories of William James, Richard Rorty, and Michel Foucault, and we will then apply them to a variety of films. (Ruf)

title: Senior Honors Thesis: Theology
description: For senior theology majors who wish to write an honors thesis under the direction of a member of the department. Proposals should be submitted to the department in the Spring of the student's junior year.

title: Senior Thesis: Theology
description: This course is available only in case of conflict with the Senior Seminar. Permission needed from the Chair.

title: Comparative Ethics
description: Comparative Ethics -- This course introduces students to the discipline and practice of comparative ethics. Students will examine and discuss a variety of influential primary sources in ethics from a variety of traditions, with a special focus on different accounts of virtue and self-cultivation practices. Students will also read studies in comparative ethics from the disciplines of religious studies, philosophy, and theology, with special attention given to the question of why comparative work is worthwhile and to methodological questions in comparative work. The overall aim of the course is not only to engage in the comparative study of ethical views and practices from different traditions, but to examine and evaluate different approaches to comparative ethics, reasons for doing comparative ethics and its practical value in a contemporary setting, and the place of comparative ethics in the wider disciplines of religious studies, philosophy, and theology.

title: Buddhism & Christianity in the Secular World
description: This course examines how Buddhism and Christianity are situated in the secular world by focusing on their respective relationships with science. The frequent tensions between Christianity and modern science arise from their intimate historical and intellectual relationship. The increasing and enthusiastic dialogue between Buddhism and science, on the other hand, is ironically aided by their lack of mutual history. More substantially, the contemporary Buddhist accommodation of modern science exercises some important Buddhist philosophical principles that are absent in Christian and scientific conceptions of truth. We will begin our examination of these issues by first theorizing the idea of "secularity," which arose in conscious opposition to Christian religious authority. The final objective of the course is to see how the concept of secularism itself presumes competition between Christianity and science, and how Buddhist philosophical and historical concepts offer one fruitful way of breaching the regnant "religious versus secular" paradigm. Partial reading list: Charles Taylor, A Secular Age The Cowherds, Moonshadows: Conventional Truth in Buddhist Philosophy The Dalai Lama, The Universe in a Single Atom John Haught, Christianity and Science: Toward a Theology of Nature

title: Religion and Violence
description: Religious traditions are ambiguous: in many of the conflicts around the world today, religions play major roles either in motivating and justifying violence or in inspiring work towards nonviolent resolutions. This course will explore the relationship between religion and violence, with attention to both historical background and contemporary conflicts. The course will discuss the ambiguous character of religious belief and practice, theories of religiously motivated violence, and strategies of nonviolent response to violence. A variety of scriptural and contemporary perspectives on violence will be examined.

title: Psychology of Religion
description: This course will examine the spaces where individual psychology meets group dynamics in religious life. This includes such familiar topics as conversion, ecstasy and mystical experience, initiation, the master-disciple relationship, charisma, love and obedience, the cult and the sect, self-surrender and self-sacrifice, as well as others. We shall try to determine on a case-by-case basis whether the so-called âreligiousâ experience is reducible to individual psychology, group psychology or neither. We shall begin with an overview of the various psychological theories that have been used in the last one hundred years and the students will focus on one among such approaches as psychoanalysis, object-relation theory, neuropsychology or others. Students will develop tools for analyzing religious phenomena in a variety of contexts ranging from traditional sources (such as scripture) to popular media (such as films or even YouTube). Using these tools students will be able to submit and deliver scholarly presentations in professional settings such as the Mid-Atlantic regional AAR or perhaps even national meetings.

title: Twentieth and Twenty-first Century Theology
description: This âgraduate-upper-undergraduateâ seminar will survey the status quaestionis of the contemporary problem of nature and grace according to Catholic theology. It will offer a critique of contemporary models and endeavor to develop a model that addresses the critique. It will then apply the new model to the problem of the âtruthâ and âsaving efficacyâ of non-Christian religions. The course will focus principally on Karl Rahner, Hans Urs von Balthasar, and âDominus Jesus,â but in so doing it will delve into Sts Athanasius, Irenaeus, Justin the Apologist, and Thomas Aquinas. The course emerges from a book manuscript I have recently completed, which expands and deepens my article: âMediating the Non-Christian Religions: Congar, Balthasar, Nature and Grace,â in Yves Congar: Theologian of the Church, ed. Gabriel Flynn (Louvain: Peeters Press/W. B. Eerdmans, 2005), 401-26. [French ed: âUn trait de lâunion avec les religions non chrÃ©tiennes: Congar, Balthasar, nature et grÃ¢ce.â Yves Congar: thÃ©ologien de lâÃ©glise. Paris: Les Ãditions du Cerf, 2007: 357-79]

title: Two Councils: TRENT AND VATICAN II
description: The purpose of the seminar is to provide a close examination of two meetings that had profound impact upon Catholicism but also upon other churches and, especially for Trent, upon broader culture. By comparing and contrasting these two councils, which are often portrayed as polar opposites, the unique qualities of each become clearer and the similarities as well as the differences emerge. Moreover, each council acts as a mirror into crucial periods of Western history, a fact that demonstrates the close relationship between religion and general culture. The course is in fact heavily historical. If you like history, you may well like the course. If not, not. Special attention will be paid to the difference between the language-game of Trent and that of Vatican II and to the significance of that difference for interpreting each of the councils. The ultimate question we ask for both councils, So what? Class procedure: lecture and discussion. The course is a seminar. It is therefore a course that requires full participation each week, based on close reading of the assigned texts. You must do the reading each week and answer the question based on it. Otherwise, you will be lost. I think you will find that for such an advanced course, the reading load is certainly not excessive. But please note that there is an assignment for the first class, January 8. Be prepared to compare and contrast the two readings.

title: Rel. Pluralism: Phil/Theo Found
description: (I) The course begins with a discussion of religious diversity as a sociological fact and its implications for the academy, society, and theology. (II) Next, it examines the concept of religious pluralism as a philosophical and theological category. It seeks (1) to understand what is meant by âreligious pluralismâ as a theory to account for religious diversity; (2) to study its roots in modern and post-modern philosophies; and (3) to hypothesize its possible foundations in a pluralistic conception of deity (philosophical and theological foundations of religious pluralism). (III) The course then examines whether truth claims can/should be made in the context of religious pluralism, and if so, how (theological epistemology). (IV) The course moves on to examine the implications of religious pluralism for the interpretation of sacred scriptures (interreligious hermeneutics). (V) Lastly, the course discusses new theological disciplines in response to religious pluralism (theology of religions and comparative theology). The course is conducted as a seminar consisting of a close reading of key texts on each of these five themes and a discussion of the issues they raised. Religious pluralism will be studied as a theological issue within a particular religious tradition (e.g., Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, etc.) and across these religious traditions. Students shall make brief presentations of the readings set for each class and at the end write a research paper (25-30 double-spaced pages), as opposed to a reflection paper or a book review, on a theme connected with one or several of the five issues under study. It is hoped that this course will also explain the rationale of the doctoral program in theological and religious studies at GU.

title: Christian Theologies Responsive to Islam
description: Discussions of pluralism tend to minimize the particularity of each relationship between two religious traditions in favor of an exploration of general themes. Since Islam emerges, at least in part, as a reforming critique of Christianity -- for the believing Muslim, this is a divinely initiated reform -- the relationship between the two is unique. This seminar will explore different proposals, both historical and contemporary, for understanding that relationship, as well as constructive moves in Christian theology that could be described as responsive to the critique rather than simply as reactive or defensive.

title: God in Christian Thought
description: This is a course on the doctrine of God in Christian thought. It explores the rich tradition of "God-talk" by Christian theologians, mystics, and philosophers throughout the centuries. Emphasis will be on method (how to speak about that which transcends speech and thought); on the relationship between faith and reason (how revelation is understood and what role philosophy can play); on types of religious experience of Godâespecially liturgical, mystical, and aesthetic experiences (how they inform our understanding of God); and on ethics (the implications particular understandings of God has for who we are to be and how we are to act). The course is designed primarily as a lecture course with both primary and secondary readings assigned and with ample time for discussion. We shall begin with Tertullianâs question, "What does Athens have to do with Jerusalem?" and with the doctrine of the Trinity as formulated and interpreted in the fourth century. We shall then proceed chronologically through major thinkers and movements, focusing on significant paradigm shifts in imaging, speaking about, and experiencing the divine. Close attention will be paid to the details of the texts, understanding them in their historical, cultural contexts. Attention will also be paid to issue of pluralism within Christianity: many of the doctrines of God we shall be studying are very different from each other, so what makes each a specifically Christian doctrine God?

title: Skepticism/Religious Pluralism in Islam
description: No concept is more central to the venture of Islam than knowledge: knowledge that guides to the good as revealed by God and discovered in nature. But knowledge, however defined, has never been an impregnable fortress. In this course, we will explore the particular ways in which knowledge has been constructed and defended in Islam but also the ways it has been challenged and attacked. Skepticism, then, has been a familiar part of Islamâs intellectual heritage. Moreover, as a phenomenon, it's been intimately connected to religious pluralism. Through a close reading of primary texts in their varied historical contexts, we will examine this relation between skepticism and pluralism in Islam over the centuries and what it might suggest for our understanding of religion.

title: Sufism: Spirituality and Society
description: Sufism: Spirituality and Society (THEO 707); T 930am-12pm, NN 202; Paul L. Heck Course Description: Sufism has been described as Islamâs mysticism, but the identification as such is problematic. In this course, we consider the various themes of Sufism over the centuries, including concepts of sainthood, companionship, martyrdom, love, and knowledge. Sufism is never a self-standing phenomenon, so it will be important to consider ways in which its practitioners have viewed the law of Islam and other actors and institutions in society, including the state; and also how Sufism has been presented at a time when modernists (both secular and religious) have viewed the beliefs and practices of Sufism as atavistic and not religiously true.
